topics.title: Stored-Program Computer Architecture - General-Purpose Cache-Based Microprocessor Architecture - Performance Metrics and Benchmarks - Moore's Law - Pipelining - Superscalarity - SIMD - Memory Hierarchies - Cache
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stored-Program Computer Architecture - General-Purpose Cache-Based Microprocessor Architecture - Performance Metrics and Benchmarks - Moore's Law - Pipelining - Superscalarity - SIMD - Memory Hierarchies - Cache

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Stored-Program Computer Architecture (von Neumann Architecture)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The *Stored-Program Computer Architecture*, proposed by John von Neumann in 1945, is a computer design paradigm in which **both the instructions (program) and data reside in a single, shared memory** that is accessed through a common bus. The CPU fetches instructions sequentially from memory, decodes them, and executes them using the ALU, with results stored back into the same memory.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a chef in a kitchen. The recipes (instructions) and the ingredients (data) are all stored in the *same* set of shelves (memory). The chef (CPU) can pull a recipe card, follow it to handle ingredients, and put modified ingredients back on the same shelves. This unified storage means the chef can dynamically choose *which* recipe to cook next — making the system extremely flexible. This is the "stored-program" idea: the program is just data that the machine itself can read and modify.

**Key Properties of von Neumann Architecture:**
- **Single memory** for instructions and data.
- **Single bus** for instruction fetch and data access (creates the *von Neumann bottleneck*).
- **Sequential instruction execution** (one instruction at a time in the basic form).
- **Programmable** — instructions are binary-encoded and treated as data.

## 1.2 General-Purpose Cache-Based Microprocessor Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A *General-Purpose Cache-Based Microprocessor* is a CPU that incorporates **one or more levels of high-speed cache memory (L1, L2, L3)** between the registers and main memory (DRAM). It is designed to execute a wide variety of workloads by exploiting temporal and spatial locality, while keeping the architectural state (registers, program counter) general enough to run arbitrary software.

**Intuition:** Think of the CPU as a busy office worker. The desk (registers) holds only a few papers, the side cabinet (cache) holds the current project's files, the office library (main memory) holds all files, and a far-away warehouse (disk) holds archives. The worker doesn't fetch from the warehouse every time — they keep *recent* files in the cabinet (cache) for instant access.

## 1.3 Performance Metrics and Benchmarks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Performance metrics* are quantitative measures used to evaluate computer system speed, efficiency, and throughput. *Benchmarks* are standardized programs (SPEC CPU, LINPACK, PARSEC, Geekbench) used to compare systems reproducibly.

**Core Performance Metrics:**
- **CPU Execution Time** $(T_{CPU})$
- **Clock Cycle Time** $(T_{clk})$
- **Clock Frequency** $(f)$
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)**
- **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)**
- **MFLOPS / GFLOPS / TFLOPS**
- **Speedup $(S)$** and **Efficiency $(E)$**
- **Throughput** (instructions/sec, requests/sec)

## 1.4 Moore's Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Moore's Law*, articulated by Gordon Moore (1965), observes that the **number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every 18–24 months**, leading to exponential growth in compute capability and a corresponding drop in cost per transistor.

**Intuition:** Every two years, engineers pack twice as many microscopic switches into the same-sized silicon chip — like fitting twice the number of lanes on the same stretch of highway. More transistors = faster, more parallel, more energy-efficient processors.

## 1.5 Pipelining

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Pipelining* is an implementation technique in which **multiple instructions are overlapped in execution** by partitioning the instruction processing into stages (e.g., IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB). Each stage operates on a different instruction concurrently, analogous to an assembly line.

**Intuition:** A car wash with 5 stations. While car A is being dried, car B is being soaped, car C is being rinsed, and so on. Five cars complete in roughly the time it would take to wash one car sequentially — vastly improving throughput.

## 1.6 Superscalarity

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** *Superscalar* processors **dispatch and execute more than one instruction per clock cycle** from a sequential instruction stream, using multiple parallel execution units. The hardware dynamically checks for data dependencies and issues instructions out-of-order when safe.

**Intuition:** Instead of one cashier serving one customer at a time, a superscalar processor is like a checkout counter with **multiple cashiers** working in parallel — all serving the same line (instruction stream) at once.

## 1.7 SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *SIMD* is a parallel computing architecture in which a **single instruction operates simultaneously on multiple data elements** packed into wide vector registers. Modern x86 CPUs use SSE/AVX; ARM uses NEON/SVE; GPUs use CUDA cores / wide SIMT lanes.

**Intuition:** Imagine a teacher asking 32 students to each add 1 to their own number. Instead of calling on each student one at a time, the teacher shouts the instruction once, and **all 32 students apply it in parallel** — same operation, different data.

## 1.8 Memory Hierarchies

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A *Memory Hierarchy* is an arrangement of storage technologies ordered by **speed, cost, and capacity**, with the fastest, smallest, and most expensive memory closest to the CPU and the slowest, largest, cheapest farthest away. Typical levels: **Registers → L1 → L2 → L3 → Main Memory (DRAM) → SSD/Disk → Tape/Cloud**.

**Intuition:** It is like a layered wardrobe: the watch you wear (registers) is fastest to access; the bedside drawer (L1) is next; the closet (L2) is slower; the warehouse (disk) is slowest. The principle is **locality**: most programs access a small subset of memory most of the time.

## 1.9 Cache

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A *Cache* is a small, fast memory that stores **copies of data from frequently used main memory locations** to reduce the average memory access time. It exploits **temporal locality** (recently accessed items are likely to be re-accessed) and **spatial locality** (items near recently accessed items are likely to be accessed soon).

**Cache Mapping Schemes:**
- **Direct Mapped** — each block maps to exactly one cache line.
- **Fully Associative** — a block can map to any cache line.
- **Set Associative** — a block maps to a specific *set*, can occupy any *way* within that set.

**Cache Performance Events:**
- **Hit** — data found in cache (fast).
- **Miss** — data not in cache (must fetch from next level).
- **Hit Rate $(h)$** / **Miss Rate $(1-h)$**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CPU Performance Fundamentals (Why and How)

The **Why:** Every architecture decision — pipelining, caches, SIMD, multi-core — is fundamentally an attempt to *reduce CPU execution time* or *increase throughput*. The canonical performance equation expresses this in measurable hardware terms.

**The How — the foundational performance chain:**

1. **CPU Execution Time** is the ultimate metric visible to the user.
2. It decomposes into **Instruction Count (IC)**, **CPI**, and **Clock Cycle Time**.
3. **IC** is determined by the *ISA and compiler*.
4. **CPI** is determined by the *microarchitecture* (pipelining, cache, branch prediction).
5. **Clock Cycle Time** is determined by the *technology and hardware design*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | CPU execution time (full equation) | seconds |
| 2 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | Clock frequency | Hz |
| 3 | $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f}$ | Alternative form using frequency | seconds |
| 4 | $MIPS = \dfrac{IC}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| 5 | $MFLOPS = \dfrac{FN_{ops}}{T_{CPU} \times 10^{6}}$ | Floating-point throughput | MFLOPS |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | Speedup of a new system | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{S}{p} = \dfrac{T_{old}}{p \cdot T_{new}}$ | Parallel efficiency (p = processors) | 0 to 1 |
| 8 | $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}$ | Amdahl's Law (f = parallel fraction, s = speedup of that fraction) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $S_{pipeline}^{ideal} = k$ | Ideal k-stage pipeline speedup (k stages) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $CPI_{pipeline} = CPI_{ideal} + \text{stalls per instruction}$ | Effective pipeline CPI | cycles/instr |
| 11 | $AMAT = T_{hit} + MR \cdot T_{miss}$ | Average Memory Access Time (cache) | seconds/cycles |
| 12 | $T_{access}^{hierarchical} = h_1 T_1 + (1-h_1)\big[h_2 T_2 + (1-h_2)T_3\big]$ | Multi-level access time (recursion) | seconds |
| 13 | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/d}$ | Moore's Law — transistors after t years (doubling period d) | transistors |
| 14 | $CPI_{superscalar} = \dfrac{\text{total cycles}}{\text{total retired instructions}}$ | Issue rate bound (≥ 1/n for n-wide) | cycles/instr |
| 15 | $Speedup_{SIMD} = \dfrac{\text{vector length}}{1 + \text{overhead ratio}}$ | SIMD theoretical speedup | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never use vertical pipes `|` inside formula tables. Use `\vert` for absolute value, conditional probability, etc. The table above strictly avoids `|` symbols for KTU PDF/HTML compatibility.

## 2.2 Moore's Law — Mathematical Form

The **Why:** It establishes a *quantitative* basis for predicting future compute capability, which architects use to plan for parallelism, multi-core, and specialized accelerators.

**Exponential form:**

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t / d}$$

Where:
- $N(t)$ = number of transistors after time $t$
- $N_0$ = initial transistor count
- $d$ = doubling period (≈ 18–24 months, traditionally 24)
- $t$ = elapsed time in same unit as $d$

**Cost implication:** Cost per transistor falls approximately as $1/N(t)$, while performance per watt has historically doubled every ~18 months (Koomey's corollary).

## 2.3 Pipelining — Latency vs Throughput

The **Why:** Pipelining does *not* reduce the latency of a single instruction; it increases the **instruction throughput** (instructions completed per cycle, IPC).

**The How — pipeline CPI components:**

$$CPI_{pipeline} = CPI_{base} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{stalls per instr from hazard } i)$$

- **Structural hazards** — resource conflicts.
- **Data hazards** — RAW, WAR, WAW dependencies.
- **Control hazards** — branch mispredictions.

## 2.4 Cache and Memory Hierarchy Mathematics

The **Why:** Modern CPUs spend up to **40–60%** of execution time waiting on memory. AMAT is the unifying metric for evaluating any cache design.

**AMAT (Average Memory Access Time):**

$$AMAT = T_{hit} + MR \cdot T_{miss-penalty}$$

For a 2-level hierarchy (L1 + main memory):

$$T_{avg} = h_1 \cdot T_1 + (1 - h_1)\big[h_2 \cdot T_2 + (1 - h_2) \cdot T_3\big]$$

**Hit time ratio** — critical design constraint:
- Lower-level caches → **smaller, faster, but with lower hit rate**.
- Higher-level caches → **larger, slower, but with higher hit rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** AMAT is used by architects at Intel, AMD, Apple, and ARM to size each cache level, choose associativity, and select replacement policies (LRU, pseudo-LRU, RRIP). Even a 5% increase in L2 hit rate can yield 10–15% performance gains in SPEC CPU workloads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Performance Equation

**Starting premise:** Total CPU time = (Instructions) × (Cycles per Instruction) × (Seconds per Cycle).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= IC \times CPI \times T_{clk} \\[4pt]
T_{CPU} &= \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f} \quad \left(\text{since } f = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}\right) \\[4pt]
T_{CPU} &= \dfrac{IC}{IPC \times f} \quad \left(\text{since } IPC = \dfrac{1}{CPI}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**
1. We start with three independent factors that together fully determine runtime.
2. $IC$ depends on the *algorithm*, *ISA*, and *compiler* — not on hardware microarchitecture.
3. $CPI$ is the *microarchitectural* lever (pipelining, issue width, branch predictor quality).
4. $T_{clk}$ is the *circuit/technology* lever (process node, pipeline depth, voltage).
5. **Conclusion:** To halve $T_{CPU}$, you can halve any *one* factor, or combine partial improvements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Killer exam line:** *"Performance is not just clock speed"* — doubling $f$ alone rarely doubles performance, because memory stalls (cache misses) do not scale with clock.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Amdahl's Law

**Starting premise:** Let $f$ be the fraction of execution time that *can* be sped up by a factor of $s$ (e.g., parallelized, vectorized, or accelerated). The remaining $(1 - f)$ runs at the original speed.

**Execution time after improvement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{new} &= (1 - f) \cdot T_{old} + \dfrac{f}{s} \cdot T_{old} \\[4pt]
T_{new} &= T_{old} \left[ (1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{s} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speedup $S$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}} \\[4pt]
S &= \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{old}\left[(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{s}\right]} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad S &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key limiting case** (infinite parallel speedup, $s \to \infty$):

$$
S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - f}
$$

**Numerical example:** If $f = 0.9$ (90% parallelizable) and $s = 100$:
- $S = 1 / [0.1 + 0.9/100] = 1 / [0.1 + 0.009] = 1 / 0.109 = 9.17$
- Even with infinite cores, $S_{max} = 1/(1-0.9) = 10$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often apply Amdahl's Law to *symmetric multi-core* and forget that memory contention and synchronization overhead further reduce $s$. Use Gustafson's Law for fixed-workload scaling.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Pipeline Speedup

**Setup:** A non-pipelined processor takes $k$ cycles per instruction (k = number of stages). An ideal k-stage pipeline completes **one instruction per cycle** after the initial fill latency.

**Time for $n$ instructions:**

- Non-pipelined: $T_{np} = n \cdot k$ cycles.
- Pipelined (ideal): $T_{pipe} = (k - 1) + n$ cycles (k−1 fill, then one per cycle).

**Speedup:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{ideal} &= \dfrac{n \cdot k}{(k - 1) + n} \\[4pt]
\lim_{n \to \infty} S_{ideal} &= k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effective CPI with stalls:**

$$
CPI_{eff} = CPI_{ideal} + \text{stalls} = 1 + \text{stalls per instruction}
$$

**Pipeline efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{k}{k + (n-1) \cdot \text{stalls per instr}} \cdot 100\%
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of AMAT for a 2-Level Cache

**Setup:** L1 has hit time $T_1$, hit rate $h_1$. On L1 miss, we go to L2 (hit time $T_2$, hit rate $h_2$). On L2 miss, we go to main memory ($T_3$).

**Average time per L1 access:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= h_1 \cdot T_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot T_{L1-miss} \\[4pt]
T_{L1-miss} &= h_2 \cdot T_2 + (1 - h_2) \cdot T_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitute back:**

$$
\therefore \quad T_{avg} = h_1 T_1 + (1 - h_1)\big[h_2 T_2 + (1 - h_2) T_3\big]
$$

**Numerical example:**
- $T_1 = 1$ ns, $h_1 = 0.95$
- $T_2 = 5$ ns, $h_2 = 0.98$
- $T_3 = 100$ ns (DRAM)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{avg} &= 0.95 \times 1 + 0.05 \times [0.98 \times 5 + 0.02 \times 100] \\[4pt]
&= 0.95 + 0.05 \times [4.9 + 2.0] \\[4pt]
&= 0.95 + 0.05 \times 6.9 \\[4pt]
&= 0.95 + 0.345 \\[4pt]
&= 1.295 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Even with 95% L1 hit rate, the L1 miss penalty (60.9 ns) is **47× the L1 hit time**. This is why modern CPUs aggressively prefetch, increase associativity, and use inclusive/exclusive cache coherence protocols.

---

## 3.5 Moore's Law Numerical Computation

**Given:** Initial transistor count in 1971 (Intel 4004) = 2,300. Doubling period $d = 2$ years. Find transistor count in 2024 ($t = 53$ years).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(53) &= 2300 \cdot 2^{53/2} \\[4pt]
&= 2300 \cdot 2^{26.5} \\[4pt]
&= 2300 \times 94{,}915{,}849 \\[4pt]
&\approx 2.18 \times 10^{11} \text{ transistors}
\end{aligned}
$$

This aligns with modern server chips (e.g., Apple M3 Ultra ≈ 134 billion, Nvidia H100 ≈ 80 billion), confirming the empirical trend.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Parallelism Technique

| Technique | Granularity | Best For | Key Bottleneck | KTU Tag |
|-----------|------------|----------|----------------|---------|
| **Pipelining** | Instruction-level | All sequential code | Data/control hazards | ILP |
| **Superscalar** | Instruction-level (multiple issue) | Code with abundant ILP | Dependency chains | ILP |
| **SIMD** | Data-parallel | Vectorizable loops (DSP, ML) | Non-vectorizable branches | DLP |
| **Multi-core (MIMD)** | Thread-level | Independent tasks | Amdahl's fraction, coherence | TLP |
| **GPU (SIMT)** | Data-parallel + thread | Massively parallel kernels | Memory bandwidth, divergence | DLP/TLP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Von Neumann Architecture (Stored-Program Computer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU Central Processing Unit"] --> CU["Control Unit CU"]
    CPU --> ALU["ALU Arithmetic Logic Unit"]
    CPU --> REG["Registers PC IR ACC"]
    MEM["Main Memory Unified"] -->|Instructions and Data| CPU
    CPU -->|Address Bus| MEM
    MEM -->|Data Bus| CPU
    IO["Input Output Devices"] <-->|Via Controller| MEM
    EXT["Secondary Storage Disk SSD"] <--> MEM
```

**Explanation of nodes:**
- **CPU** contains the Control Unit, ALU, and Registers (PC, IR, Accumulator).
- **Main Memory** holds *both* instructions and data — the defining feature.
- **I/O** and **Secondary Storage** are connected through controllers to main memory.

---

## 4.2 General-Purpose Cache-Based Microprocessor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CORE["CPU Core"] --> REG["Registers 32 or 64 x 64 bit"]
    REG --> L1["L1 Cache 32 KB Split I and D"]
    L1 --> L2["L2 Cache 256 KB to 1 MB Unified"]
    L2 --> L3["L3 Cache 8 to 64 MB Shared"]
    L3 --> CTRL["Memory Controller"]
    CTRL --> DRAM["DRAM Main Memory 8 to 128 GB"]
    DRAM --> SSD["NVMe SSD 1 to 8 TB"]
    SSD --> NET["Network or Cloud Storage"]
    L1 -.->|Hit Returns Data| CORE
    L2 -.->|Miss Fallback| L1
    L3 -.->|Miss Fallback| L2
```

**Explanation:** Modern CPUs use a 3-level cache hierarchy. L1 is split into instruction and data caches for parallel access. L3 is shared across cores for inter-core communication efficiency.

---

## 4.3 5-Stage Instruction Pipeline (MIPS-style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cycle1["Cycle 1"]
        I1["I1 IF"]
    end
    subgraph cycle2["Cycle 2"]
        I1a["I1 ID"] ~~~ I2["I2 IF"]
    end
    subgraph cycle3["Cycle 3"]
        I1b["I1 EX"] ~~~ I2a["I2 ID"] ~~~ I3["I3 IF"]
    end
    subgraph cycle4["Cycle 4"]
        I1c["I1 MEM"] ~~~ I2b["I2 EX"] ~~~ I3a["I3 ID"] ~~~ I4["I4 IF"]
    end
    subgraph cycle5["Cycle 5"]
        I1d["I1 WB"] ~~~ I2c["I2 MEM"] ~~~ I3b["I3 EX"] ~~~ I4a["I4 ID"] ~~~ I5["I5 IF"]
    end
    cycle1 --> cycle2 --> cycle3 --> cycle4 --> cycle5
```

**Explanation:** At cycle 5, five instructions are in-flight simultaneously across the five stages. Steady-state IPC = 1 (one instruction completed per cycle).

---

## 4.4 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["Registers 1 to 4 KB One cycle 1 ns"]
    L1["L1 Cache 32 to 64 KB One to two cycles 1 to 2 ns"]
    L2["L2 Cache 256 KB to 1 MB Ten to twenty cycles 5 to 10 ns"]
    L3["L3 Cache 8 to 64 MB Forty to eighty cycles 20 to 40 ns"]
    DRAM["DRAM 8 to 128 GB Two hundred cycles 100 ns"]
    SSD["NVMe SSD 1 to 8 TB Ten to fifty microseconds"]
    HDD["HDD or Tape TB to PB One to ten milliseconds"]
    TOP --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> DRAM --> SSD --> HDD
```

**Explanation:** Capacity grows downward; speed grows upward. The cost per bit and per access also grows upward.

---

## 4.5 Superscalar vs SIMD vs Multi-core

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SS["Superscalar ILP Multiple Issue per Cycle"]
        SS1["Issue Logic"]
        SS2["ALU 1"]
        SS3["ALU 2"]
        SS4["FPU 1"]
        SS5["LSU"]
    end
    subgraph SIMDBlock["SIMD DLP One Instruction Many Data"]
        SM1["256 bit Vector Register"]
        SM2["Lane 0 32 bit"]
        SM3["Lane 1 32 bit"]
        SM4["Lane 2 32 bit"]
        SM5["Lane 3 32 bit"]
        SM6["Lane 4 32 bit"]
        SM7["Lane 5 32 bit"]
        SM8["Lane 6 32 bit"]
        SM9["Lane 7 32 bit"]
    end
    subgraph MC["Multi core TLP Independent Threads"]
        MC1["Core 0"]
        MC2["Core 1"]
        MC3["Core 2"]
        MC4["Core 3"]
    end
    SS1 --> SS2
    SS1 --> SS3
    SS1 --> SS4
    SS1 --> SS5
    SM1 --> SM2
    SM1 --> SM3
    SM1 --> SM4
    SM1 --> SM5
    SM1 --> SM6
    SM1 --> SM7
    SM1 --> SM8
    SM1 --> SM9
    MC1 --- MC2
    MC2 --- MC3
    MC3 --- MC4
```

**Explanation:** Superscalar exploits ILP within one thread; SIMD exploits DLP within one instruction; multi-core exploits TLP across independent threads. They are *complementary* — modern CPUs combine all three.

---

## 4.6 Cache Mapping Schemes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DM["Direct Mapped Block to One Line"]
        DM1["Tag Index Offset"]
        DM2["Line 0"]
        DM3["Line 1"]
        DM4["Line n minus 1"]
    end
    subgraph FA["Fully Associative Block to Any Line"]
        FA1["Tag Offset"]
        FA2["Line 0"]
        FA3["Line 1"]
        FA4["Line n minus 1"]
    end
    subgraph SA["Set Associative Block to Any Way in One Set"]
        SA1["Tag Set Index Offset"]
        SA2["Set 0 Way 0 to Way 3"]
        SA3["Set 1 Way 0 to Way 3"]
        SA4["Set n minus 1 Way 0 to Way 3"]
    end
    DM1 --> DM2
    FA1 --> FA2
    SA1 --> SA2
```

**Explanation:** Direct mapped is fastest but suffers from conflict misses. Fully associative is most flexible but requires parallel tag comparison (CAM). Set associative is the practical compromise used in all modern caches.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]
**Explain the von Neumann architecture with a neat diagram. State the von Neumann bottleneck.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **von Neumann architecture** is a stored-program computer design where **instructions and data share the same memory** and are transferred over a **single bus**. The major components are: (1) **Memory** holding both instructions and data, (2) **Control Unit (CU)** fetching and decoding instructions, (3) **ALU** performing arithmetic/logic operations, (4) **Registers** (PC, IR, ACC), and (5) **I/O** and **Secondary Storage** connected via the memory.

**Von Neumann Bottleneck (1 mark):** Since instructions and data share a single bus, the CPU cannot fetch an instruction and read/write data simultaneously, limiting throughput. This is the *von Neumann bottleneck*. Modern computers mitigate it using **separate L1 instruction and data caches (Harvard-style split)** inside the CPU while retaining a unified main memory.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define Moore's Law. Mention its significance in high-performance computing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Moore's Law, articulated by Gordon Moore in 1965, states that the **number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every 18–24 months**, leading to exponential growth in compute capability.

**Significance in HPC (1.5 marks):**
1. **Predictive power** — architects can forecast future compute density and plan chip designs.
2. **Performance scaling** — more transistors enable deeper pipelines, larger caches, more execution units, and multi-core designs.
3. **Cost economics** — cost per transistor falls, making HPC systems affordable.
4. **Driver of parallelism** — as single-core ILP saturates, Moore's Law motivates multi-core and many-core HPC (GPUs, TPUs).
5. **Energy limits** — Dennard scaling broke down ~2005, shifting focus from clock frequency to parallel efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the 5-stage instruction pipeline with a diagram. Discuss the hazards that reduce pipeline performance.

**(b) [7 marks]** A processor has a clock rate of 3 GHz. The program contains $5 \times 10^{8}$ instructions, with the following CPI breakdown: 30% ALU instructions (CPI = 1), 20% load/store (CPI = 2), 25% branch (CPI = 3), 25% floating point (CPI = 4). Calculate the total CPU time and MIPS rating.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Pipelining (7 marks):**

The 5-stage pipeline partitions instruction execution into:

| Stage | Name | Function |
|-------|------|----------|
| **IF** | Instruction Fetch | Read instruction from I-cache, PC+4 |
| **ID** | Instruction Decode | Decode opcode, read registers, sign-extend immediate |
| **EX** | Execute | ALU operation, branch target computation |
| **MEM** | Memory Access | Read/write D-cache for load/store |
| **WB** | Write Back | Write result to register file |

In steady state, the pipeline completes **1 instruction per cycle (IPC = 1)**, achieving a theoretical speedup of **5×** over a non-pipelined design.

**Pipeline Hazards (3 sub-hazards, 3 marks):**

1. **Structural Hazards** — hardware resource conflict (e.g., single memory port serving both IF and MEM). *Resolution:* duplicate resources (separate I-cache and D-cache).
2. **Data Hazards** — RAW (Read After Write), WAR, WAW dependencies. *Resolution:* forwarding/bypassing, register renaming, pipeline interleaving.
3. **Control Hazards** — caused by branches and jumps. *Resolution:* branch prediction, delay slots, speculative execution, branch target buffer (BTB).

**Part (b) — CPU Time Calculation (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{avg} &= \sum_i (\text{fraction}_i \times CPI_i) \\[4pt]
&= (0.30 \times 1) + (0.20 \times 2) + (0.25 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 4) \\[4pt]
&= 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.75 + 1.00 \\[4pt]
&= 2.45
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= \dfrac{IC \times CPI_{avg}}{f} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{5 \times 10^{8} \times 2.45}{3 \times 10^{9}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1.225 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{9}} \\[4pt]
&= 0.4083 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MIPS &= \dfrac{f}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{3 \times 10^{9}}{2.45 \times 10^{6}} \\[4pt]
&= 1224.49 \text{ MIPS} \approx 1224 \text{ MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{CPU} = 0.4083$ s, MIPS = 1224.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]
**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the memory hierarchy in modern computer systems. Explain the principles of locality that justify this hierarchy.

**(b) [7 marks]** A two-level cache system has L1 with hit time = 1 ns, hit rate = 90%, and L2 with hit time = 10 ns, hit rate = 95% (on L1 misses). Main memory access time is 200 ns. Calculate (i) AMAT for the L1, (ii) overall average memory access time, and (iii) the performance improvement if L2 is removed (i.e., L1 miss goes straight to main memory).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Memory Hierarchy (7 marks):**

The memory hierarchy (top to bottom) is:

| Level | Type | Size | Latency |
|-------|------|------|---------|
| 1 | CPU Registers | 1–4 KB | ~1 cycle |
| 2 | L1 Cache | 32–64 KB | 1–2 ns |
| 3 | L2 Cache | 256 KB – 1 MB | 5–10 ns |
| 4 | L3 Cache | 8–64 MB | 20–40 ns |
| 5 | Main Memory (DRAM) | 8–128 GB | 50–100 ns |
| 6 | SSD (NVMe) | 1–8 TB | 10–50 µs |
| 7 | HDD / Tape | TB–PB | 1–10 ms |

**Two Principles of Locality (3 marks):**

1. **Temporal Locality** — if a memory location is referenced, it is *likely to be referenced again soon*. Justifies keeping recently accessed data in cache.
2. **Spatial Locality** — if a memory location is referenced, *nearby locations* are likely to be referenced soon. Justifies fetching **cache blocks (lines)** of 32–128 bytes rather than single bytes.

A third principle, **sequential locality** (related to spatial), explains prefetching and stream buffers.

**Part (b) — AMAT Calculations (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- L1: $T_1 = 1$ ns, $h_1 = 0.90$
- L2: $T_2 = 10$ ns, $h_2 = 0.95$ (on L1 misses)
- Main memory: $T_3 = 200$ ns

**(i) AMAT of L1 (with L2 present):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AMAT_{L1} &= T_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot T_{miss-L1} \\[4pt]
T_{miss-L1} &= T_2 + (1 - h_2) \cdot T_3 \\[4pt]
&= 10 + 0.05 \times 200 = 10 + 10 = 20 \text{ ns} \\[4pt]
AMAT_{L1} &= 1 + 0.10 \times 20 = 1 + 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Overall average memory access time** = AMAT = **3.0 ns**.

**(iii) Without L2 (L1 miss → main memory directly):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{miss-L1}^{noL2} &= 200 \text{ ns} \\[4pt]
AMAT_{L1}^{noL2} &= 1 + 0.10 \times 200 = 1 + 20 = 21.0 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Performance improvement** (ratio of access times):

$$
\text{Improvement} = \dfrac{21.0}{3.0} = 7\times
$$

**Final Answer:** AMAT (with L2) = 3 ns; AMAT (without L2) = 21 ns; **L2 provides 7× speedup**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CPU time and CPU clock cycle** — $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ is the *full* time; do not stop at CPI.
> 2. **Wrong unit in MIPS** — MIPS uses $10^6$ (not $10^9$). Confusing MIPS with MFLOPS costs full marks.
> 3. **Amdahl's Law misuse** — the formula is $S = 1/[(1-f) + f/s]$, NOT $1/f \cdot s$. Many students invert it.
> 4. **Pipeline speedup ≠ k** — the maximum ideal speedup of a k-stage pipeline is $k$ (only for infinite instruction stream). For finite $n$, use $(nk)/(n+k-1)$.
> 5. **AMAT formula** — it is $T_{hit} + MR \times T_{miss}$, not $T_{hit} \times MR + T_{miss}$. State it correctly.
> 6. **Cache hit rate confusion** — L2 hit rate $h_2$ is *conditional on L1 miss*, not the *global* hit rate. The product $h_1 \times h_2$ is the global L2 hit rate.
> 7. **Skipping the cache line/block explanation** — when asked about cache, always mention **block (line) size, tag, index, offset**.
> 8. **Moore's Law vs Dennard Scaling** — Moore's Law (transistor count) is still alive; Dennard Scaling (constant power density) ended ~2005. Examiners love this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points for PECST757 Module 1**

- ✅ **Von Neumann architecture** = single memory for instructions + data; bottleneck is the shared bus.
- ✅ **Modified Harvard** = separate L1 I-cache and D-cache, unified main memory (used in all modern CPUs).
- ✅ **Performance Equation:** $T_{CPU} = IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$. **Optimize any one factor.**
- ✅ **MIPS** = $f / (CPI \times 10^6)$. **MIPS is unreliable across ISAs** (use SPEC for fair comparison).
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1/[(1-f) + f/s]$; **maximum speedup is $1/(1-f)$**.
- ✅ **Gustafson's Law:** $S = p - (1-p) \cdot f$ for *scaled* workloads (opposite viewpoint).
- ✅ **Moore's Law:** transistors double every 24 months; **drives multi-core transition** after Dennard scaling ended.
- ✅ **Pipeline speedup ideal = k** (k stages); **pipelining reduces CPI to 1**, not latency.
- ✅ **CPI components** = base CPI + structural stalls + data-hazard stalls + branch stalls.
- ✅ **Superscalar** = issue width $n$; **theoretical max IPC = n**; bounded by ILP and dependencies.
- ✅ **SIMD width** = 128 (SSE), 256 (AVX2), 512 (AVX-512); **best for regular data-parallel loops**.
- ✅ **Memory hierarchy** ordered by speed: **Registers > L1 > L2 > L3 > DRAM > SSD > HDD**.
- ✅ **Locality principles** = **Temporal** (re-reference) and **Spatial** (nearby data).
- ✅ **Cache hit rate** = Hits / (Hits + Misses); **miss rate = 1 − hit rate**.
- ✅ **AMAT = $T_{hit}$ + Miss Rate × $T_{miss-penalty}$** — the **single most important** cache metric.
- ✅ **3 mapping schemes:** Direct, Set Associative (industry standard, 4–16 way), Fully Associative.
- ✅ **3 C's of cache misses:** Compulsory (cold), Capacity, Conflict.
- ✅ **Benchmarks:** SPEC CPU (integer/float), LINPACK (HPL for TOP500), PARSEC, Geekbench.
- ✅ **FLOPs scaling:** KFLOPS → MFLOPS → GFLOPS → TFLOPS → PFLOPS → EFLOPS (Frontier: 1.194 EFLOPS).
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law ceilings** make multi-core scaling hard; SIMD + algorithmic optimization are usually the first wins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** "Performance is not just GHz." Always show the **full** $IC \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ decomposition in numerical answers. Mention **locality, Amdahl's Law, and 3 C's** whenever cache or parallelism appears. Examiners reward *systematic* reasoning over *hand-waving*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cache Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cache Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cache Mapping** is the deterministic mechanism by which a specific block (or line) of data from the slower, larger **Main Memory (RAM)** is placed, located, and retrieved within the faster, smaller, and more expensive **CPU Cache Memory**. Because the cache cannot hold the entire main memory, an algorithm must dictate exactly *where* in the cache a given main memory block will reside. This translation between a physical main memory address and a physical cache location is what we call the **Cache Mapping Function**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for High Performance Computing, cache mapping is classified into three foundational architectures:

1. **Direct Mapping** – A strict, modular placement policy (each memory block has exactly one possible cache line).
2. **Fully Associative Mapping** – A flexible, content-addressable policy (a memory block can be placed in *any* cache line).
3. **Set-Associative Mapping** – A hybrid architecture (the cache is divided into *sets*, and a memory block maps to a specific set but can occupy any line within that set).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> *"Cache Mapping is the algorithmic procedure that determines the correspondence between the main memory address space and the physically smaller cache address space, governed by the constraints of locality of reference (temporal and spatial)."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Book Return System"

Imagine a massive university library with **10,000 unique books** (Main Memory), but you only have a small personal reading desk with **10 slots** (Cache). When you return a book, the librarian must follow a strict policy:

* **Direct Mapping (Modulo Policy):** The librarian calculates `Slot = Book_ID mod 10`. If Book ID is 257, it *must* go in Slot 7. Even if Slot 7 is full, the old book is kicked out. It is extremely fast to locate (just do the math), but causes constant "collisions" (called **Conflict Misses**).
* **Fully Associative (Free Choice):** The librarian can place the book in *any* empty slot on your desk. It is the most flexible and eliminates conflict misses, but finding the book later requires checking all 10 slots (like a security camera search).
* **Set-Associative (Departmental Grouping):** Your desk is divided into 2 "mini-shelves" (Sets) of 5 slots each. The librarian first calculates which mini-shelf the book belongs to (`Shelf = Book_ID mod 2`), but within that shelf, the book can go in any of the 5 slots. This is a balanced compromise used in modern Intel and AMD processors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Metrics at a Glance:**
> * **Tag:** The high-order bits of the address that uniquely identify *which* block of memory is currently stored in a cache line.
> * **Index:** The middle bits that select the specific cache set (or line in direct mapping).
> * **Block Offset:** The low-order bits that select the specific byte within the cache block/line.
> * **Word Size:** Usually **32 bits (4 Bytes)** or **64 bits (8 Bytes)** in modern architectures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Address Structure

Regardless of the mapping technique, every main memory address generated by the CPU is conceptually divided into three logical partitions. Let the physical address be of size $A$ bits.

$$A = \text{Tag bits} + \text{Index bits} + \text{Block Offset bits}$$

### 2.1.1 Block Offset Bits ($b$)
This field identifies the specific byte (or word) inside the cache block. It is purely a function of the block size.

$$b = \log_2(\text{Block Size in Bytes})$$

### 2.1.2 Index Bits ($i$)
* In **Direct Mapping**, the index selects the exact cache line.
* In **Set-Associative**, the index selects the exact set.
* The number of index bits depends on the number of cache sets ($S$).

$$i = \log_2(S)$$

### 2.1.3 Tag Bits ($t$)
The tag is whatever remains. It is stored alongside the data in the cache to verify whether the current line holds the requested memory block (i.e., it is a **Hit**) or a different block (a **Miss**).

$$t = A - i - b$$

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Each Mapping Scheme

### 2.2.1 Direct Mapping
* **How it works:** A memory block at address `M` is mapped to cache line `(M mod N)`, where $N$ is the total number of cache lines.
* **Hardware Complexity:** Lowest. A single comparator and a multiplexer suffice.
* **Drawback:** High conflict miss rate. If two frequently used blocks map to the same line, they constantly thrash (evict each other).

### 2.2.2 Fully Associative Mapping
* **How it works:** A memory block can be placed in *any* of the $N$ cache lines.
* **Hardware Complexity:** Highest. Requires a **Content Addressable Memory (CAM)** where all tags are searched in parallel.
* **Advantage:** Zero conflict misses. Ideal for small caches.
* **Drawback:** Power-hungry and expensive to scale.

### 2.2.3 Set-Associative Mapping
* **How it works:** The cache is organized into $S$ sets, each containing $K$ lines ($K$ is the **associativity** or **way**). A memory block maps to set `(M mod S)`, but can occupy any of the $K$ lines within that set.
* **Hardware Complexity:** Moderate. Requires $K$ parallel comparators per set.
* **Standard Variants:** $K=2$ (2-way), $K=4$ (4-way), $K=8$ (8-way), $K=16$ (16-way).
* **Most Common:** Modern L1/L2/L3 caches use **8-way or 16-way set-associative** mapping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why" Matters for KTU Examiners:**
> Set-associative mapping exists because of the **Principle of Compromise**. Direct mapping is too rigid (high miss rate), and fully associative is too expensive (high latency, high power). Set-associative offers $K$ "guess chances" per lookup, drastically reducing conflict misses while keeping the hardware search space bounded to $K$ comparators.

## 2.3 Performance Formulas — KTU Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Offset Bits** | $b = \log_2(B)$ | $B$ = Block size in bytes |
| **Index Bits (Direct)** | $i = \log_2(N)$ | $N$ = Total number of cache lines |
| **Index Bits (Set-Associative)** | $i = \log_2(S)$ | $S$ = Number of sets = $N / K$ |
| **Tag Bits** | $t = A - i - b$ | $A$ = Physical address width |
| **Number of Sets** | $S = \dfrac{\text{Cache Size}}{B \times K}$ | $K$ = Associativity (ways) |
| **Total Cache Lines** | $N = S \times K$ | Product of sets and ways |
| **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** | $\text{AMAT} = T_h + (M_r \times T_m)$ | $T_h$ = Hit time, $M_r$ = Miss rate, $T_m$ = Miss penalty |
| **Miss Rate** | $M_r = 1 - \text{Hit Rate}$ | Ratio of misses to total accesses |
| **Cache Capacity** | $C = N \times B \times W$ | $W$ = Word/byte width |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **CPU Microarchitecture:** Intel Core i9 and AMD Ryzen processors use a hierarchy of 8-way to 16-way set-associative L1/L2/L3 caches to balance latency and hit rate.
* **GPU Texture Units:** Use highly associative caches to handle non-coalesced memory access patterns.
* **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer):** A fully associative cache that maps virtual page numbers to physical frame numbers.
* **Database Engines:** Buffer pool managers use set-associative logic to map table pages to in-memory buffer frames.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Address Partitioning for a Set-Associative Cache

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**
A processor has a **32-bit physical address** space. The L1 cache is **32 KB** in size, with a **block size of 64 Bytes** and is **4-way set-associative**. Determine the number of Tag bits, Index bits, and Block Offset bits, and compute the total size of the tag directory.

---

### Step 1: Compute the Block Offset Bits

The block size is $B = 64$ Bytes. The offset must uniquely address every byte within the block.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \log_2(B) \\
  &= \log_2(64) \\
  &= \log_2(2^6) \\
  &= 6 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** 6 bits are required to count from `0` to `63`, covering all 64 bytes in the block.

---

### Step 2: Compute the Total Number of Cache Lines

Cache size $C = 32$ KB $= 32 \times 1024$ Bytes $= 32768$ Bytes. Each line holds 64 Bytes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{C}{B} \\
  &= \frac{32768}{64} \\
  &= 512 \text{ lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3: Compute the Number of Sets

The cache is 4-way set-associative, so each set contains $K = 4$ lines.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{N}{K} \\
  &= \frac{512}{4} \\
  &= 128 \text{ sets}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4: Compute the Index Bits

The index must uniquely select one of the 128 sets.

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= \log_2(S) \\
  &= \log_2(128) \\
  &= \log_2(2^7) \\
  &= 7 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 5: Compute the Tag Bits

The physical address is $A = 32$ bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= A - i - b \\
  &= 32 - 7 - 6 \\
  &= 19 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 6: Compute the Tag Directory Storage Overhead

In addition to the data (32 KB = 32768 Bytes), the cache must store the 19-bit tag, a 1-bit **Valid Bit** (V), and a 1-bit **Dirty Bit** (D) for each line.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead per line} &= t + V + D = 19 + 1 + 1 = 21 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Total overhead} &= N \times 21 = 512 \times 21 = 10752 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Total overhead in Bytes} &= \frac{10752}{8} = 1344 \text{ Bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 7: Final Verification of the Address Format

The 32-bit physical address is logically partitioned as:

$$
\boxed{\underbrace{\text{Tag}}_{19 \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Index}}_{7 \text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Offset}}_{6 \text{ bits}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** $19 + 7 + 6 = 32$ bits. ✓ Matches the physical address width.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of the Three Schemes

| Feature | Direct Mapping | Fully Associative | Set-Associative ($K$-way) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Placement Rule** | One specific line | Any line | Specific set, any line in set |
| **Index Bits ($i$)** | $\log_2(N)$ | $0$ | $\log_2(N/K)$ |
| **Tag Bits ($t$)** | $A - \log_2(N) - b$ | $A - b$ | $A - \log_2(N/K) - b$ |
| **Comparators Needed** | $1$ | $N$ | $K$ (per set) |
| **Conflict Misses** | Highest | Zero | Low (scales with $K$) |
| **Hardware Cost** | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| **Search Latency** | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| **Power Consumption** | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| **Scalability** | Excellent | Poor | Excellent |
| **Modern Usage** | Rare (TLB tags) | L1 ITLB (small) | L1/L2/L3 Data Caches |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Address Decoding Flow (Direct Mapping)

This schematic illustrates how a 32-bit physical address is decoded by the cache controller to locate a specific byte in a direct-mapped cache.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["32-bit Physical Address Arrives at Cache Controller"] --> B["Split into 3 Logical Fields"]
    B --> C1["Tag Field: 19 bits upper"]
    B --> C2["Index Field: 7 bits middle"]
    B --> C3["Offset Field: 6 bits lower"]

    C2 --> D["Use Index to select one specific cache line from 128 lines"]
    C1 --> E["Read the 19-bit stored Tag from the selected line"]
    E --> F{"Compare Incoming Tag with Stored Tag bit by bit"}

    C3 --> G["Read 64-byte block into a 64-to-1 multiplexer"]
    F -- "MATCH and Valid Bit is 1" --> H["CACHE HIT: Forward data to CPU"]
    F -- "MISMATCH or Valid Bit is 0" --> I["CACHE MISS: Initiate Main Memory fetch and stall CPU pipeline"]

    G --> H

    style A fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Set-Associative Cache Architecture (4-Way Block Diagram)

This block-level architecture flow shows how a 4-way set-associative cache performs a parallel tag lookup.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["CPU Core Issues Load Request"] --> ADDR["32-bit Physical Address Bus"]

    ADDR --> OFFSET["Offset: 6 bits - Byte position within block"]
    ADDR --> INDEX["Index: 7 bits - Set selector"]
    ADDR --> TAG["Tag: 19 bits - Block identifier"]

    INDEX --> DECODER["7 to 128 Set Decoder"]

    DECODER --> SET_GROUP["Selected Set: 4 cache lines activated simultaneously"]

    SET_GROUP --> WAY0["Way 0: Tag0 and Data0 registers"]
    SET_GROUP --> WAY1["Way 1: Tag1 and Data1 registers"]
    SET_GROUP --> WAY2["Way 2: Tag2 and Data2 registers"]
    SET_GROUP --> WAY3["Way 3: Tag3 and Data3 registers"]

    TAG --> COMP0["Comparator 0"]
    TAG --> COMP1["Comparator 1"]
    TAG --> COMP2["Comparator 2"]
    TAG --> COMP3["Comparator 3"]

    WAY0 --> COMP0
    WAY1 --> COMP1
    WAY2 --> COMP2
    WAY3 --> COMP3

    COMP0 -- "Match found" --> MUX["4-to-1 Output Multiplexer"]
    COMP1 -- "Match found" --> MUX
    COMP2 -- "Match found" --> MUX
    COMP3 -- "Match found" --> MUX

    OFFSET --> MUX
    MUX --> CPUOUT["Selected Byte forwarded to CPU in 1 cycle"]

    style CPU fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px
    style CPUOUT fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px
    style DECODER fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px
    style MUX fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#4338CA,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Mapping Policy Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Memory Block M arrives at Cache"] --> POLICY{"What Mapping Policy is used?"}

    POLICY -- "Direct Mapping" --> D1["Compute Line Number = M mod N"]
    D1 --> D2["Place block in that exact line. If occupied, evict the old block."]
    D2 --> D3["Result: Fast but high conflict miss rate"]

    POLICY -- "Fully Associative" --> F1["Search all N lines for an empty slot"]
    F1 --> F2["Place block in any free line. If full, use LRU replacement policy."]
    F2 --> F3["Result: Flexible but requires N parallel comparators"]

    POLICY -- "Set Associative" --> S1["Compute Set Number = M mod S"]
    S1 --> S2["Search only the K lines within that specific set"]
    S2 --> S3["Place block in any free line of the set. Evict via LRU if full."]
    S3 --> S4["Result: Balanced trade-off between speed and flexibility"]

    style START fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px
    style D3 fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:1px
    style F3 fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:1px
    style S4 fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:1px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Direct Mapping and Associative Mapping in cache memory.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Mapping | Associative Mapping |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Placement** | Each memory block maps to exactly one specific cache line using the formula `(Block Address) mod (Number of Cache Lines)`. | Each memory block can be placed in *any* available cache line. |
| **Search Method** | Uses the index bits to directly locate the line; only one tag comparison is needed. | Requires a parallel search of all cache tags using a Content Addressable Memory (CAM). |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Low (one comparator and one multiplexer). | High (requires $N$ comparators operating in parallel). |
| **Conflict Misses** | High (two blocks with the same index keep evicting each other). | Zero (any free line can be used). |

*(1 mark for the placement rule, 1 mark for search/hardware, 1 mark for the miss rate comparison.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the role of the Tag bits in a cache memory system? How is the number of tag bits calculated?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

* **Role:** Tag bits uniquely identify *which* main memory block is currently stored in a particular cache line. When the CPU generates an address, the tag bits of the incoming address are compared with the stored tag bits in the selected cache line. A **match** indicates a **Cache Hit**; a **mismatch** indicates a **Cache Miss**, requiring a fetch from the main memory.

* **Formula:**
$$t = A - i - b$$
where $A$ is the total physical address width (in bits), $i$ is the number of index bits, and $b$ is the number of block offset bits.

*(1.5 marks for role explanation, 1.5 marks for the formula and its interpretation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks. Typically divided into sub-part (a) of 7 marks and sub-part (b) of 7 marks. Students are expected to provide a complete derivation, block diagrams, and final numerical answers with units.

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A computer system uses a 32-bit physical address. The cache has a capacity of 16 KB, a block size of 8 words (assuming 1 word = 4 Bytes), and is **Direct Mapped**. Calculate the number of:
* (i) Offset bits
* (ii) Index bits
* (iii) Tag bits
* (iv) Total tag directory size (including a Valid bit for each line)

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram how the address is decoded in a direct-mapped cache. Also, discuss why direct mapping suffers from conflict misses even when the cache is not full.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Given:** Physical address $A = 32$ bits, Cache size $C = 16$ KB, Block size $B = 8 \text{ words} \times 4 \text{ Bytes/word} = 32$ Bytes.

**Step 1: Compute the Block Offset bits.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \log_2(B) \\
  &= \log_2(32) \\
  &= \log_2(2^5) \\
  &= 5 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the total number of cache lines.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{C}{B} \\
  &= \frac{16 \times 1024}{32} \\
  &= 512 \text{ lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Index bits.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= \log_2(N) \\
  &= \log_2(512) \\
  &= \log_2(2^9) \\
  &= 9 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Tag bits.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= A - i - b \\
  &= 32 - 9 - 5 \\
  &= 18 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the total tag directory storage.**
Each line requires an 18-bit Tag and 1 Valid bit, totaling 19 bits of metadata.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Directory Size} &= N \times (t + V) \\
  &= 512 \times 19 \\
  &= 9728 \text{ bits} \\
  &= \frac{9728}{8} \text{ Bytes} \\
  &= 1216 \text{ Bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Address Structure:**
$$\boxed{\underbrace{\text{Tag}}_{18} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Index}}_{9} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Offset}}_{5}}$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

The address decoding in a direct-mapped cache follows three concurrent steps:
1. The **Index** field activates one specific decoder output, selecting exactly one cache line out of $N$.
2. The stored **Tag** of the selected line is fed into a comparator, where it is matched against the incoming **Tag** from the CPU.
3. If the tags match AND the Valid bit is set to 1, a **Hit** signal is asserted. The **Offset** field then drives a multiplexer to extract the exact byte/word requested by the CPU.

**Why conflict misses occur even in an unfilled cache:**
In direct mapping, the placement of a memory block is determined by the modulo operation: `Cache Line = (Block Address) mod N`. This means that two memory blocks that differ only by a multiple of $N$ will *always* map to the same cache line. If the program alternates accesses between Block 0 and Block $N$ (for example, an array stride problem where the program jumps through memory in steps of exactly $N$ blocks), the cache will constantly evict one block to make room for the other, even if the remaining 511 lines of the cache are completely empty. This is the essence of the **3 C's Model** (Compulsory, Capacity, Conflict) and is the primary motivation for set-associative and fully associative designs.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider a **2-way set-associative** cache with the following specifications: Physical address = 32 bits, Cache size = 32 KB, Block size = 64 Bytes. Calculate the number of Tag bits, Index bits, and Offset bits. Also, determine the number of comparators required in this design.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare Direct, Fully Associative, and Set-Associative mapping techniques. Justify why modern high-performance processors (e.g., Intel Core i7) prefer multi-way set-associative L1 caches.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Given:** $A = 32$ bits, $C = 32$ KB, $B = 64$ Bytes, $K = 2$ ways.

**Step 1: Compute the Offset bits.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the number of cache lines.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{32 \times 1024}{64} = 512 \text{ lines}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the number of sets.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{N}{K} = \frac{512}{2} = 256 \text{ sets}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Index bits.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= \log_2(256) = \log_2(2^8) = 8 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Tag bits.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= A - i - b = 32 - 8 - 6 = 18 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the comparators required.**
In a 2-way set-associative cache, when the index selects a set, *both* ways within that set must have their tags compared in parallel to determine a hit.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Comparators per set} &= K = 2 \\
\text{Total comparators} &= S \times K = 256 \times 2 = 512 \text{ comparators}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Note: While there are 512 comparators in total, only 2 are active per access. The remaining 510 are power-gated.)*

**Final Address Structure:**
$$\boxed{\underbrace{\text{Tag}}_{18} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Index}}_{8} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Offset}}_{6}}$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

| Feature | Direct Mapping | Fully Associative | Set-Associative |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Search Speed** | Fastest (1 comparator) | Slowest ($N$ comparators in parallel) | Moderate ($K$ comparators) |
| **Flexibility** | Lowest (1 placement option) | Highest ($N$ options) | Balanced ($K$ options per set) |
| **Miss Rate** | High (conflict misses) | Lowest (no conflict misses) | Low (conflict misses reduced) |
| **Power Cost** | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |

**Why Intel Core i7 prefers Multi-Way Set-Associative L1 Caches:**

1. **Hardware Latency Constraint:** L1 caches must complete a lookup in roughly 4 CPU clock cycles (or ~1 ns at 4 GHz). A fully associative search across thousands of lines is physically impossible to complete in this time. Set-associativity limits the search to $K$ parallel comparators (typically $K=8$ in Core i7), keeping the access latency within the strict L1 timing budget.

2. **Conflict Miss Reduction:** Real-world programs (compilers, databases, scientific loops) frequently access memory in patterns that cause severe thrashing in direct-mapped caches (e.g., accessing two arrays that happen to map to the same modulo class). An 8-way design provides 8 "guesses" per index, statistically eliminating most conflict misses.

3. **Manufacturing Yield and Power:** Scaling to a 16-way or 32-way associativity yields diminishing returns in hit rate but doubles the comparator power budget. The industry has empirically converged on 8-way as the sweet spot.

4. **Pipeline Integration:** Set-associative lookups can be pipelined across stages (index decode in stage 1, tag compare in stage 2, data mux in stage 3), integrating seamlessly with the superscalar out-of-order execution engine of modern CPUs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Tag Directory vs. Cache Size:** Students often confuse the **cache data capacity** (e.g., 32 KB) with the **tag directory size** (e.g., 1344 Bytes). You must add the tag overhead to the data size for the true silicon footprint of the cache.
> 2. **Word vs. Byte Block Size:** If the question says *"block size = 8 words"* and the architecture is 32-bit (4 bytes/word), the *actual* block size in bytes is $8 \times 4 = 32$ Bytes. Many students write `8` directly into the $\log_2$ formula and lose 3 marks.
> 3. **Modulo Direction:** Direct mapping uses `(Block Address) mod (Number of Lines)`. Fully associative has *no* modulo. Set-associative uses `(Block Address) mod (Number of Sets)`. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 0 for the derivation.
> 4. **Comparators Counting:** For a $K$-way set-associative cache, the number of *active* comparators per access is $K$, not $S \times K$. The latter is the total hardware count.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

* ✅ **Cache Mapping** is the algorithm that governs *where* a main memory block is placed inside the smaller cache.
* ✅ **Address Partition Formula:** $A = \text{Tag} + \text{Index} + \text{Offset}$. This is the single most important equation in Module 1.
* ✅ **Offset Bits:** $b = \log_2(\text{Block Size in Bytes})$.
* ✅ **Index Bits:** $i = \log_2(\text{Number of Sets})$. For direct mapping, sets = lines. For set-associative, sets = lines $\div$ ways.
* ✅ **Tag Bits:** $t = A - i - b$. Always calculate this *last*.
* ✅ **Direct Mapping:** Fastest hardware, highest conflict miss rate. Formula: `Line = (Block Address) mod N`.
* ✅ **Fully Associative:** Most flexible, zero conflict misses, requires parallel tag comparison using a CAM.
* ✅ **Set-Associative:** The industry standard. Best balance of speed, flexibility, and cost. Modern L1 caches are typically **8-way** set-associative.
* ✅ **Comparators:** $K$ comparators per set for $K$-way set-associative; $N$ for fully associative; $1$ for direct mapped.
* ✅ **AMAT Formula:** $\text{AMAT} = T_h + (M_r \times T_m)$ — critical for all performance-based questions.
* ✅ **3 C's Model of Misses:** **Compulsory** (first access), **Capacity** (cache too small), **Conflict** (collision due to mapping policy). Set-associativity only addresses *Conflict Misses*.
* ✅ **Valid Bit:** A 1-bit flag in every cache line indicating whether the line holds valid data or contains garbage from power-on.
* ✅ **Dirty Bit:** A 1-bit flag in write-back caches indicating whether the line has been modified relative to main memory.
* ✅ **Block Size Trade-off:** Larger blocks improve **spatial locality** (fewer compulsory misses) but worsen **pollution** (fetching unused data) and reduce the number of available lines (more capacity misses).
* ✅ **Spatial Locality:** Programs tend to access memory addresses that are physically close to recently accessed addresses (justifies larger block sizes).
* ✅ **Temporal Locality:** Programs tend to re-access the same memory locations within short time windows (justifies keeping recently used blocks in the cache).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prefetch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Prefetch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Prefetching** is a *latency-hiding* technique in high-performance computing architectures whereby the system speculatively retrieves **data, instructions, or I/O blocks** from a *slower* level of the memory hierarchy (main memory, disk, or remote node) into a *faster* level (cache, local buffer, or local memory) **before** the processor or application explicitly demands it.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (course code PECST757), prefetching is studied as a **Memory Hierarchy Optimization** technique and an **I/O Optimization** strategy for HPC workloads running on multi-core CPUs, accelerators (GPUs), and distributed clusters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlight):**
> - **Latency Hiding:** Masking the delay of a slow memory access by overlapping it with computation.
> - **Speculative Access:** A guess about the future memory reference stream.
> - **Prefetch Stream / Trail:** A sequence of cache lines or I/O blocks brought in by a single prefetch trigger.
> - **Degree (d):** Number of cache lines (or blocks) fetched per prefetch request.
> - **Distance / Prefetch Aheadness:** How early the prefetch is issued relative to the demand miss.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Restaurant Waiter Analogy" 🍽️**
> Imagine you are eating in a restaurant. A *reactive waiter* only brings water when you ask for it (you wait while he goes to the kitchen). A *smart waiter* (prefetcher) notices you just finished your soup, and based on the pattern that 80% of customers order a drink after soup, he brings you the menu AND fetches a glass of water *before* you ask. By the time you reach for the drink, it is already on the table — **zero waiting**.
>
> The waiter was **speculative** (he guessed), he fetched in **parallel** (while you ate), and he **hid the latency** (you never felt the kitchen delay). If he guessed wrong, the extra water glass is wasted — just like a *cache pollution* caused by a wrong prefetch.

## 1.3 Where Prefetching Sits in HPC

| Layer | Slow Resource | Fast Destination | Prefetch Granularity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hardware | Main Memory (DRAM) | L1 / L2 / L3 Cache | Cache line (64 B) |
| I/O | HDD / SSD / NVMe | OS Page Cache | Disk block (4 KB – 256 KB) |
| Distributed | Remote Node (MPI rank) | Local Rank's Memory | Message / Sub-array tile |
| Web / File | Network File Server (NFS, Lustre) | Client-side buffer | File chunk |

The overarching goal in all four layers is identical: **bring the data closer, sooner, with minimal waste of bandwidth or cache capacity.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept: The Prefetch Pipeline

A prefetch operation is a six-stage logical pipeline. Understanding each stage is essential for KTU 14-mark derivations.

1. **Trigger (Detection):** A *trigger event* activates the prefetcher. Common triggers:
   - A cache miss on a particular address.
   - A stride pattern detected in the miss address stream.
   - A software hint (`prefetch` intrinsic in C/C++).
   - A compiler-inserted preload instruction.
   - An I/O library call (e.g., `MPI_File_iread`, `read-ahead`).
2. **Prediction (Where to fetch):** The prefetcher computes a *target address*. For a **next-line prefetcher**, target = `miss_addr + line_size`. For a **stride prefetcher**, target = `last_addr + stride × line_size`.
3. **Issue (Request generation):** A memory request is sent to the lower level. The request must not block the demand path; it is tagged as *non-critical*.
4. **Transfer (Data movement):** The cache line / I/O block travels up the hierarchy. Bandwidth is a shared resource — prefetches compete with demand requests.
5. **Placement (Where to store):** The line is placed in the cache. To avoid evicting useful data, it may go into a *stream buffer* or be marked as *low priority* in LRU.
6. **Validation (Use or Discard):** When the processor finally requests the line, a *prefetch hit* is recorded. If never used, the line is *pollution*.

## 2.2 Classification of Prefetchers (KTU-Favorite Diagram Topic)

| Class | Who Decides | Granularity | Pros | Cons |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hardware Prefetcher** | CPU / Memory Controller | Cache line (64 B) | Transparent, no code change | Limited pattern window, hard to verify |
| **Software Prefetcher** | Compiler / Programmer (`__builtin_prefetch`) | Cache line / struct | Programmer has semantic knowledge | Pollutes instruction cache, programmer burden |
| **Hybrid Prefetcher** | HW + SW cooperation | Cache line | Best accuracy | Complex coordination |
| **I/O Read-Ahead** | OS / File System | Page / Block (4 KB+) | Hides disk latency | Wastes disk bandwidth |
| **MPI / Distributed Prefetch** | Runtime / User code | Sub-array / Halo region | Overlaps network with compute | Extra memory footprint |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following four equations are the **highest-yield formulas** for KTU ESE questions on prefetching. Memorise the variable names and units exactly.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Each Term |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1. AMAT with Prefetch** | $\text{AMAT} = h_{L1} \cdot t_{L1} + (1-h_{L1})\cdot t_{L1\to L2}$ | $h_{L1}$ = L1 hit rate, $t_{L1}$ = L1 hit time |
| **2. Prefetch Accuracy** | $A = \dfrac{U}{U+W}$ | $U$ = Useful prefetches, $W$ = Wasted prefetches |
| **3. Prefetch Coverage** | $C = \dfrac{U}{U+N}$ | $N$ = Demand misses that prefetch **missed** |
| **4. Polluted Misses** | $P = \dfrac{W}{W + \text{UsefulEvictions}}$ | Measures cache damage caused by prefetch |
| **5. Effective L2 Latency** | $t_{\text{eff}} = (1-C)\cdot t_{L2} + C\cdot t_{L2\to L1}$ | $C$ is coverage; $t_{L2\to L1}$ is L2 hit after prefetch |
| **6. Timeliness Condition** | $T_{\text{issue}} + T_{\text{mem}} \le T_{\text{use}}$ | Prefetch must complete *before* demand access |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are used in commercial CPUs (Intel IP-Prefetcher, AMD Stream Detector), GPU L2 prefetchers (NVIDIA Hopper, AMD CDNA), and HPC runtimes like OpenMP, MPI-IO, and HDF5.

## 2.4 Hardware vs Software Prefetch — When to Use What

* **Hardware prefetchers** dominate in *regular* workloads (matrix multiply, streaming kernels). They are useless for *pointer-chasing* (linked lists, B-trees) and *irregular* HPC codes (graph analytics, sparse solvers).
* **Software prefetchers** (`#pragma GCC ivdep`, `__builtin_prefetch(addr, 0, 1)`) are essential for **irregular access patterns** where the programmer *knows* the access schedule that the hardware cannot infer.
* In MPI applications, **prefetching halo regions** (ghost cells in stencil codes) is a textbook example of distributed prefetch.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: AMAT Improvement Due to Prefetching

We start from the classical **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** model, then introduce the *coverage* $C$ and *accuracy* $A$ of a prefetcher to quantify its benefit.

### Step 1 — Base AMAT (no prefetch)

For a two-level cache (L1 → L2), the average access time is:

$$\text{AMAT}_{0} = h_{L1}\cdot t_{L1} + (1-h_{L1})\cdot\bigl(t_{L1\to L2} + (1-h_{L2})\cdot t_{\text{mem}}\bigr)$$

> **Logic:** A fraction $h_{L1}$ of accesses hit in L1 (cost $t_{L1}$). The rest go to L2. Of those, a fraction $h_{L2}$ hit in L2, the rest pay the DRAM penalty $t_{\text{mem}}$.

### Step 2 — Introduce Prefetch Coverage $C$

A *coverage* of $C$ means a fraction $C$ of the L2-miss references will have been brought into L2 *before* the demand arrives. The effective L2 miss penalty is therefore:

$$t_{\text{eff}} = (1-C)\cdot t_{L2} + C\cdot t_{L2\to L1}$$

> **Logic:** If coverage is high, the demand usually finds the line already in L2, paying only the L2→L1 transfer time $t_{L2\to L1}$ instead of the full DRAM round-trip.

### Step 3 — Combine into New AMAT

$$\text{AMAT}_{\text{pre}} = h_{L1}\cdot t_{L1} + (1-h_{L1})\cdot t_{L2}^{\text{new}}$$

where:

$$t_{L2}^{\text{new}} = t_{L1\to L2} + (1-h_{L2})\cdot t_{\text{eff}}$$

### Step 4 — Speedup Expression

The fractional reduction in AMAT is the **Speedup** $S$:

$$S = \frac{\text{AMAT}_{0}}{\text{AMAT}_{\text{pre}}}$$

Substituting the two expressions above and simplifying (for the common case $h_{L1}\ll 1$, $h_{L2}\ll 1$):

$$S \;\approx\; 1 + (1-h_{L1})\cdot(1-h_{L2})\cdot C\cdot\frac{t_{\text{mem}} - t_{L2\to L1}}{t_{L1\to L2} + t_{L2\to L1}}$$

> **Logic:** $S$ is monotonically increasing in $C$ (more coverage helps) and in $t_{\text{mem}} - t_{L2\to L1}$ (bigger DRAM-vs-L2 gap means more latency to hide). It decreases as $h_{L1}$ or $h_{L2}$ rises (less DRAM traffic to hide).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked problem (model-style for ESE 14-marker):**
> Given: $h_{L1} = 0.95$, $t_{L1} = 1$ ns, $t_{L1\to L2} = 10$ ns, $h_{L2} = 0.70$, $t_{\text{mem}} = 200$ ns, $t_{L2\to L1} = 10$ ns, prefetch coverage $C = 0.40$. Compute the AMAT speedup.

### Step A — Compute $\text{AMAT}_{0}$ (no prefetch)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT}_{0} &= 0.95 \cdot 1 + 0.05 \cdot \bigl(10 + 0.30 \cdot 200\bigr) \\
&= 0.95 + 0.05 \cdot (10 + 60) \\
&= 0.95 + 0.05 \cdot 70 \\
&= 0.95 + 3.5 \\
&= 4.45 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step B — Compute $t_{\text{eff}}$ with coverage

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{eff}} &= (1-0.40)\cdot 200 + 0.40 \cdot 10 \\
&= 0.60\cdot 200 + 4 \\
&= 120 + 4 \\
&= 124 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Compute $\text{AMAT}_{\text{pre}}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT}_{\text{pre}} &= 0.95 \cdot 1 + 0.05 \cdot \bigl(10 + 0.30 \cdot 124\bigr) \\
&= 0.95 + 0.05 \cdot (10 + 37.2) \\
&= 0.95 + 0.05 \cdot 47.2 \\
&= 0.95 + 2.36 \\
&= 3.31 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step D — Compute Speedup

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{4.45}{3.31} \approx 1.344
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result interpretation:** Even a modest 40% coverage reduces the AMAT by ~26%, yielding a **1.34× speedup** for the memory subsystem. In a real HPC stencil loop at 80% memory-bound utilisation, this roughly translates to a **1.20–1.25× wall-clock speedup**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Prefetch Trade-off Triangle

Every prefetch design is constrained by three competing forces. A KTU 14-marker often asks you to *discuss* this trade-off.

| Goal | Increase by… | Cost Paid |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Accuracy ↑** (fewer wasted lines) | Stride / Markov predictors | Hardware cost, complexity |
| **Coverage ↑** (catch more misses) | Aggressive degree, larger stream buffer | Cache pollution, bandwidth |
| **Timeliness ↓** (issue earlier) | Increase prefetch distance | Stale data, conflict misses |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Prefetch Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU issues LOAD addr X] --> B{X hit in L1?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Return data to CPU]
    B -- No --> D[X miss in L1: send to L2]
    D --> E{Prefetcher Triggered?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Predict next address X prime]
    E -- No --> G[Standard L2 lookup]
    F --> H[Issue non critical prefetch to L2]
    H --> I[Prefetch fills stream buffer or L2]
    G --> J{X hit in L2?}
    I --> J
    J -- Yes --> K[Return to L1 and CPU]
    J -- No --> L[DRAM access penalty T mem]
    L --> M[Return to L2 then L1 then CPU]
    K --> N[End]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Hardware Stride Prefetcher (Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Address Table RPT] --> B[Stride Detection Logic]
    B --> C{Stride constant?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Compute target addr plus stride]
    C -- No --> E[Mark entry as no stride]
    D --> F[Prefetch Request Queue]
    F --> G[L2 Cache Controller]
    G --> H[Cache Line placed in L2 or Stream Buffer]
    H --> I{Demand arrives in time?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Prefetch hit: useful]
    I -- No --> K[Late prefetch: wasted or stale]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Distributed Prefetch in MPI Stencil Code

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Rank0[Rank 0 Node]
        A0[Compute Inner Cells] --> B0[Non blocking MPI Irecv halo left]
        B0 --> C0[Issue prefetch for next timestep halo]
        C0 --> D0[Wait for actual data]
    end
    subgraph Rank1[Rank 1 Neighbour]
        A1[Send halo to Rank 0] --> D0
        A1 --> A1b[Compute inner cells]
    end
    D0 --> E0[Stencil update with prefetched halo]
    E0 --> F0[Synchronise and repeat]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The *non-blocking* `MPI_Irecv` + a software hint to the runtime to **prefetch the next iteration's halo region** is the canonical distributed prefetch pattern in HPC. The MPI runtime uses Remote Direct Memory Access (RDMA) to overlap network latency with compute.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define prefetching in the context of memory hierarchy. Mention any two types of prefetchers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Prefetching is a latency-hiding technique in which data or instructions are speculatively fetched from a lower (slower) level of the memory hierarchy into a higher (faster) level before they are demanded by the processor. (2 marks)*
> *Two types: (i) Hardware prefetcher — present inside the memory controller, transparent to software; (ii) Software prefetcher — explicit hints inserted by the compiler or programmer (e.g., `__builtin_prefetch`). (1 mark)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]
**Distinguish between prefetch accuracy and prefetch coverage. Why are both metrics needed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Accuracy = useful prefetches / (useful + wasted). It measures the fraction of issued prefetches that are actually used. (1.5 marks)*
> *Coverage = useful prefetches / (useful + demand-missed-by-prefetcher). It measures the fraction of all misses that the prefetcher successfully catches. (1 mark)*
> *Both are needed because a prefetcher may be 100% accurate (no pollution) but only catch 5% of misses (low coverage), OR catch 100% of misses (high coverage) but thrash the cache (low accuracy). A *good* prefetcher must balance both. (0.5 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **stream buffer-based hardware prefetcher**. Discuss the role of *degree* and *distance* parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(a) Model Solution:**

* **Definition (2 marks):** A *stream buffer* is a small FIFO placed adjacent to the L2 cache. When a cache miss occurs, the address is allocated into a stream buffer; the prefetcher then issues a series of *prefetch requests* for the next `d` (degree) cache lines, starting at the miss address plus a *distance* `D` ahead.

* **Block diagram (3 marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 — Reference Address Table → Stride Detector → Prefetch Queue → L2 / Stream Buffer).*

* **Role of parameters (2 marks):**
  - **Degree $d$** — number of cache lines fetched per trigger. Higher $d$ = more coverage but more bandwidth.
  - **Distance $D$** — how many lines *ahead* of the demand miss the prefetch starts. Larger $D$ = more time to hide latency but higher risk of staleness / pollution.

> **(b)** A system has L1 hit rate 0.92, L1 hit time 1 ns, L2 access time 12 ns, L2 hit rate 0.60, and main memory access time 250 ns. A prefetcher with coverage 0.50 is introduced such that the effective L2 miss penalty becomes 130 ns. **(i)** Compute the AMAT *without* prefetch. **(ii)** Compute the AMAT *with* prefetch. **(iii)** Find the percentage improvement. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Model Solution:**

**(i) AMAT without prefetch (3.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT}_{0} &= h_{L1}\cdot t_{L1} + (1-h_{L1})\cdot\bigl(t_{L1\to L2} + (1-h_{L2})\cdot t_{\text{mem}}\bigr) \\
&= 0.92\cdot 1 + 0.08\cdot\bigl(12 + 0.40\cdot 250\bigr) \\
&= 0.92 + 0.08\cdot(12 + 100) \\
&= 0.92 + 0.08\cdot 112 \\
&= 0.92 + 8.96 \\
&= 9.88 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) AMAT with prefetch (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT}_{\text{pre}} &= 0.92\cdot 1 + 0.08\cdot\bigl(12 + 0.40\cdot 130\bigr) \\
&= 0.92 + 0.08\cdot(12 + 52) \\
&= 0.92 + 0.08\cdot 64 \\
&= 0.92 + 5.12 \\
&= 6.04 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage improvement (1.5 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Improvement} &= \frac{9.88 - 6.04}{9.88}\cdot 100 = \frac{3.84}{9.88}\cdot 100 \approx 38.87\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]*

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **I/O prefetching (read-ahead)** as used in high-performance file systems such as Lustre and parallel HDF5. How does it differ from cache-line prefetching? **(7 marks)**

**(a) Model Solution:**

* **Concept (3 marks):** I/O prefetching, also called *read-ahead*, anticipates that sequential file accesses will continue. When the application issues a `read()` for file offset $o$, the OS or parallel file system *also* issues asynchronous prefetch reads for offsets $o + B, o + 2B, \ldots$ where $B$ is the read-ahead block size. The blocks are placed in the OS page cache. Subsequent `read()` calls hit the cache.

* **HPC specifics (2 marks):** In **Lustre** (parallel FS), each OST (Object Storage Target) client maintains a *readahead window* per file descriptor. In **parallel HDF5 / MPI-IO**, the collective I/O layer prefetches entire *chunks* (e.g., a hyperslab) so that when a compute rank begins processing, the data is already in its buffer.

* **Differences from cache-line prefetching (2 marks):**
  1. *Granularity:* I/O prefetch fetches pages (4 KB – 1 MB), not 64 B cache lines.
  2. *Trigger source:* File-system hints, access-pattern history of the file, not CPU-side miss stream.
  3. *Storage destination:* OS page cache or application buffer, not hardware cache.
  4. *Failure cost:* A wasted I/O prefetch wastes disk *and* network bandwidth (in parallel FS), much costlier than a wasted cache-line prefetch.

> **(b)** Discuss the **three-way trade-off** between prefetch accuracy, coverage, and timeliness with a suitable example. Justify why a *late* prefetch is worse than no prefetch. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Model Solution:**

* **Trade-off explanation (4 marks):**
  - *Accuracy* avoids pollution (wasted cache lines evicted real demand data).
  - *Coverage* ensures misses are caught (otherwise the prefetcher is useless).
