  - *Timeliness* ensures the prefetched line arrives *before* the demand reference.
  - These three cannot be simultaneously maximised with a fixed bandwidth budget. Increasing the prefetch *degree* raises coverage but reduces accuracy. Increasing the *distance* improves timeliness but raises pollution (the data may be evicted before use).

* **Numerical example (2 marks):** A next-line prefetcher with degree $d=2$ and distance $D=2$ on a matrix-transpose kernel: accuracy = 30% (transpose is non-sequential in the second loop), coverage = 95% (most inner-loop misses are caught), timeliness = 100% (sequential inner loop). The same prefetcher on a *linked-list traversal*: accuracy = 5%, coverage = 5%, timeliness = N/A. The trade-off is that the design that maximised coverage in case 1 is useless in case 2.

* **Why late prefetch is worse than no prefetch (1 mark):** A late prefetch still consumes cache capacity and memory-bandwidth (hurting *other* concurrent demand accesses), yet provides no latency hiding. Net effect: AMAT *increases* compared with disabling the prefetcher.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. *Confusing "cache hit" with "prefetch hit":* a prefetch hit means the line was placed **by a prefetch**, not by a prior demand access. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this conflation.
> 2. *Forgetting units:* $t_{\text{mem}}$ is in **ns** (or cycles). Always state units in AMAT calculations.
> 3. *Mixing up coverage $C$ and accuracy $A$:* Coverage is in the *numerator's* denominator as `demand misses missed`, accuracy uses `wasted prefetches`. Swap them and your AMAT expression becomes **physically wrong** (negatives appear).
> 4. *Ignoring pollution:* A 14-mark answer that only computes speedup without discussing wasted bandwidth and eviction cost is marked down by 2–3 marks. Always add a concluding sentence on trade-offs.
> 5. *Forgetting to convert hit rate to miss rate:* $(1 - h_{L1})$ is the *miss rate*, not $h_{L1}$. This is the #1 algebraic error in KTU scripts.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Prefetch (Module 1)**

* **Core idea:** *Fetch before it is needed, in parallel with computation, to hide slow-access latency.*
* **Three pillar metrics:** *Accuracy* (right predictions / total), *Coverage* (misses caught / total misses), *Timeliness* (arrives before demand).
* **Two main families:** *Hardware* (transparent, regular patterns) and *Software* (programmer hints, irregular patterns).
* **I/O counterpart:** *Read-ahead* in page cache / parallel file systems; granularity is pages, not cache lines.
* **Distributed counterpart:** *Non-blocking MPI communication* + prefetch of next-iteration halo regions via RDMA.
* **The Six-Stage Pipeline:** Trigger → Predict → Issue → Transfer → Place → Validate.
* **Golden formulas to memorise:**
  - $\text{AMAT} = h_{L1} t_{L1} + (1-h_{L1})\cdot(t_{L1\to L2} + (1-h_{L2}) t_{\text{mem}})$
  - $A = U / (U + W)$
  - $C = U / (U + N)$
  - $S = \text{AMAT}_{0} / \text{AMAT}_{\text{pre}}$
* **Timeliness condition:** $T_{\text{issue}} + T_{\text{mem}} \le T_{\text{use}}$
* **Trade-off triangle:** ↑ Coverage ⇔ ↓ Accuracy (for fixed bandwidth); ↑ Distance ⇔ ↓ Timeliness reliability.
* **Late prefetch is worse than no prefetch** because it costs bandwidth and cache capacity without hiding latency.
* **Real-world examples to mention in answers:** Intel L2 IP-Prefetcher, AMD Stream Detector, NVIDIA L2 prefetcher, Lustre read-ahead, MPI-IO collective buffering, `__builtin_prefetch()` GCC intrinsic, OpenMP `simd` prefetch hints.
* **One-line exam answer:** *"Prefetching reduces AMAT by $C \cdot (t_{\text{mem}} - t_{L2\to L1})$ provided the prefetcher is accurate and timely."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multicore Processors - Multithreaded Processors - Vector Processors - Design Principles - Maximum Performance Estimates - Programming for Vector Architectures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multicore Processors - Multithreaded Processors - Vector Processors - Design Principles - Maximum Performance Estimates - Programming for Vector Architectures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Multicore Processors

### Formal Definition
A **Multicore Processor** is a single computing component (a single physical integrated circuit / die) that contains **two or more independent processing units (cores)** that read and execute program instructions simultaneously. Each core typically has its own **L1 cache**, may share an **L2 cache**, and shares access to main memory via a coherent interconnect (e.g., Intel QuickPath Interconnect, AMD Infinity Fabric).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A multicore processor is a single processor chip that contains multiple processing units (cores) which execute multiple instruction streams independently, sharing on-chip resources such as last-level cache, memory controllers, and I/O subsystems to improve throughput while staying within power, thermal, and pin-bandwidth constraints.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen
Imagine a single chef (single core) trying to cook 100 dishes. He can only do one task at a time — chop, stir, plate. Now replace him with **4 chefs sharing one kitchen, one pantry, and one delivery window**. Each chef cooks independently, but they share the same refrigerator (LLC cache) and the same service counter (memory bus). This is exactly what happens on a **quad-core processor** — four instruction streams running in parallel, sharing last-level cache and memory bandwidth.

### Key Technical Metrics (Must Remember)
- **Core Count (n):** The number of independent processing units on a single die.
- **Threads per Core (TPC):** Number of hardware threads supported per physical core.
- **Base Clock (f):** Operating frequency in **GHz** (typically 2.0–5.0 GHz).
- **TDP (Thermal Design Power):** Power envelope in **Watts (W)**.
- **Die Area (A):** Measured in **mm²**.
- **Transistor Count:** Number of transistors on the chip (modern CPUs: > **50 Billion**).
- **Inter-core Latency:** Typically **20–50 ns** for L2/L3, **60–100 ns** for DRAM.

### Why Multicore? — The Power Wall
> [!NOTE]
> Around **2004–2005**, single-core CPU clock speeds hit the **"Power Wall"** — Dennard Scaling broke down. Power dissipation P ∝ C · V² · f. Increasing frequency f caused cubic-to-quadratic power growth and unacceptable heat. Industry pivoted from "faster single core" to "more slower cores" — this is the **Multicore Era**.

---

## 1.2 Multithreaded Processors

### Formal Definition
**Multithreading** is a hardware mechanism that allows a **single physical core** to maintain the state of **multiple hardware threads (contexts)** so that when one thread stalls (e.g., on a cache miss), the processor can **switch to another ready thread in zero cycles**, hiding latency and improving core utilization.

### Three Classical Variants (KTU High-Yield)
| Variant | Switch Trigger | Throughput Gain | Latency Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fine-Grained MT** | Every instruction (round-robin) | High | High |
| **Coarse-Grained MT** | On L2/expensive stall | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Simultaneous MT (SMT / Hyper-Threading)** | Issue from multiple threads same cycle | Highest | Lowest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SMT (Simultaneous Multithreading)** is the modern dominant technique. It duplicates only the **architectural state** (registers, PC, renaming tables) while sharing the **execution units**, caches, and pipelines. Example: Intel's **Hyper-Threading Technology** is a form of **2-way SMT**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Single-Person Barber Shop
A barber (single core) without multithreading: if a customer's hair color requires dye, the barber stops and waits. With **coarse-grained multithreading**, when dye is needed, the barber immediately moves to the next customer. With **SMT**, the barber works on **two customers simultaneously** — cutting one customer's hair while another waits for dye to process. Both progress in the same clock cycle.

---

## 1.3 Vector Processors

### Formal Definition
A **Vector Processor** is a CPU architecture designed to execute a single **vector instruction** on **multiple data elements** (a vector of operands) in parallel using dedicated **vector registers** and **vector functional units**. It is the hardware embodiment of the **SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data)** paradigm at the architectural level.

> [!NOTE]
> Vector processors were the dominant supercomputer architecture from the **1970s to 1990s** (Cray-1, Cray-2, NEC SX series). Modern x86 CPUs and GPUs descend from these principles through **SIMD extensions**: SSE, AVX, AVX-512 (Intel), and NEON (ARM).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Industrial Laundry Machine
A scalar processor washes **one shirt per load**. A vector processor installs a **huge industrial drum** that washes **64 shirts simultaneously** using a **single "wash-instruction"**. The control overhead is amortized over many data elements — this is **"amortizing the instruction overhead"**, the central performance idea of vector processing.

### Core Vector Architecture Components
- **Vector Registers:** Wide registers (e.g., 512 bits = 16 × 32-bit elements or 8 × 64-bit elements).
- **Vector Functional Units:** Pipelined units (Vector Add, Vector Multiply, Vector Load/Store) that process one element per cycle.
- **Vector Length Register (VL):** Holds the current vector length.
- **Vector Mask Register (VM):** Enables **predicated execution** (conditional vector ops).
- **Scalar Registers:** Standard scalar RISC registers for control and addresses.

---

## 1.4 Design Principles of HPC Systems

The foundational design principles for high-performance computing (synthesized from Hwu, Kirk, and Hennessy-Patterson):

1. **Use the Right Tool for the Job** — Match the architecture (multicore, GPU, vector) to the workload's data-parallelism vs. task-parallelism profile.
2. **Maximize Bandwidth, Minimize Latency** — Use **bandwidth-oriented design**: streams, prefetch, coalesce.
3. **Hide Latency with Parallelism** — TLP (Thread-Level), ILP (Instruction-Level), DLP (Data-Level).
4. **Balance Computation with Memory and I/O** — Use the **Roofline Model** to ensure the system is balanced.
5. **Amortize Overhead** — Vectorize; batch work; reduce per-element control cost.
6. **Locality is King** — Temporal and spatial locality drive cache hit rate and effective bandwidth.
7. **Design for Sustained, Not Peak Performance** — Real performance = 10–50% of theoretical peak.

---

## 1.5 Maximum Performance Estimates & Programming for Vector Architectures

### Amdahl's Law (Maximum Speedup)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amdahl's Law** defines the **theoretical maximum speedup** of a parallel system as a function of the **parallelizable fraction (P)** of the workload. If fraction **s** must remain serial, then:

$$\text{Maximum Speedup} = \frac{1}{s + \frac{1-s}{n}}$$

where **n** is the number of processors/cores. As **n → ∞**, Speedup → **1/s**.

### Programming for Vector Architectures
Vector programming models and techniques:
- **Auto-vectorization:** Compiler-driven (GCC `-O3 -ftree-vectorize`, ICC, MSVC).
- **Explicit SIMD Intrinsics:** `_mm256_load_ps`, `_mm512_fmadd_ps`.
- **Compiler Directives (Pragmas):** `#pragma omp simd`, `#pragma vector aligned`.
- **Vector Libraries:** Intel MKL, BLAS routines (DGEMM, DAXPY).
- **Array Programming Languages:** Fortran 90/95 array syntax, MATLAB, NumPy.
- **Data Layout:** Structure-of-Arrays (SoA) > Array-of-Structures (AoS) for vectorization.

> [!NOTE]
> **DAXPY (Double-precision A*X Plus Y)** is the canonical vector benchmark: `Y = a·X + Y`. Used in the **Linpack** benchmark and as a teaching example in every HPC textbook.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: Multicore Architecture Anatomy

### 2.1.1 The Memory Hierarchy in Multicore
A multicore chip exposes a **multi-level cache hierarchy**:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         CORE 0                CORE 1                │
│   ┌──────────┐           ┌──────────┐               │
│   │  L1 I/D  │           │  L1 I/D  │   Per-core    │
│   │ (32+32 KB)│          │ (32+32 KB)│  ~4 cycles    │
│   └────┬─────┘           └────┬─────┘               │
│        └──────────┬───────────┘                     │
│             ┌─────┴─────┐                           │
│             │  L2 Cache │   Per-core (or shared)    │
│             │ 256-1024KB│   ~12 cycles              │
│             └─────┬─────┘                           │
│                   │   Coherence (MESI/MOESI)        │
│             ┌─────┴─────┐                           │
│             │  L3 (LLC) │   Shared, Inclusive       │
│             │ 8-64 MB   │   ~40 cycles              │
│             └─────┬─────┘                           │
│                    │                                │
│             ┌─────┴─────┐                           │
│             │   DRAM    │   Off-chip ~200 cycles    │
│             └───────────┘                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.1.2 Cache Coherence Protocols
> [!IMPORTANT]
> When multiple cores cache the **same memory line**, they must agree on its state. The standard protocol is **MESI** (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) or its 5-state extension **MOESI**.

| State | Meaning | Memory Copy |
|---|---|---|
| **M**odified | This cache has the only valid copy, dirty | Stale |
| **E**xclusive | This cache has the only valid copy, clean | Up-to-date |
| **S**hared | Multiple caches have valid copies | Up-to-date |
| **I**nvalid | Line is not valid | — |
| **O**wned (MOESI) | Dirty, but other cores may have shared copy | Stale |

### 2.1.3 Performance Characteristics
- **Shared LLC Hit Latency:** ~30–50 ns
- **Local DRAM Access:** ~60–100 ns
- **Remote Socket DRAM (2-socket):** ~130–200 ns (NUMA penalty)
- **Inter-core Interconnect:** Ring, Mesh, or NoC (Network-on-Chip)

> [!NOTE]
> **NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access):** In multi-socket systems, each socket's cores access their **local** memory faster than the **remote** socket's memory. This is a critical consideration in HPC kernel design — first-touch allocation policy binds memory to the local node.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Multithreading Taxonomy

### 2.2.1 Fine-Grained Multithreading
- Switches threads on **every cycle** (round-robin).
- Hides pipeline stalls completely.
- **Disadvantage:** Slows down any single thread (no priority control).
- **Example:** Denelcor HEP, Tera MTA (early supercomputers).

### 2.2.2 Coarse-Grained Multithreading
- Switches threads only on **expensive stalls** (L2 miss, branch mispredict).
- Reduces switch overhead but **still leaves short stalls exposed**.
- **Example:** IBM RS64-IV, early Itanium "switch-on-stall" logic.

### 2.2.3 Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT)
- Issues instructions from **multiple threads in the same cycle** into the **shared execution units**.
- **Front-end** duplicates the **register file, rename tables, ROB partitions**.
- **Back-end** (ALUs, FUs, caches) is **fully shared**.
- Achieves throughput improvement of **15–30%** with **2-way SMT** on typical workloads.
- **Example:** Intel Hyper-Threading (P4 onwards), IBM POWER8/9/10, AMD Zen (SMT-2).

### 2.2.4 Comparison Summary

| Metric | Single Thread | Fine-Grain MT | Coarse-Grain MT | 2-way SMT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Throughput @ pipeline limit** | 1 IPC | 1 IPC (amortized) | ~1 IPC | ~1.3 IPC |
| **Single-thread latency** | Best | Worst (×N) | Mid | Slightly worse |
| **Hardware cost** | Baseline | Small (PC + regs) | Small | Moderate |
| **Cache contention** | None | High | Medium | Medium |

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive: Vector Processor Microarchitecture

### 2.3.1 Vector Register Architecture
A **vector register** is a wide register file holding **N** elements. For a 512-bit register with 64-bit elements, N = 8.

- **Vector Length (VL):** Actual elements to process (set by `VLR`).
- **Strip Mining:** When VL > max supported (MVL), process the loop in strips.
- **Vector Chaining:** Vector load → vector op → vector store can chain so the next vector op begins as soon as the first element of the previous op completes.
- **Conditional Execution (Predication):** Controlled by **Vector Mask Register (VM)**.

### 2.3.2 Memory-Memory vs. Register-Register Vector Machines

| Type | Loads/Store | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory-Memory** | Operands streamed directly from memory | CDC Star-100, ETA-10 |
| **Register-Register** | Operands first loaded into vector registers | Cray-1, NEC SX-Aurora TSUBASA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern GPUs are register-register vector machines** with very wide register files (NVIDIA A100: 65536 32-bit registers per SM, organized as 64 × 32-element vectors).

### 2.3.3 Vector Instruction Performance
For a vector add `VADD V1, V2, V3` with MVL = 64 elements:
- **Scalar equivalent cycles:** 64 × 4 (load+add+store+addr-gen) ≈ 256 cycles
- **Vector cycles:** 64 (1 per element) + pipeline fill/drain ≈ 70 cycles
- **Speedup:** ~3.6× on a 64-element vector, growing with vector length.

---

## 2.4 Deep Dive: Maximum Performance Estimates

### 2.4.1 Amdahl's Law — Full Derivation
Let the **total workload time** be **T(1) = 1** (normalized). Let fraction **s** be serial and **(1−s)** be parallelizable.

With **n** parallel processors, the parallel fraction is divided by **n**:

$$T(n) = s + \frac{1-s}{n}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{Speedup}(n) = \frac{T(1)}{T(n)} = \frac{1}{s + \frac{1-s}{n}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gusted's Corollary (Strong Scaling Limit):** As **n → ∞**,

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \text{Speedup}(n) = \frac{1}{s}$$

The serial fraction **s** is the **hard ceiling** on speedup, regardless of how many processors you add.

### 2.4.2 Gustafson's Law (Weak Scaling)
For **fixed-time** scaling (scaling problem size with n):

$$\text{Scaled Speedup}(n) = n - s \cdot (n - 1)$$

Real HPC applications usually scale workload with resources — Gustafson is often more realistic.

### 2.4.3 Roofline Performance Model
The **Roofline Model** bounds the achievable performance (GFLOP/s) of a kernel based on its **arithmetic intensity (AI)** and the machine's **peak compute** and **peak bandwidth**.

$$\text{Performance} = \min\left( \text{Peak Compute}, \quad \text{AI} \times \text{Peak Bandwidth} \right)$$

where:

$$\text{AI} = \frac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{Bytes Moved}} \quad \text{[FLOP/Byte]}$$

- **Compute-Bound Region:** AI > Ridge Point (machine balance). Performance is capped by peak FLOPs.
- **Memory-Bound Region:** AI < Ridge Point. Performance is capped by memory bandwidth.

### 2.4.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **KTU Exam-Relevant Formula Sheet** for Module 1. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 scheme exam.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Amdahl's Speedup | $\text{SU}(n) = \dfrac{1}{s + \dfrac{1-s}{n}}$ | $s$ = serial fraction, $n$ = cores |
| 2 | Amdahl's Max Speedup | $\text{SU}_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{s}$ | $n \to \infty$ |
| 3 | Gustafson's Scaled SU | $\text{SU}(n) = n - s(n-1)$ | Weak scaling |
| 4 | Roofline Perf | $P = \min\left( \pi, \beta \cdot \text{AI} \right)$ | $\pi$ = peak GFLOP/s, $\beta$ = B/W (GB/s) |
| 5 | Arithmetic Intensity | $\text{AI} = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{Bytes}}$ | FLOP/Byte |
| 6 | Machine Balance | $B_m = \dfrac{\pi_{\text{peak}}}{\beta_{\text{peak}}}$ | FLOP/Byte |
| 7 | Vector Speedup | $\text{SU}_{\text{vec}} \approx \dfrac{N \cdot r_{\text{scalar}}}{r_{\text{vec}} + N \cdot f_{\text{op}} - f_{\text{op}}}$ | N = vector length |
| 8 | CPU Time | $T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{\text{IC} \cdot \text{CPI}}{f}$ | IC = Inst Count, CPI = cycles/inst, $f$ = Hz |
| 9 | MFLOPS | $\text{MFLOPS} = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs}}{T \cdot 10^6}$ | $T$ in seconds |
| 10 | Parallel Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{SU}(n)}{n}$ | $0 < \eta \le 1$ |
| 11 | Karp-Flatt Metric | $e = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{\text{SU}(n)} - \dfrac{1}{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{n}}$ | Diagnoses serial fraction |
| 12 | Power Dissipation | $P = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ | Dennard scaling |
| 13 | Bandwidth-Latency Product | $\text{BL} = \beta \cdot \tau$ | — |

---

## 2.5 Deep Dive: Programming for Vector Architectures

### 2.5.1 Six Step Vectorization Process
1. **Profile the code** — Identify hotspots.
2. **Use array-style notation** — Avoid pointer aliasing.
3. **Loop interchange / fusion** — Get the inner loop to be the vectorizable one.
4. **Apply `#pragma omp simd`** — Compiler hint.
5. **Verify with compiler diagnostics** — GCC/Clang `-fopt-info-vec`.
6. **Use aligned allocators** — `_mm_malloc` (32/64-byte aligned).

### 2.5.2 Key Compiler Directives & Pragmas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are **KTU favorite exam topics** — know them cold.

| Directive | Effect |
|---|---|
| `#pragma omp simd` | Vectorize the loop across SIMD lanes |
| `#pragma omp simd aligned(a,b:64)` | Assert 64-byte alignment for `a, b` |
| `#pragma omp simd reduction(+:sum)` | Handle reduction across SIMD lanes |
| `#pragma omp parallel for simd` | Both threading and SIMD |
| `#pragma vector aligned` | Compiler-specific (Intel) |

### 2.5.3 OpenMP Parallel + SIMD Example Pattern (HPC-relevant, mentioned in CS topics only)
> Allowed because the **course is PECST757 (HPC)** — programming is part of the syllabus.

```c
#include <omp.h>
#define N 1000000
double a[N], b[N], c[N];

void saxpy_omp_simd(double x) {
    #pragma omp parallel for simd aligned(a,b,c:64)
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        a[i] = x * b[i] + c[i];   // SAXPY: a = x·b + c
    }
}
```

> This achieves **OpenMP threading (TLP)** + **compiler SIMD vectorization (DLP)** in one loop.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Amdahl's Law with Worked Numerical Examples

### Derivation Step 1: Decompose Workload

Let total execution time on **1 processor = 1 unit (normalized)**. Let **s** = serial fraction, **p = (1 − s)** = parallelizable fraction.

$$T_{\text{serial}} = s \cdot T(1) = s$$

$$T_{\text{parallel,1}} = p \cdot T(1) = 1 - s$$

### Derivation Step 2: Parallelize Across n Processors

The serial portion **s** cannot be parallelized; it remains **s**. The parallel portion **(1 − s)** is divided across **n** cores:

$$T(n) = s + \frac{1-s}{n}$$

### Derivation Step 3: Speedup Definition

$$\text{SU}(n) = \frac{T(1)}{T(n)} = \frac{1}{s + \dfrac{1-s}{n}}$$

### Worked Example 1: HPC Cluster Node Count Selection

**Problem:** A parallel program has **5%** of its execution that is inherently serial (driver init + reduction). You have access to nodes of size **8, 16, 32, 64, 128** cores. Plot the achievable speedup.

**Given:** s = 0.05, p = 0.95.

**For n = 8:**

$$\text{SU}(8) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.11875} = \frac{1}{0.16875} = 5.93$$

**For n = 16:**

$$\text{SU}(16) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.059375} = \frac{1}{0.109375} = 9.14$$

**For n = 32:**

$$\text{SU}(32) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{32}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.02969} = \frac{1}{0.07969} = 12.55$$

**For n = 64:**

$$\text{SU}(64) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{64}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.01484} = \frac{1}{0.06484} = 15.42$$

**For n = 128:**

$$\text{SU}(128) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{128}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.00742} = \frac{1}{0.05742} = 17.42$$

**For n = ∞:**

$$\text{SU}_{\max} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20.00$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Even with **128 cores**, the speedup is only **17.42×** — and the absolute ceiling is **20×**. This is why HPC engineers focus relentlessly on **eliminating serial sections** (e.g., scalable reductions, asynchronous I/O).

### Worked Example 2: Parallel Efficiency

For the **n = 64** case above:

$$\eta_{64} = \frac{\text{SU}(64)}{64} = \frac{15.42}{64} = 0.241 = 24.1\%$$

For **n = 8**:

$$\eta_{8} = \frac{5.93}{8} = 0.741 = 74.1\%$$

> Efficiency drops sharply as **n** grows. KTU students should know that beyond a "knee" point, adding cores yields diminishing returns.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Roofline Model

### Step 1: Define the Two Hardware Limits

**Peak Compute ($\pi$):** Determined by frequency, SIMD width, and number of cores.

$$\pi_{\text{peak}} = n_{\text{cores}} \times \text{Freq} \times \text{FLOP/cycle/core}$$

Example: An 8-core CPU at **3.0 GHz** with **AVX-512** (32 FLOP/cycle for FMA):

$$\pi_{\text{peak}} = 8 \times 3.0 \times 10^9 \times 32 = 768 \times 10^9 = 768 \text{ GFLOP/s}$$

**Peak Bandwidth ($\beta$):** Determined by memory subsystem.

Example: Dual-channel DDR5-5600 with 2 × 38.4 GB/s:

$$\beta_{\text{peak}} = 2 \times 38.4 = 76.8 \text{ GB/s}$$

### Step 2: Compute Machine Balance

$$B_m = \frac{\pi_{\text{peak}}}{\beta_{\text{peak}}} = \frac{768}{76.8} = 10 \text{ FLOP/Byte}$$

This is the **"ridge point"** of the Roofline. Kernels with **AI > 10** are compute-bound; **AI < 10** are memory-bound.

### Step 3: Compute Kernel AI

For **DAXPY** (Y = a·X + Y) with **N** double-precision elements:

$$\text{FLOPs} = 2N \quad \text{(1 mul + 1 add)}$$

$$\text{Bytes} = 3N \times 8 = 24N \quad \text{(read X, read Y, write Y, 8 bytes each)}$$

$$\text{AI} = \frac{2N}{24N} = \frac{1}{12} = 0.083 \text{ FLOP/Byte}$$

> Since **0.083 < 10**, DAXPY is **memory-bound** on this machine.

### Step 4: Roofline Performance Bound

$$P_{\max} = \min\left(768, \quad 0.083 \times 76.8\right) = \min(768, 6.4) = 6.4 \text{ GFLOP/s}$$

This is only **0.83%** of peak FLOPs — DAXPY is bandwidth-starved. To improve, **reduce bytes moved** (use SoA, blocking, prefetch).

### Step 5: For Matrix Multiply (DGEMM)

For a tile of size **B × B**, blocked DGEMM:

$$\text{AI} \approx \frac{2 B^3}{3 \times 8 \times B^2} = \frac{B}{12} \text{ FLOP/Byte}$$

For **B = 128**: $\text{AI} = 10.67$ — just above the ridge. Beyond this, **compute-bound**.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Vector Pipeline Speedup

### Setup
A vector processor with **MVL = 64** executes **VADD V1, V2, V3**.
- Pipeline fill time: $t_{\text{fill}}$
- Per-element latency: $\tau$ (cycles)
- Drain time: $t_{\text{drain}}$

### Vector Execution Time (with Chaining)
With chaining (load → add overlaps):

$$T_{\text{vec}} = t_{\text{startup}} + N \cdot \tau$$

For N = 1024, $t_{\text{startup}} = 30$ cycles, $\tau = 1$ cycle/element:

$$T_{\text{vec}} = 30 + 1024 \times 1 = 1054 \text{ cycles}$$

### Scalar Equivalent Time
Each scalar iteration needs 4 instructions: load, load, add, store. CPI = 4.

$$T_{\text{scalar}} = 1024 \times 4 = 4096 \text{ cycles}$$

### Vector Speedup

$$\text{SU}_{\text{vec}} = \frac{4096}{1054} \approx 3.89 \times$$

**As N → ∞:**

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} \text{SU}_{\text{vec}} = \frac{N \cdot r_{\text{scalar}}}{N \cdot \tau} = \frac{r_{\text{scalar}}}{\tau}$$

For our case: $\dfrac{4}{1} = 4\times$ (theoretical maximum, the **R_{∞}** metric).

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between Multicore Processors and Multithreaded Processors. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Multicore Processor | Multithreaded Processor |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A single chip with multiple independent physical cores | A single physical core that supports multiple hardware threads |
| **Parallelism** | **Thread-Level Parallelism (TLP)** between cores | **Instruction-Level interleaving** within a core |
| **Duplication** | Duplicates entire pipeline, L1 caches, register files | Duplicates only architectural state (regs, PC) — shares execution units |
| **Example** | AMD Ryzen 9 7950X (16 cores) | Intel Core i9 with Hyper-Threading (2-way SMT) |
| **Goal** | Increase throughput by adding cores | Increase utilization of single core by hiding stalls |

> **[Marking scheme: 1 mark definition, 1 mark difference, 1 mark example — 3 marks]**

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the term "Vector Processor" and explain the concept of "Amortizing the Instruction Overhead".**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Vector Processor** is a CPU architecture that applies a single instruction to a sequence of data elements (vectors) using wide vector registers and pipelined vector functional units. Example: Cray-1, NEC SX-Aurora.

**Amortizing the Instruction Overhead:** In a scalar loop of N iterations, an instruction must be fetched, decoded, and issued **N times** to operate on N data elements. In a vector processor, a single vector instruction is fetched/decoded once and then applied to **N elements** by the hardware pipeline. The instruction overhead (fetch + decode + issue) is **amortized over N data elements**, yielding a speedup that grows with vector length. The ideal vector speedup equals the **scalar CPI per iteration**.

> **[Marking scheme: 1.5 marks definition, 1.5 marks amortizing concept — 3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **ANY ONE** full question (A or B) for 14 marks. Each full question has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Multicore Processor. Discuss the advantages of multicore design over single-core processors, and explain cache coherence with MESI protocol.**

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Diagram (Textual):**

```
        ┌──────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐
        │   CORE 0     │         │   CORE 1     │
        │ ┌────┐┌────┐ │         │ ┌────┐┌────┐ │
        │ │L1I ││L1D │ │         │ │L1I ││L1D │ │
        │ └─┬──┘└─┬──┘ │         │ └─┬──┘└─┬──┘ │
        │   └────┬┘    │         │   └────┬┘    │
        │   ┌────┴┐    │         │   ┌────┴┐    │
        │   │  L2 │    │         │   │  L2 │    │
        │   └────┬┘    │         │   └────┬┘    │
        └────────┼─────┘         └────────┼─────┘
                 │         MESI            │
                 │   Coherence Bus         │
                 └──────────┬──────────────┘
                       ┌─────┴─────┐
                       │  L3 / LLC │
                       └─────┬─────┘
                       ┌─────┴─────┐
                       │   DRAM    │
                       └───────────┘
```

**Key Architectural Features (3 marks):**
1. **Multiple cores on a single die** (typically 2–128).
2. **Private L1/L2 caches** for each core to reduce contention.
3. **Shared Last-Level Cache (LLC)** to enable inter-core data sharing.
4. **Coherent interconnect** (ring, mesh, or NoC) using MESI/MOESI protocol.
5. **Shared memory controllers** with multi-channel DRAM access.

**Advantages over Single-Core (2 marks):**
1. **Higher throughput** — multiple instruction streams run in parallel.
2. **Better power efficiency** — multiple slower cores consume less power than one very fast core (P ∝ CV²f).
3. **Scalable performance** — chip manufacturers add cores instead of raising frequency.
4. **Shared resources** — die-level cache sharing reduces redundant memory accesses.

**Cache Coherence — MESI Protocol (2 marks):**

When two cores cache the same line, MESI keeps their views consistent via four states:
- **M (Modified):** This core's copy is dirty; no other core has the line.
- **E (Exclusive):** This core's copy is clean and unique.
- **S (Shared):** Multiple cores have read-only copies.
- **I (Invalid):** The line is not present.

When Core 0 wants to **write** to a line that is **Shared** in Core 1's cache, a **Bus Upgrade** is sent, Core 1 transitions its line to **Invalid**, and Core 0 transitions to **Modified**. This maintains the single-writer invariant.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
**Explain the three types of multithreading: Fine-Grained, Coarse-Grained, and Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT). Compare their throughput and hardware cost.**

**Model Solution:**

**Fine-Grained Multithreading (2 marks):**
- Switches to a different thread **every cycle** in round-robin order.
- **Pros:** Hides all pipeline stalls (data hazard, branch latency).
- **Cons:** Slows down any single thread by a factor of N (thread count).
- **Hardware cost:** Low — only thread state (PC, registers) duplicated.
- **Example:** Tera MTA, Denelcor HEP.

**Coarse-Grained Multithreading (2 marks):**
- Switches threads **only on costly stalls** (e.g., L2 cache miss, branch mispredict).
- **Pros:** Lower context-switch overhead than fine-grain; single-thread latency preserved when no stalls.
- **Cons:** Short stalls (L1 miss) are still exposed.
- **Hardware cost:** Low — same as fine-grain, plus a stall detector.
- **Example:** IBM RS64-IV.

**Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT) (2 marks):**
- Issues instructions from **multiple threads in the same cycle** into the shared execution units.
- **Pros:** Achieves dynamic resource sharing; up to **30%** throughput gain on 2-way SMT.
- **Cons:** Shared cache contention; security concerns (Spectre-class attacks).
- **Hardware cost:** Moderate — duplicate architectural state (regs, rename tables, ROB partitions).
- **Example:** Intel Hyper-Threading, IBM POWER, AMD Zen SMT.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Metric | Fine-Grain | Coarse-Grain | 2-way SMT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Switch trigger | Every cycle | On stall | Every cycle, multiple threads |
| Single-thread latency | Worst (×N) | Moderate | Slightly worse |
| Throughput gain | 1× of pipeline | ~1× | ~1.3× of pipeline |
| Hardware duplication | Small | Small | Moderate |

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**State and derive Amdahl's Law for parallel speedup. A program has 8% serial fraction. Calculate the maximum speedup achievable on (i) 16 cores and (ii) 64 cores. Comment on the result.**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement (1 mark):**
> *"The maximum speedup of a parallel program is limited by its serial portion. No matter how many processors you add, you cannot speedup beyond 1/s, where s is the fraction of the program that must be executed serially."* — Gene Amdahl, 1967.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Let total execution time on 1 processor = 1 unit. Let **s** = serial fraction, **(1 − s)** = parallelizable fraction.

With **n** processors, the parallel part is divided among them:

$$T(n) = s \cdot 1 + \frac{(1-s) \cdot 1}{n} = s + \frac{1-s}{n}$$

Speedup is defined as:

$$\text{SU}(n) = \frac{T(1)}{T(n)} = \frac{1}{s + \dfrac{1-s}{n}}$$

As **n → ∞**:

$$\text{SU}_{\max} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{s + \dfrac{1-s}{n}} = \frac{1}{s}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

**Given:** s = 0.08, p = 0.92.

**(i) For n = 16:**

$$\text{SU}(16) = \frac{1}{0.08 + \dfrac{0.92}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.0575} = \frac{1}{0.1375} = 7.27$$

**(ii) For n = 64:**

$$\text{SU}(64) = \frac{1}{0.08 + \dfrac{0.92}{64}} = \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.014375} = \frac{1}{0.094375} = 10.60$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
- The **theoretical maximum** is $\text{SU}_{\max} = 1/0.08 = 12.5\times$.
- Even on **64 cores**, we achieve only **10.6×** — a 4× increase in cores gives only 1.46× more speedup.
- This demonstrates the **diminishing returns** of adding cores when a serial fraction exists. The serial portion dominates scaling beyond the "knee point".

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Explain the Roofline Performance Model with a diagram. For a CPU with peak compute of 800 GFLOP/s and peak memory bandwidth of 50 GB/s, calculate the machine balance. Identify whether a matrix multiplication with AI = 8 FLOP/Byte is compute-bound or memory-bound on this machine, and calculate the achievable performance.**

**Model Solution:**

**Roofline Model Explanation (3 marks):**
The Roofline Model, proposed by Williams, Waterman, and Patterson (2009), plots the **achievable performance (GFLOP/s)** vs. the **arithmetic intensity (AI)** of a kernel, bounded by two ceilings:
1. The **horizontal ceiling** = peak compute $\pi$ (GFLOP/s).
2. The **slanted ceiling** = AI × peak bandwidth $\beta$ (GB/s).

The achievable performance is the **min** of the two:

$$P = \min(\pi, \quad \beta \cdot \text{AI})$$

The intersection defines the **machine balance** $B_m = \pi / \beta$.

**Machine Balance Calculation (1 mark):**

$$B_m = \frac{\pi}{\beta} = \frac{800 \text{ GFLOP/s}}{50 \text{ GB/s}} = 16 \text{ FLOP/Byte}$$

**Kernel Analysis (2 marks):**
- Given kernel: **Matrix Multiplication** with AI = 8 FLOP/Byte.
- Compare: **AI = 8 < B_m = 16**.
- Therefore, the kernel is in the **Memory-Bound Region** (slanted ceiling applies).

**Achievable Performance (1 mark):**

$$P = \min(800, \quad 50 \times 8) = \min(800, 400) = 400 \text{ GFLOP/s}$$

This is only **50%** of peak compute. To improve: **reduce bytes moved** via blocking/tiling until AI > 16.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions**
> 1. **Amdahl's Law:** Students often write $SU = n$ instead of $SU = 1/(s + (1-s)/n)$. **−2 marks** if the denominator is wrong.
> 2. **Roofline:** Students confuse the *machine balance* with the *kernel AI*. Machine balance is a property of the **hardware**; AI is a property of the **kernel**. **−1 mark** if swapped.
> 3. **Vector Processor:** Students forget to mention the **Vector Length Register (VL)** and **Vector Mask Register (VM)**. **−1 mark** per missing component.
> 4. **SMT vs Multicore:** Writing "SMT uses multiple cores" — **WRONG**. SMT uses **multiple threads on ONE core**. **−2 marks** for this confusion.
> 5. **MESI:** Listing only 3 of the 4 states (forgetting **Exclusive**). **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Units:** Writing GFLOP/s instead of FLOP/Byte for AI — **−1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 1**

- [x] **Multicore Processor:** Multiple independent cores on a single die, sharing LLC and memory controllers. Driven by the **Power Wall** and the breakdown of Dennard Scaling (~2004).
- [x] **Multithreading Types:** **Fine-grained** (switch every cycle), **Coarse-grained** (switch on stall), **SMT** (issue from multiple threads same cycle).
- [x] **SMT = Intel Hyper-Threading:** Duplicates only architectural state, shares execution units. ~30% gain on 2-way SMT.
- [x] **Vector Processor:** SIMD at architectural level; uses vector registers, vector functional units, VLR, VM. **Amortizes instruction overhead** over N data elements.
- [x] **Vector Chaining:** Overlaps dependent vector ops to hide latency.
- [x] **Cache Coherence:** MESI = Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid. Ensures single-writer invariant.
- [x] **NUMA:** Non-Uniform Memory Access — local socket memory faster than remote.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law:** $\text{SU}(n) = \dfrac{1}{s + \dfrac{1-s}{n}}$, max = $1/s$.
- [x] **Gustafson's Law:** $\text{SU}(n) = n - s(n-1)$ — for **scaled** (weak) problems.
- [x] **Karp-Flatt Metric:** Diagnoses effective serial fraction.
- [x] **Roofline Model:** $P = \min(\pi, \beta \cdot \text{AI})$; machine balance $B_m = \pi/\beta$.
- [x] **DAXPY:** Canonical vector benchmark. $Y = aX + Y$. AI = 1/12 FLOP/Byte.
- [x] **Six Steps to Vectorize:** Profile → array notation → loop interchange → `#pragma omp simd` → compiler diagnostics → aligned allocation.
- [x] **Compiler Directives to Know:** `#pragma omp simd`, `#pragma omp simd reduction`, `#pragma omp parallel for simd`.
- [x] **Power Dissipation:** $P = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ (Dennard scaling broke down ~2004).
- [x] **CPU Time:** $T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{\text{IC} \times \text{CPI}}{f}$.
- [x] **Parallel Efficiency:** $\eta = SU(n)/n$.
- [x] **Vector Speedup Limit:** $R_\infty = r_{\text{scalar}} / \tau$ (ratio of scalar CPI to per-element vector pipeline latency).
- [x] **First-Touch Policy:** `malloc` returns uninitialized pages; first `write` binds them to the local NUMA node.
- [x] **SoA vs AoS:** Structure-of-Arrays enables vectorization; Array-of-Structures does not.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Computers - Taxonomy of Parallel Computing Paradigms - Shared-Memory Computers - Cache Coherence - UMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parallel Computers - Taxonomy of Parallel Computing Paradigms - Shared-Memory Computers - Cache Coherence - UMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Parallel Computers

A **Parallel Computer** is a computational system that uses multiple processing elements (PEs) simultaneously to solve a single problem by dividing the workload among cooperating processors that communicate through shared memory or message passing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Adopted Standard):**
> *A parallel computer is a multiple-instruction, multiple-data (MIMD) processor system that allows concurrent execution of processes, coordinated via either a shared physical memory or a high-speed interconnection network.*

### Conceptual Analogy
> Imagine a **large kitchen preparing a wedding feast**. One chef (a single processor) would take 10 hours. If you hire 10 chefs, each handling a different dish simultaneously, the same feast could be ready in ~1.5 hours (overhead included). The chefs must occasionally share a common stove (shared memory) or shout instructions across the kitchen (message passing) to coordinate. That coordination mechanism is precisely what defines the **parallel computing paradigm**.

---

## 1.2 Taxonomy of Parallel Computing Paradigms

Parallel computing paradigms are typically classified using **Flynn's Taxonomy** (1966), which categorizes systems by the number of instruction streams and data streams they handle simultaneously.

| Class | Instruction Stream | Data Stream | Real-World Example |
|-------|-------------------|-------------|--------------------|
| **SISD** | Single | Single | Traditional desktop PC |
| **SIMD** | Single | Multiple | GPUs, Vector processors |
| **MISD** | Multiple | Single | Fault-tolerant pipelines (rare) |
| **MIMD** | Multiple | Multiple | Multicore CPUs, Clusters |

> [!NOTE]
> Beyond Flynn, modern HPC extends this taxonomy to include **SPMD** (Single Program Multiple Data) and **MPMD** (Multiple Program Multiple Data) — these are practical sub-categories of MIMD used in MPI/OpenMP programming.

---

## 1.3 Shared-Memory Computers

**Shared-Memory Computers** are MIMD systems where **all processors access a single, common logical address space**. Any processor can read/write any memory location, and communication between processors is implicit via memory reads/writes.

### Conceptual Analogy
> Picture a **large whiteboard in a conference room**. Multiple staff members (processors) stand around it simultaneously — anyone can read what is on the board, and anyone can write new information. The board itself is the *shared memory*.

Two principal sub-classes exist:
- **UMA (Uniform Memory Access)**
- **NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access)**

---

## 1.4 Cache Coherence

**Cache Coherence** is the consistency property in shared-memory multiprocessors that ensures **all cached copies of a memory location are kept consistent** with the most recent write. When one processor modifies a cached line, all other processors' caches must either see that update or invalidate their stale copy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Adopted Standard):**
> *Cache coherence is a hardware-supported protocol that maintains a single, system-wide consistent view of memory by tracking the state of each cache line across all processors, preventing stale-data reads.*

### Conceptual Analogy
> Imagine three librarians (caches) each holding a copy of the same page from a textbook. If Librarian A updates the page in his notebook, Librarians B and C must be informed to either erase their old copy or replace it with the new one — otherwise, a student asking any librarian might get the wrong answer. **That update protocol is cache coherence.**

---

## 1.5 UMA — Uniform Memory Access

**UMA (Uniform Memory Access)** is a shared-memory architecture where **every processor takes the same time to access any memory word**, regardless of which processor makes the request and which memory module holds the word. Also known as **SMP (Symmetric Multiprocessor)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Adopted Standard):**
> *UMA is a shared-memory MIMD architecture where the memory access latency is identical (uniform) for all processors accessing any location in the global shared address space — typically realized via a bus, crossbar, or switched interconnect.*

### Conceptual Analogy
> Picture **ten customers in a bank with identical service windows**. Whether you walk to Window 1 or Window 7, the time to be served is identical. There is no "faster" window because service is uniform. This is UMA — **every processor "stands" equally far from every memory bank**.

### Key UMA Metrics (KTU Board-Favorite Terminology)
- **Access Latency:** *constant* across all processor–memory pairs.
- **Interconnect:** bus, crossbar, or single-stage switch.
- **Scalability:** limited to **16–64 processors** in practice (bus contention).
- **Coherence Protocol:** typically **snooping-based**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Flynn's Taxonomy — Operational Breakdown

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **SISD (Single Instruction Single Data)**
   - One instruction stream → One data stream.
   - Traditional uniprocessor; no parallelism.
   - Latency hidden only via pipelining/superscalar techniques.

2. **SIMD (Single Instruction Multiple Data)**
   - One instruction broadcast to many ALUs operating on different data items.
   - Best for *data-parallel* problems (image processing, matrix arithmetic).
   - Implementation: Vector pipelines, GPUs (NVIDIA CUDA cores).

3. **MISD (Multiple Instruction Single Data)**
   - Multiple instructions process the same data stream.
   - Rare; used in fault-tolerant systems (e.g., space shuttle computers).

4. **MIMD (Multiple Instruction Multiple Data)**
   - Each processor executes its own instruction stream on its own data.
   - Most general class; includes multicore CPUs, clusters, NUMA systems.
   - Sub-divided into:
     - **Shared-Memory MIMD** (SMP/UMA, NUMA)
     - **Distributed-Memory MIMD** (Clusters, MPP)

---

## 2.2 Shared-Memory Architecture — Why and How

### Why Shared Memory?
- **Programming Simplicity:** A single address space → no explicit data shipping.
- **Natural fine-grained communication:** processors share data via simple loads/stores.
- **High bandwidth** for small messages vs. message passing.

### How It Works (Operational Steps)
1. Processors connect to **memory modules** through an **interconnect** (bus / crossbar / switch).
2. A **memory controller** arbitrates concurrent requests (e.g., using priority or round-robin).
3. **Cache hierarchy (L1/L2/L3)** per processor reduces average latency.
4. A **coherence protocol** (snooping or directory) keeps caches consistent.

---

## 2.3 Cache Coherence — The Core Problem & Protocols

### Why Cache Coherence is Needed
- Each processor has **private L1/L2 caches**.
- Two caches may hold **different (stale) values** of the same memory location.
- Without coherence, programs produce **non-deterministic incorrect results**.

### The Two Pillars of Coherence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Write Propagation (Coherence):** A write by P1 must eventually be visible to P2.
> **2. Write Serialization:** Writes to the same location appear in the same order to all processors.

### Snooping Coherence Protocols

| Protocol | States | Use Case |
|----------|--------|----------|
| **MSI** | Modified, Shared, Invalid | Basic baseline |
| **MESI** | Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid | Most common in x86 (Intel/AMD) |
| **MOESI** | Modified, Owned, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid | AMD's extended protocol |
| **MESIF** | Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid, Forward | Intel's quick-forward |

> **Real-world engineering utility:** Cache coherence is the foundation of correctness in multicore smartphones, servers, laptops, and embedded SoCs. Every modern CPU you use implements MESI or a close variant.

### Directory-Based Coherence
- A **centralized directory** tracks which caches hold a copy of each block.
- Scales to **hundreds/thousands of processors** (used in NUMA, e.g., SGI Origin, Intel QPI).
- Slower for small systems due to directory lookup overhead.

---

## 2.4 UMA Architecture — Operational View

### Structural Steps
1. **N processors** are connected to **M memory modules** via a **bus or crossbar**.
2. Each processor has a private L1 (and often L2) cache.
3. The memory is **physically distributed but logically unified** (single address space).
4. **Memory access time** is constant: $T_{UMA} = T_{bus} + T_{memory}$ for every (processor, address) pair.
5. **Coherence:** maintained via **snooping on the shared bus** (each cache monitors bus transactions).

### UMA vs NUMA — Why UMA is Simpler but Less Scalable

| Property | UMA (SMP) | NUMA |
|----------|-----------|------|
| Access time | **Uniform** | Non-uniform |
| Interconnect | Shared bus / crossbar | Distributed network |
| Scalability | Low (~64 cores) | High (~thousands) |
| Programming model | Easier | Harder (affinity-aware) |
| Example | Intel Xeon E5 dual-socket (early) | AMD EPYC, Intel Xeon scalable |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory formulas — memorize these for the 14-mark derivations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Speedup | $S = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p}$ | $T_s$ = serial time, $T_p$ = parallel time |
| 2 | Efficiency | $E = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{T_s}{N \cdot T_p}$ | $N$ = number of processors |
| 3 | Amdahl's Law | $S = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{N}}$ | $f$ = serial fraction |
| 4 | Amdahl's Max Speedup | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | as $N \to \infty$ |
| 5 | Gustafson's Law | $S = N - f(N - 1)$ | scaled problem size |
| 6 | Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) | $AMAT = H + M \cdot MR$ | $H$=hit time, $M$=miss penalty, $MR$=miss rate |
| 7 | UMA Bus Throughput | $BW_{bus} = \dfrac{W \cdot f}{N_{proc}}$ | $W$=bus width, $f$=clock |
| 8 | Coherence Traffic Overhead | $T_{coh} \propto \text{Invalidations} \cdot T_{bus}$ | grows with $N$ |
| 9 | Crossbar Complexity | $O(N \times M)$ | for $N$ procs, $M$ memories |
| 10 | MESI State Transition Cost | $C_{MESI} = 4 \cdot T_{bus}$ | per shared-line update |

> **Engineering utility:** Speedup/Efficiency/Amdahl's are the *most frequently asked* 14-mark derivations in KTU Module-2 ESE questions.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Amdahl's Law — Exhaustive Derivation

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-Mark Setup):**
> *A parallel program spends 15% of its execution time in inherently serial code. The remaining 85% is perfectly parallelizable. Compute the speedup when using (a) 4 processors, (b) 16 processors, and (c) an infinite number of processors.*

### Step 1 — Define the Total Serial Execution Time

Let the total serial runtime be normalized to $T_s = 1$ unit.

The serial fraction is $f = 0.15$.
The parallel fraction is $(1 - f) = 0.85$.

### Step 2 — Express Time on $N$ Processors

The serial portion remains unaffected by adding processors. The parallel portion scales down by a factor of $N$ (ideal case, no overhead).

$$
T_p = f \cdot T_s + \dfrac{(1 - f) \cdot T_s}{N}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify

$$
T_p = 0.15 \cdot 1 + \dfrac{0.85 \cdot 1}{N}
$$

$$
T_p = 0.15 + \dfrac{0.85}{N}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Speedup Formula

$$
S = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p} = \dfrac{1}{0.15 + \dfrac{0.85}{N}}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute for $N = 4$

$$
T_p = 0.15 + \dfrac{0.85}{4} = 0.15 + 0.2125 = 0.3625
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{0.3625} \approx 2.758
$$

### Step 6 — Compute for $N = 16$

$$
T_p = 0.15 + \dfrac{0.85}{16} = 0.15 + 0.053125 = 0.203125
$$

$$
S = \dfrac{1}{0.203125} \approx 4.923
$$

### Step 7 — Compute Maximum Speedup ($N \to \infty$)

$$
S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{N}} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.667
$$

### Result Table

| $N$ | $T_p$ | $S$ |
|-----|-------|-----|
| 1 | 1.000 | 1.000 |
| 4 | 0.3625 | 2.758 |
| 16 | 0.203125 | 4.923 |
| $\infty$ | 0.15 | 6.667 |

> **Conclusion:** Even with infinite processors, we cannot exceed a **6.667×** speedup because the 15% serial portion acts as a hard ceiling. This is the *Amdahl bottleneck*.

---

## 3.2 MESI State Transition — Coherence Walkthrough

### Scenario: Two Cores (P1, P2) sharing block B (initial state: Invalid in both)

**Step 1:** P1 reads B → cache miss → bus transaction `BusRd` → memory responds.
→ P1 transitions **I → S** (Shared).

**Step 2:** P2 reads B → cache miss → bus transaction `BusRd`.
→ Memory sees P1 already has copy in S → responds directly.
→ P2 transitions **I → S**.

**Step 3:** P1 writes B → state must change.
→ P1 issues `BusUpgr` (invalidate) on the bus.
→ P2 snoops the invalidate → P2 transitions **S → I**.
→ P1 transitions **S → M** (Modified — exclusive ownership).

**Step 4:** P2 reads B again → cache miss.
→ P2 issues `BusRd`.
→ P1 intercepts (cache-to-cache transfer), writes back to memory or flushes.
→ P1 transitions **M → S**.
→ P2 transitions **I → S**.

### Transition Matrix (MESI)

| Current State | Read Hit | Read Miss | Write Hit | Write Miss |
|---------------|----------|-----------|-----------|------------|
| **M** | M | M→S (flush) | M | M→M (invalidate others) |
| **E** | E | E→S | E→M | E→S (others invalidate) |
| **S** | S | S | S→M (invalidate) | S→M (invalidate) |
| **I** | I→S/E (fetch) | I→S/E | I→M (invalidate) | I→M |

---

## 3.3 Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) for UMA

Suppose a cache has:
- Hit time $H = 2$ ns
- Miss penalty $M = 100$ ns
- Miss rate $MR = 0.05$

$$
AMAT = H + MR \cdot M = 2 + 0.05 \cdot 100 = 2 + 5 = 7 \text{ ns}
$$

If the coherence protocol adds **extra invalidation traffic** averaging $M_{coh} = 20$ ns per miss:

$$
AMAT_{coh} = H + MR \cdot (M + M_{coh}) = 2 + 0.05 \cdot 120 = 2 + 6 = 8 \text{ ns}
$$

> **Insight:** The AMAT penalty from coherence traffic is a real bottleneck in UMA buses and grows non-linearly with the number of processors.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flynn's Taxonomy — Hierarchical Block View

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["Parallel Computing Paradigms"]
    root --> sisd["SISD - Single Instruction Single Data"]
    root --> simd["SIMD - Single Instruction Multiple Data"]
    root --> misd["MISD - Multiple Instruction Single Data"]
    root --> mimd["MIMD - Multiple Instruction Multiple Data"]

    sisd --> sisdEx["Example Desktop PC"]
    simd --> simdEx["Example GPU Vector Unit"]
    misd --> misdEx["Example Fault Tolerant Pipeline"]
    mimd --> smp["Shared Memory SMP UMA NUMA"]
    mimd --> dmem["Distributed Memory Clusters MPP"]
```

## 4.2 UMA Shared-Memory Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    p1["Processor P1"] --> c1["L1 Cache P1"]
    p2["Processor P2"] --> c2["L1 Cache P2"]
    p3["Processor P3"] --> c3["L1 Cache P3"]
    p4["Processor P4"] --> c4["L1 Cache P4"]

    c1 --> bus["Shared Bus or Crossbar Switch"]
    c2 --> bus
    c3 --> bus
    c4 --> bus

    bus --> m1["Memory Module M1"]
    bus --> m2["Memory Module M2"]
    bus --> m3["Memory Module M3"]
    bus --> m4["Memory Module M4"]

    bus --> cohCtrl["Snoop Controller Coherence Logic"]
```

> **Note:** Every P-to-M path traverses the same `bus` node → uniform latency by construction.

## 4.3 MESI Coherence State Machine — Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    inv["State INVALID"] -->|Read Miss BusRd| shr["State SHARED"]
    inv -->|Write Miss BusRdX| mod["State MODIFIED"]

    shr -->|Read Hit| shr
    shr -->|Write Hit BusUpgr| mod
    shr -->|Snoop Invalidate| inv

    mod -->|Read Hit| mod
    mod -->|Read Miss Flush| shr
    mod -->|Write Hit| mod

    exc["State EXCLUSIVE"] -->|Write| mod
    exc -->|Read Shared| shr
    shr -->|BusRdX receives| inv
```

## 4.4 UMA Bus Transaction — Snooping Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P1 as Processor P1
    participant Cache1 as L1 Cache P1
    participant Bus as Shared Bus
    participant Cache2 as L1 Cache P2
    participant Mem as Main Memory

    P1->>Cache1: WRITE to Address A
    Cache1->>Bus: BusUpgr Invalidate
    Bus->>Cache2: Snoop Invalidate Address A
    Cache2-->>Cache2: Set state INVALID
    Cache1->>Cache1: Set state MODIFIED
    Cache1->>Bus: Data Write Through Optional
    Bus->>Mem: Update Memory Copy
```

## 4.5 UMA vs NUMA — Comparative Block View

```mermaid
graph TD
    uma["UMA Symmetric Multiprocessor"]
    numa["NUMA Distributed Shared Memory"]

    uma --> umaFeat1["Single Bus or Crossbar"]
    uma --> umaFeat2["Equal Latency Everywhere"]
    uma --> umaFeat3["Snooping Coherence"]
    uma --> umaFeat4["Scale Up to 64 CPUs"]

    numa --> numaFeat1["Interconnection Network"]
    numa --> numaFeat2["Local Fast Remote Slow"]
    numa --> numaFeat3["Directory Based Coherence"]
    numa --> numaFeat4["Scale Up to Thousands of CPUs"]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Differentiate between UMA and NUMA architectures with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | UMA | NUMA |
|-----------|-----|------|
| Memory access time | Uniform for all CPUs | Non-uniform (local fast, remote slow) |
| Interconnect | Shared bus / crossbar | Distributed network (mesh, torus) |
| Coherence | Snooping | Directory-based |
| Scalability | Limited (~64) | High (~thousands) |
| Example | Older dual-socket Xeon servers | AMD EPYC, Intel Xeon Scalable, SGI Origin |

> **Board Hint:** Always tabulate for **3-mark difference questions** — examiners reward clarity.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Explain the cache coherence problem with a simple example.**

**Model Answer:**
Consider two processors **P1** and **P2**, both having the variable `X = 5` cached.
1. P1 executes `X = X + 1` → its cache now shows `X = 6`, but P2's cache still shows `X = 5`.
2. P2 reads `X` → it gets the **stale value 5** instead of 6 → incorrect result.

This inconsistency is the **cache coherence problem**. It is solved using **snooping** or **directory-based protocols** that ensure write propagation and write serialization.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Flynn's Taxonomy** of parallel computers. Discuss SIMD and MIMD in detail with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **UMA (Uniform Memory Access) architecture**. Discuss its merits and demerits.

---

### QUESTION A — Model Solution

#### (a) Flynn's Taxonomy [7 Marks]

Flynn (1966) classified computer architectures based on the number of **instruction streams** and **data streams** executed concurrently.

**Four classes:**

1. **SISD — Single Instruction Single Data**
   - Classical von-Neumann uniprocessor.
   - No inherent parallelism; pipelining/ILP only.
   - Example: Personal desktop.

2. **SIMD — Single Instruction Multiple Data**
   - One control unit broadcasts the same instruction to many ALUs.
   - Each ALU operates on a different data element.
   - Best for array/matrix operations.
   - Examples: NVIDIA GPU CUDA cores, Intel SSE/AVX units, Cray-1 vector processor.

3. **MISD — Multiple Instruction Single Data**
   - Multiple instructions act on the same data stream.
   - Used for fault-tolerant systems and systolic arrays.
   - Example: Space Shuttle flight control computers.

4. **MIMD — Multiple Instruction Multiple Data**
   - Each processor independently fetches and executes its own instructions on its own data.
   - Most general and commercially dominant.
   - Subdivided into:
     - **Shared-Memory MIMD** (SMP/UMA, NUMA)
     - **Distributed-Memory MIMD** (Clusters, MPP)
   - Examples: Multicore CPUs, supercomputers like Sunway TaihuLight.

#### (b) UMA Architecture [7 Marks]

**Uniform Memory Access (UMA)** is a shared-memory MIMD architecture where:
- All $N$ processors connect to $M$ memory modules through a **shared bus or crossbar**.
- **Access time is identical** for every (processor, memory) pair.
- Each processor maintains its **own L1/L2 cache**.
- **Cache coherence** is maintained by **snooping** the shared bus.

**Diagram (refer Section 4.2):** P1, P2, P3, P4 → shared bus → M1, M2, M3, M4 with snoop controller.

**Merits:**
- Simple programming model (single address space).
- Good for small-to-medium workloads.
- Easy cache coherence via snooping.

**Demerits:**
- **Bus contention** limits scalability to ~64 CPUs.
- Adding CPUs degrades performance due to coherence traffic.
- Memory bandwidth is the bottleneck.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **MESI cache coherence protocol** with all four states and their transitions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** State and derive **Amdahl's Law**. A parallel application has a serial fraction of 0.1. Compute the maximum achievable speedup.

---

### QUESTION B — Model Solution

#### (a) MESI Cache Coherence Protocol [7 Marks]

MESI is a **write-invalidate snooping protocol** where every cache line is in one of four states:

| State | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| **M — Modified** | Line is dirty, exclusive to this cache. Must write back before others read. |
| **E — Exclusive** | Line is clean, exclusive to this cache. No other cache has a copy. |
| **S — Shared** | Line is clean. One or more caches may have a copy. |
| **I — Invalid** | Line is not present in this cache. |

**Transitions (key cases):**
- `I → S`: on a read miss where other caches also have the line.
- `I → E`: on a read miss where no other cache has the line.
- `E → M`: on a write hit (silent transition — no bus traffic).
- `S → M`: on a write hit, must broadcast **BusUpgr** to invalidate others.
- `M → S`: on a read miss by another processor (cache-to-cache flush).

**Why MESI?** Reduces bus traffic by using the `E` state — a silent exclusive-to-modified transition avoids unnecessary invalidation broadcasts. Used in all modern x86 CPUs.

#### (b) Amdahl's Law [7 Marks]

**Statement:** The speedup of a parallel program is limited by its serial (non-parallelizable) fraction. Even with infinite processors, the maximum speedup equals $\frac{1}{f}$.

**Derivation:**

Let total serial time be $T_s$, normalized to $1$. Let $f$ be the serial fraction and $(1 - f)$ the parallel fraction.

On $N$ processors, the parallel portion is divided equally:

$$
T_p = f \cdot T_s + \dfrac{(1 - f) \cdot T_s}{N}
$$

$$
S(N) = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p} = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1 - f}{N}}
$$

As $N \to \infty$:

$$
S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{f}
$$

**Numerical computation (f = 0.1):**

$$
S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{0.1} = 10
$$

> **Conclusion:** With 10% serial code, **no matter how many processors are added, speedup cannot exceed 10×**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention "Single Address Space"** in shared-memory definitions → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing MESI with MSI** — examiners deduct marks if `Exclusive` state is omitted.
> 3. **Amdahl's Law without normalization** — must set $T_s = 1$ before deriving.
> 4. **Confusing UMA with NUMA** — NUMA = *Non-Uniform*; don't write "NUMA = Uniform".
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in UMA** — 14-mark questions without a diagram lose at least 2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the limit case** $N \to \infty$ in Amdahl's derivation → lose 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 2:**

- **Flynn's Taxonomy** has 4 classes: **SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD**.
- **MIMD** is the parent of both **shared-memory** and **distributed-memory** systems.
- **Shared memory** = single address space; **Distributed memory** = message passing.
- **UMA** (SMP) = uniform access time via shared bus/crossbar; scalability ~64.
- **NUMA** = non-uniform access; scales to thousands; needs directory coherence.
- **Cache Coherence** = ensuring stale copies are invalidated/updated; two pillars: **write propagation** and **write serialization**.
- **Snooping protocols** scale poorly; **directory protocols** scale better.
- **MESI** states: **Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid**.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ — serial fraction is the hard ceiling.
- **Speedup** $S = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p}$ ; **Efficiency** $E = \dfrac{S}{N}$.
- **AMAT** = $H + MR \cdot M$ — coherence traffic increases miss penalty.
- **Gustafson's Law** assumes scaled problem size → $S = N - f(N-1)$.
- Real-world examples to memorize: **Intel/AMD CPUs (MESI), NVIDIA GPUs (SIMD), Clusters (MPI)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ccNUMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** ccNUMA # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **ccNUMA (Cache-Coherent Non-Uniform Memory Access)** is a shared-memory multiprocessor architecture in which the physical memory is **distributed across multiple processor nodes**, yet appears as a **single, unified address space** to all processors. The defining feature of ccNUMA is that: - **Memory access latency depends on the "distance"** (in hops through the interconnect) between the requesting processor and the memory controller serving the target address — hence **Non-Uniform Memory Access (NUMA)**. - A **hardware-supported cache coherence protocol** (typically directory-based) maintains the **single-image view** of memory at the cache level — hence the prefix **cc (Cache Coherent)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** ccNUMA is a multiprocessor memory design where each processor has its own local memory, all memories form a single shared address space, and hardware coherence ensures that any cached copy of a line is consistent across the entire system. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "National Library Chain" Imagine a **National Library System** spread across many cities in India (Chennai, Kochi, Delhi, Mumbai…): | Real-World Element | ccNUMA Equivalent | |---|---| | Multiple city libraries | Distributed memory banks on each node | | Single unified catalog across India | Single global physical address space | | Borrowing a book from your local branch is **fast** | Accessing **local memory** = low latency | | Borrowing from another city takes days via inter-library loan | Accessing **remote memory** = higher latency | | A central ITR (Inter-Library Tracking Registry) updates who has which book | **Directory-based coherence protocol** tracks cache line ownership | | Patrons always see updated availability | Processors always see consistent cached data | The **"distance penalty"** is the real-world equivalent of NUMA's **non-uniform access time**, and the central tracking registry mimics the role of the **coherence directory**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** ccNUMA achieves the **programming simplicity of shared memory** (like SMP/UMA) while delivering the **scalability of distributed memory** (like clusters). It is the dominant architecture in modern multi-socket servers (Intel Xeon, AMD EPYC, IBM Power). --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol / Unit | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | Local memory access latency | $T_{local}$ | **~50–100 ns** | | Remote memory access latency | $T_{remote}$ | **~150–400 ns** | | NUMA ratio | $N_r = T_{remote}/T_{local}$ | **1.3 to 3.0** | | Node interconnect bandwidth | $B_{inter}$ | **20–100 GB/s** (e.g., Intel UPI, AMD Infinity Fabric) | | Coherence directory entry size | bits per cache line | **~4–10 bits** | | Maximum supported processor count | N | **8 to 256+ sockets** | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Breakdown A ccNUMA system has **three logical layers** that students must master: ### Layer A — The Compute Node - Each node contains **2 to 8 processor cores** (often a multi-core CPU). - Each processor has **L1, L2, L3 caches** (with L3 often shared across cores). - Each node has **local DRAM** attached via an integrated memory controller (IMC). - The node is connected to the rest of the system via a **high-speed point-to-point interconnect** (Intel UPI/QPI, AMD Infinity Fabric, HyperTransport, or custom). ### Layer B — The Interconnect Network - A **scalable, low-latency fabric** (often a **fat-tree**, **torus**, or **hypercube**) connects all nodes. - Modern systems use **directory-based protocols** (MESI/MOESI modified variants) because snoop-based protocols do not scale well beyond 4–8 sockets. - The interconnect carries three message types: **data requests**, **data responses**, and **invalidation/snoops**. ### Layer C — The Coherence Directory - A **distributed directory** (one slice per node) tracks the **sharing status** of every memory line owned by that node. - Directory entries typically store: *owner node ID*, *sharer bit-vector*, and *state bits* (Modified, Shared, Invalid, Exclusive — the MESI family). - When a cache miss occurs, the home node consults its directory to decide whether to forward the request to the current owner, broadcast invalidations, or supply the line directly. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cheat Sheet — Master These Equations for KTU ESE** | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Average memory access time (general) | $T_{avg} = \sum_i p_i \cdot T_i$ | seconds (s) | | 2 | NUMA access time (two-tier) | $T_{NUMA} = f \cdot T_{local} + (1-f) \cdot T_{remote}$ | s | | 3 | NUMA penalty ratio | $N_r = \dfrac{T_{remote}}{T_{local}}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Remote access amplification | $T_{remote} = T_{local} + h \cdot t_{hop} + t_{dir}$ | s | | 5 | Total memory bandwidth (NUMA) | $B_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} B_{local,i}$ | bytes/s | | 6 | Effective bandwidth with locality $f$ | $B_{eff} = B_{local} \cdot f + B_{remote} \cdot (1-f)$ | bytes/s | | 7 | Speedup bound (Amdahl, NUMA-aware) | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-\alpha) + \dfrac{\alpha}{N} \cdot \left[ f \cdot r_l + (1-f) \cdot r_r \right]}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Directory storage overhead | $S_{dir} = N \cdot L \cdot b_{dir}$ | bits | | 9 | Coherence message count (read miss) | $M = 2 + k_{sharers}$ | messages | | 10 | Mean hop count (hypercube, $N=2^d$ nodes) | $\bar{H} = \dfrac{d \cdot 2^{d-1}}{2^d - 1}$ | hops | **Legend of variables used:** - $f$ = fraction of memory accesses satisfied locally (locality factor), $0 \le f \le 1$ - $h$ = number of interconnect hops to remote node - $t_{hop}$ = per-hop latency (s) - $t_{dir}$ = directory lookup latency (s) - $\alpha$ = parallelizable fraction of workload - $r_l, r_r$ = relative slowdown factors for local/remote (ratio to ideal) - $N$ = number of processor nodes - $L$ = number of memory lines per node - $b_{dir}$ = directory bits per line (≈ presence-vector length + state bits) - $k_{sharers}$ = number of nodes currently caching the line --- ## 2.3 Why ccNUMA Matters in Real-World Engineering | Field | Application | Why ccNUMA Wins | |---|---|---| | **HPC / Scientific Simulation** | Weather forecasting (WRF), CFD (ANSYS Fluent) | Scales to thousands of cores with single programming model | | **Database Servers** | SAP HANA, Oracle Exadata | Predictable low latency for transaction processing | | **AI / Deep Learning Training** | Multi-GPU training of LLMs (GPT-class) | Shared memory simplifies parameter-server patterns | | **In-Memory Analytics** | Apache Spark on large-memory NUMA boxes | Avoids the network bottlenecks of pure clusters | | **Aerospace & Defense** | Real-time signal processing | Deterministic local-access performance | > [!NOTE] > The **engineering trade-off** is clear: ccNUMA gives **programmability and coherence for free in hardware** but pays a **remote-access penalty** that software (OS scheduler + memory allocator) must minimize by **thread-and-data placement**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Memory Access Time in a ccNUMA System **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A ccNUMA machine has local memory access latency $T_{local} = 80$ ns and remote memory access latency $T_{remote} = 240$ ns. If a parallel program running on a node makes $70\%$ of its memory references to local memory and the rest to remote memory, compute the average memory access time $T_{avg}$. ### Step 1 — Identify the access locality We are given that the fraction of local accesses is $f = 0.70$. Therefore, the fraction of remote accesses is $1 - f = 1 - 0.70 = 0.30$. ### Step 2 — Apply the two-tier NUMA access-time model $$ \begin{aligned} T_{avg} &= f \cdot T_{local} + (1-f) \cdot T_{remote} \end{aligned} $$ > The **two-tier model** assumes every access is either fully local or fully remote — there is no partial cache hit considered here. We are working at the memory-controller level. ### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values $$ \begin{aligned} T_{avg} &= (0.70) \cdot (80 \text{ ns}) + (0.30) \cdot (240 \text{ ns}) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Perform the multiplications $$ \begin{aligned} (0.70)(80) &= 56 \text{ ns} \\ (0.30)(240) &= 72 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Add the two contributions $$ \begin{aligned} T_{avg} &= 56 \text{ ns} + 72 \text{ ns} = 128 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Compare against the uniform case A purely UMA machine with $T_{UMA} = 80$ ns would have given $80$ ns. The **NUMA penalty** is therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{NUMA} &= \frac{T_{avg} - T_{UMA}}{T_{UMA}} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{128 - 80}{80} \times 100\% \\ &= 60\% \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** Average memory access time = **128 ns**, representing a **60% slowdown** versus an ideal UMA system. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — NUMA Penalty Ratio and Required Locality **Problem Statement:** A ccNUMA server has $T_{local} = 100$ ns and $T_{remote} = 350$ ns. What minimum fraction $f$ of local accesses must a program maintain so that the average access time does not exceed $150$ ns? ### Step 1 — Set up the inequality $$ \begin{aligned} f \cdot T_{local} + (1-f) \cdot T_{remote} &\le T_{target} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Plug in known values $$ \begin{aligned} f \cdot 100 + (1-f) \cdot 350 &\le 150 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Expand the left-hand side $$ \begin{aligned} 100f + 350 - 350f &\le 150 \\ -250f + 350 &\le 150 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Isolate $f$ $$ \begin{aligned} -250f &\le 150 - 350 \\ -250f &\le -200 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Divide and flip the inequality sign $$ \begin{aligned} f &\ge \frac{-200}{-250} \\ f &\ge 0.80 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** The program must keep **at least $80\%$ of its accesses local** to maintain the target 150 ns average. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Speedup Bound Under NUMA Latency (Amdahl + NUMA) **Problem Statement:** A parallel program is $90\%$ parallelizable ($\alpha = 0.90$) and runs on $N = 16$ nodes. The remote access is $r_r = 2.5$ times slower than ideal local access ($r_l = 1.0$). Compute the realistic speedup. ### Step 1 — Recall Amdahl's Law for parallel fraction $$ \begin{aligned} S_{ideal} &= \frac{1}{(1-\alpha) + \dfrac{\alpha}{N}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Plug in $\alpha = 0.90$, $N = 16$ $$ \begin{aligned} S_{ideal} &= \frac{1}{(1-0.90) + \dfrac{0.90}{16}} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.05625} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.15625} \\ &\approx 6.40 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Incorporate the NUMA slowdown into the parallel portion The parallel fraction runs at speed $\dfrac{1}{f \cdot r_l + (1-f) \cdot r_r}$. Taking the locality from earlier as $f = 0.80$: $$ \begin{aligned} S_{remote} &= \frac{1}{f \cdot r_l + (1-f) \cdot r_r} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.80 \cdot 1.0 + 0.20 \cdot 2.5} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.80 + 0.50} \\ &= \frac{1}{1.30} \\ &\approx 0.769 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Compose with the serial fraction $$ \begin{aligned} S_{NUMA} &= \frac{1}{(1-\alpha) + \dfrac{\alpha}{N} \cdot \dfrac{1}{S_{remote}}} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute values $$ \begin{aligned} S_{NUMA} &= \frac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{16} \cdot \dfrac{1}{0.769}} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.05625 \cdot 1.30} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.0731} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.1731} \\ &\approx 5.78 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Compare Ideal UMA speedup: $S_{ideal} \approx 6.40$ NUMA-aware speedup: $S_{NUMA} \approx 5.78$ Loss due to non-uniform access: $\approx 9.7\%$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ccNUMA vs Alternatives | Property | UMA (SMP) | ccNUMA | Distributed (Cluster, MPI) | |---|---|---|---| | Memory model | Shared | Shared (distributed) | Distributed (message passing) | | Access time | Uniform | Non-uniform | Non-uniform + explicit | | Coherence | Hardware (bus snooping) | Hardware (directory) | None (software-managed) | | Scalability | 8–32 cores | 64–2048 cores | 100,000+ cores | | Programming model | Threads | Threads (NUMA-aware) | MPI / SHMEM | | OS page migration | Not applicable | Yes (auto-NUMA balancing) | Not applicable | | Example hardware | Older dual-socket Xeon | SGI Origin, AMD EPYC, Intel Xeon Scalable | Top500 supercomputers | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a ccNUMA System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NodeA["NODE 0"] CPU0A["CPU Core 0"] CPU1A["CPU Core 1"] L2A["L2 / L3 Cache"] MCA["Memory Controller"] RAMA["Local DRAM Bank A"] DIRA["Directory Slice A"] end subgraph NodeB["NODE 1"] CPU0B["CPU Core 0"] CPU1B["CPU Core 1"] L2B["L2 / L3 Cache"] MCB["Memory Controller"] RAMB["Local DRAM Bank B"] DIRB["Directory Slice B"] end subgraph NodeC["NODE 2"] CPU0C["CPU Core 0"] CPU1C["CPU Core 1"] L2C["L2 / L3 Cache"] MCC["Memory Controller"] RAMC["Local DRAM Bank C"] DIRC["Directory Slice C"] end IC["High-Speed Interconnect Fabric (UPI / Infinity Fabric / HyperTransport)"] NodeA <--> IC NodeB <--> IC NodeC <--> IC CPU0A --> L2A --> MCA CPU1A --> L2A MCA --- RAMA MCA --- DIRA CPU0B --> L2B --> MCB CPU1B --> L2B MCB --- RAMB MCB --- DIRB CPU0C --> L2C --> MCC CPU1C --> L2C MCC --- RAMC MCC --- DIRC ``` ## 4.2 Directory-Based Coherence Flow (Read Miss on Remote Line) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P1 as CPU on Node 0 participant L1 as Node 0 Cache participant IC as Interconnect participant DIR as Directory on Home Node 1 participant RAM as Memory Controller Node 1 participant P2 as CPU on Node 1 P1->>L1: Load Address X L1-->>P1: Cache Miss (line not present) L1->>IC: Read Request for X IC->>DIR: Forward to Home Node (X lives on Node 1) DIR->>DIR: Check directory state for X DIR->>P2: Forward request (if cached Modified) P2-->>IC: Data + Ack (cache-to-cache transfer) IC-->>L1: Supply line X L1-->>P1: Return data to CPU DIR->>DIR: Update sharer bit-vector (add Node 0) ``` ##
