4.3 Write / Invalidation Flow (Write Hit on Shared Line) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P1 as CPU on Node 0 participant L1 as Node 0 Cache participant IC as Interconnect participant DIR as Directory on Home Node participant P2 as CPU on Node 1 participant P3 as CPU on Node 2 P1->>L1: Store to Address Y (in Shared state) L1->>IC: Invalidate Request (Exclusive upgrade) IC->>DIR: Forward to Home Node DIR->>P2: Send Invalidate DIR->>P3: Send Invalidate P2-->>IC: Invalidate Ack P3-->>IC: Invalidate Ack IC-->>L1: All acks received L1->>P1: Store completes, line now Modified DIR->>DIR: Update state to Exclusive, owner = Node 0 ``` ## 4.4 Memory Access Latency Hierarchy in ccNUMA ```mermaid graph LR L1["L1 Cache Hit
~1 ns"] L2["L2 Cache Hit
~3-5 ns"] L3["L3 Cache Hit
~10-20 ns"] LOCAL["Local DRAM Access
~50-100 ns"] REMOTE["Remote DRAM Access
~150-400 ns
via interconnect + directory"] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> LOCAL --> REMOTE ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Define ccNUMA. Why is it called "non-uniform"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** ccNUMA stands for **Cache-Coherent Non-Uniform Memory Access**. It is a multiprocessor architecture in which physical memory is distributed across multiple nodes but exposed as a single shared address space, with hardware-maintained cache coherence. It is called **"non-uniform"** because the memory access latency depends on the physical location of the memory relative to the requesting processor — **local memory** (attached to the same node) is accessed faster than **remote memory** (attached to a different node and reached through the interconnect). **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for non-uniform justification, 1 mark for cache coherence aspect.)** --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: What is the role of a directory in a ccNUMA coherence protocol? Mention any one directory-based protocol.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **directory** is a distributed data structure (one slice per home node) that keeps track of which nodes have currently cached copies of each memory line and in what state (Modified / Shared / Invalid / Exclusive). When a request arrives, the home-node directory decides whether to: - Supply the data directly from memory, OR - Forward the request to the current owner node, AND - Send invalidations to other sharers. Any one standard protocol: **MESI** (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) or **MOESI** (adds Owned state for cache-to-cache transfer without writing back to memory). **(1 mark for directory purpose, 1 mark for protocol name, 1 mark for brief description.)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architecture of a ccNUMA system** with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between **local** and **remote** memory access with respect to latency and the path traversed. **(b) [7 Marks]** A ccNUMA system has local memory latency of **100 ns** and remote memory latency of **300 ns**. A parallel application shows **75% local references** and **25% remote references**. (i) Compute the average memory access time. (ii) Compute the NUMA penalty compared to a uniform-memory machine of 100 ns. (iii) What fraction of accesses must be local to bring the average below 130 ns? --- #### Model Solution for B-A (a): **Architecture (4 marks):** - Each node contains processor cores, private/shared caches, a **memory controller**, **local DRAM**, and a **directory slice**. - All nodes are interconnected through a **high-bandwidth, low-latency fabric** (Intel UPI / AMD Infinity Fabric / HyperTransport). - The **directory** is distributed — each home node owns the metadata for its own memory lines. - The system presents a **single global physical address space** to all processors. **Local vs Remote Access (3 marks):** | Aspect | Local Access | Remote Access | |---|---|---| | Path | CPU → Cache → Local MC → Local DRAM | CPU → Cache → Local MC → Interconnect → Remote MC → Remote DRAM (and may consult remote directory) | | Latency | ~50–100 ns | ~150–400 ns | | Coherence check | Local cache only | Directory lookup + possible invalidations | | Bandwidth | Dedicated to local DRAM | Shared across the interconnect | --- #### Model Solution for B-A (b): **(i) Average memory access time:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{avg} &= f \cdot T_{local} + (1-f) \cdot T_{remote} \\ &= (0.75)(100) + (0.25)(300) \\ &= 75 + 75 \\ &= 150 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) NUMA penalty:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{NUMA} &= \frac{T_{avg} - T_{local}}{T_{local}} \times 100\% \\ &= \frac{150 - 100}{100} \times 100\% \\ &= 50\% \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Required locality for $T_{avg} \le 130$ ns:** $$ \begin{aligned} f \cdot 100 + (1-f) \cdot 300 &\le 130 \\ 100f + 300 - 300f &\le 130 \\ -200f &\le -170 \\ f &\ge 0.85 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the program must maintain at least **85% local references**. --- ### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 – Alternative]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **cache coherence problem in distributed shared memory** systems. Explain how a **directory-based protocol** solves it. Compare it briefly with the **snoopy bus protocol**. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-node ccNUMA system has the following per-hop interconnect characteristics: hop latency = **40 ns**, directory lookup latency = **30 ns**, local DRAM access = **60 ns**. - (i) Compute the latency for a remote access that traverses **2 hops**, assuming a clean line read from a remote node's memory. - (ii) If the directory already has the data cached on a remote node (1 hop away), compute the cache-to-cache transfer latency. - (iii) Calculate the speedup ratio between local and remote (clean memory) accesses. --- #### Model Solution for B-B (a): **Cache Coherence Problem (3 marks):** When multiple processors cache the same memory line, **writes by one processor** can leave other caches holding **stale values**, violating memory consistency. Without coherence, programs can produce incorrect results (e.g., two processors updating the same shared counter and reading stale values). **Directory-Based Solution (2 marks):** - Each memory line has a **directory entry** at its home node storing the *owner* and *sharer set*. - On a write, the home node consults the directory and sends **invalidation messages** to all current sharers. - The writer is guaranteed exclusive ownership before completing the store. - Protocols: **MESI**, **MOESI**, **Directory-based Illinois**. **Comparison with Snoopy Bus (2 marks):** | Feature | Snoopy Bus | Directory-Based | |---|---|---| | Coherence traffic | Broadcast to all nodes | Point-to-point via directory | | Scalability | Limited (~4–8 processors) | Scales to thousands | | Bandwidth requirement | High (shared bus) | Moderate (distributed) | | Complexity | Simple | Complex (directory storage) | | Used in | UMA / small SMP | ccNUMA, large systems | --- #### Model Solution for B-B (b): **(i) Remote access — 2 hops, clean line read from remote memory:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{remote} &= T_{local} + h \cdot t_{hop} + t_{dir} \\ &= 60 + (2)(40) + 30 \\ &= 60 + 80 + 30 \\ &= 170 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Cache-to-cache transfer — 1 hop, remote node holds modified copy:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{c2c} &= T_{local} + h \cdot t_{hop} + t_{dir} + \text{data transfer time} \\ &\approx 60 + (1)(40) + 30 + 40 \\ &= 170 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ (Simplified: $T_{local} + t_{hop} \cdot h + t_{dir}$, assuming symmetric hops.) **(iii) Speedup ratio (local vs remote clean memory read, 2 hops):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Ratio} &= \frac{T_{remote}}{T_{local}} \\ &= \frac{170}{60} \\ &\approx 2.83 \end{aligned} $$ So remote access is **~2.83× slower** than local access. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing NUMA with cluster / MPI systems.** NUMA is **hardware-shared memory**; cluster is **software message-passing**. Mixing these up costs the architecture definition marks. > 2. **Forgetting the `cc` prefix.** Writing just "NUMA" loses you the coherence marks. Always emphasize **hardware cache coherence**. > 3. **Using the wrong formula.** Many students use $T_{avg} = T_{local} + T_{remote}$ (arithmetic mean). The correct NUMA formula is the **weighted average** $f \cdot T_{local} + (1-f) \cdot T_{remote}$. > 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** Always state `ns`, `MB/s`, or `%`. > 5. **Not drawing the block diagram** in architecture questions. Even a rough sketch showing nodes + interconnect + memory + directory earns easy marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - [x] **ccNUMA = Cache-Coherent + Non-Uniform Memory Access** — single address space, distributed physical memory, hardware coherence. - [x] **Memory latency depends on node distance** — local ≤ remote. - [x] **Architecture = {Compute Node + Interconnect + Distributed Directory}**, repeated across N nodes. - [x] **Coherence protocol = directory-based** (MESI/MOESI family) — scalable beyond bus limits. - [x] **Directory tracks owner + sharer bit-vector** for each memory line. - [x] **Key formula**: $T_{avg} = f \cdot T_{local} + (1-f) \cdot T_{remote}$. - [x] **NUMA penalty** = $\dfrac{T_{avg} - T_{local}}{T_{local}} \times 100\%$. - [x] **NUMA ratio** $N_r = T_{remote}/T_{local}$ typically between **1.3 and 3.0** for production systems. - [x] **Locality-aware scheduling + first-touch memory allocation** are critical software optimizations. - [x] **Examples**: SGI Origin 2000/3000, AMD EPYC, Intel Xeon Scalable, IBM Power, HPE Superdome. - [x] **ccNUMA bridges UMA's programmability and cluster's scalability** — the dominant HPC server architecture. - [x] **For ESE**: always draw a labelled block diagram with at least 3 nodes, interconnect, memory, and directory. - [x] **For derivations**: substitute numerically *step-by-step*; show every arithmetic transition. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module 2 questions on ccNUMA frequently appear as either a **14-mark full-length question** with sub-parts requiring numerical computation, or a **3-mark direct definition**. Practice writing the **weighted average formula from memory** and a **clean 3-node block diagram** — these two alone secure ~70% of the marks on this topic.

topics.title: Distributed-Memory Computers - Hierarchical (Hybrid) Systems - Networks - Basic Performance Characteristics of Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Distributed-Memory Computers - Hierarchical (Hybrid) Systems - Networks - Basic Performance Characteristics of Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Distributed-Memory Computers

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A *Distributed-Memory Computer* is a parallel architecture in which each processor possesses its own **private local memory**, and processors cooperate by exchanging **messages** over a high-speed **interconnection network**. No single processor can directly address the memory of another.

**Key Properties:**
- Memory is **physically distributed**, though logically it may appear as a single global address space (in DSM systems).
- Communication is **explicit** — the programmer (or compiler) must invoke primitives such as `send` and `recv`.
- **Memory Reference Time (MRT)** is uniform and predictable (no NUMA penalties inside a node).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Highlight:** The defining test for a *distributed-memory* system is: *Does any communication between two processors require software intervention (message-passing)?* If yes → distributed-memory.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a group of **independent office workers in different cities**, each with their own personal filing cabinet. They cannot open each other's cabinets directly. To share a document, they must **mail a sealed envelope** to each other. The *mail service* is the interconnection network, the *envelope* is the message, and the *travel time* is the latency.

---

### 1.2 Hierarchical (Hybrid) Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *Hierarchical* or *Hybrid* parallel computer combines two or more levels of parallelism — typically **shared-memory multiprocessors (SMPs/NUMA nodes)** interconnected by a **distributed-memory network** to form a larger machine.

**Architectural Tiers:**
| Tier | Organization | Examples |
|------|--------------|----------|
| Tier-1 | Shared memory within a node | Multi-core CPU sockets, SMPs |
| Tier-2 | Distributed memory across nodes | Cluster of SMPs, MPPs |
| Tier-3 | (Optional) Distributed cluster of clusters | Grid, Cloud |

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a **large multinational company**. Each *branch office* (node) has an internal shared filing system where all employees can read each other's files instantly. But branches in different cities cannot access each other's files directly — they must use the **corporate courier service (interconnection network)** to exchange documents. This is *hierarchical* — fast inside, slower across.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hierarchical?** Pure shared-memory machines don't scale beyond ~hundreds of cores (cache-coherence bottleneck). Pure distributed-memory is hard to program. Hierarchical systems give the *best of both worlds* — programmability inside a node + scalability across nodes.

---

### 1.3 Interconnection Networks

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> An *Interconnection Network* is the set of **switches, links, and routing logic** that connects processors and memories in a parallel computer, enabling data movement between them.

**Two Principal Classes:**

1. **Direct (Static) Networks** — Each switch is attached to a processor. The topology is fixed. *Mesh, Torus, Hypercube, Ring, Tree*.
2. **Indirect (Dynamic) Networks** — Switches are standalone; processors connect via these switches. *Crossbar, Omega, Butterfly, Baseline, Benes*.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
- A **direct network** is like the seating arrangement in a classroom — each student (processor) sits at a fixed desk (switch) connected to specific neighbours.
- An **indirect network** is like a telephone exchange — calls are routed through intermediate operator boards (switches) before reaching the destination.

---

### 1.4 Basic Performance Characteristics of Networks

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield List):**
>
> 1. **Latency (L)** — Time to send a zero-byte (control) message. Measured in **microseconds (μs)**.
> 2. **Bandwidth (B)** — Maximum data rate per link. Measured in **MB/s** or **Gb/s**.
> 3. **Throughput (T)** — Aggregate useful data rate across the network.
> 4. **Diameter (D)** — Maximum shortest-path length (in hops) between any two nodes.
> 5. **Node Degree (d)** — Number of physical links incident on each node.
> 6. **Bisection Width (B_w)** — Minimum number of links that must be cut to split the network into two equal halves.
> 7. **Bisection Bandwidth (B_b)** — Bisection Width × Bandwidth per link.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Memory Trick:** *Low diameter + High bisection bandwidth = Good network.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Distributed-Memory Programming Model — Operational Walkthrough

The execution of a distributed-memory program follows this structured sequence:

1. **Process Spawning** — Each processor loads its own copy of the program; variables are *local* by default.
2. **Local Computation** — Each processor operates on its private data with full memory bandwidth (no contention).
3. **Communication Phase** — When data is needed by another processor:
   - Process A packs the data into a *message buffer*.
   - The message is injected into the network.
   - The network **routes** the message hop-by-hop toward Process B.
   - Process B receives the message into its local buffer.
4. **Synchronization** — Often a barrier or acknowledgment ensures correctness.
5. **Local Computation Resumes** — With fresh remote data now locally available.

**The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
- Locality is preserved → cache behaviour is excellent.
- Message passing introduces **latency** that is independent of data size — this is why sending many small messages is worse than one big one.
- The network can become a **bottleneck** under heavy traffic.

---

### 2.2 Hierarchical System — Operational Walkthrough

1. **Within-Node Shared Memory Access** — Threads in the same node read/write a common DRAM via a coherence protocol (MESI/MOESI).
2. **Cache Coherence Stop** — Beyond a node boundary, hardware coherence usually **stops**; software must explicitly use MPI, SHMEM, PGAS, or RDMA.
3. **Inter-Node Messaging** — Data crosses the node boundary via the **network interface card (NIC)** into the global fabric (InfiniBand, Ethernet, Omni-Path).
4. **Hybrid Parallelism Layers**:
   - **Threading layer** (OpenMP/pthreads) inside the node.
   - **Process layer** (MPI) across nodes.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** Modern HPC systems like **Sunway TaihuLight, Fugaku, Frontier** are all hierarchical — each node has many cores sharing memory; thousands of nodes are linked via a high-speed network.

---

### 2.3 Network Performance Characteristics — Why They Matter

- **Latency** dominates for short messages — important in fine-grained parallel algorithms (e.g., graph traversal).
- **Bandwidth** dominates for bulk data transfer — important in scientific simulation (e.g., climate modelling).
- **Diameter** dictates the worst-case communication time and determines scalability.
- **Bisection Bandwidth** dictates how the system performs under all-to-all communication (e.g., FFT, N-body).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Message Passing Time (Linear Model) | $t(n) = t_0 + \dfrac{n}{r_\infty}$ | seconds | $t_0$ = latency, $n$ = bytes, $r_\infty$ = asymptotic bandwidth |
| 2 | Latency-bound regime | $t(n) \approx t_0$ when $n \ll t_0 \cdot r_\infty$ | seconds | Short messages |
| 3 | Bandwidth-bound regime | $t(n) \approx \dfrac{n}{r_\infty}$ | seconds | Long messages |
| 4 | Network Diameter of p-node Hypercube | $D_{hc} = \log_2 p$ | hops | Best among classic topologies |
| 5 | Diameter of k-ary d-cube (Torus) | $D_{tor} = d \cdot \lfloor k/2 \rfloor$ | hops | k nodes per dimension, d dimensions |
| 6 | Bisection Width of Hypercube | $B_w = p/2$ | links | Optimal scaling |
| 7 | Bisection Width of 2-D Mesh | $B_w = \sqrt{p}$ | links | Scales poorly |
| 8 | Bisection Bandwidth | $B_b = B_w \times B_{link}$ | MB/s | True measure of aggregate capacity |
| 9 | Cost (links) of p-node Hypercube | $C = \dfrac{p \log_2 p}{2}$ | links | Each of p nodes has degree $\log_2 p$ |
| 10 | Effective Network Throughput | $T = \dfrac{n_{\text{msg}} \cdot p_{\text{active}}}{t_{\text{total}}}$ | bytes/s | Aggregate deliverable data rate |
| 11 | Amdahl's Bound (Max Speedup) | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{f_s + (1-f_s)/p}$ | dimensionless | $f_s$ = serial fraction |
| 12 | Network Power Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Bandwidth}}{\text{Power}}$ | MB/s/W | Important in exascale design |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever you see a numerical problem, *immediately* identify whether the network is *latency-bound* or *bandwidth-bound* — this decides which term of $t(n)$ dominates.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application | Why This Topic Matters |
|-------|-------------|------------------------|
| Weather Forecasting | Atmospheric simulation on clusters | Hierarchical nodes + low-latency fabric |
| AI/Deep Learning | Distributed training (AllReduce) | Bisection bandwidth critical |
| CFD (Aerospace) | Airfoil simulation | Mesh topology matches problem grid |
| Genomics | Sequence alignment | Bandwidth-bound workload |
| Cryptography | Distributed key search | Latency-bound workload |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linear Message Passing Time Model

We model the time $t(n)$ to send a message of $n$ bytes across the network as the sum of three contributions:

**Step 1 — Decompose total time into three components:**

$$
t(n) = t_{\text{startup}} + t_{\text{per-hop}} \cdot h + t_{\text{per-byte}} \cdot n
$$

where:
- $t_{\text{startup}}$ = software overhead of issuing the message (in seconds)
- $t_{\text{per-hop}}$ = router forwarding latency (seconds per hop)
- $h$ = number of hops on the chosen route
- $t_{\text{per-byte}}$ = time to transmit one byte onto the wire ($= 1 / r_{\text{link}}$)
- $n$ = message length in bytes

**Step 2 — Combine start-up and per-hop into a single effective latency:**

$$
t_0 \;=\; t_{\text{startup}} \;+\; t_{\text{per-hop}} \cdot \bar{h}
$$

where $\bar{h}$ is the average number of hops. Thus:

$$
t(n) \;=\; t_0 \;+\; \dfrac{n}{r_\infty}
$$

where $r_\infty = 1 / t_{\text{per-byte}}$ is the **asymptotic bandwidth** (the slope of $t$ vs. $n$ for large $n$).

**Step 3 — Interpret the two regimes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Short message } (n \to 0): \quad t(n) \;\approx\; t_0 \quad \text{(latency-bound)}\\[4pt]
&\textbf{Long message } (n \to \infty): \quad t(n) \;\approx\; \dfrac{n}{r_\infty} \quad \text{(bandwidth-bound)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The crossover point occurs when both terms are equal:

$$
t_0 \;=\; \dfrac{n^*}{r_\infty} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad n^* \;=\; t_0 \cdot r_\infty
$$

This $n^*$ is the **critical message size** below which latency dominates.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bisection Bandwidth of a $k$-ary $d$-cube Torus

A $k$-ary $d$-cube contains $p = k^d$ nodes arranged in $d$ dimensions, with $k$ nodes per dimension.

**Step 1 — Visualise the cut:** A bisection cut slices the torus perpendicular to one dimension, splitting $k^d$ nodes into two halves of $k^d / 2$ nodes each.

**Step 2 — Count links crossing the cut:** In one dimension, $k^d / 2$ pairs of nodes are matched, giving $k^d / 2$ parallel links crossing the cut.

**Step 3 — Generalise:** Since $d$ dimensions are equivalent, the bisection width is:

$$
B_w(\text{torus}) \;=\; \dfrac{k^d}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{p}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by per-link bandwidth** $B_{\text{link}}$:

$$
B_b \;=\; \dfrac{p}{2} \cdot B_{\text{link}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with a 2-D mesh:** A mesh lacks wrap-around links, so the bisection width becomes:

$$
B_w(\text{mesh}) \;=\; \sqrt{p} \quad \text{(for } d=2\text{, } p = k^2\text{)}
$$

This shows that **a torus has asymptotically higher bisection bandwidth** than a mesh of the same size.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Topologies

Let $p = 2^d$ for a hypercube (d-dim) and $p = k^d$ for a $k$-ary $d$-cube torus.

| Topology | Degree $d_n$ | Diameter $D$ | Bisection Width $B_w$ | Total Links $L$ |
|----------|--------------|--------------|------------------------|------------------|
| Linear Array | 2 (ends: 1) | $p - 1$ | 1 | $p - 1$ |
| Ring | 2 | $\lfloor p/2 \rfloor$ | 2 | $p$ |
| 2-D Mesh | 4 (corners: 2) | $2(\sqrt{p} - 1)$ | $\sqrt{p}$ | $2(p - \sqrt{p})$ |
| 2-D Torus | 4 | $\lfloor \sqrt{p}/2 \rfloor$ | $p/2$ | $2p$ |
| Hypercube | $\log_2 p$ | $\log_2 p$ | $p/2$ | $\dfrac{p \log_2 p}{2}$ |
| $k$-ary $d$-cube Torus | $2d$ | $d \cdot \lfloor k/2 \rfloor$ | $p/2$ | $d \cdot p$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Hypercubes minimise diameter for fixed $p$, but cost grows as $p \log_2 p$. Tori minimise cost (linear in $p$) at the price of higher diameter. **This is why modern HPC uses Tori** (e.g., Blue Gene uses 3-D torus).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Communication Cost in Hierarchical Hybrid Systems

Consider a hybrid machine with $N$ nodes, each node having $c$ cores. Total processes $= N \cdot c$.

**Step 1 — Total communication time:**

$$
t_{\text{comm}} = t_{\text{intra-node}} + t_{\text{inter-node}}
$$

**Step 2 — Intra-node (shared memory)** is essentially a cache-line load:

$$
t_{\text{intra-node}} \approx t_{\text{cache}} \cdot n_{\text{shared}} \quad (\sim 50 \text{ ns per cache line})
$$

**Step 3 — Inter-node (distributed memory)** uses the network model from §3.1:

$$
t_{\text{inter-node}} = t_0 + \dfrac{n_{\text{distributed}}}{r_\infty}
$$

**Step 4 — Total wall-clock communication:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{comm}} &= \alpha_{\text{shared}} \cdot n_{\text{shared}} + \alpha_{\text{dist}} \cdot n_{\text{dist}} + t_{0,\text{dist}} \\[4pt]
\text{where } \alpha_{\text{shared}} &\ll \alpha_{\text{dist}} \text{ and } t_{0,\text{dist}} \gg 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Design heuristic:** Maximise *intra-node* data sharing and minimise *inter-node* traffic — this is the **fundamental optimization rule of hybrid HPC programming**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Distributed-Memory Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Processor P1] --- M1[Local Memory M1]
    P2[Processor P2] --- M2[Local Memory M2]
    P3[Processor P3] --- M3[Local Memory M3]
    P4[Processor P4] --- M4[Local Memory M4]

    P1 -. "Network Interface" .-> NET[Interconnection Network]
    P2 -. "Network Interface" .-> NET
    P3 -. "Network Interface" .-> NET
    P4 -. "Network Interface" .-> NET

    NET --> P2
    NET --> P3
    NET --> P4

    MSG1[Message Send Buffer] --- P1
    MSG2[Message Recv Buffer] --- P2
```

**Interpretation:** Each processor holds private memory; communication occurs only through the network using message-passing primitives. No shared address space exists.

---

### 4.2 Hierarchical (Hybrid) System — Multi-Tier Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NODE_A[NODE A - SMP]
        A1[Core A1] --- ASHMEM[Shared Cache and DRAM]
        A2[Core A2] --- ASHMEM
        A3[Core A3] --- ASHMEM
        A4[Core A4] --- ASHMEM
    end

    subgraph NODE_B[NODE B - SMP]
        B1[Core B1] --- BSHMEM[Shared Cache and DRAM]
        B2[Core B2] --- BSHMEM
        B3[Core B3] --- BSHMEM
        B4[Core B4] --- BSHMEM
    end

    NODE_A -- "Network - Message Passing" --> NIC1[Network Interface NIC]
    NODE_B -- "Network - Message Passing" --> NIC2[Network Interface NIC]

    NIC1 --> FABRIC[High Speed Interconnect Fabric InfiniBand or Ethernet]
    NIC2 --> FABRIC
```

**Interpretation:** Inside a node → shared-memory threads. Across nodes → distributed-memory messages. The fabric (e.g., InfiniBand) handles inter-node traffic.

---

### 4.3 Topology Comparison — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RING[Ring Topology]
        R1[Node 1] --- R2[Node 2]
        R2 --- R3[Node 3]
        R3 --- R4[Node 4]
        R4 --- R5[Node 5]
        R5 --- R1
    end

    subgraph MESH[2-D Mesh]
        M1[Node 1] --- M2[Node 2]
        M2 --- M3[Node 3]
        M1 --- M4[Node 4]
        M2 --- M5[Node 5]
        M3 --- M6[Node 6]
        M4 --- M5
        M5 --- M6
    end

    subgraph HYPERCUBE[Hypercube p=8]
        H1[000] --- H2[001]
        H1 --- H3[010]
        H1 --- H4[100]
        H2 --- H5[011]
        H2 --- H6[101]
        H3 --- H5
        H3 --- H7[110]
        H4 --- H6
        H4 --- H7
        H5 --- H8[111]
        H6 --- H8
        H7 --- H8
    end
```

**Interpretation:** Visual comparison of three classic topologies — *Ring* (degree 2, high diameter), *Mesh* (degree ≤ 4), *Hypercube* (degree = $\log_2 p$, diameter = $\log_2 p$). Hyercube has the lowest diameter but at a wiring cost.

---

### 4.4 Message Passing Time — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sender Process] --> B[Pack Message into Buffer]
    B --> C[Inject into Network Interface]
    C --> D[Route Through Hops h1, h2 ... hn]
    D --> E[Receiver Network Interface]
    E --> F[Unpack into Local Buffer]
    F --> G[Receiver Process]

    T1[Latency t0] -.-> C
    T2[Per Hop Delay] -.-> D
    T3[Bandwidth n over r] -.-> D
```

**Interpretation:** The journey of a single message, with each stage contributing to the total transmission time $t(n) = t_0 + n/r_\infty$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**
Differentiate between **distributed-memory** and **shared-memory** parallel architectures. State one example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Shared Memory | Distributed Memory |
|---------|---------------|--------------------|
| Memory Location | Centralized, all processors access it | Local to each processor |
| Communication | Implicit (load/store) | Explicit (send/recv) |
| Programming | OpenMP, pthreads | MPI, PVM |
| Scalability | Limited (~100 cores) | High (10⁶ cores) |
| Example | Multicore CPU, SMP | Cluster, MPP |

*(Award 1.5 marks for table, 1 mark for example, 0.5 mark for any additional correct distinction.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, KTU Model]`**
Define **bisection bandwidth** of an interconnection network. Why is it considered a more reliable performance indicator than simple bandwidth?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Bisection bandwidth is the *minimum aggregate bandwidth across any cut* that divides the network into two equal halves. Formally, $B_b = B_w \times B_{\text{link}}$, where $B_w$ is the bisection width (minimum number of links crossing any balanced partition).
- **Why it's better (1.5 marks):** Simple bandwidth only measures the rate of *one* link, but a network is bottlenecked at its *narrowest point*. Bisection bandwidth captures the *worst-case* communication capacity, which determines all-to-all and permutation performance.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Distributed-Memory + Network Performance**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Sample]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a **distributed-memory computer** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of message-passing programming. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cluster has a network with latency $t_0 = 5 \,\mu\text{s}$ and asymptotic bandwidth $r_\infty = 100 \,\text{MB/s}$. Calculate the message passing time for:
  (i) $n = 1\,\text{KB}$
  (ii) $n = 1\,\text{MB}$
  Also determine the critical message size $n^*$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Distributed-Memory Architecture (7 marks):**

**Architecture description (3 marks):**
- Each processor $P_i$ has its own local memory $M_i$ and local cache.
- Processors are connected via a dedicated **interconnection network** (e.g., Torus, Fat-Tree).
- Communication occurs through **message passing**: `send` to inject a message, `recv` to receive it.
- A **network interface controller (NIC)** handles protocol translation.
- *Block diagram:* Draw a flowchart with N processors each linked to local memory, and all connected to a central network. (Refer to §4.1.)

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **High scalability** — Adding more nodes increases memory and compute proportionally.
2. **No cache-coherence overhead** — Each node manages its own cache.
3. **Fault isolation** — Failure of one node doesn't corrupt others' memory.
4. **Cost-effective** — Built from commodity components.

**Disadvantages (2 marks):**
1. **Programming complexity** — Programmer must manage data distribution explicitly.
2. **Communication overhead** — Message-passing latency is high compared to load/store.
3. **Irregular data access** is hard to express.
4. **Load balancing** must be done manually.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $t_0 = 5 \,\mu\text{s} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{s}$, $r_\infty = 100 \,\text{MB/s} = 10^{8} \,\text{B/s}$.

**Formula:**
$$
t(n) = t_0 + \frac{n}{r_\infty}
$$

**Case (i): $n = 1\,\text{KB} = 1024 \,\text{B}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t(1024) &= 5 \times 10^{-6} + \frac{1024}{10^{8}} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-6} + 1.024 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 5.00 \times 10^{-6} + 10.24 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 15.24 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{s} \\
&= \mathbf{15.24 \,\mu\text{s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case (ii): $n = 1\,\text{MB} = 10^{6} \,\text{B}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t(10^6) &= 5 \times 10^{-6} + \frac{10^6}{10^8} \\
&= 5 \times 10^{-6} + 10^{-2} \\
&= 0.000005 + 0.01 \\
&= \mathbf{0.010005 \,\text{s}} \;\;\approx 10 \,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical message size:**

$$
n^* = t_0 \cdot r_\infty = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{8} = 500 \,\text{bytes}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**
- For $n = 1\,\text{KB} > 500\,\text{B}$: bandwidth dominates slightly.
- For $n = 1\,\text{MB} \gg 500\,\text{B}$: completely bandwidth-bound.
- Short messages (< 500 B) suffer mostly from latency overhead.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Hierarchical Systems + Topology Metrics**

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **hierarchical (hybrid) parallel architecture**. Discuss why modern HPC systems prefer hybrid designs over pure shared-memory or pure distributed-memory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the following topologies for $p = 16$ nodes: **(i) Ring, (ii) 2-D Mesh, (iii) Hypercube**. Compute degree, diameter, bisection width, and total links for each. Identify which is best for all-to-all communication. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hierarchical Architecture (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A hierarchical system combines multiple levels of parallelism — typically shared-memory inside a node (SMP/NUMA) and distributed-memory across nodes. The communication inside a node uses load/store with cache coherence, while across nodes it uses message passing.

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to §4.2 — two SMP nodes connected via a high-speed fabric. Each node contains cores sharing memory; nodes communicate via network interfaces (e.g., InfiniBand HCA).

**Why hybrid is preferred (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pure Shared | Pure Distributed | Hybrid |
|--------|-------------|------------------|--------|
| Programmability | Easy | Hard | Easy inside, moderate across |
| Scalability | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cost | High (crossbar) | Low | Moderate |
| Communication Speed | Very fast (ns) | Slow (μs) | Fast inside + moderate across |
| Real-world use | Single server | Theoretical extreme | All modern HPC |

**Justification (1 mark):** Real HPC workloads (climate, CFD, AI training) have both *fine-grained* parallelism (handled well by threads in a node) and *coarse-grained* parallelism (handled well by MPI across nodes). Hybrid captures both.

---

**(b) Topology Comparison for $p = 16$ (7 marks):**

**Recall general formulas:**

- **Ring:** Degree = 2, Diameter = $\lfloor p/2 \rfloor$, $B_w = 2$, Links = $p$
- **2-D Mesh:** $p = k^2 \Rightarrow k = 4$. Degree = 4 (corners = 2), Diameter = $2(k-1)$, $B_w = k$, Links = $2(p - k)$
- **Hypercube:** $d = \log_2 p = 4$. Degree = 4, Diameter = 4, $B_w = p/2$, Links = $\dfrac{p \log_2 p}{2}$

**Calculations for $p = 16$:**

| Topology | Degree | Diameter | Bisection Width | Total Links |
|----------|--------|----------|------------------|--------------|
| Ring | 2 | $\lfloor 16/2 \rfloor = 8$ | 2 | 16 |
| 2-D Mesh | $\le 4$ | $2(4-1) = 6$ | 4 | $2(16 - 4) = 24$ |
| Hypercube | 4 | 4 | 8 | $\dfrac{16 \times 4}{2} = 32$ |

**Best for all-to-all communication (1 mark):**
The **Hypercube** has the *highest bisection width* (8), which directly determines the rate at which data can cross any partition during permutation-style traffic. Therefore, **Hypercube** is best.

**Trade-off note (1 mark):** Hypercube uses the most links (32) — wiring cost grows as $p \log_2 p$. For very large $p$, modern systems use **Tori** (which achieve $B_w = p/2$ at only $dp$ links).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mistake #1:** Confusing *diameter* (max hops) with *average distance* (mean hops). Always state "diameter = maximum shortest path."
> 2. **Mistake #2:** In numerical problems, students forget to **convert units** ($KB \to B$, $\mu s \to s$). The KTU checker deducts 1 mark for unit mismatch.
> 3. **Mistake #3:** Calling bisection width and bisection bandwidth the same thing — *width* is in links, *bandwidth* is in MB/s.
> 4. **Mistake #4:** Writing $t(n) = t_0 + n/r$ without defining the variables — full marks require a clear list of symbols.
> 5. **Mistake #5:** In hierarchical system diagrams, students often forget to show the **network interface card (NIC)** — examiners deduct marks for missing hardware layers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Distributed Memory** = private memory + message passing via network; *no shared address space across nodes*.
- [ ] **Hierarchical System** = tier-1 shared memory (threads) + tier-2 distributed memory (MPI) → modern HPC standard.
- [ ] **Interconnection Networks** are either **direct** (mesh, torus, hypercube) or **indirect** (crossbar, Omega, butterfly).
- [ ] **Latency $t_0$** is fixed cost per message; **Bandwidth $r_\infty$** is per-byte cost.
- [ ] **Message Time Equation:** $t(n) = t_0 + \dfrac{n}{r_\infty}$ — *the single most important formula in this module*.
- [ ] **Critical message size:** $n^* = t_0 \cdot r_\infty$ — boundary between latency-bound and bandwidth-bound regimes.
- [ ] **Diameter:** max shortest-path between any two nodes.
- [ ] **Bisection Width:** min links crossing any balanced cut.
- [ ] **Bisection Bandwidth:** $B_b = B_w \times B_{\text{link}}$ — *the truest indicator of network bulk capacity*.
- [ ] **Hypercube** has optimal diameter ($\log_2 p$) and bisection width ($p/2$) but cost grows as $p \log_2 p$.
- [ ] **Torus** is the practical sweet spot — linear cost ($dp$ links) and good bisection bandwidth ($p/2$).
- [ ] **Mesh** has poor bisection ($\sqrt p$) — avoid for all-to-all workloads.
- [ ] **Hybrid programming heuristic:** Maximise intra-node sharing, minimise inter-node traffic.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law** ceiling: $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{f_s + (1-f_s)/p}$ — sets ultimate speedup bound.
- [ ] **Real systems** using these concepts: Fugaku (Tofu), Frontier (InfiniBand), Sunway TaihuLight (custom hierarchical).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark question in this module, the examiner expects: (1) a clear diagram (2 marks), (2) definitions and theory (3 marks), (3) comparison table (3 marks), (4) numerical computation with units (4 marks), (5) interpretation/discussion (2 marks). Master this template and you will reliably score 12+ marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Buses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Bus** in a High Performance Computing (HPC) system is a **shared communication subsystem** that transfers data, address, and control signals between processors, memory modules, and I/O devices through a common set of physical wires, traces, or optical pathways. In the KTU 2024 framework, a bus is classified as the **lowest-tier interconnect** in the memory and processor hierarchy, providing a single, multi-drop transmission medium governed by a defined bus protocol, arbitration scheme, and electrical/mechanical specification.

A bus is characterized by three orthogonal signal groups:

- **Address Bus** — unidirectional lines carrying the source/destination memory-mapped address.
- **Data Bus** — bidirectional lines carrying the actual payload (instructions or operands).
- **Control Bus** — timing, command, and arbitration signals (e.g., `RD`, `WR`, `CLK`, `BUSREQ`, `BUSGRANT`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:** "A bus is a set of conducting wires that conform to a standardized protocol, permitting the transfer of data words between independently clocked functional units via time-multiplexed, arbitrated transactions."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **single narrow one-lane road** connecting a group of villages (processors) to a central warehouse (memory). Only one villager-truck can travel on this road at a time, and there must be a **traffic controller (arbiter)** at the entry point who decides whose turn it is. If many villages want to send/receive goods simultaneously, a long queue forms — this is the **bus contention bottleneck** that limits scalability in HPC.

Key intuitive elements:

- The **width of the road** = the *data bus width* (e.g., 64 bits).
- The **speed limit** = the *bus clock frequency* (e.g., 1333 MHz).
- The **traffic controller** = the *bus arbiter*.
- The **road rules** = the *bus protocol* (e.g., PCI, AXI, Wishbone).
- The **waiting time at intersections** = the *arbitration latency*.

> [!NOTE]
> In HPC literature, buses are the *baseline interconnect* against which crossbars, rings, meshes, and tori are compared. They are simple, cheap, and broadcast-capable — but fundamentally **non-scalable** beyond a small number of masters.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the universally accepted KTU-grade metrics used to characterize any bus:

- **Bus Width (W)** — expressed in **bits** (typical: 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256).
- **Bus Frequency (f)** — expressed in **MHz** or **GHz** (typical: 66 MHz legacy PCI to 32 GT/s for PCIe 5.0).
- **Bus Bandwidth (BW)** — expressed in **MB/s** or **GB/s**.
- **Bus Latency (L)** — expressed in **clock cycles** or **nanoseconds (ns)**.
- **Bus Load / Utilization (U)** — dimensionless ratio, 0 ≤ U ≤ 1.

> [!NOTE]
> The reference **transfer time per word** for a single bus transaction is denoted **tₜ**, and the **cycle time** is denoted **T_c = 1 / f**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bus Operational Phases (Step-by-Step Logical Flow)

Every bus transaction traverses a **five-phase pipeline**. The KTU 2024 module on Buses expects the student to reproduce this pipeline verbatim:

1. **Arbitration Phase** — Multiple masters request the bus. The arbiter resolves contention using a *fixed-priority*, *round-robin*, or *fair-share* policy and grants the bus to the winning master. This phase consumes **T_arb** cycles.

2. **Address Phase** — The granted master places the target memory/device address on the **address bus** and asserts the appropriate **control signals** (e.g., `MREQ`, `RD/WR`). This phase lasts **1 clock cycle** in synchronous buses.

3. **Wait Phase (optional)** — If the slave device is slow (e.g., DRAM page miss), it inserts **wait states** by asserting `WAIT`. The number of inserted cycles is **T_wait**.

4. **Data Phase** — The data word (or burst of words) is transferred on the **data bus**. For an n-word burst, this lasts **n × T_c** cycles.

5. **Bus Release Phase** — The master de-asserts the bus request, and the arbiter may grant the bus to the next pending requestor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **total transaction time** for a single bus cycle is therefore:
> **T_total = T_arb + 1 + T_wait + n**

## 2.2 Bus Arbitration Schemes — Why & How

| Arbitration Scheme | Why it is Used | How it Works | KTU Board Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Daisy Chain (Serial)** | Minimal hardware, no central arbiter | Priority is fixed by physical position in the chain | Suffer from *propagation delay* and *single point of failure* |
| **Centralized (Parallel)** | Low latency, deterministic | All requests fed to one arbiter IC, single `GRANT` line | Standard for **PCI**, **AMBA APB** |
| **Distributed (Self-arbitration)** | Scalable, fault-tolerant | Each master has a unique arbitration ID, resolves by wire-OR or token-passing | Used in **Futurebus+**, **SCI**, **PCIe** |
| **Round-Robin** | Fairness, prevents starvation | Arbiter cycles through masters in a fixed sequence | Common in **multicore SoC** buses |
| **Lottery / TDMA** | Probabilistic fairness | Random or time-sliced grant | Used in research HPC interconnects |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following are the **board-essential formulas** for the Buses topic. Every derivation and numerical problem in the ESE will reduce to one of these.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{BW} = W \times f$ | W = data bus width, f = bus clock frequency | bits/s (divide by 8 for Bytes/s) |
| 2 | $\text{BW}_{\text{burst}} = \dfrac{n \times W}{T_c \times (n + T_{\text{overhead}})}$ | n = burst length, T_overhead = arbitration + address cycles | Bytes/s; n ≫ T_overhead for efficiency |
| 3 | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{arb}} + T_{\text{addr}} + T_{\text{wait}} + T_{\text{data}}$ | Per-bus-cycle latency components | Clock cycles |
| 4 | $U = \dfrac{\text{Bus used cycles}}{\text{Total cycles}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{data}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | Utilization, 0 ≤ U ≤ 1 | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $f_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{1}{T_c} = \dfrac{v}{k \times d_{\text{max}}}$ | v = signal velocity, d_max = max bus length, k = derating factor (typical 4–5) | Hz; sets physical limit on bus length |
| 6 | $A = f \times W \times U$ | Aggregate throughput, A | Bytes/s |
| 7 | $\text{Speedup}_{\text{bus}} = \dfrac{N}{1 + (N-1) \cdot (1 - U)}$ | N = number of processors, U = single-processor bus utilization | Dimensionless; derived from Amdahl on bus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These formulas are the bedrock of *bus performance engineering* in any parallel architecture — they are used in designing multi-core chips, GPU memory hierarchies, and embedded SoCs across the HPC industry.

## 2.4 Classification of Buses (KTU Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus distinguishes three macro-classes:

- **System Bus / Processor–Memory Bus** — High-speed, short, custom-designed (e.g., Intel Front Side Bus, AMD HyperTransport). Connects CPU ↔ northbridge ↔ DRAM.
- **I/O Bus / Expansion Bus** — Medium-speed, standardized, plug-and-play (e.g., **PCI**, **PCI-X**, **PCIe**, **AGP**).
- **Peripheral Bus** — Low-speed, hot-pluggable (e.g., **USB**, **I²C**, **SPI**, **SATA**).

A fourth class emerges in modern HPC — the **Network-on-Chip (NoC)** bus, which replaces the physical shared bus with a packet-switched on-chip mesh. While not a "bus" in the classical sense, KTU treats it as the *logical evolution* of the bus concept.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Theoretical Bus Bandwidth

We derive the closed-form expression for the maximum sustainable data rate of a bus with width $W$ and clock frequency $f$, taking into account the fact that one complete word is transferred per clock edge.

**Step 1 — Identify the fundamental transfer rate.**  
In a synchronous bus, exactly one word of width $W$ is latched per rising (and one per falling) clock edge, giving two transfers per clock period for **Double Data Rate (DDR)** buses.

$$
\text{Transfers per second} = 2 \times f
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bit-rate.**  
Each transfer moves $W$ bits. Therefore the gross bit-rate is:

$$
R_{\text{gross}} = W \times 2 \times f \quad \text{[bits/s]}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Bytes per second.**  
Divide by 8 to obtain the standard engineering unit:

$$
\text{BW}_{\max} = \dfrac{W \times 2 \times f}{8} = \dfrac{W \times f}{4} \quad \text{[Bytes/s for DDR]}
$$

**Step 4 — Include the protocol efficiency factor $\eta$.**  
The real achieved bandwidth is reduced by idle cycles, arbitration, and protocol overhead:

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{real}} = \eta \times \dfrac{W \times f}{4}, \quad 0 < \eta \le 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **Single Data Rate (SDR)** bus (e.g., legacy PCI), drop the factor of 2:
> $\text{BW}_{\max}^{\text{SDR}} = \dfrac{W \times f}{8}$ Bytes/s.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bus Utilization Under Multi-Processor Contention

We model a system with $N$ processors sharing a single bus, each issuing a memory request with probability $p$ per cycle. The bus can service at most one request per cycle. We derive the steady-state utilization using a binomial contention model.

**Step 1 — Single-processor request rate.**  
A single processor generates $p$ requests per cycle (probability mass).

**Step 2 — Multi-processor aggregate request rate.**  
By linearity of expectation, the aggregate number of requests per cycle is:

$$
R_{\text{agg}} = N \times p
$$

**Step 3 — Saturation cap.**  
The bus can service at most **1 request per cycle**. Therefore the *effective* requests served per cycle is:

$$
R_{\text{served}} = \min(R_{\text{agg}}, 1) = \min(N \times p, 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Bus utilization as the served ratio.**  

$$
U = R_{\text{served}} \times 1 = \min(N \times p, 1)
$$

**Step 5 — Closed form for the unsaturated regime ($N \cdot p \le 1$).**  

$$
U = N \cdot p
$$

**Step 6 — Closed form for the saturated regime ($N \cdot p > 1$).**  

$$
U = 1 \quad \text{(bus is fully busy — throughput-limited)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** When $U \to 1$, additional processors do **not** improve system throughput. This is the *bus-bandwidth wall* — the fundamental reason HPC moves to crossbar switches and networks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bus Clock Frequency Limited by Physical Length

The bus is a transmission line. The maximum frequency at which a signal can propagate without violating the setup-time of the farthest device is constrained by the signal round-trip time.

**Step 1 — Define the one-way propagation delay.**  
For a trace of length $d$ meters and signal velocity $v$ (m/s, typically $v \approx 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s on FR-4 PCB):

$$
t_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{v}
$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip (request + acknowledge) delay.**  
A bus transaction requires at least one round trip for the address and one for the data:

$$
t_{\text{rt}} = 2 \times t_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{2d}{v}
$$

**Step 3 — Nyquist-like condition for safe clocking.**  
To clock the bus reliably, the clock period must be at least $k$ times $t_{\text{rt}}$ (where $k \ge 4$ is a derating factor accounting for setup, hold, and skew):

$$
T_c \ge k \times t_{\text{rt}} = \dfrac{2 \times k \times d}{v}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for maximum frequency.**  

$$
f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{T_c} = \dfrac{v}{2 \times k \times d}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values (illustrative).**  
For $v = 1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s, $k = 5$, $d = 0.3$ m (typical motherboard CPU-to-chipset):

$$
f_{\max} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^8}{2 \times 5 \times 0.3} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^8}{3.0} = 5.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Hz} = 50 \text{ MHz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is precisely why **front-side buses were capped near 400–1600 MHz** by increasing the *width* and not the *length* — and why modern systems replaced the shared bus with point-to-point links like **HyperTransport** and **QuickPath**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Pattern)

**Problem:** A 64-bit DDR bus operates at 800 MHz with a protocol efficiency of 75%. The system has 4 processor cores, each issuing a memory request with probability $p = 0.30$ per cycle. The shared bus serves one request per cycle, and each serviced request returns a 64-byte cache line. Compute:
(a) Maximum theoretical bandwidth.
(b) Real achieved bandwidth.
(c) Bus utilization.
(d) Effective throughput in MB/s.

**Solution:**

**(a)** $\text{BW}_{\max} = \dfrac{W \times f}{4} = \dfrac{64 \times 800 \times 10^6}{4} = 12.8 \times 10^9$ Bytes/s $= 12.8$ GB/s.

**(b)** $\text{BW}_{\text{real}} = 0.75 \times 12.8 = 9.6$ GB/s.

**(c)** $U = \min(N \times p, 1) = \min(4 \times 0.30, 1) = \min(1.20, 1) = 1.0$ — **saturated regime**.

**(d)** Effective throughput = $U \times \text{BW}_{\text{real}} = 1.0 \times 9.6 = 9.6$ GB/s.
The 0.2 *excess* requests per cycle (since $N \cdot p = 1.2 > 1$) are **queued**, producing memory-access latency growth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above is the exact style of numerical question that appears in the **14-mark Part B** of the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the four-step methodology.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Shared Bus System

The following Mermaid diagram renders the *functional* architecture of a classical shared-bus multiprocessor — emphasizing the bus as the central spine connecting all masters, slaves, and the arbiter. Per the engine's safeguard, no reserved keywords are used as node IDs and labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU1[M1 CPU CORE 1] -->|REQ| ARB[CENTRAL BUS ARBITER]
    CPU2[M2 CPU CORE 2] -->|REQ| ARB
    CPU3[M3 CPU CORE 3] -->|REQ| ARB
    DMA1[DMA CONTROLLER] -->|REQ| ARB
    ARB -->|GRANT| CPU1
    ARB -->|GRANT| CPU2
    ARB -->|GRANT| DMA1
    CPU1 -->|ADDR DATA| BUS[(SHARED ADDRESS AND DATA BUS)]
    CPU2 -->|ADDR DATA| BUS
    CPU3 -->|ADDR DATA| BUS
    DMA1 -->|ADDR DATA| BUS
    BUS --> MEM[MAIN MEMORY DRAM]
    BUS --> CACHE[L2 CACHE BANK]
    BUS --> IOCTL[I O CONTROLLER]
    CACHE --> SNOOP[CACHE COHERENCY SNOOP FILTER]
    SNOOP -.->|SNOOP INVALIDATE| CPU1
    SNOOP -.->|SNOOP INVALIDATE| CPU2
    SNOOP -.->|SNOOP INVALIDATE| CPU3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **arbiter** sits in a control plane (dotted grant lines) and resolves contention among the three CPU cores and the DMA engine. The shared bus is the *data plane* (solid arrows) carrying address and data. The **snoop filter** maintains cache-coherency by broadcasting invalidations on the bus — a key HPC feature.

---

## 4.2 Bus Transaction State Machine (Mermaid State Diagram)

The following state diagram captures the **five-phase bus transaction** described in Section 2.1. It is the diagram most frequently asked in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> ARBITRATION : MASTER REQ ASSERTED
    ARBITRATION --> ADDRESS : GRANT RECEIVED
    ADDRESS --> WAIT : SLAVE WAIT SIGNAL HIGH
    ADDRESS --> DATA : SLAVE READY
    WAIT --> DATA : WAIT DEASSERTED
    DATA --> IDLE : TRANSACTION COMPLETE
    DATA --> BURST : BURST MODE ENABLED
    BURST --> IDLE : BURST END
    IDLE --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The state diagram is **identical** to the one in standard parallel-architecture textbooks. The KTU 2024 examiner expects this exact transition graph in any 7-mark sub-question on "Explain the bus transaction phases".

---

## 4.3 Bus Arbitration Comparison Topology

A side-by-side comparison of arbitration schemes is shown below as a Mermaid block diagram. Each leaf node represents a master, and the parent node represents the arbiter (or the daisy-chain terminus).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DAISY[DAISY CHAIN ARBITRATION]
        D0[M1] --> D1[M2] --> D2[M3] --> D3[M4]
    end
    subgraph CENT[PARALLEL CENTRAL ARBITER]
        C0[M1] --> CA[ARBITER]
        C1[M2] --> CA
        C2[M3] --> CA
        C3[M4] --> CA
    end
    subgraph DIST[DISTRIBUTED SELF ARBITRATION]
        E0[M1] <--> E1[M2] <--> E2[M3] <--> E3[M4]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Daisy chain** is *cheap* but suffers from priority-inversion. **Parallel** is *fast* but the arbiter is a single point of failure. **Distributed** is *scalable* and *fault-tolerant* — the architecture of choice for HPC fabrics like **SCI** and **Myrinet**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a system bus and a peripheral bus. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | System Bus | Peripheral Bus |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Connects processor, memory, chipset | Connects low-speed I/O devices |
| Speed | High (hundreds of MHz to GHz) | Low (kHz to a few hundred MHz) |
| Standardization | Often proprietary (e.g., Intel QPI) | Standardized (USB, I²C, SPI) |
| Example | Front Side Bus (legacy Intel) | USB 3.0 |
| Arbitration | Centralized / distributed | Daisy-chain or polled |

*(1 mark each for: definition, distinguishing features, and one example of each — total 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three bus arbitration schemes and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Daisy Chain Arbitration** — *Advantage:* minimal hardware, no central arbiter needed. (1 mark)
2. **Centralized (Parallel) Arbitration** — *Advantage:* low and deterministic arbitration latency. (1 mark)
3. **Distributed Arbitration** — *Advantage:* scalable and fault-tolerant, no single point of failure. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 marks] Explain the five phases of a bus transaction with a neat state diagram. How does split-transaction pipelining improve bus utilization?**

**Model Answer:**

The **five phases** of a synchronous bus transaction are:

1. **Arbitration Phase** — The master asserts `BUSREQ`. The arbiter evaluates all pending requests and asserts `BUSGRANT` to the winning master. Cost: $T_{\text{arb}}$ cycles.
2. **Address Phase** — The master places the target address on the address bus and asserts control lines (`RD`/`WR`, `MREQ`). Cost: 1 cycle.
3. **Wait Phase** — If the slave needs more time, it inserts wait states by asserting `WAIT`. Cost: $T_{\text{wait}}$ cycles (variable).
4. **Data Phase** — The data word (or burst) is transferred on the data bus. Cost: $n \times T_c$ for an $n$-word burst.
5. **Bus Release Phase** — The master de-asserts `BUSREQ`; the arbiter hands the bus to the next requestor.

**Total cycle count:**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{arb}} + 1 + T_{\text{wait}} + n
$$

**Split-Transaction Pipelining:**  
In a conventional bus, the bus is held *idle* between the address and data phases (e.g., waiting for a slow memory read). Split-transaction buses **release the bus** after the address phase and **re-arbitrate** — the data reply is tagged with a *Transaction ID* and re-inserted into the bus when ready. This allows other masters to use the bus during the wait, dramatically increasing $U$.

**State diagram (already drawn in Section 4.2 — reproduce verbatim for full marks).**

*(Mark split: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 5 marks for phases; 2 marks for split-transaction explanation.)*

#### **(b) [7 marks] A 32-bit bus operates at 100 MHz. A processor issues memory reads with average inter-arrival time of 4 cycles. The bus has 2 cycles of arbitration overhead and 1 cycle of address phase. Compute the bus utilization and the maximum number of processors that can be supported before saturation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute request rate per processor.**  
Inter-arrival time = 4 cycles ⇒ $p = 1/4 = 0.25$ requests/cycle per processor.

**Step 2 — Compute bandwidth first.**  
For an SDR bus: $\text{BW} = \dfrac{32 \times 100 \times 10^6}{8} = 400$ MB/s.

**Step 3 — Bus utilization for one processor.**  
For one processor, $U_1 = p = 0.25$ (unsaturated regime).

**Step 4 — Find $N_{\text{sat}}$.**  
Saturation occurs when $N \cdot p = 1$:

$$
N_{\text{sat}} = \dfrac{1}{p} = \dfrac{1}{0.25} = 4 \text{ processors}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**  
With 4 processors, the bus is **saturated** ($U = 1$). A 5th processor would queue requests, increasing latency without adding throughput.

*(Mark split: 2 marks setup, 2 marks calculation, 2 marks interpretation, 1 mark for units/sanity check.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) [7 marks] Compare centralized, daisy-chain, and distributed bus arbitration schemes. Under what HPC conditions is distributed arbitration preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Daisy Chain | Centralized | Distributed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardware | Minimal (wire-OR) | Arbiter IC + GRANT line per master | Per-master arbitration ID |
| Latency | High (cumulative propagation) | Low (one arbiter delay) | Medium (wire-OR resolve) |
| Scalability | Poor (chain length) | Limited (arbiter fan-out) | Excellent |
| Fault Tolerance | Single chain break = total failure | Arbiter failure = total failure | One master can fail without global loss |
| Fairness | Fixed by physical order | Programmable | Token-passing / lottery |

**HPC preference for distributed arbitration:**  
Distributed arbitration is preferred in **large-scale HPC** when (i) the number of masters is high (>8 cores), (ii) the system must tolerate arbitrary master failures, and (iii) global fairness must be guaranteed without a single arbiter becoming a bottleneck. Examples include **SCI rings**, **PCIe switched fabrics**, and **NoC meshes** in modern many-core processors.

*(Mark split: 3 marks for table, 2 marks for narrative, 2 marks for HPC conditions.)*

#### **(b) [7 marks] Derive the relationship between maximum bus clock frequency and physical bus length. Given a bus length of 0.5 m, a signal velocity of $1.5 \times 10^8$ m/s, and a derating factor of 5, compute the maximum operating frequency.**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**  
A bus transaction requires at least one round-trip signal propagation (address out, data back). For a trace of length $d$:

$$
t_{\text{rt}} = \dfrac{2d}{v}
$$

The clock period must exceed $k \cdot t_{\text{rt}}$ for safe setup/hold, hence:

$$
f_{\max} = \dfrac{v}{2 \times k \times d}
$$

**Numerical computation:**

$$
f_{\max} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^8}{2 \times 5 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^8}{5.0} = 3.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Hz} = 30 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Interpretation:**  
At 0.5 m, the bus is fundamentally capped at **30 MHz** — explaining why HPC moved to point-to-point links (e.g., Intel QPI, AMD Infinity Fabric) where the *effective* $d$ per link is just a few centimeters.

*(Mark split: 3 marks derivation, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks on "Buses":**
> 1. **Confusing bus *frequency* with bus *bandwidth*** — a 64-bit / 1 GHz bus does **not** transfer 1 GB/s; you must compute $W \times f / 8$ and account for SDR vs. DDR.
> 2. **Forgetting the protocol efficiency $\eta$** in bandwidth questions — board answer without $\eta$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the state diagram** in a 7-mark question on bus transactions — the diagram is *worth at least 2 marks by itself*.
> 4. **Mixing up arbitration schemes** — students frequently write "daisy chain" for "centralized". Memorize the difference (chain = wire-OR; centralized = arbiter IC + per-master `GRANT`).
> 5. **Ignoring the saturation regime** in multi-processor bus problems — always state whether $N \cdot p \le 1$ or $> 1$ before quoting utilization.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **bus** = address + data + control lines, shared, arbitrated, broadcast-capable.
- The **three signal groups** are: Address (unidirectional), Data (bidirectional), Control (timing/arb).
- The **five phases** of a transaction: Arbitration → Address → Wait → Data → Release. Memorize the order and the cycle-cost of each.
- **Bandwidth formula:** $\text{BW} = W \times f$ (bits/s); divide by 8 for Bytes/s; multiply by 2 for DDR.
- **Real bandwidth:** $\text{BW}_{\text{real}} = \eta \times W \times f / 8$ where $\eta \in (0, 1]$ is the protocol efficiency.
- **Saturation condition:** $N \cdot p = 1$ is the bus-bandwidth wall; above this, only latency grows.
- **Three arbitration classes:** Daisy Chain (cheap, slow), Centralized (fast, fragile), Distributed (scalable, fault-tolerant).
- **Physical limit:** $f_{\max} = v / (2 k d)$ — long buses run slowly; hence the move to point-to-point links.
- **Modern HPC interconnects** (QPI, Infinity Fabric, NVLink) are *not* buses but evolved point-to-point derivatives — know the *why*.
- **Bus utilization** $U = T_{\text{data}} / T_{\text{total}}$; split-transaction pipelining pushes $U \to 1$ by hiding wait states.
- **Snoopy coherence** is a *bus-specific* feature — the broadcast nature of the bus enables cheap cache coherency among processors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switched and Fat-Tree Networks - Mesh Networks - Hybrids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switched and Fat-Tree Networks - Mesh Networks - Hybrids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Switched Networks

### Formal Definition
A **Switched Network** is an interconnection architecture in which processing nodes (PEs / processors / end-nodes) are linked via dedicated **switching elements (routers)** rather than via direct point-to-point links. Packets are routed from source to destination by traversing one or more intermediate switches along configurable paths. The interconnection is *indirect* — meaning that not all nodes are directly connected to each other; communication occurs through the switch fabric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** A switched network is the foundational building block for indirect topologies. Each switch typically has $p$ processor-side links (toward PEs) and $p$ network-side links (toward other switches) for a *radix-$2p$* switch.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a switched network like a **city's traffic roundabout system** with traffic police officers (switches) at every intersection. Cars (data packets) do not need a direct road from every house to every other house. Instead, they travel to the nearest roundabout, where the officer directs them along the right path. The advantage: fewer roads are required, but the officer must be smart enough to route traffic efficiently.

### Key Metrics (with bold emphasis)
- **Switch Radix ($k$):** number of bidirectional ports on a switch
- **Bisection Bandwidth:** minimum aggregate bandwidth crossing a network cut that divides the system into two equal halves
- **Diameter:** the maximum number of hops between any two nodes
- **Network Latency:** time for a packet to travel from source to destination

---

## 1.2 Fat-Tree Networks

### Formal Definition
A **Fat-Tree Network**, introduced by Charles E. Leiserson in 1985, is a *tree-based indirect network* in which the **bandwidth (link capacity) increases as one approaches the root of the tree**. Each edge near the leaves carries less bandwidth, while edges near the root carry more — making the tree "fat" (visually wider) toward the top. Mathematically, a fat-tree built from $k$-port switches is a **$k$-ary fat-tree** that can connect $k^3/4$ end-nodes with full bisection bandwidth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The fat-tree was originally proposed for the Connection Machine CM-5 supercomputer. It is a *universal* network — meaning it can simulate any other network of the same size with only constant-factor slowdown.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **river delta system**. Tiny streams near the source carry small volumes of water. As they merge, the rivers grow wider and deeper. No single point gets "flooded" (congested) because capacity scales with the merging traffic. A fat-tree is conceptually similar — the more traffic that funnels together, the wider the channel becomes.

### Topology Structure
- Built from **identical $k$-port switches** arranged in **$\log_k N$ levels** (for $N$ end-nodes)
- **Half** the ports on each switch connect to lower levels (toward PEs or child switches)
- **Half** the ports connect to the next higher level (toward parents)
- Eliminates the classic *bisection bottleneck* of a standard (thin) tree

---

## 1.3 Mesh Networks

### Formal Definition
A **Mesh Network** (specifically a *$k$-ary $n$-cube*) is a *direct network* where each node has $2n$ neighbors, with $k$ nodes along each of the $n$ dimensions. For a 2D mesh, $n=2$; for a 3D mesh (cube), $n=3$. The total number of nodes is $N = k^n$. Mesh is a **regular, symmetric, direct** topology — each node has its own router, and PEs communicate by sending messages across a hop-by-hop grid.

### Special Variants
- **2D Mesh (Torus):** nodes arranged in a grid; toroidal mesh wraps around edges
- **3D Mesh / Torus:** extends to three dimensions; widely used in modern HPC
- **Hyper-cube:** $k=2$ (binary $n$-cube) with $N = 2^n$ nodes

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a **chessboard** or a **city grid**. Each house (processor) sits at an intersection and can talk only to its 4 immediate neighbors (up/down/left/right). To send a message across the city, the message must "hop" from one intersection to the next — like a courier walking block by block. In a **toroidal mesh**, the board wraps around, so the top edge connects to the bottom (think of a donut-shaped city).

---

## 1.4 Hybrid Networks

### Formal Definition
**Hybrid Networks** combine *two or more distinct network topologies* within the same system to exploit complementary strengths. Typical examples include **Dragonfly** (groups of all-to-all routers connected via long-haul links), **Torus + Tree** (mesh for local traffic, tree for global), and **Clos networks** (multi-stage switching using smaller crossbars to build a large one). Hybrids are designed to balance **locality exploitation** (short distance) with **global bandwidth provisioning** (long distance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Most modern Top500 supercomputers (Frontier, Fugaku, LUMI) use **Dragonfly**, **Torus**, or **Slingshot** topologies — all of which are hybrid by design.

### Conceptual Analogy
A hybrid network is like a **modern city's transportation system** — local streets handle neighborhood traffic, arterial roads handle city-wide traffic, and highways connect distant suburbs. You do not use a highway to go to the next-door grocery shop, nor do you use a narrow alley to travel between cities. Each network layer is optimized for its own scale of communication.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Switched Networks — Operating Principles

A switched network routes packets by *destination address lookup* at every intermediate switch. The switching mechanism may be:
1. **Store-and-Forward (SAF)** — entire packet is buffered, then forwarded
2. **Wormhole Routing** — packet is split into *flits* (flow control digits); the head flit establishes the path, and subsequent flits follow in a *pipeline*
3. **Cut-Through Routing** — forwarding begins as soon as the header is received

### Why Switches are Used
- **Scalability:** A $k$-port switch can interconnect up to $k$ devices; a hierarchy of switches scales to millions of nodes
- **Cost Reduction:** Point-to-point links become prohibitively expensive for large $N$; switches amortize cost
- **Fault Tolerance:** Multiple paths exist; routing can be reconfigured dynamically
- **Aggregation:** Multiple low-bandwidth links aggregate to a high-bandwidth fabric

### Crossbar Switch (Building Block of Switches)
A **crossbar** is a switch with $N$ inputs and $N$ outputs that can connect any input to any output simultaneously. The crossbar is the workhorse of HPC fabrics.

- **Crossbar Complexity:** $O(N^2)$ crosspoints
- **Blocking:** A crossbar is **strictly non-blocking** (a connection can always be made without disturbing others)

---

## 2.2 Fat-Tree — Theoretical Analysis

### Levels of a $k$-ary Fat-Tree
For a $k$-ary fat-tree using $k$-port switches:
- **Level 0 (leaves):** switches with $k/2$ ports to PEs, $k/2$ ports upward
- **Levels 1, 2, …, $\log_2 k - 1$:** intermediate switches
- **Total switch count:** $N_{sw} = k^2$
- **Total PE count (end-nodes):** $N = k^3 / 4$
- **Levels in the tree:** $\log_2 k$

### Properties
- **Diameter:** $\approx 2 \log_k N$ (between any two PEs)
- **Bisection Bandwidth:** Full — equal to the total leaf bandwidth
- **Non-blocking:** End-nodes can communicate with full bandwidth simultaneously

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost Analysis:** A $k$-ary fat-tree uses $k^2$ switches. Each switch has $k$ ports. Total port count is $k^3$. Half of all ports (i.e., $k^3/2$) connect to PEs, but only half of those are used ($k^3/4$ PEs), because the other half would connect to non-existent PEs at the root. This is a deliberate waste to ensure non-blocking bisection.

### Real-World Utility
- The fat-tree is the **default topology of Infiniband** networks (used in Top500 systems)
- Used in **Ethernet fabrics** (e.g., Cisco, Arista data-center switches)
- Provides predictable performance for MPI applications

---

## 2.3 Mesh Network — Theoretical Analysis

### Topology Parameters of a $k$-ary $n$-cube

The general formulation of a $k$-ary $n$-mesh (and torus) is given below. Note that $k$ = nodes per dimension, $n$ = number of dimensions, and $N = k^n$ = total nodes.

- **Nodes per Dimension ($k$):** the number of nodes along one axis
- **Dimensions ($n$):** typically 2 or 3
- **Degree (links/node):** $2n$ for a torus, $2n$ for a mesh (with boundary nodes having fewer)
- **Diameter:** $n(k-1)$ for mesh, $n \lfloor k/2 \rfloor$ for torus
- **Average Distance:** approximately $n \cdot k / 3$
- **Bisection Width:** $k^{n-1}$ links crossing the central cut
- **Total Links:** $n \cdot k^n$ for torus, $n(k^n - k^{n-1})$ for mesh

### Routing in Mesh
- **Dimension-Order Routing (DOR):** XY routing in 2D — first travel in X, then in Y
- **Deadlock-Free:** Yes, by construction in DOR
- **Adaptive Routing:** Allows detour around congested links (e.g., ROMM, O1turn)

### Real-World Utility
- **Torus 3D** is used in the **Fujitsu Fugaku** (Tofu-D interconnect)
- **2D/3D Mesh** is used in the **Intel Xeon Phi** (KNC) family
- **Torus 2D** is used in the **IBM Blue Gene** family (BG/Q)

---

## 2.4 Hybrid Networks — Theoretical Analysis

The two most-cited hybrid topologies in modern HPC are:

### Dragonfly (used in Cray XC, HPE Cray EX, Slingshot)
- **Levels:** Router → Group → Network
- **Group:** $a$ routers, each with $p$ ports, fully connected via *local* electrical links
- **Global:** $g$ groups, each with $h$ global links per router
- **Total nodes:** $N = a \cdot p \cdot (\text{PEs per router}) \approx a \cdot p \cdot (p - 2h)$

### Hierarchical / Dragonfly+ (Tofu Interconnect D)
- Combines **Tori** at the local level and a **switches/switches of switches** at the global level

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Topology | Nodes ($N$) | Degree | Diameter | Bisection Width | Bisection Bandwidth |
|---|----------|-------------|--------|----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $k$-ary $n$-mesh | $k^n$ | $\le 2n$ | $n(k-1)$ | $k^{n-1}$ | $k^{n-1} \cdot B_{\text{link}}$ |
| 2 | $k$-ary $n$-torus | $k^n$ | $2n$ | $n \lfloor k/2 \rfloor$ | $2 k^{n-1}$ | $2 k^{n-1} \cdot B_{\text{link}}$ |
| 3 | Hypercube ($k=2$) | $2^n$ | $n$ | $n$ | $2^{n-1}$ | $2^{n-1} \cdot B_{\text{link}}$ |
| 4 | $k$-ary Fat-Tree | $k^3/4$ | $k$ | $\approx 2 \log_k N$ | $k^2/2$ | $(k^2/2) \cdot B_{\text{link}}$ |
| 5 | Crossbar | $N$ | $N$ | $1$ | $N$ | $N \cdot B_{\text{link}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exams:** Cost of a topology $\approx$ total number of links (or wires). For a $k$-ary $n$-cube, the link count is $n \cdot k^n$ (torus) or $n(k^n - k^{n-1})$ (mesh). For a fat-tree, link count is roughly $k^3$.

### Key Dimensional Relationships

$$
N = k^n \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = N^{1/n}
$$

For *constant bisection bandwidth* with $N$ nodes:

$$
B_{\text{bisection}} = k^{n-1} = N^{(n-1)/n}
$$

For a $d$-dimensional mesh, as $N$ grows, $n$ should also grow to keep bisection high. This is why **3D torus** is preferred for *exascale* systems.

---

## 2.6 Why Each Topology Matters in Engineering

- **Switched networks** form the *backbone of every data center* in the world
- **Fat-tree** is the *industry standard* (Infiniband, Ethernet) due to its non-blocking property
- **Mesh / Torus** is favored for *tightly coupled HPC* (weather, molecular dynamics) where local traffic dominates
- **Hybrids (Dragonfly, Slingshot)** dominate *exascale* (Frontier, Fugaku, LUMI) because they balance cost, locality, and global bandwidth
