> If $N$ is *not* divisible by $P$, the **last thread** receives the remainder iterations. E.g., for $N = 1003, P = 4$, chunk = 251, and Thread 3 gets iterations 753 to 1002.

### 3.2 Derivation: Guided Scheduling Chunk Size Evolution

**Given:** $N = 100, P = 4, \text{minimum chunk} = 2$.

**Iteration 1 of scheduling:**
$$\text{chunk}_1 = \lceil N / P \rceil = \lceil 100 / 4 \rceil = 25$$

**Iteration 2 of scheduling (after Thread 0 takes chunk₁):**
$$\text{remaining} = 100 - 25 = 75$$
$$\text{chunk}_2 = \lceil 75 / 4 \rceil = 19$$

**Iteration 3 of scheduling:**
$$\text{remaining} = 75 - 19 = 56$$
$$\text{chunk}_3 = \lceil 56 / 4 \rceil = 14$$

**Iteration 4 of scheduling:**
$$\text{remaining} = 56 - 14 = 42$$
$$\text{chunk}_4 = \lceil 42 / 4 \rceil = 11$$

**General formula at step $i$:**
$$\text{chunk}_i = \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{N_i}{P} \right\rceil, k_{\min}\right)$$

where $N_i$ is the number of unassigned iterations at step $i$, and $k_{\min}$ is the user-specified minimum chunk.

### 3.3 Derivation: Total Overhead for Dynamic Scheduling

**Given:** $N = 1000, P = 4, k = 50, T_{\text{lock}} = 10 \mu s$.

**Step 1 — Total number of chunks.**
$$\text{Chunks} = \lceil N / k \rceil = \lceil 1000 / 50 \rceil = 20$$

**Step 2 — Each thread processes how many chunks?**
Since chunks are taken greedily, each thread processes approximately:
$$\text{Chunks per thread} = \lceil \text{Chunks} / P \rceil = \lceil 20 / 4 \rceil = 5$$

**Step 3 — Total synchronization overhead (acquire + release).**
$$T_{\text{overhead}} = \text{Chunks} \cdot 2 \cdot T_{\text{lock}} = 20 \cdot 2 \cdot 10\,\mu s = 400\,\mu s$$

**Step 4 — If we increase $k$ to 100:**
$$\text{Chunks} = 10, \quad T_{\text{overhead}} = 10 \cdot 2 \cdot 10\,\mu s = 200\,\mu s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Larger chunk size ⇒ Less overhead but poorer load balance.** This is the *central trade-off* in loop scheduling and is a guaranteed KTU question.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for Choosing a Schedule

| Workload Characteristic | Recommended Schedule | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Uniform iteration cost | `static` | No imbalance, no overhead needed |
| Mildly irregular cost | `static, k=1` (round-robin) | Spreads imbalance across threads |
| Highly irregular cost | `dynamic, k=10` | Load balancing outweighs overhead |
| Decreasing irregularity | `guided` | Adaptive chunk sizing |
| Unknown at compile time | `runtime` or `auto` | Defers decision to runtime |
| Nested loops with outer parallelism | `static` (outer) + `dynamic` (inner) | Hierarchical balance |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: OpenMP Loop Scheduling Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Parallel For Construct] --> B{Schedule Kind}
    B --> C[Static Scheduler]
    B --> D[Dynamic Scheduler]
    B --> E[Guided Scheduler]
    B --> F[Runtime Scheduler]
    B --> G[Auto Scheduler]

    C --> C1[Pre-compute Chunk Size]
    C1 --> C2[Round Robin Distribution]
    C2 --> C3[Thread 0 Block]
    C2 --> C4[Thread 1 Block]
    C2 --> C5[Thread 2 Block]
    C2 --> C6[Thread 3 Block]

    D --> D1[Initialize Shared Work Queue]
    D1 --> D2[Atomic Lock Acquire]
    D2 --> D3[Pop Chunk of K Iterations]
    D3 --> D4[Execute Iterations]
    D4 --> D5{Queue Empty?}
    D5 -- No --> D2
    D5 -- Yes --> D6[Barrier Sync]

    E --> E1[Compute Adaptive Chunk Size]
    E1 --> E2[Decreasing Chunk Distribution]
    E2 --> D3

    F --> F1[Read OMP SCHEDULE Env Variable]
    F1 --> C
    F1 --> D
    F1 --> E

    G --> G1[Compiler Runtime Decision]
    G1 --> C
    G1 --> D
    G1 --> E
```

### 4.2 Execution Flow Diagram: Dynamic Scheduling with Chunks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Loop Start: i=0 to N-1]) --> init[Initialize Work Queue with N Iterations]
    init --> fork[Fork P Threads]
    fork --> workQ{Work Queue Empty?}

    workQ -- No --> lock1[Acquire Atomic Lock]
    lock1 --> pop[Pop Chunk of K Iterations]
    pop --> unlock1[Release Lock]
    unlock1 --> exec[Execute Chunk]
    exec --> workQ

    workQ -- Yes --> barrier[Barrier Synchronization]
    barrier --> join([Join Threads and End Loop])

    style start fill:#90EE90
    style join fill:#FFB6C1
    style lock1 fill:#FFD700
    style pop fill:#87CEEB
    style exec fill:#DDA0DD
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Static vs Dynamic vs Guided

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Static[STATIC SCHEDULING]
        S0[Thread 0: Iter 0-249]
        S1[Thread 1: Iter 250-499]
        S2[Thread 2: Iter 500-749]
        S3[Thread 3: Iter 750-999]
    end

    subgraph Dynamic[DYNAMIC SCHEDULING]
        D0[Thread Pool]
        D1[Shared Queue]
        D2[Pull K Iterations Each Time]
    end

    subgraph Guided[GUIDED SCHEDULING]
        G0[Initial Large Chunks]
        G1[Progressively Smaller Chunks]
        G2[Final Small Chunks]
    end

    Static --> SNote[Low Overhead, Fixed Assignment]
    Dynamic --> DNote[High Overhead, Load Balanced]
    Guided --> GNote[Medium Overhead, Adaptive]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Static scheduling uses *pre-computed* partition (one-time cost). Dynamic uses *runtime atomic operations* (per-chunk cost). Guided strikes a balance by amortizing overhead over decreasing chunk sizes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define loop scheduling in OpenMP. List any two scheduling kinds supported by OpenMP.**

**Model Answer:**
Loop scheduling is the mechanism used by OpenMP to distribute the iterations of a parallel loop among the threads in a team. It is specified using the `schedule` clause on the `parallel for` directive.

The two scheduling kinds are:
1. **Static scheduling** — Iterations are divided into chunks of approximately equal size and pre-assigned to threads in a round-robin manner.
2. **Dynamic scheduling** — Iterations are placed in a shared queue; threads dynamically pull chunks as they finish their current work.

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is the difference between `schedule(static)` and `schedule(dynamic, 1)`?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `schedule(static)` | `schedule(dynamic, 1)` |
|---|---|---|
| Chunk size | $\lceil N / P \rceil$ | 1 iteration |
| Distribution | Pre-computed at start | Runtime atomic pull |
| Overhead | None | High (lock per iteration) |
| Load balance | Poor for irregular work | Excellent |
| Best for | Uniform loops | Highly irregular loops |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **ONE** of the two choices. Each question is divided into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain in detail the different loop scheduling clauses supported by OpenMP with suitable examples.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

OpenMP provides five loop scheduling kinds, each suited to a different workload characteristic:

**1. `schedule(static [, chunk])`**
- The default chunk size is `ceil(N/P)`. With `chunk = k`, thread *t* receives iterations $t \cdot k, t \cdot k + 1, \ldots, (t+1) \cdot k - 1$.
- No synchronization overhead.
- **Example:** `#pragma omp parallel for schedule(static, 4)` — each thread gets 4 consecutive iterations at a time.

**2. `schedule(dynamic [, chunk])`**
- Threads pull `chunk` iterations from a shared work queue at runtime.
- Implemented using atomic operations on a counter.
- **Example:** `#pragma omp parallel for schedule(dynamic, 10)` — chunks of 10 iterations are taken dynamically.

**3. `schedule(guided [, chunk])`**
- Initial chunk size is `ceil(remaining_iterations / P)`, decreasing geometrically.
- The `chunk` parameter sets the *minimum* chunk size.
- **Example:** `#pragma omp parallel for schedule(guided, 2)` — chunks start large, shrink to a minimum of 2.

**4. `schedule(runtime)`**
- Scheduling decision deferred to runtime; reads the `OMP_SCHEDULE` environment variable.
- Used for portable code where the optimal schedule is machine-dependent.

**5. `schedule(auto)`**
- Compiler and runtime jointly choose the schedule.
- Implementation-defined behavior; optimized for the target architecture.

---

**(b) Consider a parallel loop with $N = 2000$ iterations executed on $P = 8$ threads using `schedule(dynamic, 25)`. Calculate:**
1. The total number of chunks created.
2. The approximate number of chunks each thread executes.
3. The total synchronization overhead if each lock acquire-release costs $5 \mu s$.

*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Total number of chunks:**
$$\text{Chunks} = \lceil N / k \rceil = \lceil 2000 / 25 \rceil = 80$$

**Part 2 — Chunks per thread:**
$$\text{Chunks per thread} = \lceil 80 / 8 \rceil = 10$$

So each thread processes approximately **10 chunks of 25 iterations = 250 iterations per thread**.

**Part 3 — Total synchronization overhead:**
Each chunk acquisition requires **2 atomic operations** (lock + unlock).
$$T_{\text{overhead}} = \text{Chunks} \cdot 2 \cdot T_{\text{lock}} = 80 \cdot 2 \cdot 5\,\mu s = 800\,\mu s$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget the factor of **2** (one for lock acquire, one for lock release). Marks are deducted if the atomic operation is counted as a single event. Also, the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ must be explicitly shown when $N$ is not divisible by $k$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Discuss the trade-off between static and dynamic loop scheduling. Under what conditions would you prefer one over the other?** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Static Scheduling:**
- **Advantages:**
  - Zero runtime overhead.
  - Excellent cache locality (each thread works on a contiguous block).
  - Deterministic execution.
- **Disadvantages:**
  - Poor load balancing for irregular workloads.
  - A slow thread can starve the entire parallel region.

**Dynamic Scheduling:**
- **Advantages:**
  - Excellent load balancing — work is distributed based on actual completion.
  - Tolerates thread heterogeneity (e.g., mixed CPU performance cores).
- **Disadvantages:**
  - High synchronization overhead due to atomic queue operations.
  - Poor cache locality — threads jump across iteration indices.
  - Non-deterministic execution order.

**Decision Criteria:**

| Choose Static When... | Choose Dynamic When... |
|---|---|
| Iteration cost is uniform | Iteration cost is highly variable |
| Loop count is small (overhead dominates) | Loop count is large |
| Cache locality is critical | Load balance is critical |
| Hardware is homogeneous | Hardware is heterogeneous |

**Numerical Example of Trade-off:**
For $N = 1000, P = 4, k = 10$ in dynamic scheduling:
- Overhead = $\lceil 1000/10 \rceil \cdot 2 \cdot T_{\text{lock}}$
- If $T_{\text{lock}} = 50\,\mu s$, overhead = $100 \cdot 2 \cdot 50 = 10,000\,\mu s = 10\,ms$
- This is acceptable *only* if the imbalance cost exceeds 10 ms.

---

**(b) For `schedule(guided, 4)` with $N = 500$ iterations and $P = 4$ threads, compute the chunk sizes assigned in the first three scheduling rounds.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Round 1:**
$$\text{chunk}_1 = \lceil 500 / 4 \rceil = 125$$
$$\text{remaining} = 500 - 125 = 375$$

**Round 2:**
$$\text{chunk}_2 = \lceil 375 / 4 \rceil = 94$$
$$\text{remaining} = 375 - 94 = 281$$

**Round 3:**
$$\text{chunk}_3 = \lceil 281 / 4 \rceil = 71$$
$$\text{remaining} = 281 - 71 = 210$$

**Verification using general formula:**
$$\text{chunk}_i = \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{N_i}{P} \right\rceil, k_{\min}\right) = \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{N_i}{4} \right\rceil, 4\right)$$

Since all computed values (125, 94, 71) exceed $k_{\min} = 4$, no clamping occurs in early rounds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Two common mistakes:
> 1. Forgetting to **subtract** the previous chunk from the remaining iterations.
> 2. Not applying the `max` operation with the minimum chunk size when the computed size drops below $k_{\min}$.
> Always show the formula *before* substitution to earn full method marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deduction Zones**
> 1. **Confusing `runtime` with `auto`:** Runtime reads `OMP_SCHEDULE`; auto lets the compiler decide. They are *not* synonyms.
> 2. **Forgetting the default chunk size:** Static default is `ceil(N/P)`, *not* `N/P`. Use ceiling division.
> 3. **Ignoring synchronization cost:** In dynamic scheduling questions, examiners expect students to compute **both** compute time and overhead time.
> 4. **Mixing up `guided` with `dynamic`:** Guided chunk size *decreases*; dynamic chunk size is *constant*.
> 5. **Not stating the `schedule` clause syntax:** Always write `schedule(kind, chunk)` with both parameters.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Loop scheduling** = distribution strategy for loop iterations across threads in a parallel region.
- **Five OpenMP scheduling kinds:** `static`, `dynamic`, `guided`, `runtime`, `auto`.
- **Default chunk for static** = $\lceil N / P \rceil$.
- **Trade-off axis:** Static ⇒ low overhead, poor balance. Dynamic ⇒ high overhead, good balance. Guided ⇒ middle ground.
- **Guided chunk formula:** $\text{chunk}_i = \max(\lceil \text{remaining} / P \rceil, k_{\min})$.
- **Dynamic overhead formula:** $T_{\text{ovh}} = \lceil N/k \rceil \cdot 2 \cdot T_{\text{lock}}$.
- **`schedule(runtime)`** defers decision to the `OMP_SCHEDULE` environment variable.
- **`schedule(auto)`** is compiler/implementation-defined.
- **Performance metrics:** Speedup $S = T_s / T_p$, Efficiency $E = S / P$.
- **Best static for uniform loops, dynamic for irregular, guided for decreasing-imbalance loops, runtime for portable code, auto for hardware-tuned code.**
- **Synchronization overhead is proportional to the number of chunks**, not the number of iterations.
- **Cache locality is best with static** (contiguous block per thread), worst with dynamic (1-by-1 traversal).
- **KTU mantra:** *Larger chunk ⇒ less overhead, more imbalance. Smaller chunk ⇒ more overhead, better balance.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tasking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tasking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tasking (OpenMP Tasking Model)** is a parallel programming paradigm in which parallelism is expressed in terms of **dynamically generated units of work called *tasks*** rather than statically partitioned iterations of loops or independent regions. A *task* is a specific instance of executable code together with its **own data environment** that the OpenMP runtime can schedule onto any thread for execution.

In the **OpenMP 3.0+ specification**, a task is generated the moment a thread encounters a `#pragma omp task` construct. The runtime is then responsible for:
- **Task creation & allocation** — placing the task into a task pool (often a thread-local deque).
- **Task scheduling** — assigning a free thread to pick up a task and execute it.
- **Task synchronization** — using `taskwait`, `taskgroup`, or dependency clauses.

Tasks are essential for expressing **irregular parallelism** (recursive algorithms, dynamic data structures, search trees, divide-and-conquer algorithms), which cannot be elegantly expressed through loop parallelism (`#pragma omp parallel for`).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Office Mailroom

Imagine an **office mailroom with 6 workers** (threads):

- The manager places *work orders* (tasks) on a **to-do board** (the runtime task pool).
- A *work order* has a **description** (the code) and a **list of required files** (the data environment).
- Any worker who is free can **grab a work order** off the board and execute it. They might **put new sub-orders** on the board (child tasks) while working.
- Workers can **wait for completion** of certain orders (`taskwait`) before continuing, or declare **dependencies** ("I need order #7's output before I can start").

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy explains why **tasking outperforms pure loop parallelism** in recursive or irregular workloads: the workload is **dynamically balanced** — no worker is stuck waiting for the loop's slowest iteration.

---

## 1.3 Core Constructs and Clauses (Syllabus Highlights)

| Construct / Clause | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `#pragma omp task` | Generates a new task |
| `#pragma omp taskwait` | Waits for **all** child tasks of the current task to complete |
| `#pragma omp taskgroup` | Creates a region; `taskwait` here is scoped to the group |
| `depend(in: x)` | Task reads *x* |
| `depend(out: x)` | Task writes *x* |
| `depend(inout: x)` | Task both reads and writes *x* |
| `final(expr)` | If `expr` is true, task becomes a **final task** (its child tasks execute *immediately* and *inline*) |
| `untied` | A suspended task may be **resumed by any thread** |
| `mergeable` | The task's data environment is **shared with the parent** (memory optimization) |
| `priority(p)` | Hint to runtime about scheduling priority |
| `#pragma omp taskloop` | Loop whose iterations are converted into tasks |
| `#pragma omp taskyield` | Suspends the current task to allow other tasks to run |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Granularity** is a critical KTU exam concept: *fine-grained tasks* (too many tiny tasks) cause **runtime overhead**, while *coarse-grained tasks* (too few large tasks) cause **load imbalance**. The art of tasking lies in choosing the correct **cutoff depth** (in recursion) or **chunk size** (in taskloops).

---

## 1.4 Key Physical / Standard Metrics

- **Task Granularity (G):** Average execution time of a single task in microseconds (µs).
- **Task Overhead (Tₒ):** Time to create, schedule, and synchronize a task (~1–10 µs in modern OpenMP).
- **Critical Path Length (Cₚ):** The longest chain of dependent tasks in the DAG.
- **Degree of Concurrency (D_c):** Number of independent tasks available at any point in time.
- **Task Parallelism Ratio (R_tp):** Fraction of program that can be expressed as tasks; bounded by **Amdahl's Law**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Task Execution Lifecycle (Structured Logic Steps)

The OpenMP tasking model follows a strict **four-phase lifecycle**:

1. **Task Generation (Creation Phase)**
   - When a thread encounters `#pragma omp task`, the runtime creates a **task descriptor** containing the code, the data environment (shared variables, firstprivate copies), and scheduling hints.
   - The task is pushed into the **thread's task pool** (typically a LIFO deque for locality).

2. **Task Scheduling (Allocation Phase)**
   - Idle threads **steal work** from other threads' task pools (work-stealing scheduler).
   - The scheduler honors **dependencies** — a task is only eligible to run when its predecessors complete.

3. **Task Execution (Compute Phase)**
   - The bound thread runs the task's structured block.
   - The task can itself spawn **child tasks** (nested parallelism in task form).
   - At **task scheduling points** (implicit or explicit), the thread may switch tasks.

4. **Task Completion (Finalization Phase)**
   - The task's structured block finishes; the task descriptor is **destroyed**; its `taskwait` counter is **decremented**; dependent tasks are activated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Implicit Task Scheduling Points** in OpenMP occur at:
> - The end of the `task` construct (after generation).
> - Inside `taskwait` and `taskgroup` constructs.
> - At implicit barriers (unless `nowait` is specified).
> - At `#pragma omp taskyield`.

---

## 2.2 Task Data Environment (Memory Model)

Each task has its own **Data Environment**, established according to clauses:

| Clause | Variable Treatment |
|---|---|
| (default) | Variable inherits from the enclosing context |
| `shared(x)` | All tasks share the same storage of *x* |
| `firstprivate(x)` | Each task gets an **uninitialized copy** of *x* from the generating thread |
| `private(x)` | Each task gets an **uninitialized** local *x* (original is undefined after) |
| `reduction(operator: list)` | Performs reduction across all tasks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If no data-sharing clause is given, the **default behavior is determined by the enclosing `task` construct's `default(shared)`, `default(private)`, or `default(firstprivate)`**, and ultimately by the surrounding `parallel` region.

---

## 2.3 Task Dependencies — The DAG Model

The `depend` clause allows the programmer to express **precedence constraints** between tasks, which the runtime builds into a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**.

$$\text{Dependence Types: } \text{depend}(in:S), \ \text{depend}(out:S), \ \text{depend}(inout:S)$$

The runtime uses a **task dependency graph** where:
- **Edge (T₁ → T₂)** means *T₂ cannot start until T₁ completes*.
- The **Critical Path** determines the **lower bound on parallel execution time**:
  $$T_{\text{parallel}} \geq T_{\text{critical path}} = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{N_{\text{tasks}}} \cdot L_{\text{crit}}$$

where $L_{\text{crit}}$ is the cumulative work along the longest dependency chain.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU-board-testable** and appear frequently in Module-3 derivations. Use `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Amdahl's Law (Tasking Speedup)** | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - R_{tp}) + \dfrac{R_{tp}}{N}}$ | Max speedup with *N* threads; $R_{tp}$ = task-parallel fraction |
| 2 | **Task Granularity** | $G = \dfrac{T_{\text{task}}}{T_{\text{overhead}}}$ | Ratio of work per task to creation cost (target $\geq$ **100**) |
| 3 | **Cutoff Depth (Recursion)** | $d_{cut} = \lceil \log_{2} P \rceil$ | Recommended depth to stop spawning tasks (*P* = thread count) |
| 4 | **Parallel Execution Time (Ideal)** | $T_p = \dfrac{T_s}{N} + T_{\text{overhead}} \cdot N_{\text{tasks}}$ | $T_s$ = serial time, $N$ = # of processors |
| 5 | **Task Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{task}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{useful}} + T_{\text{overhead}}}$ | Should approach **1.0** for well-tuned tasking |
| 6 | **Critical Path Bound** | $T_p \geq T_{\text{CP}} = \sum_{i \in \text{crit}} w_i$ | Sum of weights along longest DAG path |
| 7 | **Load Imbalance Factor** | $\beta = \dfrac{T_{\text{max-thread}} - T_{\text{avg-thread}}}{T_{\text{avg-thread}}}$ | Goal: $\beta \rightarrow 0$ |
| 8 | **Work-Stealing Threshold** | $K_{\text{steal}} \geq 2 \cdot G$ | Minimum tasks per deque before stealing pays off |
| 9 | **Memory per Task (approx.)** | $M_{\text{task}} \approx 64 + 8 \cdot n_{\text{firstpriv}}$ bytes | Descriptor overhead; *n* = firstprivate vars |
| 10 | **Gustafson's Scaled Speedup** | $S'(N) = N - (N-1) \cdot (1 - R_{tp})$ | Speedup when problem size scales with *N* |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tasking is the **workhorse of modern HPC applications**:

- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD):** Adaptive mesh refinement spawns a new task for each sub-cell that needs refinement.
- **Sparse Linear Algebra (LU/QR Factorization):** Dependency DAGs map naturally to task dependencies.
- **Recursive N-Body Simulations (Barnes-Hut):** Octree traversal uses task recursion with cutoff.
- **AI / Deep Learning Graph Schedulers:** TensorFlow / PyTorch's graph executor is essentially a **task DAG scheduler**.
- **Cryptography & Branch-and-Bound Solvers:** Search tree exploration is a classic tasking use-case.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **Intel OpenMP runtime** (libiomp5) and **LLVM OpenMP** (libomp), the default scheduler is a **work-stealing scheduler**, which is what makes dynamic tasking scalable on many-core CPUs (e.g., 64+ cores on Xeon Phi, AMD EPYC).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Cutoff Depth for Recursive Tasking

**Problem Setup:** A recursive divide-and-conquer algorithm spawns 2 child tasks at each level. There are $P$ processors. Find the optimal cutoff depth $d_{cut}$ at which the recursion should stop spawning tasks and execute serially.

**Step 1 — Count the tasks generated.**

At depth $d$, the recursion tree has $2^d$ leaves. The total number of generated tasks is:
$$N_{\text{tasks}}(d) = 2^{d+1} - 1$$
where the “-1” accounts for the root, and "+1" is the standard full-tree count.

**Step 2 — Total computation time per leaf.**

Let $T_{\text{leaf}}$ be the work at the cutoff. Total serial work is:
$$T_s = N_{\text{tasks}}(d_{cut}) \cdot T_{\text{leaf}}$$

**Step 3 — Ideal parallel time.**

With perfect load balancing across $P$ threads:
$$T_p = \frac{T_s}{P} + T_{\text{overhead}} \cdot N_{\text{tasks}}(d_{cut})$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$T_p = \frac{(2^{d+1} - 1) \cdot T_{\text{leaf}}}{P} + T_{\text{overhead}} \cdot (2^{d+1} - 1)$$

**Step 5 — Define Granularity.**

Let $G = \dfrac{T_{\text{leaf}}}{T_{\text{overhead}}}$. Then:
$$T_p = T_{\text{overhead}} \cdot (2^{d+1} - 1) \left[ \frac{G}{P} + 1 \right]$$

**Step 6 — Find the optimal $d_{cut}$ that minimizes $T_p$.**

For large $2^{d+1}$, the overhead term dominates when $G < P$. The optimal balance is achieved when:
$$2^{d_{cut}} \approx P$$

Taking $\log_2$ on both sides:
$$d_{cut} \approx \log_2 P$$

**Step 7 — Final boxed result.**

$$\boxed{\ d_{cut} = \lceil \log_2 P \rceil\ }$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** For a 16-core machine, stop spawning new tasks at recursion depth $\lceil \log_2 16 \rceil = 4$. Going deeper creates more tasks than cores, which is wasteful overhead. Going shallower underutilizes the cores.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Task Parallelism Speedup Using Amdahl's Law

**Problem Setup:** A program has a serial portion of fraction $(1 - R_{tp})$ and a task-parallel portion of fraction $R_{tp}$ that uses $N$ threads.

**Step 1 — Serial execution time.**
$$T_s = T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{task}}$$

**Step 2 — Parallel execution time.**
$$T_p = T_{\text{serial}} + \frac{T_{\text{task}}}{N} + T_{\text{overhead}} \cdot N_{\text{tasks}}$$

**Step 3 — Define $R_{tp}$.**
$$R_{tp} = \frac{T_{\text{task}}}{T_s}$$

**Step 4 — Speedup definition.**
$$S(N) = \frac{T_s}{T_p}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute, assuming negligible overhead first.**
$$S(N) = \frac{T_s}{T_{\text{serial}} + \dfrac{T_{\text{task}}}{N}} = \frac{1}{(1 - R_{tp}) + \dfrac{R_{tp}}{N}}$$

**Step 6 — Include task creation overhead $O$.**

Let $O = \dfrac{T_{\text{overhead}} \cdot N_{\text{tasks}}}{T_s}$. Then:
$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - R_{tp}) + \dfrac{R_{tp}}{N} + O}$$

**Step 7 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$.**
$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - R_{tp} + O}$$

**Step 8 — Final boxed result (with overhead).**

$$\boxed{\ S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - R_{tp}) + \dfrac{R_{tp}}{N} + O}\ }$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that **task creation overhead $O$ directly subtracts from the achievable speedup**, just like the serial fraction. This is why *coarse-grained tasks* (large $T_{\text{task}}$ per task) are critical for HPC performance.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Tasking vs. Loop Parallelism

| Performance Dimension | `#pragma omp parallel for` | `#pragma omp task` |
|---|---|---|
| **Workload Type** | Regular, loop-structured | Irregular, dynamic, recursive |
| **Scheduling** | Static / dynamic chunks | Work-stealing from deques |
| **Load Balance** | Depends on `schedule` clause | **Naturally dynamic** (good) |
| **Overhead** | Low per iteration | Higher per task (descriptor) |
| **Scalability on Many-Cores** | Limited (chunk imbalance) | **High** (work-stealing excels) |
| **Dependency Support** | None (loop iterations assumed independent) | **Native** via `depend()` |
| **Best for** | Dense matrix ops, vector kernels | Sparse algebra, N-body, search trees |
| **Cutoff / Granularity Control** | `schedule(static/dynamic/guided)` | `cutoff` depth, `if(false)` clauses |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Question:** A recursive Fibonacci program is parallelized using OpenMP tasks on a machine with **$P = 8$** cores. The serial work is $T_s = 100$ ms and task creation overhead is $T_o = 5 \,\mu s$ per task. Find the optimum cutoff depth.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1.** Using the formula derived in §3.1:
$$d_{cut} = \lceil \log_2 P \rceil = \lceil \log_2 8 \rceil = \lceil 3 \rceil = 3$$

**Step 2.** At cutoff depth 3, total tasks generated:
$$N_{\text{tasks}} = 2^{3+1} - 1 = 15$$

**Step 3.** Work per leaf (cutoff task):
$$T_{\text{leaf}} = \frac{T_s}{N_{\text{tasks}}} = \frac{100 \text{ ms}}{15} \approx 6.67 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4.** Total task-creation overhead:
$$T_{\text{overhead}} = N_{\text{tasks}} \cdot T_o = 15 \times 5 \,\mu s = 75 \,\mu s = 0.075 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5.** Ideal parallel time (perfect load balance):
$$T_p = \frac{T_s}{P} + T_{\text{overhead}} = \frac{100}{8} + 0.075 = 12.5 + 0.075 = 12.575 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6.** Achieved speedup:
$$S = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{100}{12.575} \approx 7.95$$

**Step 7.** Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{S}{P} = \frac{7.95}{8} \approx 99.4\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** With 8 cores and a correctly chosen cutoff depth, the speedup is **~7.95×** (efficiency **99.4%**), demonstrating that **tasking is near-optimal for recursive algorithms** when granularity is properly tuned.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Task Lifecycle State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> GENERATED : thread encounters omp task
    GENERATED --> SCHEDULED : pushed to task deque
    SCHEDULED --> READY : dependencies satisfied
    READY --> RUNNING : thread assigned
    RUNNING --> SUSPENDED : task scheduling point hit
    SUSPENDED --> RUNNING : resumed by same or untied thread
    RUNNING --> COMPLETED : structured block finishes
    COMPLETED --> [*] : descriptor destroyed and counters decremented
    READY --> BLOCKED : depend not satisfied yet
    BLOCKED --> READY : predecessor task completes
```

**Block-Level Interpretation of the Diagram:**

- **GENERATED** — The runtime has allocated a task descriptor in the parent's task pool.
- **SCHEDULED** — The task descriptor is sitting in a thread's LIFO deque, ready to be picked up.
- **READY** — All `depend` clauses are satisfied; the task is eligible to run.
- **RUNNING** — A thread is actively executing the task's code.
- **SUSPENDED** — The task hit a scheduling point (e.g., `taskwait`) and was paused.
- **BLOCKED** — A predecessor task has not yet produced the required data.
- **COMPLETED** — The task is finished; dependent tasks are unblocked.

---

## 4.2 Task Dependency DAG for a Sparse Matrix Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task A: read matrix blocks] --> B[Task B: compute block 0 0]
    A --> C[Task C: compute block 0 1]
    A --> D[Task D: compute block 1 0]
    A --> E[Task E: compute block 1 1]
    B --> F[Task F: combine block row 0]
    C --> F
    D --> G[Task G: combine block row 1]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Task H: write final result]
    G --> H
```

**Engineering Reading of the DAG:**

- All four compute tasks (B, C, D, E) are **independent** — they can run in parallel.
- F depends on B and C (`depend(in: B_out, C_out)`).
- G depends on D and E.
- H depends on both F and G.
- The **critical path** is `A → B → F → H` (or equivalently `A → D → G → H`), so:
  $$T_p \geq T_A + T_B + T_F + T_H$$

---

## 4.3 Recursive Task Spawning with Cutoff (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Root Task at depth 0] --> L1A[Left Child at depth 1]
    R --> R1B[Right Child at depth 1]
    L1A --> L2A[Left Grandchild at depth 2]
    L1A --> R2A[Right Grandchild at depth 2]
    R1B --> L2B[Left Grandchild at depth 2]
    R1B --> R2B[Right Grandchild at depth 2]
    L2A --> S1[Serial Execution at depth dcut]
    R2A --> S2[Serial Execution at depth dcut]
    L2B --> S3[Serial Execution at depth dcut]
    R2B --> S4[Serial Execution at depth dcut]
```

**Block-Level Interpretation:**

- The recursion tree **branches** at each level until depth $d_{cut}$.
- At $d_{cut}$, the algorithm **switches to serial execution** to avoid overhead.
- The number of leaves at $d_{cut}$ is $2^{d_{cut}}$, and with $P$ cores, we set $d_{cut} = \lceil \log_2 P \rceil$.

---

## 4.4 Work-Stealing Task Scheduler (Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1 [Thread 1]
        D1[Local Deque]
    end
    subgraph T2 [Thread 2]
        D2[Local Deque]
    end
    subgraph T3 [Thread 3]
        D3[Local Deque]
    end
    subgraph T4 [Thread 4]
        D4[Local Deque]
    end
    R[Runtime Scheduler] --> D1
    R --> D2
    R --> D3
    R --> D4
    D1 -.steal.-> D2
    D2 -.steal.-> D3
    D3 -.steal.-> D4
    D4 -.steal.-> D1
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- Each thread maintains a **LIFO local deque** of tasks it has generated.
- When a thread's deque empties, it **steals** tasks from other threads' deques (FIFO from the *bottom* to reduce contention with the owner's LIFO push/pop from the *top*).
- This is the **canonical scheduler** in modern OpenMP runtimes (Intel, GNU libgomp, LLVM libomp).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define an OpenMP task. List any two clauses that can be used with the `omp task` construct.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> An OpenMP task is a unit of work generated dynamically when a thread encounters a `#pragma omp task` construct. Each task has its own data environment and is scheduled by the OpenMP runtime onto an available thread.

**Two clauses (1.5 marks each):**

1. **`depend(type: var)`** — specifies data dependencies between tasks (e.g., `in`, `out`, `inout`).
2. **`final(expr)`** — when `expr` evaluates to true, the task becomes a final task whose child tasks are executed inline by the same thread, avoiding task creation overhead.

*(Valid alternatives: `if`, `private`, `firstprivate`, `shared`, `untied`, `priority`, `mergeable`.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is the role of the `taskwait` directive? How does it differ from a barrier?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The `taskwait` directive synchronizes the current task with **only its immediate child tasks**, ensuring they all complete before execution proceeds past the directive. **(1.5 marks)**

> In contrast, a **barrier** (`#pragma omp barrier`) synchronizes **all threads** in the current team. (1 mark) Additionally, `taskwait` can have an explicit `depend` list to wait for only a subset of children. **(0.5 marks)**

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the OpenMP tasking model in detail, including task generation, scheduling, and completion. Discuss the significance of the `depend` clause. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Task Generation (2 marks):**
A task is created when any thread in a parallel region encounters the `omp task` construct. The OpenMP runtime allocates a *task descriptor* containing:
- The code (a pointer to the structured block).
- The data environment (shared, private, firstprivate variables).
- Scheduling information (`depend`, `final`, `priority`, etc.).

The new task is pushed into the generating thread's **task deque** (LIFO structure for cache locality).

**2. Task Scheduling (2.5 marks):**
Modern OpenMP uses a **work-stealing scheduler**:
- A thread that becomes idle (its deque is empty) **steals** the oldest task from the *bottom* of another thread's deque.
- The scheduler respects **dependency constraints** — a task with `depend(in: x)` runs only after the producer of *x* completes.
- For `final(expr) == true` tasks, the runtime **inlines** the task (executes immediately on the same thread), avoiding scheduler overhead.

**3. Task Completion (1 mark):**
Upon finishing, the task descriptor is destroyed, and the **completion counter** of the parent task is decremented. If the parent was blocked in `taskwait`, it may now resume.

**4. Significance of `depend` clause (1.5 marks):**
- Expresses **producer-consumer** relationships between tasks.
- Builds an internal **DAG** that the runtime schedules.
- Avoids **race conditions** on shared data without explicit locks.
- Three types: `in` (read), `out` (write), `inout` (read-modify-write).
- Enables **fine-grained synchronization** more efficient than coarse barriers.

---

#### (b) Consider a recursive divide-and-conquer algorithm parallelized using OpenMP tasks on a 16-core processor. Derive the optimal cutoff depth and compute the parallel execution time and speedup. Assume the serial runtime is $T_s = 256$ ms and task creation overhead is $T_o = 10\,\mu s$ per task. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Optimal Cutoff Depth (2 marks):**

Using the derived formula $d_{cut} = \lceil \log_2 P \rceil$:
$$d_{cut} = \lceil \log_2 16 \rceil = 4$$

**Step 2 — Number of Tasks (1.5 marks):**

For a binary recursion at depth 4:
$$N_{\text{tasks}} = 2^{4+1} - 1 = 31$$

**Step 3 — Work per Leaf (1 mark):**
$$T_{\text{leaf}} = \frac{T_s}{N_{\text{tasks}}} = \frac{256 \text{ ms}}{31} \approx 8.258 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Total Overhead (1 mark):**
$$T_{\text{overhead}} = N_{\text{tasks}} \cdot T_o = 31 \times 10\,\mu s = 310\,\mu s = 0.310 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Parallel Time & Speedup (1.5 marks):**
$$T_p = \frac{T_s}{P} + T_{\text{overhead}} = \frac{256}{16} + 0.310 = 16.0 + 0.310 = 16.31 \text{ ms}$$

$$S = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{256}{16.31} \approx 15.7$$

$$\eta = \frac{S}{P} = \frac{15.7}{16} \approx 98.1\%$$

**Conclusion:** A cutoff depth of **4** yields a near-ideal speedup of **15.7×** with **98.1% efficiency** on 16 cores.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### (a) Discuss the differences between the OpenMP *task* model and the *parallel for* model. When would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | `parallel for` | `task` |
|---|---|---|
| Parallelism source | Loop iterations | Dynamically generated units |
| Workload | Regular, predictable | Irregular, recursive, dynamic |
| Scheduling | `schedule(static/dynamic/guided/runtime)` | Work-stealing from deques |
| Load balance | Static split can be imbalanced | Naturally balanced |
| Dependencies | Not expressed | Expressed via `depend()` |
| Overhead | Low (loop pre-computed) | Higher (descriptor per task) |

**1. When to use `parallel for` (2 marks):**
- Dense matrix operations, vector arithmetic.
- When loop count is known and iterations are independent.
- When the iteration time is roughly uniform.

**2. When to use `task` (2 marks):**
- Recursive algorithms (Fibonacci, Quicksort, FFT, N-body).
- Sparse data structures (linked lists, trees, graphs).
- When work is generated dynamically (e.g., branch-and-bound).
- Pipeline / DAG-structured workflows.

**3. Hybrid use (1.5 marks):**
A common HPC pattern is `#pragma omp parallel` containing a `taskloop` or `parallel for` with task-internal substructures — combining the best of both models.

**4. Code example for clarity (1.5 marks):**

```c
#pragma omp parallel
{
    #pragma omp single
    {
        process_chunk(0, N/2);
        #pragma omp task
        process_chunk(N/2, N);
        #pragma omp taskwait
    }
}
```

This pattern is **idiomatic OpenMP tasking** — only one thread generates tasks, and the rest steal from its deque.

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of **task granularity** and the **cutoff strategy** in OpenMP tasking. Using Amdahl's Law, derive the maximum achievable speedup when $R_{tp} = 0.92$ and overhead $O = 0.03$ on $N = 32$ cores. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Task Granularity (2 marks):**
Task granularity $G = T_{\text{task}} / T_{\text{overhead}}$ is the ratio of useful work to creation overhead.
- $G < 1$: tasks are too small; overhead dominates — **bad**.
- $G \gg 1$: tasks are too large; load imbalance and poor scalability — **also bad**.
- **Optimal:** $G \approx 100$ to $1000$.

**2. Cutoff Strategy (1.5 marks):**
For recursive algorithms, **stop spawning tasks** at depth $d_{cut} = \lceil \log_2 P \rceil$ and execute serially. This bounds the number of tasks to roughly the number of cores, minimizing overhead.

**3. Amdahl's Law Derivation (2 marks):**

Start with:
$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - R_{tp}) + \dfrac{R_{tp}}{N} + O}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute** $R_{tp} = 0.92$, $N = 32$, $O = 0.03$:
$$S(32) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.92) + \dfrac{0.92}{32} + 0.03}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each term:**
$$1 - 0.92 = 0.08, \quad \frac{0.92}{32} = 0.02875$$

$$S(32) = \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.02875 + 0.03}$$

**Step 3 — Sum denominator:**
$$0.08 + 0.02875 + 0.03 = 0.13875$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**
$$\boxed{S(32) = \frac{1}{0.13875} \approx 7.21}$$

**4. Analysis (1.5 marks):**
- Linear (ideal) speedup would be $32\times$.
- Achieved speedup is only $7.21\times$ → efficiency $\eta = 7.21/32 \approx 22.5\%$.
- **Bottleneck:** The serial fraction $1 - R_{tp} = 0.08$ caps the *ideal* speedup at $1/0.08 = 12.5\times$.
- **Recommendation:** Refactor the serial portion to expose more task parallelism (e.g., parallelize the I/O or graph setup phase) to push $R_{tp}$ closer to 1.0.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `taskwait` with `barrier`.** Many students write that `taskwait` synchronizes *all* threads. It does NOT — it only waits for *child tasks of the current task* to complete.
> 2. **Forgetting the "-1" in task count.** Recursive tasking has $2^{d+1} - 1$ total tasks, not $2^{d+1}$. Marks lost: 1–2 per question.
> 3. **Using `depend(in: x)` incorrectly.** Some students write `depend(read: x)` — there is no `read`; the correct keyword is `in`.
> 4. **Ignoring overhead in speedup derivations.** Always include the $O$ term in Amdahl's Law for task-based code; examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Setting cutoff depth = total recursion depth.** The cutoff is the depth at which you *stop* spawning, not the total recursion depth.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU Exams):**

- ⭐ **Task:** A dynamically generated unit of work with its own data environment; created by `#pragma omp task`.
- ⭐ **Task Pool / Deque:** Per-thread LIFO structure where new tasks are pushed; other threads *steal* from the bottom.
- ⭐ **Task Lifecycle States:** GENERATED → SCHEDULED → READY → RUNNING → COMPLETED (with SUSPENDED and BLOCKED as side states).
- ⭐ **Task Scheduling Points:** Implicit at end of `task`, inside `taskwait`/`taskgroup`, and at barriers.
- ⭐ **Key Directives:** `task`, `taskwait`, `taskgroup`, `taskloop`, `taskyield`.
- ⭐ **Key Clauses:** `depend(in/out/inout)`, `final()`, `untied`, `mergeable`, `priority()`, `if()`.
- ⭐ **Default Scheduling:** Work-stealing (deques), as opposed to chunk-based for loops.
- ⭐ **Optimal Cutoff Depth (Recursion):** $d_{cut} = \lceil \log_2 P \rceil$.
- ⭐ **Task Granularity Target:** $G = T_{\text{task}} / T_{\text{overhead}} \geq 100$ ideally.
- ⭐ **Amdahl's Law (with overhead):** $S(N) = 1 / \left[ (1 - R_{tp}) + (R_{tp}/N) + O \right]$.
- ⭐ **Gustafson's Law (alternative view):** $S'(N) = N - (N-1)(1 - R_{tp})$.
- ⭐ **Tasking is preferred for:** Recursion, sparse structures, irregular/divergent workloads, DAG-based computations.
- ⭐ **Loop parallelism is preferred for:** Dense, regular, loop-bound computations (e.g., BLAS kernels).
- ⭐ **Critical Path Lower Bound:** $T_p \geq$ sum of weights along the longest dependency chain in the task DAG.
- ⭐ **Final Clause Behavior:** When true, child tasks of this task are *inlined* (no further task creation).
- ⭐ **Untied Clause:** A suspended task may be resumed by a *different* thread (default: same thread only).
- ⭐ **Mergeable Clause:** The child task's data environment is shared with the parent (memory optimization).
- ⭐ **High-Yield Application Domains:** CFD (adaptive mesh), N-body (Barnes-Hut), sparse factorizations, branch-and-bound, deep learning graph schedulers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Miscellaneous
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Miscellaneous

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Miscellaneous HPC Performance Concepts

In High Performance Computing (HPC), beyond the core paradigms of parallelism, distributed systems, and GPU computing, there exists a **miscellaneous yet exam-critical** cluster of performance concepts. These concepts govern *how* we **measure**, *quantify*, and *predict* the behavior of parallel systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Insight:** The "Miscellaneous" module typically covers **Performance Metrics, Amdahl's Law, Gustafson's Law, Karp-Flatt Metric, Isoefficiency, Scalability Analysis, Load Balancing, and Heterogeneous Computing fundamentals**. These are heavily weighted in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

### 1.2 Core Performance Metrics — Formal Definitions

**Speedup ($S_p$):**
The ratio of the time taken to solve a problem on a single processor (sequential time) to the time taken to solve the *same problem* on a parallel system with $p$ processors.

$$S_p = \frac{T_s}{T_p}$$

Where $T_s$ is the **sequential execution time** and $T_p$ is the **parallel execution time**.

**Efficiency ($E_p$):**
The ratio of speedup to the number of processors, indicating how well the processors are utilized.

$$E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{T_s}{p \cdot T_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ideal case:** $S_p = p$ and $E_p = 1$ (linear speedup). In practice, $0 < E_p \le 1$.

**Cost ($C_p$):**
The product of the number of processors and the parallel execution time, representing the *total processor-time investment*.

$$C_p = p \cdot T_p$$

**Cost-Optimal (Cost-Efficient) System:**
A parallel system is **cost-optimal** if the cost of solving a problem on a parallel computer is **equal to** the cost of solving the same problem on a sequential computer.

$$C_p = \Theta(T_s)$$

---

### 1.3 Amdahl's Law — The Fundamental Limit

**Formal Definition:** Amdahl's Law states that the *maximum achievable speedup* of a parallel program is **limited by its sequential (non-parallelizable) portion**, regardless of how many processors are used.

$$S_{\text{max}}(p) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$$

Where:
- $f$ = fraction of the program that is **inherently sequential** (must run on one processor)
- $(1-f)$ = fraction that is **parallelizable**
- $p$ = number of processors

**Intuitive Analogy — The Toll Booth:** Imagine a highway with 100 toll booths. If **30% of the highway** is a single-lane bridge (sequential portion), then no matter how many booths you add, traffic will *always* bottleneck at that bridge. Even 1000 booths give you less than 3.3x speedup. This is Amdahl's Law in action.

---

### 1.4 Gustafson's Law — The Realistic Counterpoint

**Formal Definition:** Gustafson's Law observes that for **scaled problems** (where problem size grows with the number of processors), the sequential portion does *not* dominate.

$$S_{\text{Gustafson}}(p) = p - f \cdot (p - 1)$$

**Intuitive Analogy — The Expanding Workshop:** Instead of building 100 chairs with 100 workers (fixed task), give each worker *more work proportional to their number*. With 100 workers, build 1000 chairs. The overhead of "opening the workshop" (sequential portion) becomes negligible as the workshop scales up.

---

### 1.5 Karp-Flatt Metric — Diagnosing Parallelism

A metric that **identifies the source of inefficiency** in a parallel program.

$$e = \frac{\frac{1}{S_p} - \frac{1}{p}}{1 - \frac{1}{p}}$$

Where $e$ is the **serial fraction experimentally observed**. A *constant* $e$ across different $p$ values indicates inefficiency is due to **inherently sequential code**. A *decreasing* $e$ suggests **overhead-related inefficiency** (load imbalance, communication).

---

### 1.6 Scalability — Strong vs. Weak

| Type | Definition | Metric |
|------|------------|--------|
| **Strong Scaling** | Problem size $W$ is **fixed**; $p$ increases. | Speedup $S_p$ for constant $W$ |
| **Weak Scaling** | Problem size **grows proportionally** with $p$. | Speedup $S_p$ where $W = O(p)$ |

**Isoefficiency:** A metric showing how efficiently a parallel algorithm scales as both problem size and number of processors increase.

---

### 1.7 Heterogeneous Computing — A Modern Paradigm

**Formal Definition:** A computing model that uses **different types of computational units** (CPUs, GPUs, FPGAs, DSPs) cooperatively, each handling tasks best suited to its architecture.

**Intuitive Analogy — The Construction Site:** The **CPU** (foreman) handles complex planning, branching decisions, and sequential tasks. The **GPU** (labor crew) handles massive repetitive tasks (e.g., millions of pixel operations). Together, they build a skyscraper far faster than either alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Context:** Heterogeneous computing is the foundation of modern HPC platforms (e.g., **CUDA**, **OpenCL**, **Heterogeneous System Architecture - HSA**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive: Amdahl's Law — Structural Analysis

The derivation of Amdahl's Law assumes a problem with a **sequential fraction $f$** and a **parallel fraction $(1-f)$**. The total execution time on a single processor is normalized to **1 unit**.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Sequential portion execution time:** $T_{\text{seq}} = f \cdot 1 = f$ (cannot be reduced, runs on 1 processor)
2. **Parallel portion execution time on $p$ processors:** $T_{\text{par}} = \frac{1-f}{p}$ (parallelizable work divided among $p$ processors)
3. **Total parallel execution time:** $T_p = f + \frac{1-f}{p}$
4. **Speedup:** $S_p = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$

**The 'Why' behind each step:**

- The sequential fraction $f$ is **atomic** — it cannot benefit from parallelism, so its contribution remains $f$ regardless of $p$.
- The parallel fraction $(1-f)$ is **divided equally** across $p$ processors (ideal load balance assumption).
- As $p \to \infty$, the term $\frac{1-f}{p} \to 0$, giving $S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{f}$.

**Engineering Utility:** Amdahl's Law guides **code optimization strategy** — it tells designers to focus efforts on reducing the sequential bottleneck. A program with $f = 0.05$ (5% sequential) has a maximum speedup of **20x** even on infinite processors.

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Gustafson's Law — Scaled Workload Analysis

Gustafson's Law **reframes the problem**: instead of solving a *fixed* problem faster, we solve a **larger problem** in the *same wall-clock time*.

**Operational logic:**

1. Let the **scaled parallel runtime** be $T_p = a + b$, where $a$ = sequential time, $b$ = parallel time on 1 processor.
2. On $p$ processors, the parallel portion runs as $\frac{b}{p}$, so total time becomes: $T_p' = a + \frac{b}{p}$
3. For the **scaled workload** (problem size grows with $p$), the sequential time is $a$ and parallel time is $b \cdot p$ (since $p$ times more work is done).
4. Speedup: $S_{\text{Gustafson}} = \frac{a + b \cdot p}{a + b}$
5. Letting $f = \frac{a}{a+b}$ (serial fraction), we obtain: $S_{\text{Gustafson}} = p - f(p-1)$

**The 'Why':** Gustafson observed that real HPC users don't run a fixed problem on more machines — they **run larger problems** to leverage the additional compute. In this regime, the serial fraction becomes a *small overhead*, not a *limiting bottleneck*.

---

## 2.3 Deep Dive: Karp-Flatt Metric — Diagnostic Analysis

The Karp-Flatt metric **exposes hidden inefficiencies** that Amdahl's Law alone cannot detect.

**Derivation logic:**

1. Rearrange Amdahl's formula: $\frac{1}{S_p} = f + \frac{(1-f)}{p}$
2. Solve for $f$ (the experimentally observed serial fraction $e$):

$$e = \frac{\frac{1}{S_p} - \frac{1}{p}}{1 - \frac{1}{p}}$$

3. **Interpretation guide:**
   - If $e$ is **constant** as $p$ varies → serial code is the bottleneck → **optimize sequential code**
   - If $e$ **decreases** as $p$ increases → overhead per processor (communication, synchronization) dominates → **optimize parallel overhead**
   - If $e$ **increases** as $p$ increases → load imbalance or memory contention → **improve load balancing**

**Engineering Utility:** Used in tools like **TAU (Tuning and Analysis Utilities)** and **Scalasca** for parallel performance analysis on systems like the **PARAM** series (India's national HPC).

---

## 2.4 Deep Dive: Isoefficiency Analysis

**Definition:** Isoefficiency $\Theta(p)$ is the **rate at which problem size $W$ must grow** with $p$ to maintain a fixed efficiency $E$.

$$W = K \cdot \frac{T_o(p)}{1 - E}$$

Where $K$ is a constant, $T_o(p)$ is the **total overhead** of parallel execution, and $E$ is the target efficiency.

**Interpretation:**
- **Low isoefficiency slope** → highly scalable algorithm
- **High isoefficiency slope** → poorly scalable algorithm (requires exponential $W$ growth to keep efficiency constant)

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | KTU Use Case |
|---|---------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | $S_p = \frac{T_s}{T_p}$ | Speedup definition | Direct calculation |
| 2 | $E_p = \frac{S_p}{p}$ | Efficiency definition | Direct calculation |
| 3 | $C_p = p \cdot T_p$ | Cost (processor-time product) | Cost-optimality check |
| 4 | $S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$ | Amdahl's Law | Max speedup estimation |
| 5 | $\lim_{p \to \infty} S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{f}$ | Amdahl's upper bound | Theoretical limit |
| 6 | $S_{\text{Gustafson}} = p - f(p-1)$ | Gustafson's Law | Scaled workload |
| 7 | $e = \frac{\frac{1}{S_p} - \frac{1}{p}}{1 - \frac{1}{p}}$ | Karp-Flatt metric | Diagnose inefficiency |
| 8 | $W = K \cdot \frac{T_o(p)}{1 - E}$ | Isoefficiency | Scalability analysis |
| 9 | $\text{Flops} = \frac{\text{Operations}}{T_p}$ | Throughput / Performance | Benchmarking |
| 10 | $T_{\text{comm}} = T_{\text{startup}} + n \cdot T_{\text{data}}$ | Communication time model | MPI/OpenMP analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For 14-mark Part B questions, **always** quote the formula first, define each variable in your own words, then plug in numerical values. A clear 3-step structure (formula → substitution → result) fetches full marks.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Industry Application |
|---------|---------------------|
| **Amdahl's Law** | CPU multi-core design, GPU shader core allocation, compiler auto-parallelization limits |
| **Gustafson's Law** | Exascale computing (frontier, Fugaku), Big Data analytics scaling |
| **Karp-Flatt** | Performance debugging in MPI applications, HPC cluster optimization |
| **Isoefficiency** | Algorithm design for petascale systems (CRAY, PARAM Siddhi) |
| **Heterogeneous Computing** | Mobile SoCs (Snapdragon), AI accelerators (TPU, NVIDIA Grace-Hopper) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Amdahl's Law

**Given:** A parallel program with sequential fraction $f$ and parallel fraction $(1-f)$, executed on $p$ processors. Sequential runtime is $T_s$.

**Step 1: Decompose the sequential runtime.**

The total sequential runtime is partitioned into:
- Sequential portion: $T_{\text{seq}} = f \cdot T_s$
- Parallel portion: $T_{\text{par}} = (1-f) \cdot T_s$

**Step 2: Analyze parallel execution on $p$ processors.**

The sequential portion **cannot be parallelized**, so it remains:
$$T_{\text{seq, parallel}} = f \cdot T_s$$

The parallel portion is **divided among $p$ processors** (assuming perfect load balance):
$$T_{\text{par, parallel}} = \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}$$

**Step 3: Sum the two components to get total parallel execution time $T_p$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_p &= T_{\text{seq, parallel}} + T_{\text{par, parallel}} \\
&= f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p} \\
&= T_s \cdot \left( f + \frac{1-f}{p} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic note:* We assume the sequential portion remains unchanged because splitting a single thread across multiple processors introduces synchronization overhead that often negates any gains — hence it is treated as atomic.

**Step 4: Apply the speedup definition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_p &= \frac{T_s}{T_p} \\
&= \frac{T_s}{T_s \cdot \left( f + \frac{1-f}{p} \right)} \\
&= \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic note:* $T_s$ cancels out cleanly, leaving a dimensionless speedup ratio that depends only on $f$ and $p$.

**Step 5: Compute the asymptotic limit as $p \to \infty$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{p \to \infty} S_p &= \lim_{p \to \infty} \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}} \\
&= \frac{1}{f + 0} \\
&= \frac{1}{f}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic note:* As $p$ grows infinitely large, the parallel portion's time contribution vanishes, leaving only the sequential bottleneck as the *hard ceiling* on speedup.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Gustafson's Law

**Given:** A problem with serial time $a$ and parallel time $b$ on a *single* processor. The scaled workload is executed on $p$ processors.

**Step 1: Express sequential runtime on 1 processor.**

$$T_s = a + b$$

*Logic note:* $a$ is the fixed setup cost, $b$ is the parallelizable work on one processor.

**Step 2: Express parallel runtime on $p$ processors.**

The serial portion remains $a$ (cannot be split). The parallel portion is divided across $p$ processors:
$$T_p = a + \frac{b}{p}$$

*Logic note:* This is the runtime to solve the *original* problem on $p$ processors.

**Step 3: Model the *scaled* problem on $p$ processors.**

The user keeps the runtime fixed at $a + b$ (the original sequential time) but **increases the parallel work** to fill $p$ processors. The new parallel work is $b \cdot p$ (proportional to processors).

$$T_{\text{scaled, p}} = a + b$$

*Logic note:* The user solves a problem $p$ times larger in the same wall-clock time.

**Step 4: Compute the scaled speedup.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Gustafson}} &= \frac{\text{Work done on } p \text{ processors in time } T_p}{\text{Work done on 1 processor in time } T_s} \\
&= \frac{a + b \cdot p}{a + b}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic note:* Numerator is the *scaled* workload; denominator is the *original* workload.

**Step 5: Express in terms of serial fraction $f$.**

Let $f = \frac{a}{a+b}$ be the serial fraction. Then:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{Gustafson}} &= \frac{a + b \cdot p}{a + b} \\
&= \frac{a}{a+b} + \frac{b \cdot p}{a+b} \\
&= f + (1-f) \cdot p \\
&= p - f \cdot (p - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic note:* This is the canonical form tested in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Example: Speedup & Efficiency Calculation

**Problem [KTU Pattern]:** A parallel program takes $T_s = 100$ seconds on a single processor. The serial fraction is $f = 0.10$ (10% sequential). Calculate the speedup, efficiency, and cost for $p = 4$, $p = 16$, and $p = 64$.

**Step 1: Apply Amdahl's Law for $p = 4$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_4 &= \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{1-0.10}{4}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{4}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.225} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.325} \\
&\approx 3.077
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic note:* With 4 processors and 10% serial code, we get ~3x speedup (not 4x) due to the sequential bottleneck.

**Step 2: Efficiency for $p = 4$.**

$$E_4 = \frac{S_4}{4} = \frac{3.077}{4} \approx 0.769 \text{ or } 76.9\%$$

**Step 3: Repeat for $p = 16$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{16} &= \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{16}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.05625} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.15625} \\
&= 6.40
\end{aligned}
$$

$$E_{16} = \frac{6.40}{16} = 0.40 \text{ or } 40\%$$

**Step 4: Repeat for $p = 64$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{64} &= \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{64}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.01406} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.11406} \\
&\approx 8.768
\end{aligned}
$$

$$E_{64} = \frac{8.768}{64} \approx 0.137 \text{ or } 13.7\%$$

**Step 5: Compare with Gustafson's Law (scaled workload).**

For $p = 64$, $f = 0.10$:
$$S_{\text{Gustafson}} = 64 - 0.10 \cdot (64 - 1) = 64 - 6.3 = 57.7$$

*Logic note:* Gustafson's Law shows that for **scaled problems**, 64 processors can yield ~57.7x speedup, while Amdahl's Law (fixed problem) caps at ~8.77x. This contrast is a **favourite KTU question**.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Karp-Flatt Metric Calculation

**Given:** Observed speedups: $S_4 = 3.077$, $S_{16} = 6.40$, $S_{64} = 8.768$.

**For $p = 16$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e &= \frac{\frac{1}{S_{16}} - \frac{1}{16}}{1 - \frac{1}{16}} \\
&= \frac{\frac{1}{6.40} - 0.0625}{1 - 0.0625} \\
&= \frac{0.15625 - 0.0625}{0.9375} \\
&= \frac{0.09375}{0.9375} \\
&= 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic note:* The Karp-Flatt serial fraction $e = 0.10$ matches our assumption $f = 0.10$, confirming serial code is the dominant inefficiency source.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Performance Law Comparison

| Aspect | Amdahl's Law | Gustafson's Law | Karp-Flatt Metric |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Problem Size** | Fixed (W = constant) | Scaled (W grows with p) | Fixed (diagnostic for fixed W) |
| **Speedup Upper Bound** | $S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{f}$ | $S_{\text{max}} = p$ (linear) | N/A (diagnostic) |
| **Key Insight** | Sequential code is the bottleneck | Sequential cost amortized over larger work | Identifies source of inefficiency |
| **Best Use** | Predicting limits of parallelism | Justifying HPC investment | Debugging parallel programs |
| **Assumption** | Perfect load balance, no overhead | Constant serial runtime | Experimentally measured $S_p$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: HPC Performance Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: HPC Application] --> B{Measured Runtime Ts Tp}
    B --> C[Compute Speedup Sp = Ts / Tp]
    C --> D{Is Sp approximately equal to p?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Linear Speedup: Ideal Parallelism]
    D -- No --> F[Compute Efficiency Ep = Sp / p]
    F --> G{Is Efficiency greater than 0.5?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Good Scalability]
    G -- No --> I[Apply Karp-Flatt Metric]
    I --> J[Compute Serial Fraction e]
    J --> K{Is e constant as p varies?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Inherently Sequential Code: Optimize f]
    K -- No --> M[Overhead Dominant: Optimize Communication]
    L --> N[Apply Amdahl Limit: Smax = 1/f]
    M --> O[Apply Gustafson Law: Sgust = p - f p-1]
    N --> P[End: Performance Report]
    O --> P
    H --> P
    E --> P
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Heterogeneous HPC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Host Processor] --> PCIe[PCIe Bus]
    PCIe --> GPU1[GPU Device 0]
    PCIe --> GPU2[GPU Device 1]
    PCIe --> FPGA[FPGA Accelerator]
    CPU --> DDR[Host DDR Memory]
    GPU1 --> VRAM1[Device VRAM 0]
    GPU2 --> VRAM2[Device VRAM 1]
    FPGA --> BRAM[Block RAM]
    CPU -- Control Commands --> GPU1
    CPU -- Data Transfer --> DDR
    GPU1 -- Kernel Execution --> VRAM1
    GPU2 -- Parallel Compute --> VRAM2
    FPGA -- Custom Logic --> BRAM
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Speedup Curves Comparison (Amdahl vs. Gustafson)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Amdahl Curve
    A1[Fixed problem: f = 0.10] --> A2[S4 approximately 3.08]
    A1 --> A3[S16 approximately 6.40]
    A1 --> A4[S64 approximately 8.77]
    A1 --> A5[Smax = 1/f = 10x]
    end
    subgraph Gustafson Curve
    G1[Scaled problem: f = 0.10] --> G2[S4 approximately 3.7]
    G1 --> G3[S16 approximately 14.5]
    G1 --> G4[S64 approximately 57.7]
    G1 --> G5[Linear growth: Sp = p]
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Karp-Flatt Diagnostic Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Run Parallel Program on varying p] --> Measure[Measure Speedup Sp for each p]
    Measure --> Compute[Compute Karp-Flatt e for each p]
    Compute --> Check{Trend of e vs p}
    Check -- Constant --> Serial[Serial code dominant: Refactor sequential parts]
    Check -- Decreasing --> Overhead[Parallel overhead dominant: Reduce communication]
    Check -- Increasing --> Load[Load imbalance dominant: Improve task distribution]
    Serial --> Optimize1[Apply Amdahl bound analysis]
    Overhead --> Optimize2[Profile MPI and OpenMP regions]
    Load --> Optimize3[Apply dynamic load balancing]
    Optimize1 --> Result[Optimization complete]
    Optimize2 --> Result
    Optimize3 --> Result
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define Amdahl's Law. State its significance in parallel computing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> Amdahl's Law, formulated by **Gene Amdahl in 1967**, states that the maximum speedup $S_p$ achievable by a parallel program on $p$ processors is given by:
> $$S_p = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$$
> where $f$ is the **fraction of the program that is inherently sequential** and $(1-f)$ is the parallelizable fraction.
>
> **Significance (2 marks):**
> 1. It establishes a **theoretical upper bound** on the speedup of any parallel program, highlighting that the sequential portion becomes the dominant bottleneck as the number of processors increases.
> 2. It guides **code optimization strategy** — designers must minimize the serial fraction $f$ to extract maximum performance from parallel hardware.
> 3. As $p \to \infty$, $S_p \to \frac{1}{f}$, demonstrating that **infinite processors cannot overcome sequential bottlenecks**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Distinguish between strong scaling and weak scaling in HPC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Strong Scaling | Weak Scaling |
|--------|----------------|--------------|
| **Problem size** | Fixed ($W$ = constant) | Grows with $p$ ($W = O(p)$) |
| **Metric** | Speedup $S_p$ for constant workload | Speedup $S_p$ for scaled workload |
| **Goal** | Solve fixed problem *faster* | Solve *larger* problem in same time |
| **Law governing** | Amdahl's Law | Gustafson's Law |
| **Practical use** | Benchmarking fixed-size simulations | Exascale data analytics, Big Data |

> **Conclusion:** Strong scaling tests *latency reduction*; weak scaling tests *throughput scalability*. Both are essential for evaluating HPC systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** Derive Amdahl's Law for parallel speedup. What is the maximum speedup achievable when the serial fraction is 5%? Comment on the practical implications.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let the **sequential execution time** of a program be $T_s = 1$ (normalized). Let $f$ be the **serial fraction** and $(1-f)$ be the **parallel fraction**.

The parallel execution time on $p$ processors is:
$$T_p = f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p} = T_s \cdot \left( f + \frac{1-f}{p} \right)$$

The speedup is:
$$S_p = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$$

**Maximum speedup calculation (2 marks):**

For $f = 0.05$:
$$S_{\text{max}} = \lim_{p \to \infty} S_p = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20$$

**Practical implications (1 mark):**
Even with **infinite processors**, the speedup cannot exceed 20x due to the 5% sequential code. This means HPC architects must aggressively **refactor serial code paths** (e.g., replace sequential I/O with parallel I/O, eliminate global locks).

---

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** A parallel program achieves the following speedups on 2, 4, 8, and 16 processors: $S_2 = 1.80$, $S_4 = 2.91$, $S_8 = 4.00$, $S_{16} = 5.06$. Calculate the Karp-Flatt metric for each case. Identify the dominant source of inefficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**Formula (1 mark):** $e = \frac{\frac{1}{S_p} - \frac{1}{p}}{1 - \frac{1}{p}}$

**Calculations (4 marks):**

For $p = 2$:
$$e_2 = \frac{\frac{1}{1.80} - 0.5}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{0.5556 - 0.5}{0.5} = \frac{0.0556}{0.5} = 0.1111$$

For $p = 4$:
$$e_4 = \frac{\frac{1}{2.91} - 0.25}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{0.3436 - 0.25}{0.75} = \frac{0.0936}{0.75} = 0.1248$$

For $p = 8$:
$$e_8 = \frac{\frac{1}{4.00} - 0.125}{1 - 0.125} = \frac{0.25 - 0.125}{0.875} = \frac{0.125}{0.875} = 0.1429$$

For $p = 16$:
$$e_{16} = \frac{\frac{1}{5.06} - 0.0625}{1 - 0.0625} = \frac{0.1976 - 0.0625}{0.9375} = \frac{0.1351}{0.9375} = 0.1441$$

**Analysis (2 marks):**

| $p$ | Karp-Flatt $e$ |
|-----|----------------|
| 2 | 0.1111 |
| 4 | 0.1248 |
| 8 | 0.1429 |
| 16 | 0.1441 |

**Trend observation:** The serial fraction $e$ is *approximately constant* (~0.12 to 0.14) as $p$ increases. This indicates that the **inherently sequential code** is the dominant source of inefficiency. The program should be optimized by **reducing the serial fraction** (e.g., parallelizing sequential loops, reducing I/O bottlenecks, replacing serial reductions with parallel reductions).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute Karp-Flatt for only one value of $p$ and stop. To fetch full marks, you **MUST** compute $e$ for *at least three* values of $p$ and explicitly state whether the trend is **constant, increasing, or decreasing** to identify the bottleneck source.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q (a) [7 marks]:** Explain Gustafson's Law. For a parallel program with serial fraction $f = 0.04$ running on $p = 32$ processors, calculate the speedup. How does it differ from Amdahl's Law in interpretation?

**Model Solution:**

**Gustafson's Law Explanation (3 marks):**

Gustafson's Law, proposed by **John Gustafson in 1988**, addresses the limitation of Amdahl's Law by considering **scaled workloads** — problems whose size *grows proportionally* with the number of processors.

**Mathematical formulation (2 marks):**

$$S_{\text{Gustafson}}(p) = p - f \cdot (p - 1)$$

**Numerical calculation (1 mark):**

For $f = 0.04$, $p = 32$:
$$S_{\text{Gustafson}} = 32 - 0.04 \cdot (32 - 1) = 32 - 0.04 \cdot 31 = 32 - 1.24 = 30.76$$

**Comparison with Amdahl (1 mark):**

For the *same* parameters under Amdahl's Law (fixed problem):
$$S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{0.04 + \frac{0.96}{32}} = \frac{1}{0.04 + 0.03} = \frac{1}{0.07} \approx 14.29$$

Thus, Amdahl predicts **14.29x** (fixed problem) while Gustafson predicts **30.76x** (scaled problem). Gustafson's Law is more **realistic for HPC** because users typically increase problem size to leverage more processors.

---

**Q (b) [7 marks]:** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a heterogeneous computing system. Discuss the role of CPU and GPU in such systems with an example.

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture Diagram Description (2 marks):**

A heterogeneous computing system consists of:
- A **CPU (host)** managing control flow, sequential tasks, and task scheduling.
- One or more **accelerators** (GPUs, FPGAs, DSPs) handling data-parallel, compute-intensive workloads.
- A **high-bandwidth interconnect** (PCIe, NVLink) enabling data transfer.
- **Separate memory hierarchies** — host DDR memory and device VRAM.

**Roles explained (3 marks):**

**CPU Role (Host):**
- Executes the **sequential portion** of the program.
- Manages **memory allocation** and **data transfer** to accelerators.
- Handles **branching, I/O, and OS services**.
- Schedules **kernel launches** on accelerators.

**GPU Role (Device):**
- Executes **data-parallel kernels** (e.g., SIMD instructions).
- Performs **massive floating-point operations** (e.g., matrix multiplication, convolutions).
- Has **thousands of simple cores** optimized for throughput.
- Operates on its own **global memory (VRAM)** with high bandwidth.

**Example: Deep Learning Training (2 marks):**

In training a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) on **CUDA-enabled hardware**:
1. The **CPU** loads the dataset, performs data augmentation, and feeds mini-batches to the GPU.
2. The **GPU** executes the **forward pass** (matrix multiplications, convolutions) using cuDNN libraries.
3. The **GPU** also computes **gradients** via backpropagation (highly parallel).
4. The **CPU** synchronizes updates to model weights (Adam, SGD optimizers).

This division of labor achieves **10-100x speedup** over CPU-only training, making heterogeneous computing the **backbone of modern AI/HPC systems**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Miscellaneous HPC" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Amdahl's and Gustafson's Laws:** Always state explicitly whether the problem has a **fixed or scaled workload**. Examiners deduct 2 marks for ambiguity.
> 2. **Skipping variable definitions:** When writing $S_p = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$, you **MUST** define $f$ (serial fraction) and $p$ (number of processors) before substituting values. A formula without variable definitions loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring units in efficiency:** Efficiency must be expressed as a *decimal* (0 < E < 1) or *percentage* (0% < E < 100%). Writing $E = 0.5$ without units or $E = 50\%$ is acceptable; writing $E = 50$ alone is **wrong**.
> 4. **Karp-Flatt without trend analysis:** Computing $e$ for one $p$ is insufficient. You need **at least three data points** to identify the bottleneck type.
> 5. **Heterogeneous computing diagrams:** Students often draw the CPU and GPU as separate boxes without showing the **interconnect** (PCIe/NVLink) and **memory hierarchy**. This loses 2-3 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the asymptotic limit of Amdahl's Law:** Always mention $\lim_{p \to \infty} S_p = \frac{1}{f}$ in long-answer questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Speedup $S_p$** = ratio of sequential to parallel runtime; ideal value is $p$.
- [x] **Efficiency $E_p$** = $\frac{S_p}{p}$; ideal value is 1 (or 100%).
- [x] **Cost $C_p$** = $p \cdot T_p$; cost-optimality requires $C_p = \Theta(T_s)$.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law** = $\frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$; applies to **fixed workload**; upper bound is $\frac{1}{f}$.
- [x] **Gustafson's Law** = $p - f(p-1)$; applies to **scaled workload**; near-linear speedup.
- [x] **Karp-Flatt metric** = $\frac{1/S_p - 1/p}{1 - 1/p}$; diagnoses if bottleneck is **serial code, overhead, or load imbalance**.
- [x] **Isoefficiency** = $W = K \cdot \frac{T_o(p)}{1-E}$; lower slope = better scalability.
- [x] **Strong scaling** = fixed $W$, increasing $p$; **Weak scaling** = $W \propto p$.
- [x] **Heterogeneous computing** = CPU + GPU/FPGA cooperation; CPU for control, GPU for data-parallel kernels.
- [x] **Communication model** = $T_{\text{comm}} = t_s + n \cdot t_w$ (startup latency + data transfer time).

### 📌 Key Numerical Values to Memorize

| $f$ | Amdahl's $S_{\text{max}} = 1/f$ | Gustafson $S_{64}$ |
|-----|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| 0.01 | 100x | 63.37x |
| 0.05 | 20x | 60.85x |
| 0.10 | 10x | 57.70x |
| 0.25 | 4x | 48.25x |
| 0.50 | 2x | 32.50x |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For a 14-mark Part B question on this topic, structure your answer as: **(1) Definition with formula → (2) Numerical substitution with units → (3) Result interpretation → (4) Comparison with related laws.** This 4-step structure consistently fetches 12-14 marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: OpenMP-Parallel Jacobi Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Study: OpenMP-Parallel Jacobi Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Case Study: OpenMP-Parallel Jacobi Algorithm

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Jacobi Iterative Method (Definition):**
> The Jacobi method is a stationary iterative numerical algorithm used to solve a square system of linear equations $A\vec{x} = \vec{b}$ by repeatedly refining an initial guess $\vec{x}^{(0)}$ using the formula:
> $$\vec{x}^{(k+1)} = D^{-1}\left(\vec{b} - (L+U)\vec{x}^{(k)}\right)$$
> where $A = D + L + U$ is decomposed into **diagonal ($D$)**, **strictly lower-triangular ($L$)**, and **strictly upper-triangular ($U$)** components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OpenMP-Parallel Jacobi (Definition):**
> An SMP (Symmetric Multiprocessing) realization of the Jacobi method where computational work over the row dimension is **statically or dynamically partitioned** across multiple threads (workers) of a shared-memory multicore node. The dominant parallel construct is the `for` work-sharing directive (`#pragma omp parallel for`) which assigns rows of the iterate vector $\vec{x}$ to distinct threads.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large rectangular metal plate** placed on a grid, and each cell of the grid has an initial temperature. The plate is in contact with a heat source/sink on its boundary, and you wish to find the *steady-state* temperature at every interior cell.

**The Jacobi update rule is exactly Newton's law of cooling discretized:**

> "Each cell's new temperature = (sum of its four neighbours' *previous* temperatures) / 4"

Why does it parallelize beautifully? Because the **new value of every cell depends only on OLD values from the previous iteration**. No cell ever looks at a neighbour's value from the *current* iteration. This is the *algorithmic independence* that makes Jacobi **embarrassingly parallel at the per-row granularity**.

- A *sequential* simulation is one person with one calculator going cell-by-cell.
- A *parallel* simulation is **8 workers** each owning a horizontal slab of the plate, **simultaneously** updating their assigned cells using a *frozen snapshot* of last iteration's temperatures.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Constants & Metrics

- **Number of unknowns:** $n$ (size of matrix $A$)
- **Number of threads:** $p$ (OpenMP team size, $\leq$ `# of physical cores`)
- **Convergence tolerance:** $\epsilon$ (typically $10^{-6}$)
- **Diagonal dominance ratio:** $\rho = \max_i \frac{\sum_{j \neq i} \vert a_{ij} \vert}{\vert a_{ii} \vert}$
- **Speedup (Amdahl's law ceiling):** $S_p^{\max} = \dfrac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{f_p}{p}}$ where $f_s$ = serial fraction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Decomposition of $A$

Given an $n \times n$ real matrix $A$, the Jacobi method requires the *diagonal dominance* condition (a sufficient, not necessary, condition for convergence):

$$\vert a_{ii} \vert > \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} \vert a_{ij} \vert, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

The matrix is split as:

$$
A = D + L + U \quad \text{where} \quad
D = \begin{pmatrix} a_{11} & & & \\ & a_{22} & & \\ & & \ddots & \\ & & & a_{nn} \end{pmatrix}
$$

The update for the $i^{\text{th}}$ component of $\vec{x}$ at iteration $(k+1)$ is:

$$x_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{a_{ii}} \left( b_i - \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} a_{ij} \, x_j^{(k)} \right)$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Parallelism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The fundamental reason Jacobi is parallelizable:**
> The new value $x_i^{(k+1)}$ depends **exclusively on $x_j^{(k)}$** (the previous iteration). It does **NOT** read from $x_i^{(k)}$ or any $x_m^{(k+1)}$ for $m \neq i$.
