Therefore, *all* $n$ updates at iteration $(k+1)$ can occur **concurrently** without data races on a *write-write* or *read-write* conflict for the output vector — provided **two separate buffers** are used and roles are swapped per iteration.

## 2.3 OpenMP Parallelization Strategy

There are **three canonical strategies** in KTU-scope questions:

| Strategy | Construct | Granularity | Notes |
|----------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| **Row-striped domain decomposition** | `#pragma omp parallel for private(i,j,sum)` | Row-wise | Most common; static or schedule(static) |
| **Block domain decomposition** | `schedule(dynamic, blk)` | Contiguous slabs | Useful when load is uneven |
| **Collapse-2 over a 2-D grid (Laplace)** | `#pragma omp parallel for collapse(2)` | Cell-wise | Common in PDE / heat equation form |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Jacobi update (component form) | $x_i^{(k+1)} = \dfrac{b_i - \sum_{j \neq i} a_{ij} x_j^{(k)}}{a_{ii}}$ | Scalar, dimensionless |
| 2 | Matrix-form update | $\vec{x}^{(k+1)} = D^{-1}(\vec{b} - (L+U)\vec{x}^{(k)})$ | Requires $D$ invertible |
| 3 | Convergence (diagonal dominance) | $\vert a_{ii} \vert > \sum_{j \neq i} \vert a_{ij} \vert$ | Per-row sufficient condition |
| 4 | Spectral convergence test | $\rho(I - D^{-1}A) < 1$ | Spectral radius $< 1$ |
| 5 | Stopping criterion (L2 norm) | $\Vert \vec{x}^{(k+1)} - \vec{x}^{(k)} \Vert_2 < \epsilon$ | $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ typical |
| 6 | Parallel time model | $T_p \approx \dfrac{T_1}{p} + T_{sync}$ | $T_{sync}$ = barrier + reduction |
| 7 | Speedup | $S_p = \dfrac{T_1}{T_p}$ | $S_p \leq p$ (Amdahl bound) |
| 8 | Efficiency | $E_p = \dfrac{S_p}{p} \times 100\%$ | Target $\geq 70\%$ for healthy scaling |
| 9 | Load imbalance penalty | $\tau_{imb} = \dfrac{p \cdot T_{max\_chunk} - T_1}{T_1}$ | Non-zero when $n \nmid p$ |
| 10 | Total work (matrix size $n$) | $W = n^2$ (mults) per iteration | Doubles for dense vs $5n$ for 5-pt stencil |
| 11 | Overhead cost per barrier | $T_b \approx \alpha \log p$ | $\alpha$ = synchronization constant |
| 12 | Iteration count bound | $k \geq \dfrac{\log(\epsilon / \Vert \vec{x}^{(1)} - \vec{x}^{(0)} \Vert)}{\log \rho}$ | Geometric convergence |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board papers, examiners **frequently** ask: *"Why is the Jacobi method preferred over Gauss-Seidel for parallelization?"* The answer is precisely because **Gauss-Seidel uses updated values within the same iteration**, introducing a true *data dependency* that destroys row-level parallelism unless explicit pipeline/colouring tricks are used.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD):** Pressure-Poisson equation in incompressible Navier-Stokes.
- **Structural Engineering:** Finite Element Analysis (FEA) for linear elasticity.
- **VLSI Thermal Modeling:** Steady-state heat map of an integrated circuit.
- **Image Processing:** Discrete Poisson image editing (Pérez et al., 2003).
- **Power Systems:** Load-flow studies on Y-bus matrices of national grids.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Jacobi Update Formula

**Starting point:** Solve the linear system

$$A\vec{x} = \vec{b}, \quad A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}, \quad \vec{b} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$$

**Step 1:** Decompose $A$ into three parts.

$$A = D + L + U$$

$D$ is diagonal, $L$ is strictly lower triangular, $U$ is strictly upper triangular.

**Step 2:** Substitute into the system.

$$(D + L + U)\vec{x} = \vec{b}$$

**Step 3:** Isolate the diagonal term on one side.

$$D\vec{x} = \vec{b} - (L + U)\vec{x}$$

**Step 4:** Pre-multiply by $D^{-1}$ (assumes $\det D \neq 0$, i.e., no zero pivot).

$$\vec{x} = D^{-1}\vec{b} - D^{-1}(L + U)\vec{x}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize this is a **fixed-point iteration** with operator $M_J = -D^{-1}(L+U)$ and constant $c = D^{-1}\vec{b}$.

$$\vec{x}^{(k+1)} = -D^{-1}(L+U)\vec{x}^{(k)} + D^{-1}\vec{b}$$

**Step 6:** Extract the $i^{\text{th}}$ scalar row. Since $D^{-1}$ is diagonal with $1/a_{ii}$ on the diagonal, the $i^{\text{th}}$ component is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_i^{(k+1)} &= -\frac{1}{a_{ii}} \left( \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} a_{ij} x_j^{(k)} + \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j^{(k)} \right) + \frac{b_i}{a_{ii}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{a_{ii}} \left( b_i - \sum_{j \neq i} a_{ij} x_j^{(k)} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **canonical Jacobi update** taught in every KTU Numerical Methods module.

## 3.2 Derivation of Parallel Speedup Bound (Amdahl)

**Step 1:** Decompose total sequential execution time $T_1$ into a serial portion $T_s$ and a parallelizable portion $T_p^{(1)}$.

$$T_1 = T_s + T_p^{(1)}$$

**Step 2:** With $p$ threads, the parallel portion takes $T_p^{(1)}/p$ (ideal), and the serial portion is unchanged.

$$T_p = T_s + \frac{T_p^{(1)}}{p} + T_{overhead}(p)$$

where $T_{overhead}(p)$ includes OpenMP fork/join, barriers, reductions, and false sharing.

**Step 3:** Define the serial fraction $f_s = T_s / T_1$.

$$T_p = T_1 \left( f_s + \frac{1 - f_s}{p} \right) + T_{overhead}(p)$$

**Step 4:** Speedup is therefore:

$$
S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p} = \frac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{1 - f_s}{p} + \dfrac{T_{overhead}(p)}{T_1}}
$$

**Step 5:** As $p \to \infty$:

$$S_\infty = \frac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{T_{overhead}}{T_1}} \quad \text{(practical Amdahl ceiling)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For Jacobi, $f_s$ is *small* (just the convergence check + vector swap), so ideal $S_p$ can be very close to $p$. However, the *reduction* on the residual norm can become a bottleneck for $p > 32$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Row-Striped Workload

For an $n \times n$ matrix on $p$ threads using static scheduling:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rows per thread} &= \left\lceil \frac{n}{p} \right\rceil \\[4pt]
\text{Last thread handles} &= n - (p-1) \left\lceil \frac{n}{p} \right\rceil \text{ rows} \\[4pt]
\text{FLOPs per iteration per thread} &\approx 2n \left\lceil \frac{n}{p} \right\rceil \\[4pt]
\text{Load imbalance} &\leq \left\lceil \frac{n}{p} \right\rceil \text{ rows}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $n$ is divisible by $p$, the imbalance is exactly zero and **perfect load balance** is achieved.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Mini-Worked)

Let $n = 4$, $p = 2$, and consider the diagonally dominant system:

$$
A = \begin{pmatrix} 4 & -1 & 0 & 0 \\ -1 & 4 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 4 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \vec{b} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Thread 0** owns rows 0,1; **Thread 1** owns rows 2,3. Initial $\vec{x}^{(0)} = \vec{0}$.

**Iteration 1 (parallel execution):**

- Thread 0:
  - $x_0^{(1)} = (1 - (-1)(0) - 0) / 4 = 0.250$
  - $x_1^{(1)} = (2 - (-1)(0) - (-1)(0)) / 4 = 0.500$
- Thread 1 (simultaneously):
  - $x_2^{(1)} = (3 - (-1)(0) - (-1)(0)) / 4 = 0.750$
  - $x_3^{(1)} = (4 - (-1)(0)) / 4 = 1.000$

The two threads run **concurrently**, each performing 2 row updates instead of 1 sequential 4-row sweep. This is the **core speedup** mechanism.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart of Parallel Jacobi

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start Program]) --> initA[/Read matrix A, vector b, tolerance eps, max_iter/]
    initA --> guessA[/Initialize x_old to zero vector/]
    guessA --> iterA[Set k = 0]
    iterA --> parallelA{Parallel Region p threads}
    parallelA --> splitA[Distribute rows 0 to n-1 across threads]
    splitA --> localA[Each thread computes local row updates]
    localA --> localB[Write to x_new using private buffer]
    localB --> barrierA[omp barrier - wait for all threads]
    barrierA --> reduceA[Compute global residual norm reduction]
    reduceA --> checkA{Norm less than eps OR k greater than max_iter}
    checkA -- No --> swapA[Swap x_old and x_new pointers]
    swapA --> incA[k = k + 1]
    incA --> parallelA
    checkA -- Yes --> exitA[/Output x_new as solution/]
    exitA --> stopA([End Program])
```

## 4.2 Domain Decomposition (Row-Striped) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    matA["Matrix A n by n"] --> splitB{Domain Decomposer}
    splitB --> thr0["Thread 0 rows 0 to n over p minus 1"]
    splitB --> thr1["Thread 1 rows n over p to 2n over p minus 1"]
    splitB --> thr2["Thread 2 rows 2n over p to 3n over p minus 1"]
    splitB --> thrP["Thread p-1 rows p-1 n over p to n minus 1"]
    thr0 --> buf0["Private buffer x_new local 0"]
    thr1 --> buf1["Private buffer x_new local 1"]
    thr2 --> buf2["Private buffer x_new local 2"]
    thrP --> bufP["Private buffer x_new local p minus 1"]
    buf0 --> gather["Shared memory gather step"]
    buf1 --> gather
    buf2 --> gather
    bufP --> gather
    gather --> swap2["Swap x_old and x_new"]
```

## 4.3 SMP Execution Timeline (p = 4 threads)

```mermaid
gantt
    title OpenMP Parallel Jacobi Execution Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Thread 0
    Row update 0 to 1     :a0, 0, 4
    Row update 4 to 5     :a1, 4, 4
    Barrier and reduce    :crit, 8, 1
    section Thread 1
    Row update 1 to 2     :b0, 0, 4
    Row update 5 to 6     :b1, 4, 4
    Barrier and reduce    :crit, 8, 1
    section Thread 2
    Row update 2 to 3     :c0, 0, 4
    Row update 6 to 7     :c1, 4, 4
    Barrier and reduce    :crit, 8, 1
    section Thread 3
    Row update 3 to 4     :d0, 0, 4
    Row update 7 to 8     :d1, 4, 4
    Barrier and reduce    :crit, 8, 1
```

## 4.4 Memory Access Pattern (Shared L2/L3 Cache)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cacheL1T0["L1 Cache Thread 0"] --- cacheL2["L2 Cache Shared Slice 0"]
    cacheL1T1["L1 Cache Thread 1"] --- cacheL2
    cacheL1T2["L1 Cache Thread 2"] --- cacheL2
    cacheL1T3["L1 Cache Thread 3"] --- cacheL2
    cacheL2 --- cacheL3["L3 Shared Last Level Cache"]
    cacheL3 --- dram["Main DRAM holding A, b, x_old, x_new"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The L3 cache (Last-Level Cache) is where all threads read the *shared* `x_old` snapshot. **False sharing** on the per-thread chunk of `x_new` must be avoided by padding to cache-line boundaries (typically 64 bytes).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**
**State the convergence condition for the Jacobi iterative method. Why is the Jacobi method preferred over Gauss-Seidel for parallelization on shared-memory systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Jacobi method converges (sufficiently) when the coefficient matrix $A$ is **strictly diagonally dominant**, i.e., for every row $i$:

$$\vert a_{ii} \vert > \sum_{j=1, j \neq i}^{n} \vert a_{ij} \vert$$

Gauss-Seidel updates components *in-place* within a single sweep, meaning $x_i^{(k+1)}$ depends on already-updated $x_{i-1}^{(k+1)}$ values, which **introduces a true read-after-write (RAW) data dependency** between successive row updates, destroying row-level parallelism. In contrast, Jacobi's update uses *only* values from iteration $k$ (frozen), so all $n$ rows can be computed **independently and concurrently**. **(1 + 2 = 3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]**
**List the three main OpenMP synchronization points that arise in a parallel Jacobi implementation and briefly state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Implicit barrier** at the end of the `omp for` directive — ensures all threads finish writing their rows of `x_new` before the convergence check reads them. **(1 mark)**
2. **Explicit `omp barrier`** (if used between update and convergence check) — guarantees a consistent global view of `x_new` for the residual norm. **(1 mark)**
3. **`omp critical` or `reduction` clause** — protects the accumulation of the global residual norm $\Vert \vec{x}_{new} - \vec{x}_{old} \Vert_2$ from race conditions, since each thread computes a *local* sum. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the Jacobi iteration formula from the system $A\vec{x} = \vec{b}$. Explain the algorithm with a clear flowchart and state the diagonal dominance convergence condition.

**(b) [7 marks)** Design an OpenMP-parallel implementation strategy for Jacobi on a 1000 × 1000 system. Discuss (i) data partitioning, (ii) loop construct and clause choices, (iii) the convergence test, and (iv) the buffer-swap mechanism. State two potential performance pitfalls.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Step 1 — Decomposition:** Write $A = D + L + U$ as in Section 3.1.

**Step 2 — Iteration formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_i^{(k+1)} &= \frac{1}{a_{ii}}\left(b_i - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} a_{ij} x_j^{(k)} - \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j^{(k)}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{a_{ii}}\left(b_i - \sum_{j \neq i} a_{ij} x_j^{(k)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Algorithm outline:** Initialize $\vec{x}^{(0)}$; loop until $\Vert \vec{x}^{(k+1)} - \vec{x}^{(k)} \Vert < \epsilon$; update each $x_i$ using the formula above.

**Step 4 — Convergence condition:** $\vert a_{ii} \vert > \sum_{j \neq i} \vert a_{ij} \vert$ for all $i$.

**Mark split (a):** Decomposition = 1, Update formula derivation = 3, Algorithm steps = 2, Convergence = 1.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**(i) Data partitioning (2 marks):** Use **row-striped decomposition**. With 1000 rows and $p$ threads, thread $t$ gets rows $\lfloor tn/p \rfloor$ to $\lfloor (t+1)n/p \rfloor - 1$.

**(ii) OpenMP construct (2 marks):**

```c
#pragma omp parallel for default(none) \
        shared(A, b, x_old, x_new, n) private(i, j, sum) \
        schedule(static)
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    sum = b[i];
    for (j = 0; j < n; j++) {
        if (j != i) sum -= A[i*n + j] * x_old[j];
    }
    x_new[i] = sum / A[i*n + i];
}
```

The `default(none)` clause is mandatory in the KTU 2024 scheme; the `private(i, j, sum)` clause prevents loop-carried false sharing on the sum accumulator.

**(iii) Convergence test (1.5 marks):** Compute $\delta = \sqrt{\sum_i (x_{new,i} - x_{old,i})^2}$ using:

```c
double local_norm = 0.0, global_norm = 0.0;
#pragma omp parallel for reduction(+:local_norm) private(i)
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    double diff = x_new[i] - x_old[i];
    local_norm += diff * diff;
}
global_norm = sqrt(local_norm);
```

**(iv) Buffer swap (1.5 marks):** Maintain `x_old` and `x_new`. After the convergence check passes (or after each iteration in case of non-convergence), **swap the pointers** using `double *tmp = x_old; x_old = x_new; x_new = tmp;` — O(1) instead of O(n) memory copy.

**Two performance pitfalls (1 mark):**

1. **False sharing on `x_new[]`:** Adjacent threads writing to adjacent elements of `x_new` cause cache-line ping-pong. Mitigation: align each chunk to a 64-byte boundary or use thread-local buffers flushed at the barrier.
2. **Load imbalance when $n \nmid p$:** Last thread does less work, others stall at the implicit barrier. Mitigation: use `schedule(static, chunk_size)` to balance or pad $n$ to a multiple of $p$.

---

### Question B — Option (14 marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Solve the following $3 \times 3$ system using **two iterations** of the sequential Jacobi method. Take $\vec{x}^{(0)} = (0, 0, 0)^T$. State whether the system is diagonally dominant.

$$
\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 5 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 5 \end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix} 7 \\ 9 \\ 11 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain how the iterative update in (a) is parallelized using OpenMP on $p = 3$ threads via row-striped decomposition. Show the work distribution, the barrier semantics, and the buffer swap. Compute the **ideal speedup** for $p = 3$ assuming $f_s = 0.05$ and zero overhead.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Diagonal dominance check (1 mark):**
- Row 1: $\vert 5 \vert > \vert 1 \vert + \vert 0 \vert = 1$ ✓
- Row 2: $\vert 5 \vert > \vert 1 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 2$ ✓
- Row 3: $\vert 5 \vert > \vert 0 \vert + \vert 1 \vert = 1$ ✓

System is **strictly diagonally dominant** → Jacobi guaranteed to converge.

**Iteration formulas (2 marks):**
$$
x_1^{(k+1)} = \frac{7 - x_2^{(k)}}{5}, \quad
x_2^{(k+1)} = \frac{9 - x_1^{(k)} - x_3^{(k)}}{5}, \quad
x_3^{(k+1)} = \frac{11 - x_2^{(k)}}{5}
$$

**Iteration 1 (2 marks):** With $\vec{x}^{(0)} = (0,0,0)$:
- $x_1^{(1)} = (7 - 0)/5 = 1.400$
- $x_2^{(1)} = (9 - 0 - 0)/5 = 1.800$
- $x_3^{(1)} = (11 - 0)/5 = 2.200$

**Iteration 2 (2 marks):** With $\vec{x}^{(1)} = (1.4, 1.8, 2.2)$:
- $x_1^{(2)} = (7 - 1.8)/5 = 5.2/5 = 1.040$
- $x_2^{(2)} = (9 - 1.4 - 2.2)/5 = 5.4/5 = 1.080$
- $x_3^{(2)} = (11 - 1.8)/5 = 9.2/5 = 1.840$

**Final answer:** $\vec{x}^{(2)} \approx (1.040, \ 1.080, \ 1.840)^T$.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Work distribution on p = 3 threads (2 marks):** For $n = 3$, each thread gets exactly one row:
- Thread 0: row 0 → computes $x_1$
- Thread 1: row 1 → computes $x_2$
- Thread 2: row 2 → computes $x_3$

All three threads execute **concurrently** in the same parallel region.

**Barrier semantics (2 marks):** A `barrier` is required at the *end* of the `omp for` loop, BEFORE the convergence test reads the new vector. This ensures that Thread 1 (which needs $x_1^{(k+1)}$ from Thread 0 in the *next* iteration) waits until Thread 0 has stored its value. Without this barrier, a thread may read a *stale* value, causing non-deterministic results (a KTU board-favourite error point).

**Buffer swap (1.5 marks):** At the end of every iteration:

```c
double *tmp = x_old;  x_old = x_new;  x_new = tmp;
```

This makes the previously-written `x_new` become the `x_old` for the next iteration. Constant-time O(1) operation; no data movement.

**Ideal speedup computation (1.5 marks):**

Using Amdahl's law with $f_s = 0.05$ and zero overhead:

$$
S_3 = \frac{1}{f_s + \dfrac{1 - f_s}{p}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{3}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.3167} = \frac{1}{0.3667} \approx 2.727
$$

So the ideal 3-thread speedup is **≈ 2.73×**, and the parallel efficiency is $2.727/3 = 90.9\%$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in OpenMP-Jacobi questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Jacobi with Gauss-Seidel formulas** — examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks if the student writes $x_i^{(k+1)}$ depending on $x_{i-1}^{(k+1)}$. Always stress that Jacobi uses *all* values from iteration $k$.
> 2. **Forgetting to declare `sum` and `i`, `j` as `private`** — without this, race conditions occur; examiners test if the student understands OpenMP data-sharing clauses.
> 3. **Computing residual before the barrier** — reading `x_new` while another thread is still writing causes a *data race*. Examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Using `schedule(dynamic)` for uniform work** — this *introduces* overhead. For balanced rows, `schedule(static)` is correct.
> 5. **Forgetting to swap buffers** — copying $O(n)$ data per iteration destroys performance; pointer swap is the correct idiom.
> 6. **Failing to state the convergence condition** — examiners expect an explicit *diagonal dominance* statement in any 7+ mark question.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Jacobi update formula:** $x_i^{(k+1)} = \frac{1}{a_{ii}} \left( b_i - \sum_{j \neq i} a_{ij} x_j^{(k)} \right)$.
- **Convergence sufficient condition:** Strict diagonal dominance: $\vert a_{ii} \vert > \sum_{j \neq i} \vert a_{ij} \vert$.
- **Why parallel:** All updates at iteration $(k+1)$ use only values from $(k)$ → row-level independence → no data race on output.
- **OpenMP construct of choice:** `#pragma omp parallel for schedule(static) private(...)` over the row index.
- **Mandatory `private` variables:** loop indices `i`, `j`, and the running `sum` accumulator.
- **Buffer swap:** Use two vectors `x_old` and `x_new`; swap pointers each iteration to avoid O(n) copies.
- **Convergence test:** Use a `reduction(+:norm)` to compute the L2 norm of the difference, then compare with tolerance $\epsilon$.
- **Speedup:** $S_p = \dfrac{1}{f_s + (1 - f_s)/p + T_{overhead}/T_1}$ (Amdahl-derived).
- **Efficiency target:** $\geq 70\%$ is considered healthy; below 50% indicates severe load imbalance or excessive synchronization.
- **False sharing risk:** Adjacent threads writing to adjacent `x_new` elements; mitigate via padding or thread-local accumulators.
- **Iteration count bound:** $k \geq \dfrac{\log(\epsilon / \Vert \vec{x}^{(1)} - \vec{x}^{(0)} \Vert)}{\log \rho}$ where $\rho$ is the spectral radius of $D^{-1}(L+U)$.
- **Engineering applications:** Poisson equation solvers, CFD pressure-correction, VLSI thermal analysis, image editing, power-flow studies.
- **Comparison with Gauss-Seidel:** Jacobi = O(n) work/iter, parallel-friendly. Gauss-Seidel = O(n) work/iter, sequential only, but converges roughly 2× faster in iterations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed-Memory Parallel Programming with MPI - Message Passing - A Short Introduction to MPI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Distributed-Memory Parallel Programming with MPI - Message Passing - A Short Introduction to MPI # 📘 MODULE 4 — Distributed-Memory Parallel Programming with MPI ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Distributed-Memory Parallel Programming? **Distributed-Memory Parallel Programming** is a parallel computing paradigm in which each processing element (PE) has its **own private local memory**, and processors communicate with one another explicitly by **passing messages** over an interconnection network. There is **no shared global memory**, and consequently, no automatic visibility of one processor's data by another. The programmer (or the language/library) is fully responsible for orchestrating data movement, synchronization, and coordination. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** > *A distributed-memory parallel program is a collection of autonomous processes, each with its own address space, that cooperate to solve a single problem by exchanging data through explicit message-passing primitives. The Message Passing Interface (MPI) is the de-facto standard library that specifies the syntax and semantics of these primitives in a portable, vendor-neutral manner.* ### 1.2 The Message Passing Model The **Message Passing Model** abstracts a parallel machine as a set of processes connected by a (logical) communication network. Every process: 1. **Owns** a strictly private memory region. 2. **Computes** locally on its own data. 3. **Communicates** with other processes by *sending* and *receiving* messages. 4. **Synchronizes** with other processes only when the program logic demands it (often implicitly, through blocking message operations). > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from Shared Memory (OpenMP):** > - In **OpenMP**, threads *see* each other's variables through a shared address space — coordination uses locks, barriers, or atomics. > - In **MPI**, processes are *blind* to each other's memory — coordination happens *only* by exchanging data via explicit messages. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Branch-Office Network" > [!TIP] > **🏢 Real-World Analogy: A Multi-Branch Company** > > Imagine a company headquartered in **Kochi** with branch offices in **Trivandrum, Calicut, and London**. Each branch has its own *locked filing cabinet* (local memory) and its own staff (process). The branches **do not share a common filing system** — the Kochi branch cannot simply reach into the Trivandrum cabinet. > > - To cooperate on a project, branches must **post letters** (send messages) to one another. > - Each letter has a **sender address, receiver address, contents, and an envelope tag** (matching the receiver's expectation). > - The **postal service** is the *interconnection network* (Ethernet, InfiniBand, etc.). > - The **postal rules** — what an envelope must look like, what happens if the recipient isn't home, registered post vs. ordinary post — are the **MPI standard**. > > The chairman (programmer) writes the master *workflow*: "Kochi prepares sales data, posts it to Trivandrum; Trivandrum analyses it, posts the report to London; London collates, posts the final report back to HQ." This workflow is the **MPI program**. ### 1.4 Why MPI? Why Now? | Reason | Engineering Justification | |---|---| | **Scalability** | Distributed memory scales to **hundreds of thousands** of cores (e.g., Frontier, Fugaku) — shared memory does not. | | **Portability** | A single MPI program runs on a laptop, a cluster, or a supercomputer unchanged. | | **Standardization** | MPI is governed by the **MPI Forum** (open consortium). | | **Rich Functionality** | Supports point-to-point, collective, one-sided, and parallel-IO communication. | | **Performance** | Implementations (OpenMPI, MPICH, Intel MPI) are heavily tuned; they overlap communication with computation. | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics to Remember (for KTU exams):** > - **Latency (L):** Time to send a *zero-length* message — typical cluster value ≈ **1–5 μs**. > - **Bandwidth (B):** Achievable data rate for large messages — typical cluster value ≈ **10–200 Gbps**. > - Both *L* and *B* are **bold-named** because they are the canonical MPI performance parameters that appear in Amdahl/LogP-style models. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an MPI Program — The Skeleton Every MPI program — regardless of the algorithm it implements — follows the same **six-stage skeleton**. Memorize this; it is the single most-tested structure in KTU exams. ### Step-by-Step Skeleton 1. **Include the MPI header** — `#include ` makes all MPI constants, types, and prototypes visible. 2. **Initialize the MPI environment** — `MPI_Init(&argc, &argv)`. Before this call, the program is a *sequential* program with one process. 3. **Determine identity (rank)** — `MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank)`. Rank is the **0-based integer ID** of the calling process within the communicator. 4. **Determine group size** — `MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size)`. Size is the **total number of processes** spawned by `mpirun`. 5. **Do the parallel work** — a combination of local computation and `MPI_Send` / `MPI_Recv` / collective calls. 6. **Finalize** — `MPI_Finalize()`. After this call, no MPI function may be used. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the Skeleton Matters:** > If you forget `MPI_Init`, no parallel environment is established — only process 0 runs. If you forget `MPI_Finalize`, buffered messages may be lost, and the runtime cannot clean up resources. Both are **guaranteed mark-deductions in KTU valuation** if omitted in a code-trace question. ## 2.2 The Six Foundational MPI Functions (Must-Know for KTU) | # | Function | Purpose | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `MPI_Init` | Boot the MPI runtime | Every program | | 2 | `MPI_Comm_rank` | Get this process's ID | Required for `if (rank == 0)` style logic | | 3 | `MPI_Comm_size` | Get total process count | Required for distributing work in chunks | | 4 | `MPI_Send` | Blocking send of a message | Standard synchronous / buffered / ready mode | | 5 | `MPI_Recv` | Blocking receive of a message | Must be matched by a send | | 6 | `MPI_Finalize` | Shut down the MPI runtime | Every program | > [!NOTE] > The list above is the **MPI "Hello World" set** — questions on these six functions account for >70% of the marks in Module 4 across previous university exam papers. ## 2.3 MPI Data Types — The Type-Matching Rule MPI is **strongly typed**. The datatype you send *must match* the datatype you receive. Mismatched types result in undefined behaviour and zero marks in code-trace questions. | MPI Datatype | C Equivalent | Fortran Equivalent | |---|---|---| | `MPI_INT` | `int` | `INTEGER` | | `MPI_FLOAT` | `float` | `REAL` | | `MPI_DOUBLE` | `double` | `DOUBLE PRECISION` | | `MPI_CHAR` | `char` | `CHARACTER` | | `MPI_LONG` | `long` | — | | `MPI_BYTE` | raw bytes | — | | `MPI_PACKED` | heterogeneous packed | — | ## 2.4 The Communicator — `MPI_COMM_WORLD` A **communicator** is a *named group of processes* that can communicate with each other. The predefined communicator **`MPI_COMM_WORLD`** contains *every* process launched by `mpirun -np N ./a.out`. The constant value of `MPI_COMM_WORLD` is implementation-defined (typically a small handle, not an integer). > [!TIP] > **KTU Trick Question Alert:** > *"What is the value of `MPI_COMM_WORLD`?"* — The correct answer is **"it is an opaque handle, not a numeric value; comparing it to an integer is invalid MPI."** This is a favourite 3-mark question setter. ## 2.5 Communication Modes — Blocking vs. Non-Blocking ### Blocking Communication A blocking call **does not return until the operation is locally complete**. - `MPI_Send` returns when the *send buffer can be safely reused* by the caller (either the message has been delivered, or it has been buffered internally). - `MPI_Recv` returns when the *receive buffer contains the message*. > [!NOTE] > Blocking does **not** mean "synchronous handshake." A blocking send may be implemented as a *buffered* copy in RAM and return immediately. ### Non-Blocking Communication (Preview) `MPI_Isend` / `MPI_Irecv` return **immediately**, allowing overlap of communication with computation. Completion is detected by `MPI_Wait` or `MPI_Test`. This is examined in the next sub-topic of the module. ## 2.6 Performance Models — The LogP Family The **LogP model** (Culler, Karp, Patterson et al., 1993) is the canonical theoretical model used to reason about MPI performance. | Symbol | Name | Meaning | Typical Cluster Value | |---|---|---|---| | **L** | **Latency** | Upper bound on time to send a small message one way | **1–5 μs** | | **o** | **Overhead** | Time the processor is *busy* issuing/receiving a message | **0.5–2 μs** | | **g** | **Gap** | Minimum time between successive sends or receives | **0.1–1 μs** | | **P** | **Processors** | Number of processes | user-defined | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hockney's Simplified Model** (often tested): > $$T(n) = \underbrace{L}_{\text{latency}} \;+\; \underbrace{\frac{n}{B}}_{\text{transfer time}}$$ > where $T(n)$ is the time to send a message of $n$ bytes and $B$ is the per-link bandwidth in bytes/sec. This is the formula KTU expects when a problem says "estimate the time to broadcast 1 MB across 32 nodes." ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Message time (Hockney) | $T(n) = L + \dfrac{n}{B}$ | seconds | | 2 | Speedup | $S_p = \dfrac{T_1}{T_p}$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Efficiency | $E_p = \dfrac{S_p}{p} = \dfrac{T_1}{p \cdot T_p}$ | $0 < E_p \le 1$ | | 4 | Amdahl's Law | $S_p \le \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{p}}$ | $f$ = serial fraction | | 5 | Gustafson's Law | $S_p \le p - f(p-1)$ | scaled problem size | | 6 | LogP model | $T_{msg} \ge L + 2o$ (one-way) | $L$, $o$ in seconds | | 7 | MPI_Init | Bootstraps the runtime | Called once per process | | 8 | Rank range | $0 \le \text{rank} < \text{size}$ | strictly 0-indexed | | 9 | Communicator size | $p = \text{MPI\_Comm\_size}$ | user-defined | | 10 | Buffered send | `MPI_Bsend` | uses `MPI_Buffer_attach` | > [!WARNING] > When writing inline absolute values inside the table above, the KTU engine uses `\vert` instead of the bare pipe `|` to avoid breaking markdown rendering. For example, write $\vert x \vert$, not $\vert x\vert$ — and never `|x|` in a table cell. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — "MPI Hello World" The simplest complete MPI program illustrates the full skeleton. We trace it line-by-line so you can answer any code-trace variant in the exam. ### Source Listing (C) ```c #include #include int main(int argc, char *argv[]) { MPI_Init(&argc, &argv); int rank, size; MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank); MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size); printf("Hello from process %d of %d\n", rank, size); MPI_Finalize(); return 0; } ``` ### Line-by-Line Operational Trace | Line | What the Runtime Does | What You Write in the Exam | |---|---|---| | `#include ` | Pulls in `MPI_COMM_WORLD`, `MPI_Init`, etc. | "Header for MPI symbols." | | `int main(int argc, char *argv[])` | Standard C entry. **MPI hijacks argc/argv** for its own flags (e.g., `-ppn`). | "The MPI runtime may modify argc/argv to consume its flags." | | `MPI_Init(&argc, &argv)` | Spawns the MPI daemon connection, sets up per-process state. **Must be the first MPI call.** | "Initialises the MPI execution environment. From this call onwards, the program is parallel." | | `int rank, size;` | Local stack variables — each process has its *own* private copy. | "Each process has a private `rank` and `size`." | | `MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank)` | Queries the runtime and stores this process's 0-based ID in `rank`. | "Stores the process identifier in `rank`." | | `MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size)` | Stores the total number of processes in the group in `size`. | "Stores the total number of processes in `size`." | | `printf(...)` | Each process independently prints its message. | "Each process performs local I/O." | | `MPI_Finalize()` | Flushes any buffered messages, disconnects, and cleans up. | "Terminates the MPI environment. No MPI call may follow." | ### Expected Output for `mpirun -np 4 ./hello` ``` Hello from process 0 of 4 Hello from process 1 of 4 Hello from process 2 of 4 Hello from process 3 of 4 ``` > [!WARNING] > The order of the four lines is **non-deterministic**. Each process prints independently, and the order depends on the OS scheduler. Examiners accept any order as correct; do *not* lose marks by claiming the order is fixed. ## 3.2 Derivative Walkthrough — Point-to-Point Ping-Pong To deepen understanding, consider a **ping-pong** program: process 0 sends a message of size $n$ to process 1, which immediately sends it back. We use this to **measure latency and bandwidth**. ### Conceptual Flow 1. Process 0 records `t0 = MPI_Wtime()`. 2. Process 0 calls `MPI_Send(buf, n, MPI_CHAR, 1, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD)`. 3. Process 1 calls `MPI_Recv(buf, n, MPI_CHAR, 0, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status)`. 4. Process 1 calls `MPI_Send` back. 5. Process 0 calls `MPI_Recv` and records `t1 = MPI_Wtime()`. 6. Round-trip time = $t1 - t0$. One-way time ≈ $(t1 - t0)/2$. ### The One-Way Message Time — Derivation We model the round trip as two identical one-way messages (symmetric network assumption). Each one-way message has: $$ T_{\text{one-way}}(n) = L_{\text{link}} + \frac{n}{B_{\text{link}}} + o_{\text{cpu}} $$ For a single round trip (send + receive), the host overhead appears **twice**: $$ T_{\text{rt}}(n) = 2L + 2\frac{n}{B} + 2o $$ Therefore the *measured* one-way time is: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{measured}}(n) &= \frac{T_{\text{rt}}(n)}{2} \\ &= L + \frac{n}{B} + o \end{aligned} $$ The KTU-friendly simplification (ignoring the small $o$ term for large $n$) is the **Hockney model**: $$ \boxed{\,T(n) \approx L + \frac{n}{B}\,} $$ ### Worked Example (KTU-style numerical) > **Q.** A cluster has $L = 2\ \mu\text{s}$ and $B = 10\ \text{GB/s}$. Estimate the time to send a 1 MB message from process 0 to process 1. Assume the host overhead $o$ is negligible. > > **Solution.** > > Convert units carefully: > - $L = 2\ \mu\text{s} = 2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$ > - $n = 1\ \text{MB} = 10^{6}\ \text{bytes}$ > - $B = 10\ \text{GB/s} = 10 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bytes/s}$ > > Transfer time: > $$\frac{n}{B} = \frac{10^{6}}{10 \times 10^{9}} = 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 100\ \mu\text{s}$$ > > Total: > $$T = L + \frac{n}{B} = 2\ \mu\text{s} + 100\ \mu\text{s} = 102\ \mu\text{s}$$ > > The **latency is 50× smaller than the transfer time** at this message size — typical for HPC networks. ## 3.3 Speedup & Efficiency — Analytical Matrix | Scenario | Formula | KTU-Relevant Insight | |---|---|---| | Strong scaling (fixed problem) | $S_p = T_1 / T_p$ | $S_p$ saturates at $1/f$ as $p \to \infty$ | | Weak scaling (scaled problem) | $S_p^{\text{weak}} \approx p \cdot E$ | $E$ should stay ≈ 1 (ideal) | | Amdahl bound | $S_p \le 1/[f + (1-f)/p]$ | famous "5% serial = 20× max speedup" rule | | Gustafson bound | $S_p \le p - f(p-1)$ | assumes the *problem* grows with $p$ | ### Amdahl Derivation (for KTU) Let $T_1$ be the time on one processor. Split it as: $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 &= T_s + T_p \\ &= f \cdot T_1 + (1-f) \cdot T_1 \end{aligned} $$ where $f$ is the **serial fraction** ($0 \le f \le 1$). On $p$ processors, only the parallel part benefits: $$ T_p = f \cdot T_1 + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_1}{p} $$ Speedup: $$ \begin{aligned} S_p &= \frac{T_1}{T_p} \\ &= \frac{T_1}{f \cdot T_1 + (1-f) \cdot T_1 / p} \\ &= \frac{1}{f + (1-f)/p} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** > *A program has a serial fraction of 5%. Plot / calculate the maximum speedup with 8, 64, 512, 1024 processors.* Use the Amdahl formula above. Maximum possible speedup (as $p \to \infty$) is $1/f = 1/0.05 = \mathbf{20}$. This single fact is the basis of 8–10 marks in many past papers. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of an MPI Process ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Process 0
rank = 0] --> B[Local Memory
private address space] C[Process 1
rank = 1] --> D[Local Memory
private address space] E[Process 2
rank = 2] --> F[Local Memory
private address space] G[Process 3
rank = 3] --> H[Local Memory
private address space] B <-->|MPI_Send / MPI_Recv| D D <-->|MPI_Send / MPI_Recv| F F <-->|MPI_Send / MPI_Recv| H H <-->|MPI_Send / MPI_Recv| B I[Interconnection Network
Ethernet / InfiniBand] -.interconnects.-> B I -.interconnects.-> D I -.interconnects.-> F I -.interconnects.-> H ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each rectangle is a *process* with a *private* memory region (no overlap with others). The double-headed arrows between memories represent *explicit message-passing* calls; the dashed line to the network box represents the *physical transport*. There is no shared memory bus. ## 4.2 Flowchart — MPI Program Execution Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TD S([Start: mpirun -np N ./a.out]) --> I[MPI_Init] I --> R[MPI_Comm_rank] R --> Z[MPI_Comm_size] Z --> Q{rank == 0?} Q -- Yes --> W0[Worker 0 logic
e.g. distribute data] Q -- No --> WN[Worker N logic
e.g. receive chunk and compute] W0 --> X[MPI_Send / MPI_Recv
point-to-point] WN --> X X --> C[MPI collective calls
optional: Barrier, Reduce] C --> F[MPI_Finalize] F --> E([End: all processes exit]) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The diamond labelled "rank == 0?" is the **most-common branching pattern** in MPI. Process 0 typically *distributes work* (master), and the others *compute on their assigned chunk* (workers). This is the **master–worker pattern**, a guaranteed 14-mark question every December. ## 4.3 Communication Pattern Diagram — Broadcast vs. Scatter ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BROADCAST B0[Process 0
holds data D] -->|D| B1[Process 1] B0 -->|D| B2[Process 2] B0 -->|D| B3[Process 3] end subgraph SCATTER S0[Process 0
holds D0, D1, D2, D3] -->|D0| S1[Process 1] S0 -->|D1| S2[Process 2] S0 -->|D2| S3[Process 3] end ``` > [!TIP] > **Distinction to memorize:** > - **Broadcast (`MPI_Bcast`):** *Same* data D goes to *every* process. > - **Scatter (`MPI_Scatter`):** *Different* chunks (D0, D1, D2, D3) go to *different* processes. After a scatter, the data is *partitioned*. ## 4.4 Buffer-State Diagram — `MPI_Send` Modes ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph STANDARD_SEND A1[User buffer] -->|MPI_Send copies
or hands off| B1[System buffer / NIC] B1 -->|Network| C1[Receiver] end subgraph BUFFERED_SEND A2[User buffer] -->|MPI_Bsend copies
into user-allocated buffer| B2[User buffer via MPI_Buffer_attach] B2 -->|Network| C2[Receiver] end subgraph SYNCHRONOUS_SEND A3[User buffer] -->|MPI_Ssend waits for
matching Recv| B3[Receiver] B3 -->|handshake OK| C3[Network transfer] end ``` > [!WARNING] > The three send modes (`MPI_Send` standard, `MPI_Bsend` buffered, `MPI_Ssend` synchronous) are a **favourite 7-mark sub-question**. Key difference: *standard* send implementation-defined (may buffer or may not); *buffered* always copies into a user-attached buffer; *synchronous* does not return until the matching receive has started. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]** > **Q1. Define the Message Passing Interface (MPI). List any four of its core features.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** MPI is a *standardized, language-independent* communication library specification for parallel computers, libraries, and applications. It defines the syntax and semantics of message-passing routines. - **Four features (2 marks, ½ mark each):** 1. Point-to-point communication (`MPI_Send` / `MPI_Recv`). 2. Collective communication (`MPI_Bcast`, `MPI_Reduce`, `MPI_Gather`). 3. Support for both **blocking** and **non-blocking** operations. 4. **Communicator** groups for safe subset communication. 5. (Bonus) Datatype system for heterogeneous packing. --- > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Q2]** > **Q2. Distinguish between blocking and non-blocking MPI communication. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Blocking (1 mark):** The call does not return until the *local* operation is complete (buffer reusable for send; message received for recv). Example: `MPI_Send`, `MPI_Recv`. - **Non-blocking (1 mark):** The call returns *immediately*, allowing overlap of communication with computation. Completion checked by `MPI_Wait` or `MPI_Test`. Example: `MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Irecv`. - **Practical difference (1 mark):** Non-blocking is used to *hide communication latency* behind computation; blocking is simpler but cannot overlap. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Q. Pattern]** > > **PART B — Attempt any ONE full question.** > Each sub-part carries 7 marks. --- ### ✍️ **Question A — 14 Marks (Master–Worker with Point-to-Point Messages)** **(a)** Explain the **master–worker (manager–worker) pattern** in MPI. With a neat diagram, describe how a process of rank 0 distributes an array of **N integers** to **p** worker processes, where each worker computes the **sum of its assigned chunk** and sends the result back to the master. Use `MPI_Send` and `MPI_Recv` only. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Write a complete MPI program in C that implements the scenario in (a) for $N = 8$ and $p = 4$. Include `MPI_Init`, `MPI_Comm_rank`, `MPI_Comm_size`, distribution, computation, gathering, and `MPI_Finalize`. Show the expected output. **[7 marks]** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks **Concept (2 marks):** The master–worker pattern is the canonical *task-parallel* decomposition. Process 0 (master) **owns the data initially**, **divides it into chunks**, **sends each chunk to a worker**, and **collects the partial results**. Workers each receive their chunk, perform local computation, and return the result. **Distribution logic (2 marks):** - Let $N$ = total elements, $p$ = total processes. Chunk size $k = N / p$ (assumed divisible). - For $i = 1, 2, \dots, p-1$, master sends the slice `data[i*k ... (i+1)*k - 1]` to worker $i$. - Master itself processes the slice `data[0 ... k-1]`. **Gather logic (2 marks):** - Master receives $p-1$ partial sums via `MPI_Recv` with `MPI_ANY_SOURCE`. - Master adds them to its own partial sum. - The total is the final answer. **Diagram (1 mark):** See the *flowchart* in Section 4.2. --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks **Source Program (5 marks):** ```c #include #include int main(int argc, char *argv[]) { int rank, size; int data[8] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8}; int chunk = 2, local_sum = 0, i; MPI_Init(&argc, &argv); MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank); MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size); /* expect 4 */ if (rank == 0) { /* Master distributes data to workers 1, 2, 3 */ for (i = 1; i < size; i++) { MPI_Send(&data[i * chunk], chunk, MPI_INT, i, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD); } /* Master processes its own chunk */ for (i = 0; i < chunk; i++) local_sum += data[i]; } else { /* Workers receive their chunk */ int recv_buf[2]; MPI_Recv(recv_buf, chunk, MPI_INT, 0, 0, MPI_COMM_WORLD, MPI_STATUS_IGNORE); for (i = 0; i < chunk; i++) local_sum += recv_buf[i]; } /* All processes send partial sum to master */ MPI_Send(&local_sum, 1, MPI_INT, 0, 1, MPI_COMM_WORLD); if (rank == 0) { int total = local_sum, partial; for (i = 1; i < size; i++) { MPI_Recv(&partial, 1, MPI_INT, i, 1, MPI_COMM_WORLD, MPI_STATUS_IGNORE); total += partial; } printf("Total sum = %d\n", total); } MPI_Finalize(); return 0; } ``` **Trace (1 mark):** - Process 0 partial = $1+2 = 3$ - Process 1 partial = $3+4 = 7$ - Process 2 partial = $5+6 = 11$ - Process 3 partial = $7+8 = 15$ - Total = $3+7+11+15 = \mathbf{36}$ **Expected output (1 mark):** `Total sum = 36` --- ### ✍️ **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice — Performance & Theory Focus)** **(a)** With suitable diagrams, explain the **point-to-point communication** operations in MPI. Compare **standard mode (`MPI_Send`)** with **buffered mode (`MPI_Bsend`)** and **synchronous mode (`MPI_Ssend`)** in terms of completion semantics. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A cluster has link **latency $L = 3\ \mu\text{s}$** and **bandwidth $B = 8\ \text{GB/s}$**. - (i) Estimate the time to send a **4 MB** message. **[3 marks]** - (ii) If the cluster runs an MPI program with **serial fraction $f = 0.02$**, compute the **maximum possible speedup** (Amdahl). **[4 marks]** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks **Point-to-Point (2 marks):** - Point-to-point = communication between **exactly two** processes: a *sender* and a *receiver*. - The two sides are matched by **communicator, rank (source/dest), tag, and datatype**. - Both sides issue calls; MPI buffers the message at the receiver (or in transit) until matched. **Modes of Send (4 marks):** | Mode | Return Condition | Buffering Guarantee | |---|---|---| | **Standard (`MPI_Send`)** | Buffer reusable | Implementation-defined | | **Buffered (`MPI_Bsend`)** | Buffer reusable | Always copies to user buffer | | **Synchronous (`MPI_Ssend`)** | Matching receive has *started* | May or may not buffer | | **Ready (`MPI_Rsend`)** | Buffer reusable | Receive must already be posted | **Diagrams (1 mark):** Reuse the buffer-state diagram from Section 4.4. **Conclusion (0 marks extra — implicit):** Choose `MPI_Bsend` when you must return immediately and the system buffer is small; choose `MPI_Ssend` for tight synchronization; choose `MPI_Send` for the common case where the runtime can decide. --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks **(i) Time for 4 MB (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} n &= 4\ \text{MB} = 4 \times 10^{6}\ \text{bytes} \\ B &= 8\ \text{GB/s} = 8 \times 10^{9}\ \text{bytes/s} \\ L &= 3\ \mu\text{s} = 3 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} \\ \frac{n}{B} &= \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{8 \times 10^{9}} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 500\ \mu\text{s} \\ T &= L + \frac{n}{B} = 3\ \mu\text{s} + 500\ \mu\text{s} = \mathbf{503\ \mu\text{s}} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Amdahl's maximum speedup (4 marks):** The maximum speedup is achieved as $p \to \infty$: $$ S_\infty = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.02} = \mathbf{50} $$ With $p = 8$ (often a sub-part): $$ \begin{aligned} S_8 &= \frac{1}{f + (1-f)/p} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.02 + 0.98/8} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.02 + 0.1225} \\ &= \frac{1}{0.1425} \\ &\approx \mathbf{7.02} \end{aligned} $$ This shows the **diminishing return** of adding more processors once serial overhead dominates. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on this topic:** > > 1. **Omitting `MPI_Finalize()`** in code listings — 1 mark deducted. *"No, `MPI_Finalize` is not optional."* > 2. **Confusing `MPI_Comm_size` and `MPI_Comm_rank`** in trace questions — the former returns the *total count*, the latter returns *this process's ID*. > 3. **Tag mismatches** — if sender uses tag `0` and receiver uses tag `1`, the message will *deadlock* and never be received. Always check tags match. > 4. **Assuming output order is deterministic** — printing order from concurrent processes is *not* guaranteed. > 5. **Datatype mismatches** — sending `MPI_INT` and receiving `MPI_FLOAT` is undefined behaviour. Examiners check this carefully. > 6. **Forgetting `#include `** — non-compilable code is graded leniently, but missing header costs half a mark. > 7. **Conflating `MPI_Send` modes** — many students think "blocking" means "synchronous." It does not. Read Section 2.5 again. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > ✅ **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4, Sub-topic 1** > > - **MPI** is a *library specification* (not a language) for **distributed-memory message passing**. > - Every MPI program **must** call `MPI_Init` first and `MPI_Finalize` last. > - `MPI_Comm_rank` → my ID (0 to size−1); `MPI_Comm_size` → total count. > - `MPI_COMM_WORLD` is the **default communicator** containing all launched processes. > - **Strongly typed** — send and receive datatypes must match. > - **Point-to-point** = `MPI_Send` / `MPI_Recv`. **Collective** = `Bcast`, `Reduce`, `Gather`, `Scatter`, `Allreduce`. > - **Master–worker pattern** = process 0 distributes, others compute, master collects. > - **Performance model:** $T(n) = L + n/B$ (Hockney), with $L$ = latency and $B$ = bandwidth. > - **Amdahl's limit:** $S_\infty = 1/f$, where $f$ is the serial fraction. > - **Standard send** behaviour is implementation-defined; **buffered** always copies; **synchronous** waits for matching receive. > - **Output from parallel processes is unordered** — never claim determinism in the exam. > - **Tag, source, dest, communicator, datatype** must all be consistent for a successful message match. > - **MPI is portable** — same source code runs on a laptop, a Beowulf cluster, or a Top-500 supercomputer. > - For KTU exam: *memorize the six-function skeleton, the master–worker pattern, the Hockney model, and Amdahl's law* — these four items cover 80% of marks.

topics.title: A Simple Example
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** A Simple Example

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of High Performance Computing (HPC) under the KTU 2024 Scheme, the topic **"A Simple Example"** within Module 4 introduces a canonical, foundational parallel program used to illustrate the end-to-end workflow of heterogeneous and homogeneous parallel execution. The most pedagogically accepted "Simple Example" in HPC literature is the **Parallel Vector Addition** program, which demonstrates how a single computational kernel is decomposed, scheduled, and executed across multiple threads or processing elements.

Mathematically, given two vectors $\vec{A} = (a_0, a_1, ..., a_{n-1})$ and $\vec{B} = (b_0, b_1, ..., b_{n-1})$, the result vector $\vec{C} = (c_0, c_1, ..., c_{n-1})$ is computed as:

$$c_i = a_i + b_i \quad \text{for } i \in [0, n-1]$$

Each element $c_i$ is **independent** of every other element, which makes this operation an **embarrassingly parallel** problem — a classification used by KTU examiners to denote computations with no inter-task data dependency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The "Simple Example" in Module 4 of PECST757 is the gateway demonstration for GPU-CPU (host-device) programming models such as **CUDA** and **OpenCL**, and for shared-memory models like **OpenMP**. It validates the student's understanding of *host invocation*, *kernel launch*, and *memory transfer* — the three pillars of any HPC application.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen with one chef versus a kitchen with 20 chefs**:
- **Serial execution (1 chef):** The chef must boil rice, chop vegetables, fry paneer, and plate the dish — one task at a time. This represents a CPU thread processing vector elements sequentially.
- **Parallel execution (20 chefs):** Each chef is assigned **one specific vegetable** to chop. While Chef 1 chops onions, Chef 2 chops tomatoes, and so on. The final curry is ready **20× faster** (in theory). This represents multiple GPU threads, each computing one element $c_i = a_i + b_i$ simultaneously.

The "Simple Example" is the kitchen test case — the smallest dish that proves the parallel kitchen works. Once this is understood, every larger HPC algorithm (matrix multiplication, FFT, convolution, molecular dynamics) is just a more complex kitchen with more chefs and more intricate recipes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics in HPC (Kerala B.Tech context):**
> - **Speedup** ($S_p$) — ratio of serial time to parallel time
> - **Efficiency** ($E_p$) — Speedup divided by the number of processors
> - **FLOPs** — Floating Point Operations per second (computational throughput)
> - **Bandwidth** — measured in **GB/s**, representing memory transfer rate

---

## 1.3 Relevance in the KTU 2024 Scheme

The "Simple Example" topic is **high-yield** because:
1. It is the **first program** students write when learning CUDA/OpenCL/OpenMP.
2. It appears in **Part A (3-mark)** questions as a direct definition or short code-trace.
3. It is the **Part B (14-mark)** favorite — examiners ask students to write a complete parallel program, identify the kernel, and compute speedup.

The example also maps to **Course Outcome CO4** of PECST757: *"Develop parallel programs using contemporary HPC frameworks and evaluate their performance."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of a Simple Parallel Example

The "Simple Example" is typically broken into **six logical stages** that every KTU student must master:

### Stage 1 — Host-Side Memory Allocation
The CPU (host) allocates three arrays in RAM: `A_h`, `B_h`, and `C_h` (the `_h` suffix denotes *host memory*). The size of each array is $N \times \text{sizeof}(\text{double})$.

### Stage 2 — Data Initialization
The host populates `A_h` and `B_h` with sample values. In the canonical example, $a_i = i$ and $b_i = 2i$ so that the expected output is $c_i = 3i$.

### Stage 3 — Memory Transfer (Host → Device)
In a GPU model (CUDA/OpenCL), the host memory is copied to the **device global memory** using functions like `cudaMemcpy()` with the flag `cudaMemcpyHostToDevice`. This is a **bandwidth-bound** operation governed by:

$$T_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{\text{Data Size (bytes)}}{\text{Bandwidth (GB/s)}}$$

### Stage 4 — Kernel Launch (The Parallel Core)
The kernel is launched with a specific **grid-block-thread hierarchy**:
- `gridDim` — number of blocks in the grid
- `blockDim` — number of threads per block
- Total parallel threads: $\text{gridDim} \times \text{blockDim} \geq N$

Each thread computes **one element** $c_i$ using its globally unique thread index:

$$i = \text{blockIdx.x} \times \text{blockDim.x} + \text{threadIdx.x}$$

### Stage 5 — Result Transfer (Device → Host)
The computed array `C_d` is copied back to the host using `cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost`.

### Stage 6 — Validation and Cleanup
The host verifies $c_i = a_i + b_i$ for a sample of indices, then frees memory using `cudaFree()` and `free()`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why each stage matters:**
> - Stages 1–2 and 5–6 are **sequential overhead** (Amdahl's Law penalty).
> - Stage 4 is the **only fully parallel** segment.
> - Reducing sequential overhead is the *entire* challenge of HPC engineering.

## 2.2 Amdahl's Law — The Theoretical Ceiling

For a parallel program with fraction $f$ of code that is strictly serial, the maximum theoretical speedup using $p$ processors is:

$$S_p = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$$

In the vector addition example, if data transfer takes 5% of total runtime ($f = 0.05$), even with $p = 1024$ GPU cores:

$$S_{1024} = \frac{1}{0.05 + \frac{0.95}{1024}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.000928} \approx 19.61$$

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question** — students are expected to compute the achievable speedup and discuss the bottleneck.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_p = \frac{T_s}{T_p}$ | Speedup definition | dimensionless | Direct speedup calculation |
| $E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{T_s}{p \cdot T_p}$ | Parallel efficiency | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Quality of parallelization |
| $S_p = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$ | Amdahl's Law bound | dimensionless | Theoretical max speedup |
| $i = \text{blockIdx.x} \cdot \text{blockDim.x} + \text{threadIdx.x}$ | Global thread index | integer | CUDA/OpenCL indexing |
| $T_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{D}{B}$ | Memory transfer time | seconds | Bandwidth-bound ops |
| $\text{Throughput} = \frac{N}{T_p}$ | Performance rate | ops/second | HPC benchmarking |
| $\text{FLOPs} = 2 \times N$ (for add) | Computational cost | FLOPs | Vector add cost is 1 add = 1 FLOP per element |
| $\text{Grid Size} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{B_s} \right\rceil$ | Number of blocks | integer | CUDA launch configuration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This Simple Example forms the **microbenchmark foundation** for real HPC applications in CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics), weather modeling (WRF), molecular dynamics (GROMACS), and AI training (cuDNN). Every GPU library in production traces its lineage back to this elementary kernel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Computing Speedup for Vector Addition

### Problem Statement
A vector addition program takes **$T_s = 100\,\text{ms}$** to run sequentially on a single CPU core. Of this, the **kernel computation** takes **80 ms**, and the **memory transfer + setup** takes **20 ms**. If the kernel is parallelized on a GPU with **$p = 256$ threads**, compute:
1. The serial fraction $f$
2. The Amdahl's Law speedup $S_p$
3. The parallel efficiency $E_p$

### Step 1 — Identify the Serial and Parallel Portions
- Total serial time: $T_s = T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel}} = 20 + 80 = 100\,\text{ms}$
- Parallel portion (kernel): $T_{\text{parallel}} = 80\,\text{ms}$
- Serial portion (transfer + setup): $T_{\text{serial}} = 20\,\text{ms}$

The serial fraction $f$ is the fraction of code that **cannot** be parallelized:

$$f = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_s} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20$$

Therefore, the parallel fraction is:

$$1 - f = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$$

### Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law
With $p = 256$ parallel threads, the parallel portion executes in:

$$T_p = f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{p}$$

Substituting values:

$$T_p = 0.20 \times 100 + \frac{0.80 \times 100}{256}$$

$$T_p = 20 + \frac{80}{256} = 20 + 0.3125 = 20.3125\,\text{ms}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Speedup

$$S_p = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{100}{20.3125} = 4.923$$

### Step 4 — Compute Parallel Efficiency

$$E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{4.923}{256} = 0.01923 \approx 1.923\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (for KTU valuation):** Even though we used 256 threads, the speedup is only **~4.92×** because the serial 20% dominates. This is the **Amdahl bottleneck** — the central reason real HPC systems invest heavily in reducing host-device transfer overhead (e.g., using pinned memory, asynchronous streams, and unified memory).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Performance Scaling for Vector Addition

| **Processors (p)** | **$T_p$ (ms)** | **$S_p$** | **$E_p$ (%)** | **Comment** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100.000 | 1.00 | 100.00 | Baseline serial |
| 2 | 60.000 | 1.67 | 83.33 | Sub-linear |
| 4 | 40.000 | 2.50 | 62.50 | Sub-linear |
| 8 | 30.000 | 3.33 | 41.67 | Sub-linear |
| 16 | 25.000 | 4.00 | 25.00 | Approaching limit |
| 32 | 22.500 | 4.44 | 13.89 | Diminishing returns |
| 64 | 21.250 | 4.71 | 7.35 | Diminishing returns |
| 128 | 20.625 | 4.85 | 3.79 | Diminishing returns |
| 256 | 20.313 | 4.92 | 1.92 | Diminishing returns |
| 1024 | 20.078 | 4.98 | 0.49 | Near ceiling |
| $\infty$ | 20.000 | 5.00 | 0.00 | Amdahl's hard limit |

**Limit analysis** — as $p \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} S_p = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.00$$

No matter how many GPU cores are added, the program can never run faster than **5×** because the 20% serial portion acts as a hard floor. This is the **Amdahl asymptote**.

## 3.3 Index Mapping Derivation (CUDA/OpenCL)

For an array of size $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ elements using `blockDim.x = 256` threads per block:

$$\text{gridDim.x} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{\text{blockDim.x}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{256} \right\rceil = \lceil 3906.25 \rceil = 3907 \text{ blocks}$$

For a thread in block `blockIdx.x = 5` and `threadIdx.x = 12`:

$$i = 5 \times 256 + 12 = 1280 + 12 = 1292$$

This thread computes:

$$C[1292] = A[1292] + B[1292]$$

A **boundary check** is mandatory because `gridDim.x × blockDim.x = 3907 × 256 = 1{,}000{,}192 > 1{,}000{,}000`. Threads with $i \geq 1{,}000{,}000$ must return early to prevent out-of-bounds memory access — a **common deduction point** in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Simple Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host CPU Application Starts] --> B[Allocate Host Memory A_h, B_h, C_h]
    B --> C[Initialize A_h and B_h with sample data]
    C --> D[Allocate Device Memory A_d, B_d, C_d]
    D --> E[Copy A_h and B_h from Host to Device]
    E --> F[Configure Grid and Block Dimensions]
    F --> G[Launch Parallel Kernel]
    G --> H{Kernel Thread i active}
    H -->|Yes| I[Compute C_d i = A_d i + B_d i]
    I --> J[Write Result to Global Memory]
    J --> K{All threads done}
    H -->|No| K
    K -->|No| H
    K -->|Yes| L[Copy C_d from Device to Host C_h]
    L --> M[Validate Results on Host]
    M --> N[Free Device and Host Memory]
    N --> O[Host Application Terminates]
```

## 4.2 Thread Hierarchy Diagram (CUDA-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1[Grid 1D] --> B0[Block 0 - 256 threads]
    G1 --> B1[Block 1 - 256 threads]
    G1 --> B2[Block 2 - 256 threads]
    G1 --> B3[Block N - boundary threads]
    B0 --> T0[Thread 0 - index 0]
    B0 --> T1[Thread 1 - index 1]
    B0 --> T2[Thread 2 - index 2]
    B0 --> T3[Thread 255 - index 255]
    B1 --> T4[Thread 0 - index 256]
    B1 --> T5[Thread 1 - index 257]
```

## 4.3 Memory Transfer and Execution Timeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as Host CPU
    participant BUS as PCIe Bus
    participant GPU as Device GPU
    CPU->>CPU: Allocate and initialize A_h, B_h
    CPU->>GPU: cudaMalloc A_d, B_d, C_d
    CPU->>BUS: cudaMemcpy HostToDevice
    BUS->>GPU: Transfer A_h, B_h
    CPU->>GPU: Launch kernel grid
    Note over GPU: Threads compute C_d in parallel
    GPU->>BUS: cudaMemcpy DeviceToHost
    BUS->>CPU: Transfer C_d to C_h
    CPU->>CPU: Validate and free
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** Notice how the PCIe bus transfer creates a **bottleneck** between Stages 3 and 5. This visual is exactly what examiners draw when they ask "Identify the performance bottleneck in this parallel program." The answer is always the **memory transfer phase**, governed by Amdahl's serial fraction $f$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **embarrassingly parallel** computation. State why vector addition qualifies as one.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An embarrassingly parallel computation is one in which the sub-tasks (or work units) can be executed **independently and concurrently** with **minimal or no inter-process communication**. (1 mark)

In vector addition $\vec{C} = \vec{A} + \vec{B}$, each output element $c_i = a_i + b_i$ depends **only** on the corresponding input elements $a_i$ and $b_i$. (1 mark) There is no data dependency between any two elements $c_i$ and $c_j$ for $i \neq j$, so all $N$ additions can be assigned to $N$ parallel threads with no synchronization required. (1 mark)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Write the CUDA expression to compute the global thread index $i$ for a 1D grid of 1D blocks.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The global thread index in a 1D grid of 1D blocks is computed as:

$$i = \text{blockIdx.x} \times \text{blockDim.x} + \text{threadIdx.x}$$

Here, $\text{blockIdx.x}$ is the block index within the grid, $\text{blockDim.x}$ is the number of threads per block, and $\text{threadIdx.x}$ is the thread's index within its block. (3 marks — full expression 2 marks, variable explanation 1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Program + Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Write a complete CUDA program for parallel vector addition of two arrays of size $N = 500{,}000$. The program should include host memory allocation, device memory allocation, host-to-device transfer, kernel launch, device-to-host transfer, and result validation. Assume 256 threads per block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the sequential execution time on a CPU is **40 ms** and the parallel execution time on a GPU is **5 ms** using **$p = 200$** threads, calculate the **speedup**, **efficiency**, and identify the **bottleneck** using **Amdahl's Law** if the serial fraction is **$f = 0.05$**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Complete CUDA Program (7 marks):**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <cuda.h>

#define N 500000
#define BLOCK_SIZE 256

__global__ void vectorAdd(int *A, int *B, int *C, int n) {
    int i = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x;
    if (i < n) {
        C[i] = A[i] + B[i];
    }
}

int main(void) {
    int *A_h, *B_h, *C_h;
    int *A_d, *B_d, *C_d;
    size_t size = N * sizeof(int);

    // Stage 1: Host allocation
    A_h = (int*)malloc(size);
    B_h = (int*)malloc(size);
    C_h = (int*)malloc(size);

    // Stage 2: Initialize
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        A_h[i] = i;
        B_h[i] = 2 * i;
    }

    // Stage 3: Device allocation
    cudaMalloc((void**)&A_d, size);
    cudaMalloc((void**)&B_d, size);
    cudaMalloc((void**)&C_d, size);

    // Stage 4: Host to Device transfer
    cudaMemcpy(A_d, A_h, size, cudaMemcpyHostToDevice);
    cudaMemcpy(B_d, B_h, size, cudaMemcpyHostToDevice);

    // Stage 5: Kernel launch
    int gridSize = (N + BLOCK_SIZE - 1) / BLOCK_SIZE;
    vectorAdd<<<gridSize, BLOCK_SIZE>>>(A_d, B_d, C_d, N);
    cudaDeviceSynchronize();

    // Stage 6: Device to Host transfer
    cudaMemcpy(C_h, C_d, size, cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost);

    // Stage 7: Validate
    for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        if (C_h[i] != A_h[i] + B_h[i]) {
            printf("Mismatch at index %d\n", i);
            break;
        }
    }

    // Cleanup
    cudaFree(A_d);
    cudaFree(B_d);
    cudaFree(C_d);
    free(A_h);
    free(B_h);
    free(C_h);
    return 0;
}
```

**Mark Distribution (a):**
- Host allocation + initialization — 1.5 marks
- Device allocation + transfer — 1.5 marks
- Kernel definition with correct index formula — 2 marks
- Kernel launch with correct `gridSize` — 1 mark
- Validation and cleanup — 1 mark

**(b) Performance Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $T_s = 40\,\text{ms}$, $T_p = 5\,\text{ms}$, $p = 200$, $f = 0.05$

**Step 1 — Speedup:**

$$S_p = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{40}{5} = 8.00$$

**Step 2 — Efficiency:**

$$E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{8.00}{200} = 0.04 = 4\%$$

**Step 3 — Amdahl's Law theoretical bound:**

$$S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + \frac{0.95}{200}}$$

$$S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.00475} = \frac{1}{0.05475} \approx 18.26$$

**Step 4 — Bottleneck identification:**

The achieved speedup (8×) is far below the Amdahl bound (18.26×), indicating **non-ideal scaling**. The bottleneck is the **serial fraction $f = 0.05$** (memory transfer + setup), which cannot be parallelized. To improve performance, reduce $f$ by:
- Using **pinned (page-locked) memory** for faster host-device transfer
- Using **CUDA streams** to overlap transfer with computation
- Using **unified memory** to avoid explicit transfers

**Mark Distribution (b):**
- Correct speedup formula and value — 1.5 marks
- Correct efficiency formula and value — 1.5 marks
- Correct Amdahl's Law substitution — 2 marks
- Bottleneck identification and optimization suggestions — 2 marks

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Comprehensive Question

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **six stages** of a typical simple parallel program (vector addition) on a GPU. For each stage, indicate whether it is **host-side**, **device-side**, or **transfer** operation, and whether it is **parallelizable**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simple parallel program runs in **12 seconds** on a single core. When parallelized on **$p = 8$ cores**, it takes **2 seconds**. The serial fraction of the program is found to be **$f = 0.10$**. Verify the result using **Amdahl's Law**, and comment on whether the parallelization is **efficient**. If not, suggest **two techniques** to improve performance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Six Stages of a Simple Parallel Program (7 marks):**

| **Stage** | **Operation** | **Location** | **Parallelizable?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Host memory allocation (`malloc`) | Host | No |
| 2 | Data initialization | Host | No |
| 3 | Device memory allocation (`cudaMalloc`) | Host-API call to device | No |
| 4 | Host → Device transfer (`cudaMemcpy`) | Transfer (PCIe) | No (bandwidth-limited) |
| 5 | Kernel launch and execution | Device (GPU) | **Yes (fully parallel)** |
| 6 | Device → Host transfer + validation | Transfer (PCIe) + Host | No |

**Mark Distribution (a):**
- Six stages listed correctly — 3 marks
- Correct classification of each stage — 2 marks
- Parallelizability identified correctly — 2 marks

**(b) Amdahl's Law Verification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute actual speedup:**

$$S_p = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{12}{2} = 6.00$$

**Step 2 — Compute parallel efficiency:**

$$E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{6.00}{8} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

**Step 3 — Apply Amdahl's Law with $f = 0.10$ and $p = 8$:**

$$S_{\text{Amdahl}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + \frac{0.90}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.10 + 0.1125} = \frac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.71$$

**Step 4 — Observation:**

The actual speedup (6.00) is **greater** than the Amdahl prediction (4.71). This seems contradictory, but it indicates that the **measured $f = 0.10$** was likely **overestimated**, or there is some **super-linear** effect due to cache reuse. The efficiency of 75% is reasonably good but not excellent.

**Step 5 — Improvement techniques:**

1. **Reduce serial fraction** by overlapping computation with communication using asynchronous streams.
2. **Increase workload granularity** by using larger block sizes or grid-stride loops to reduce kernel launch overhead.
3. **Use shared memory** to reduce redundant global memory accesses.

**Mark Distribution (b):**
- Speedup and efficiency calculation — 2 marks
- Amdahl's Law computation — 2 marks
- Comparative analysis (actual vs theoretical) — 1.5 marks
- Two valid optimization techniques — 1.5 marks

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary check** `if (i < n)` inside the kernel — **−1.5 marks** deduction. If $N$ is not a multiple of `BLOCK_SIZE`, threads with $i \geq N$ will cause **out-of-bounds memory access** and undefined behavior.
> 2. **Missing `cudaDeviceSynchronize()`** after kernel launch — **−0.5 marks**. Without it, `cudaMemcpy` may begin before the kernel finishes, leading to **race conditions**.
> 3. **Confusing host and device pointers** in `cudaMemcpy` arguments — **−1 mark**. The first argument is the **destination**, not the source.
> 4. **Incorrect grid size** — using `N / BLOCK_SIZE` instead of `(N + BLOCK_SIZE - 1) / BLOCK_SIZE` causes the **last chunk of elements to be skipped** — **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Not stating the Amdahl asymptote** $1/f$ in 14-mark answers — **−1 mark** deduction. Examiners expect the student to explicitly derive the theoretical limit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "A Simple Example" (Module 4, PECST757)**

### Core Concepts
- **Embarrassingly Parallel:** No inter-task data dependency. Vector addition is the canonical example.
- **Vector Addition Formula:** $c_i = a_i + b_i$, each $c_i$ is computed by one thread.
- **CUDA Index Formula:** $i = \text{blockIdx.x} \times \text{blockDim.x} + \text{threadIdx.x}$
- **Grid Size Formula:** $\text{gridDim} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{\text{blockDim}} \right\rceil$

### Performance Metrics
- **Speedup:** $S_p = T_s / T_p$
- **Efficiency:** $E_p = S_p / p$
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S_p = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$
- **Amdahl's Asymptote:** $\lim_{p \to \infty} S_p = 1/f$ — the **hard speedup ceiling**

### Six Stages of a Simple GPU Program
1. Host memory allocation
2. Host data initialization
3. Device memory allocation
4. Host → Device memory transfer
5. Kernel launch (only parallel stage)
6. Device → Host transfer + validation + cleanup

### Must-Remember Code Safeguards
- Always include the **boundary check** `if (i < n)` in the kernel.
- Always use **`cudaDeviceSynchronize()`** before reading device results.
- Always use the **ceiling division** `(N + B - 1) / B` for grid size.
- Always **free** both host (`free()`) and device (`cudaFree()`) memory.

### Common Exam Traps
- Confusing **grid dimension** with **block dimension**.
- Forgetting that **memory transfer** is serial and counts toward $f$.
- Assuming **more threads = linear speedup** (Amdahl's Law disproves this).
- Forgetting to mention that the **last block may have idle threads** if $N$ is not a multiple of `BLOCK_SIZE`.

### Real-World Connection
- The "Simple Example" is the **microbenchmark** for every production GPU library: cuBLAS, cuDNN, cuFFT, and TensorRT all contain vector addition as a primitive operation.
- The **6-stage flow** is identical for all GPU programs — only the kernel changes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Messages and Point-to-Point Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Messages and Point-to-Point Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Message

In the context of High Performance Computing (HPC) and the Message Passing Interface (MPI) standard, a **message** is a logically contiguous block of data that is exchanged between two or more cooperating processes executing on potentially different processing elements. Formally, a message $M$ is a tuple defined as:

$$ M = (\text{buffer}, \text{count}, \text{datatype}, \text{communicator}, \text{tag}, \text{source}, \text{destination}) $$

where the **buffer** is the contiguous memory location, **count** specifies the number of elements, **datatype** defines the elemental type, **communicator** defines the process group, **tag** is a non-negative integer used for matching, and **source/destination** identify the rank(s) of the participating process(es).

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Point-to-Point Communication

**Point-to-Point Communication** (PPC) is the fundamental communication paradigm in HPC where exactly **one** sending process transmits a message to exactly **one** receiving process. The complete communication operation involves three distinct phases:

1. **Local preparation phase** – the sender assembles data into a contiguous send buffer.
2. **Transport phase** – data traverses the underlying network fabric (shared memory, InfiniBand, Ethernet, etc.).
3. **Receipt phase** – the destination process places the data into a receive buffer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Point-to-Point Communication is the atomic building block upon which all collective operations (broadcasts, reductions, barriers) are constructed. Mastering PPC is mandatory for solving all subsequent HPC problems on parallel architectures.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Think of **Point-to-Point Communication** like a **registered postal courier system** between two houses in a neighborhood:

- The **letter (message)** contains the actual content (payload).
- The **envelope** contains the *recipient address (destination rank)*, the *sender address (source rank)*, and a *reference number (tag)* for tracking.
- The **post office (MPI runtime / network)** ensures the letter is delivered reliably.
- The **recipient must be home** to accept the letter (receive operation must be posted) — otherwise the letter may bounce back (deadlock) or remain unclaimed.

Similarly, just like a courier offers multiple service levels — *standard mail*, *registered post*, *speed post*, *overnight express* — MPI provides multiple **communication modes** (standard, buffered, synchronous, ready), each offering different trade-offs between speed, safety, and synchronization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** All operations discussed follow the **MPI 3.1 standard** (Message Passing Interface Forum, 2015), which remains the canonical reference for HPC message-passing on distributed memory systems.

## 1.4 Message Envelope vs. Message Payload

| Component | Purpose | Analogy |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **Envelope** | Routing information (source, destination, tag, communicator) | Address on an envelope |
| **Payload** | Actual data being transferred (count × datatype) | Letter content inside |
| **Datatype** | Type signature defining how bytes map to elements | Language of the letter |

The **datatype signature** ensures portability — the same MPI program runs correctly regardless of whether the underlying architecture is 32-bit or 64-bit, big-endian or little-endian.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Message Transfer

The complete point-to-point message transfer consists of **five logical steps**, which map to the operation of every MPI send/receive call:

1. **Marshalling:** The application packs user data from non-contiguous memory into a contiguous send buffer, converting native types into a network-portable representation.
2. **Enveloping:** The MPI runtime attaches metadata (source rank, destination rank, tag, communicator handle, datatype map).
3. **Transmission:** Data is serialized, segmented into packets, and pushed onto the network interface card (NIC) via Direct Memory Access (DMA).
4. **Routing & Reception:** The destination NIC receives packets, the MPI layer verifies the envelope, and data is copied into the destination process's receive buffer.
5. **Demarshalling & Synchronization:** The runtime signals completion via the request handle, allowing the application to safely reuse the buffer.

## 2.2 Communication Modes — The Core Conceptual Hierarchy

The MPI standard defines two orthogonal dimensions: **communication type** (blocking vs. non-blocking) and **communication mode** (standard, buffered, synchronous, ready).

> [!NOTE]
