> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice**. The two alternatives are completely independent, allowing the examiner to assess depth on either dimension of the module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the three-phase model of nonblocking point-to-point communication in MPI. Describe the role of `MPI_Request` and the various `MPI_Wait*` and `MPI_Test*` completion routines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel application executes 100 iterations. Each iteration performs 40 ms of computation and sends a 2 MB message. The blocking send takes 25 ms; with nonblocking send and full overlap, what is the speedup? Show all calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The three-phase model of nonblocking communication is:

**Phase 1 — Initiation (1.5 marks):**
- User calls `MPI_Isend` or `MPI_Irecv`.
- MPI library copies call arguments (buffer, count, datatype, rank, tag, communicator) into an internal control block.
- A new `MPI_Request` handle is allocated and returned to the user.
- The call returns *immediately* without waiting for any data transfer.

**Phase 2 — Data Movement / Asynchronous Progress (1.5 marks):**
- The MPI runtime progresses the data transfer concurrently with the calling process's execution.
- Depending on message size and protocol (eager vs. rendezvous), the runtime may:
  - Copy data into an internal network buffer (eager), or
  - Perform a handshake with the receiver (rendezvous), or
  - Use shared-memory copy (intra-node).
- The user process is *free* to execute useful computation in parallel.

**Phase 3 — Completion (2 marks):**
- The user *must* verify completion via the `MPI_Wait*` or `MPI_Test*` family.
- `MPI_Request` is the opaque handle that uniquely identifies the pending operation.
- Completion routines:
  - `MPI_Wait` — blocks until a *specific* request completes.
  - `MPI_Test` — non-blocking poll returning a *flag*.
  - `MPI_Waitany`, `MPI_Waitall`, `MPI_Waitsome` — operate on arrays of requests.
  - `MPI_Testany`, `MPI_Testall`, `MPI_Testsome` — non-blocking versions.
- After successful completion, the user buffer is safe to reuse.

**Persistent Communication (1 mark):**
- `MPI_Send_init` and `MPI_Recv_init` create *templates* that can be re-triggered with `MPI_Start`, saving per-call setup cost.
- Used in iterative solvers and time-stepping codes.

**Best Practice (1 mark):**
- Deallocate with `MPI_Request_free` to avoid resource leaks.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $N_{iter} = 100$ iterations
- $T_{comp} = 40$ ms per iteration
- $T_{comm} = 25$ ms per iteration (blocking equivalent)

**Step 1 — Total time with blocking approach (2 marks):**

$$T_{block} = N_{iter} \times (T_{comp} + T_{comm}) = 100 \times (40 + 25) = 100 \times 65 = 6500\,ms$$

**Step 2 — Total time with nonblocking + full overlap (3 marks):**

With nonblocking send and perfect overlap, the per-iteration time becomes:

$$T_{iter}^{NB} = \max(T_{comp}, T_{comm}) = \max(40, 25) = 40\,ms$$

Therefore:

$$T_{NB} = N_{iter} \times T_{iter}^{NB} = 100 \times 40 = 4000\,ms$$

**Step 3 — Speedup (2 marks):**

$$S = \frac{T_{block}}{T_{NB}} = \frac{6500}{4000} = 1.625$$

**Answer:** The speedup is **1.625×** (i.e., the nonblocking version is **62.5% faster**, saving **2500 ms** overall).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Compare the four nonblocking send modes (`MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Ibsend`, `MPI_Issend`, `MPI_Irsend`) with respect to local return conditions, buffer-state guarantees, and deadlock risk. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of persistent nonblocking communication. Write the function signatures for `MPI_Send_init`, `MPI_Recv_init`, and `MPI_Start`. State two practical scenarios where persistent communication provides a measurable performance benefit. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Mode | Local Return | Buffer Reuse Safe | Deadlock Risk | Internal Action |
|:-----|:-------------|:------------------|:-------------:|:----------------|
| **`MPI_Isend`** (Standard) | Immediately (within eager limit) | After `MPI_Wait` | Medium | Eager buffer copy or rendezvous handshake |
| **`MPI_Ibsend`** (Buffered) | After buffer copy to user `MPI_Buffer_attach` | After `MPI_Wait` | Low | Always copies to user buffer |
| **`MPI_Issend`** (Synchronous) | Only after matching `MPI_Recv` has *started* | After `MPI_Wait` | High if no matching recv | Always rendezvous protocol |
| **`MPI_Irsend`** (Ready) | Immediately, **only if** matching recv is posted | After `MPI_Wait` | **Very high** if recv missing | User assumes recv is ready |

**Key Insights (2 marks):**
- The four modes form a **spectrum of trade-offs** between *eager local return* and *delivery guarantees*.
- `MPI_Isend` is the most commonly used (default choice); `MPI_Issend` is safest; `MPI_Ibsend` is preferred when buffer memory is plentiful; `MPI_Irsend` is rarely used in modern HPC because of its strict precondition.

**Deadlock Analysis:**
- Blocking `MPI_Send` + `MPI_Recv` on two processes can deadlock if both call `MPI_Send` first (when messages exceed the eager limit).
- Nonblocking `MPI_Isend` + `MPI_Irecv` from both sides **never deadlocks** because each side posts a receive *before* (or simultaneously with) the send.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Concept of Persistent Communication (3 marks):**

Persistent nonblocking communication allows a *communication pattern* to be **pre-initialized once** and **reused many times**. It is a two-step mechanism:

1. **Initialization** (one-time, low overhead): Create a *persistent request* template via `MPI_Send_init` or `MPI_Recv_init`. No data transfer occurs at this step.
2. **Activation** (per-iteration): Trigger the saved template via `MPI_Start` (single) or `MPI_Startall` (array). The transfer happens *exactly* as if a fresh `MPI_Isend`/`MPI_Irecv` had been called, but with **reduced per-call overhead**.

**Function Signatures (2 marks):**

```
int MPI_Send_init(const void *buf, int count, MPI_Datatype datatype,
                  int dest, int tag, MPI_Comm comm, MPI_Request *request);

int MPI_Recv_init(void *buf, int count, MPI_Datatype datatype,
                  int source, int tag, MPI_Comm comm, MPI_Request *request);

int MPI_Start(MPI_Request *request);

int MPI_Startall(int count, MPI_Request array_of_requests);
```

**Practical Scenarios (2 marks):**

1. **Iterative Linear Solvers (e.g., Conjugate Gradient, GMRES):** Each iteration sends/receives the same halo region in a domain-decomposed mesh. Persistent requests avoid re-parsing the argument list and re-building the internal match queue — saving microseconds per call × thousands of iterations.

2. **Time-Stepping PDE Codes (e.g., explicit Euler for heat equation):** Each timestep exchanges boundary data with the same neighbors using the same buffer layout. Persistent communication amortizes the argument-copy cost and reduces per-step latency by 10–30% in production codes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Module 4 Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `MPI_Request` argument** in function signatures (deducted 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing `MPI_Wait` (returns status) with `MPI_Test` (returns flag + status)** — a recurring error worth 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Assuming nonblocking *always* gives a speedup** — without sufficient computation to overlap, the overhead may dominate. Students must explicitly state the *overlap condition* $T_{comp} \geq T_{comm}$ for net speedup.
> 4. **Mixing up modes** — for example, calling `MPI_Irsend` without a prior matching receive. Examiners test this with a "what happens?" question.
> 5. **Buffer-reuse rule violation** — using the send buffer before `MPI_Wait` completes. Worth 1–2 marks if not mentioned.
> 6. **Confusing "nonblocking" with "asynchronous"** — nonblocking refers to *local* return semantics; the actual transfer may still be synchronous or buffered internally. Examiners appreciate precise wording.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Nonblocking Point-to-Point Communication**

- [x] **Definition:** Nonblocking calls return *immediately* with an `MPI_Request` handle.
- [x] **Three phases:** Initiation → Asynchronous Data Movement → Explicit Completion.
- [x] **Key nonblocking sends:** `MPI_Isend`, `MPI_Ibsend`, `MPI_Issend`, `MPI_Irsend`.
- [x] **Key nonblocking receives:** `MPI_Irecv` (only one form).
- [x] **Completion routines:** `MPI_Wait`, `MPI_Test` (and their `*any`/`*all`/`*some` variants).
- [x] **Buffer reuse rule:** *Never* reuse a nonblocking send/recv buffer until `MPI_Wait` or successful `MPI_Test`.
- [x] **Persistent communication:** `MPI_Send_init`, `MPI_Recv_init`, `MPI_Start`, `MPI_Startall`, `MPI_Request_free`.
- [x] **Performance model:** Hockney $T_{comm} = T_0 + B \cdot T_b + T_c$.
- [x] **Overlap efficiency:** $\eta = T_{overlap}/T_{comm}$; ideal $\eta = 1$.
- [x] **Speedup condition:** Nonblocking helps *only if* $T_{comp}$ is comparable to $T_{comm}$.
- [x] **Deadlock safety:** Nonblocking sends/receives are inherently deadlock-free when both sides post a receive.
- [x] **Constants to remember:** `MPI_REQUEST_NULL`, `MPI_ANY_SOURCE`, `MPI_ANY_TAG`, `MPI_SUCCESS`.
- [x] **Real-world impact:** Used in CESM, GROMACS, PETSc, Horovod — typical speedup 2×–5×.
- [x] **KTU 2024 keywords to memorize:** *latency hiding*, *communication–computation overlap*, *asynchronous progress*, *request handle*, *persistent request*.
- [x] **Common exam question types:** Compare blocking vs nonblocking (table); explain three phases; compute speedup; list four send modes; explain persistent communication.

> **Final Tip:** When in doubt on a KTU 2024 exam, **draw the three-phase diagram** — it earns partial credit even if the rest of the answer is incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Topologies - Example: MPI Parallelization of a Jacobi Solver - MPI Implementation - Performance Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST757 - HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Virtual Topologies - Example: MPI Parallelization of a Jacobi Solver - MPI Implementation - Performance Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Virtual Topologies — Formal Definition

In the **Message Passing Interface (MPI)** standard, a **Virtual Topology** is an abstract coordinate-based naming scheme superimposed on the set of MPI processes, which maps each process onto a structured communication graph. Processes are organized in a way that reflects the *logical* communication pattern of the parallel application, even though the *physical* (hardware) interconnect may be a generic network.

According to the MPI standard, two principal virtual topology constructors are provided:

1. **Cartesian Topologies** — `MPI_Cart_create` and friends (grids, tori, meshes)
2. **Graph Topologies** — `MPI_Graph_create` and `MPI_Dist_graph_create` (arbitrary sparse graphs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST757 Module 4):**
> Virtual topology is *not* a physical reconfiguration of the network. It is a **process-grouping and addressing abstraction** that allows programmers to write algorithms in a way that mirrors the natural geometry of the data.

A virtual topology provides:
- **Convenient naming** of processes (e.g., coordinates `(i, j)` instead of rank `17`).
- **Efficient collective communication** tailored to the topology (e.g., neighbor collectives in MPI 4.0).
- **A clean model for partitioning** regular data structures such as matrices and grids.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Newsroom Control Room

Imagine a giant national newspaper with **256 reporters** spread across the country, but all reporting to a single chief editor. The chief editor has two options:

| Option | Description | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat Address** | Identify reporters by flat ID 0…255 and broadcast instructions over a single phone line. | Slow, confusing, prone to mix-ups. |
| **Virtual Topology** | Arrange reporters in a 16 × 16 grid on a whiteboard: "North-Block row 4, column 7." | The editor can whisper to neighbours, route stories along rows/columns, and parallelize the press run naturally. |

The whiteboard grid is the **virtual topology** — it does not change where the reporters physically sit, but it dramatically simplifies the editor's communication logic. MPI's virtual topologies do exactly the same for parallel programs: they impose a *grid/graph mental model* on the processes so the algorithm becomes readable, and the runtime can exploit locality of communication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term Mapping for Exam Writing:**
> - **Logical Topology** = the *user-defined* virtual grid/graph
> - **Physical Topology** = the *actual* network (FAT-tree, torus, dragonfly, etc.)
> - **Neighborhood** = the set of processes that the topology declares as adjacent
> - **Reordering** = MPI is *allowed* to physically remap ranks for performance; logical adjacency is preserved.

## 1.3 Why Virtual Topologies Matter in HPC

A student new to HPC often asks: *"If I know my neighbour ranks, why bother creating a topology?"* The answer has three pillars:

1. **Algorithmic clarity** — Code mirrors the mathematics of the problem.
2. **Portability** — The same program runs on 4, 64, or 4096 cores by changing one parameter.
3. **Performance** — MPI libraries can optimise neighbour communication (Cartesian-aware algorithms, hardware-MPI mapping) and use efficient collectives (`MPI_Neighbor_allgather`).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Mnemonic — "TCP":**
> - **T**opology-aware algorithms
> - **C**ode clarity & modularity
> - **P**erformance via locality

## 1.4 The Jacobi Solver — Why It Is the Canonical Example

The **Jacobi iterative method** for solving 2D Poisson/Laplace equations is the *de facto* teaching example in HPC courses worldwide because:

- It exposes a **regular stencil** (4-point or 9-point) — perfect for Cartesian decomposition.
- It has **strict data-dependency boundaries** (a cell only needs neighbour cells of the previous iteration).
- It is **embarrassingly regular**, making it ideal to discuss load balance, boundary halos, and ghost-cell exchange.
- It exhibits **near-linear strong scaling** up to a point, then suffers from communication-to-computation ratio decay — a beautiful illustration of performance properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A Jacobi iteration for a 2D grid with $N \times N$ interior points computes, for every cell $(i,j)$:
> $$u^{(k+1)}_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\left(u^{(k)}_{i+1,j} + u^{(k)}_{i-1,j} + u^{(k)}_{i,j+1} + u^{(k)}_{i,j-1}\right) - \frac{h^2}{4}f_{i,j}$$
> The superscript $(k)$ is the iteration number. Each cell needs the four *cardinal* neighbours from the *previous* iteration — a textbook **nearest-neighbour communication pattern**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of MPI Virtual Topologies

### 2.1.1 Cartesian Topology

A Cartesian topology organises $P$ processes into a $d$-dimensional grid with dimensions $\mathbf{n} = (n_0, n_1, \ldots, n_{d-1})$ such that $\prod_{i=0}^{d-1} n_i = P$.

**Key MPI Routines (KTU exam favourites):**

| Routine | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `MPI_Cart_create` | Build a new communicator with Cartesian layout. |
| `MPI_Dims_create` | Suggest a balanced factorization of $P$ into a $d$-D grid. |
| `MPI_Cart_coords` | Convert a rank to its coordinates in the grid. |
| `MPI_Cart_rank` | Convert coordinates to a rank. |
| `MPI_Cart_shift` | Compute the rank of a neighbour in a given direction (very useful for Jacobi stencils). |
| `MPI_Cart_sub` | Split a Cartesian grid into lower-dimensional sub-grids. |

**Periodicity:**
- `periods[i] = 0` → non-periodic (boundary, "mesh")
- `periods[i] = 1` → periodic ("torus" — wrap-around)

> [!NOTE]
> For 1D Jacobi solver we use a **1D Cartesian topology with `periods=0`**. For 2D Jacobi we use a **2D Cartesian grid** matching the natural 2D data layout.

### 2.1.2 Graph Topology

A graph topology is more general — it allows arbitrary directed edges between processes. Two variants exist:
- **General Graph** (`MPI_Graph_create`) — deprecated in MPI-3, but still examined.
- **Distributed Graph** (`MPI_Dist_graph_create`) — modern, scalable, MPI-3+.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Check:** If the problem is a *grid/stencil*, use **Cartesian**. If the problem is *irregular* (e.g., sparse matrix, FEM mesh), use **Graph**.

## 2.2 The Jacobi Solver — Parallelisation Strategy

The standard 2D Jacobi solver with a $P = p_x \times p_y$ process grid uses **domain decomposition**:

1. Split the global $N \times N$ grid into $p_x \times p_y$ sub-grids, each owned by one process.
2. Each sub-grid has halo rows/columns on the four sides, holding copies of the *neighbouring* sub-grid's boundary.
3. At each iteration:
   a. **Local computation** on interior points.
   b. **Halo exchange** with the four neighbours.
   c. **Convergence test** (global reduction of local residuals).

### 2.2.1 Halo Exchange Communication Pattern

The halo (also called *ghost cells*) is the buffer of boundary data that must be exchanged at every iteration. For a 2D Cartesian grid:

- Each process sends its **top row** to the **upper neighbour** and receives its **bottom halo**.
- Each process sends its **bottom row** to the **lower neighbour** and receives its **top halo**.
- The same applies to **left/right** columns.

This results in **4 point-to-point messages per iteration per process** (or fewer at physical boundaries).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **persistent communication** pattern (using `MPI_Send_init`/`MPI_Recv_init` + `MPI_Startall`) is preferred because the same buffers are exchanged every iteration. This avoids repeated setup overhead of non-blocking calls.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **commonly tested** in KTU university exams. Memorise the table; the symbols are universal in HPC literature.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Global problem size** | $W$ (FLOP) or $N$ (grid points) | Total work of one Jacobi iteration on the *serial* code is $5N^2$ FLOPs for 4-point stencil. |
| 2 | **Parallel runtime** | $T_P = T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}} + T_{\text{idle}}$ | Amdahl-like decomposition |
| 3 | **Computation time per process** | $T_{\text{comp}} = \dfrac{5N^2}{P} \cdot t_c$ | $t_c$ = time per FLOP, $P$ = #processes |
| 4 | **Communication time per iteration (2D)** | $T_{\text{comm}} = \alpha \cdot 4 + \beta \cdot 4m$ | $\alpha$ = latency (s), $\beta$ = bandwidth$^{-1}$ (s/byte), $m$ = halo message size in bytes |
| 5 | **Halo message size per neighbour (2D)** | $m = n_x \cdot n_y \cdot \text{sizeof(double)}$ | $n_x, n_y$ = sub-grid dimensions including halos |
| 6 | **Speedup** | $S_P = \dfrac{T_1}{T_P}$ | Ratio; ideal = $P$ |
| 7 | **Efficiency** | $E_P = \dfrac{S_P}{P} = \dfrac{T_1}{P \cdot T_P}$ | $0 < E_P \le 1$ |
| 8 | **Amdahl's Law** | $S_P \le \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{P}}$ | $f$ = serial fraction |
| 9 | **Gustafson's Law (scaled speedup)** | $S_P = f + (1-f) \cdot P$ | Work grows with $P$ |
| 10 | **Hockney model (message time)** | $t_m = \alpha + \beta \cdot m$ | Linear in message size $m$ |
| 11 | **Iso-efficiency function** | $W = \mathcal{O}(P \cdot T_{\text{comm}})$ | Work needed to keep $E$ constant |
| 12 | **Strong scaling efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{t_m}{t_c} \cdot \dfrac{P}{N^2}}$ | For Jacobi, halo grows as $N/\sqrt{P}$ |
| 13 | **Karplus–Flatt metric** | $e = \dfrac{T_1/P - T_P}{T_1 - T_P} = f$ | Numerically estimates serial fraction |
| 14 | **Surface-to-volume ratio** | $\text{STV} = \dfrac{\text{halo cells}}{\text{interior cells}} = \dfrac{4\sqrt{P}}{N/\sqrt{P}}$ | Drives the communication bound |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all table rows above, the symbol `\vert` and `\mid` are used in place of vertical bars `|` so that markdown table syntax is preserved. Students copying formulas into an answer sheet should write $\mid \cdot \mid$ in LaTeX form for absolute value.

## 2.4 Performance Properties — The Three Pillars

A KTU 14-mark question often asks: *"Discuss the performance properties of MPI parallelisation of the Jacobi solver."* The expected answer must cover the **three classical HPC performance properties**:

### Pillar 1 — Strong Scaling
- **Definition:** Keep the *total problem size $N$* fixed; increase the number of processes $P$.
- **What it measures:** How fast does the parallel code execute on $P$ cores compared to 1 core?
- **Jacobi behaviour:** Initially $S_P \approx P$ (perfect). As $P$ grows, the halo-to-interior ratio grows $\propto \sqrt{P}$ and eventually dominates. **Strong scaling hits a wall** when each process has only a handful of cells.

### Pillar 2 — Weak Scaling
- **Definition:** Keep the *work per process* fixed; increase $P$ (and total problem size $N^2 \propto P$).
- **What it measures:** How well does the algorithm handle growing problem sizes?
- **Jacobi behaviour:** With careful halo sizing, **weak scaling is close to ideal** because the halo fraction per process is constant. This is why Jacobi is called *weakly scalable*.

### Pillar 3 — Iso-Efficiency
- **Definition:** The total work $W(P)$ required to keep efficiency $E$ constant as $P$ grows.
- **Jacobi result:** $W(P) = \mathcal{O}(P^{3/2})$ for 2D Jacobi on a $P$-process grid with the *best* partitioning.
- This means the solver has **good but not perfect** scalability — closer to optimal than dense linear algebra ($\mathcal{O}(P^2)$), but worse than FFT ($\mathcal{O}(P \log P)$).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

The 2D Jacobi / Poisson solver is the workhorse of:
- **Computational electromagnetics** (finite-difference time-domain).
- **Computational fluid dynamics** (steady-state heat, Navier–Stokes pressure-Poisson).
- **Image processing** (inpainting, anisotropic diffusion).
- **Graphics** (Laplacian mesh smoothing).
- **Geophysics** (gravitational potential of terrain).

Performance properties of such solvers directly inform the design of *stencil libraries* (e.g., YASK, HALO) and the choice of interconnect (e.g., dragonfly vs. torus for global halos).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Parallel Jacobi Iteration Runtime

We derive the per-iteration runtime $T_{\text{iter}}$ for a 2D Jacobi solver on a $p_x \times p_y$ Cartesian process grid solving a global $N \times N$ grid.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let:
- Global grid: $N \times N$ interior cells.
- Process grid: $p_x \times p_y$, so each process owns an $n_x \times n_y$ sub-grid where
$$n_x = \left\lceil \dfrac{N}{p_x} \right\rceil, \quad n_y = \left\lceil \dfrac{N}{p_y} \right\rceil.$$

- Each sub-grid has 4 halos (top, bottom, left, right) of size $n_x$ (horizontal) and $n_y$ (vertical) doubles.

### 3.1.2 Computation Time

**Step 1 — Count FLOPs per interior point:**

For each interior cell, the Jacobi update is:
$$u_{i,j}^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{4}\left(u_{i+1,j} + u_{i-1,j} + u_{i,j+1} + u_{i,j-1}\right) - \frac{h^2}{4}f_{i,j}.$$
This requires 4 additions, 1 multiplication by $\tfrac{1}{4}$, and 1 multiplication by $-\tfrac{h^2}{4}f_{i,j}$ (precomputed) — total **6 FLOPs per cell**.

**Step 2 — Total FLOPs per process:**

Number of interior cells per process is approximately $n_x \cdot n_y$. Hence:
$$F_{\text{proc}} = 6 \cdot n_x \cdot n_y.$$

**Step 3 — Computation time:**

$$T_{\text{comp}} = F_{\text{proc}} \cdot t_c = 6 n_x n_y \cdot t_c.$$

### 3.1.3 Communication Time

**Step 4 — Message sizes:**

Each halo message (e.g., top-row exchange) is $n_x$ doubles. With 8 bytes per double:
$$m = 8 \cdot n_x \quad \text{(bytes, vertical halos)}, \quad m' = 8 \cdot n_y \quad \text{(bytes, horizontal halos)}.$$

**Step 5 — Latency × message count:**

Each process performs 2 vertical + 2 horizontal exchanges = 4 messages per iteration. Using the Hockney model $t_m = \alpha + \beta m$:

$$T_{\text{comm}} = 4\alpha + 2 \beta m + 2 \beta m'.$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$T_{\text{comm}} = 4\alpha + 2\beta(8 n_x) + 2\beta(8 n_y) = 4\alpha + 16\beta(n_x + n_y).$$

### 3.1.4 Total Per-Iteration Time

$$\boxed{\,T_{\text{iter}} = \underbrace{6 n_x n_y \cdot t_c}_{T_{\text{comp}}} + \underbrace{4\alpha + 16\beta(n_x + n_y)}_{T_{\text{comm}}}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** students should derive in a 14-mark question.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Speedup Bound

### 3.2.1 Serial Reference

The serial algorithm performs $5N^2$ FLOPs in $T_1 = 5N^2 t_c$ (ignoring I/O). The factor 5 includes the 4 neighbour adds plus the right-hand-side scaling per cell.

### 3.2.2 Parallel Runtime

$$T_P = K \cdot T_{\text{iter}},$$
where $K$ is the iteration count to convergence. For a clear demonstration, treat $K$ as a constant (typical Jacobi: $K \sim \mathcal{O}(\log N)$ — but for the **speedup bound** we use the per-iteration view).

### 3.2.3 Speedup Expression

$$S_P = \dfrac{T_1}{T_P} = \dfrac{5 N^2 t_c}{6 n_x n_y t_c + 4\alpha + 16\beta(n_x + n_y)}.$$

Using $n_x \approx N/p_x$ and $n_y \approx N/p_y$:

$$S_P \approx \dfrac{5 P}{6 + \dfrac{P \cdot [4\alpha + 16\beta(N/p_x + N/p_y)]}{5N^2 t_c}}.$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As $P \to \infty$ with $N$ fixed, $S_P$ saturates. The **saturating value** is the **communication-bound** regime. Setting $N^2 = C$ for clarity:
> $$\lim_{P \to \infty} S_P = \dfrac{5 N^2 t_c}{4\alpha P} = \mathcal{O}(N^2 / P) \to 0 \text{ unless } \alpha = 0.$$
> Hence strong scaling of Jacobi hits a hard wall at finite $P$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Iso-Efficiency Function

### 3.3.1 Definition

The iso-efficiency function $W(P)$ is the *total work* (problem size) needed to keep efficiency $E = T_1 / (P T_P)$ constant as $P$ grows.

### 3.3.2 Efficiency of Jacobi

With $N^2 = W$ (total cells):

$$E = \dfrac{5 W t_c}{P \left[ \dfrac{6W}{P} t_c + 4\alpha + 16\beta\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{W}{P}} \cdot 2 \right) \right]}.$$

(We used $n_x + n_y = 2\sqrt{W/P}$ assuming a square sub-grid.)

### 3.3.3 Solving for $W$ at Constant $E$

Set $E = E_0$ and isolate $W$:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_0 P \cdot \dfrac{6W}{P} t_c + E_0 P \left[ 4\alpha + 32\beta \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{P}} \right] &= 5 W t_c.
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
6 E_0 W t_c + 4\alpha E_0 P + 32\beta E_0 P \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{P}} &= 5 W t_c.
\end{aligned}$$

Group the $W$ terms on the right and the non-$W$ terms on the left:

$$\begin{aligned}
4\alpha E_0 P + 32\beta E_0 \sqrt{P W} &= 5 W t_c - 6 E_0 W t_c = W t_c (5 - 6 E_0).
\end{aligned}$$

Solve for $W$ (quadratic in $\sqrt{W}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{W} &= \dfrac{32 \beta E_0 \sqrt{P} \pm \sqrt{(32 \beta E_0)^2 P + 16 \alpha E_0 P \cdot 4 t_c (5-6E_0)}}{2 t_c (5-6E_0)}.
\end{aligned}$$

For large $P$, the term with $\beta^2$ dominates inside the square root (if $\beta$ scales similarly to $\alpha$), giving:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{W} \sim \mathcal{O}(\beta \sqrt{P}), \quad \text{so} \quad W = \mathcal{O}(\beta^2 P) = \mathcal{O}(P).
\end{aligned}$$

However, the more conservative bound keeping the $\alpha P$ term alive gives:

$$\begin{aligned}
W(P) = \mathcal{O}(P^{3/2}) \quad \text{for 2D Jacobi}.
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the classical textbook result.

> [!NOTE]
> **For 3D Jacobi**, the iso-efficiency is $W(P) = \mathcal{O}(P^2)$ because each process communicates with 6 neighbours instead of 4, and the surface area is $\mathcal{O}(P^{2/3})$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Stencils

| Property | 1D Jacobi | 2D Jacobi | 3D Jacobi |
|---|---|---|---|
| Neighbours per cell | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| Halo messages per process | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| Halo bytes per process | $\mathcal{O}(N/P)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N/\sqrt{P})$ | $\mathcal{O}(N/P^{2/3})$ |
| Iso-efficiency $W(P)$ | $\mathcal{O}(P)$ | $\mathcal{O}(P^{3/2})$ | $\mathcal{O}(P^2)$ |
| Strong-scaling efficiency | Excellent | Good | Limited |
| Memory per process | $\mathcal{O}(N/P)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N^2/P)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N^3/P)$ |
| Best MPI topology | 1D Cart | 2D Cart | 3D Cart |

## 3.5 Performance Properties — Numerical Worked Example

> [!TIP]
> **Typical KTU 14-mark part (b) question pattern:**
> "Consider a $1024 \times 1024$ Jacobi solver on $P = 64$ processes in a $8 \times 8$ grid. Given $\alpha = 1\,\mu s$, $\beta = 0.01\,\mu s/\text{byte}$, $t_c = 0.1\,ns$, compute the per-iteration time, speedup, and efficiency. Comment on the dominant cost."

**Step 1 — Sub-grid size:**

$$n_x = n_y = \dfrac{1024}{8} = 128 \text{ cells per process}.$$

**Step 2 — Computation time per iteration:**

$$T_{\text{comp}} = 6 \cdot 128 \cdot 128 \cdot 0.1\,\text{ns} = 6 \cdot 16384 \cdot 10^{-10}\,\text{s} \approx 9.83\,\mu s.$$

**Step 3 — Communication time per iteration:**

$$T_{\text{comm}} = 4 \cdot 1\,\mu s + 16 \cdot 0.01\,\mu s \cdot 128 \cdot 2 = 4\,\mu s + 40.96\,\mu s \approx 44.96\,\mu s.$$

**Step 4 — Total per-iteration time:**

$$T_{\text{iter}} = 9.83 + 44.96 \approx 54.79\,\mu s.$$

**Step 5 — Communication-to-computation ratio:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{T_{\text{comm}}}{T_{\text{comp}}} = \dfrac{44.96}{9.83} \approx 4.57.$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discussion:** Here $\rho > 1$ — the solver is **communication-bound** even at $P=64$. The student should observe that **bandwidth ($\beta$ term) dominates latency ($\alpha$ term)** by a factor of $\sim 10$. Optimisation must focus on (i) overlap of communication and computation using `MPI_Compute_post`/`MPI_Compute_wait` (MPI-4.0) or non-blocking halos, and (ii) reducing halo size (e.g., wider stencils, fewer iterations).

**Step 6 — Speedup:**

Serial time per iteration: $T_1 = 5 \cdot 1024^2 \cdot 0.1\,\text{ns} = 5 \cdot 1.05 \times 10^6 \cdot 10^{-10} \approx 524.3\,\mu s$.

$$S_{64} = \dfrac{524.3}{54.79} \approx 9.57.$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency:**

$$E_{64} = \dfrac{9.57}{64} \approx 0.149 \;\;(14.9\%).$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often report only $S_{64} \approx 9.57$ and forget the efficiency. A complete answer must include both, plus a comment on the **communication bottleneck**.

## 3.6 Performance Properties — The Overlap Trick (Non-Blocking Halos)

A crucial performance technique in MPI Jacobi solvers is to **overlap communication with computation**:

1. `MPI_Irecv` on all 4 halos (non-blocking receive).
2. `MPI_Isend` on all 4 halos (non-blocking send).
3. `MPI_Waitall` (or `MPI_Testall`) on the requests.
4. Local computation on interior.

The overlapped time becomes:
$$T_{\text{overlap}} = \max(T_{\text{comp}}, T_{\text{comm}}) \text{ (in best case)}.$$

For our example: $T_{\text{overlap}} \approx \max(9.83, 44.96) = 44.96\,\mu s$, recovering nearly all of $T_{\text{iter}} \approx 54.79\,\mu s$ to a lower bound of $44.96\,\mu s$ — a substantial improvement.

In MPI-4.0, the dedicated routines `MPI_Neighbor_allgather`, `MPI_Neighbor_allgatherv`, and the **partitioned communication** APIs (`MPI_Psend_init`, `MPI_Parrive`, `MPI_Pready`) provide topology-aware, low-overhead variants.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define a virtual topology in MPI. Differentiate between Cartesian and Graph topologies.*

**Model Answer:**

A **virtual topology** in MPI is a process-naming scheme that overlays a logical communication structure (grid, ring, tree, general graph) onto the set of processes. It does not alter the physical network but exposes a topology-aware API so the application can address processes by coordinates and use efficient neighbour collectives.

| Aspect | Cartesian Topology | Graph Topology |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | $d$-dimensional regular grid | Arbitrary directed graph |
| Constructor | `MPI_Cart_create` | `MPI_Graph_create` / `MPI_Dist_graph_create` |
| Suitability | Regular stencils (Jacobi, heat, CFD) | Irregular data (sparse matrices, FEM, social networks) |
| Neighbour query | `MPI_Cart_shift` (predefined directions) | `MPI_Graph_neighbors` (general adjacency) |
| Flexibility | Lower (regular only) | Higher (any graph) |
| Performance hint | Best for uniform halo exchange | Best for non-uniform neighbour sets |

**(3 marks — definition 1, table 2.)**

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three MPI routines used to construct or query a Cartesian virtual topology, and state their purpose.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **`MPI_Cart_create`** — Constructs a new Cartesian communicator from an existing one, given the dimensions $\mathbf{n}$, periodicity array, and a reordering flag. It returns a new communicator that supports topology-aware operations.
2. **`MPI_Cart_coords`** — Given a rank in the Cartesian communicator, returns its $\mathbf{d}$-dimensional coordinate vector $(c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_{d-1})$.
3. **`MPI_Cart_shift`** — Given a coordinate direction and a displacement, returns the source and destination ranks of the two neighbours along that direction. This is the most-used routine in **Jacobi-style stencil** implementations because it directly provides the four (or six) neighbour ranks.

**(3 marks — 1 mark each.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — MPI Implementation and Performance Properties

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, Module 4, PECST757]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the domain-decomposition strategy for parallelizing a 2D Jacobi solver on a $p_x \times p_y$ MPI Cartesian process grid. Discuss the role of halo (ghost) cells and the exchange pattern. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the per-iteration parallel runtime $T_{\text{iter}}$ of the 2D Jacobi solver. State clearly the assumptions (Hockney model, balanced partition). Use the result to obtain the speedup $S_P$ and efficiency $E_P$, and discuss how $S_P$ scales as $P$ grows for a fixed $N$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Domain Decomposition and Halo Exchange

**Step 1 — Partition the global grid:**
The global $N \times N$ grid is divided into $p_x \times p_y$ rectangular sub-grids, each owned by one process. Process $(i,j)$ owns the sub-grid from row $\lfloor N i / p_x \rfloor$ to row $\lfloor N (i+1) / p_x \rfloor - 1$ and similarly for columns.

**Step 2 — Halo allocation:**
Each process allocates a local array of size $(n_x + 2) \times (n_y + 2)$ doubles — the extra +2 rows and +2 columns are the **halo cells** (also called ghost cells). One row on each side and one column on each side.

**Step 3 — Neighbour ranks via `MPI_Cart_shift`:**
For each of the four directions, the routine returns the rank of the neighbour and `MPI_PROC_NULL` if the process lies on the physical boundary (no neighbour). The four directions are:
- Direction 0, disp +1 → bottom neighbour
- Direction 0, disp −1 → top neighbour
- Direction 1, disp +1 → right neighbour
- Direction 1, disp −1 → left neighbour

**Step 4 — Halo exchange (per iteration):**
- Send the **top interior row** to the *top neighbour* and receive the *bottom halo*.
- Send the **bottom interior row** to the *bottom neighbour* and receive the *top halo*.
- Send the **left interior column** to the *left neighbour* and receive the *right halo*.
- Send the **right interior column** to the *right neighbour* and receive the *left halo*.

The exchange can be done with `MPI_Sendrecv` (simpler) or with `MPI_Irecv`/`MPI_Isend` + `MPI_Waitall` (overlapped). The diagram in **Section 4.3** captures this flow.

**Step 5 — Local computation:**
After halos are updated, the process applies the 4-point stencil to its $n_x \times n_y$ interior cells.

**Step 6 — Convergence check:**
A local $\ell_2$ residual is computed and combined with `MPI_Allreduce` (or a column-sub-communicator `MPI_Cart_sub` to do it on rows only, which is more efficient).

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.2):** Shows the 4×4 process grid with 4-neighbour connections.

**(7 marks — 1 mark per step, 3 marks for the diagram/explanation.)**

#### Model Solution for (b) — Per-Iteration Runtime Derivation

**Assumptions:**
- Hockney communication model: $t_m = \alpha + \beta m$ for a message of size $m$ bytes.
- Balanced partition: $n_x = N / p_x$, $n_y = N / p_y$.
- Ignore convergence variability — analyse *one* iteration.

**Step 1 — Computation time per process:**

Each cell requires 6 FLOPs in the 4-point Jacobi update. Hence:
$$T_{\text{comp}} = 6 \cdot n_x \cdot n_y \cdot t_c.$$

**Step 2 — Communication time per process:**

Four messages per iteration. Two vertical messages of size $8 n_x$ bytes; two horizontal messages of size $8 n_y$ bytes.
$$T_{\text{comm}} = 4\alpha + 2\beta(8 n_x) + 2\beta(8 n_y) = 4\alpha + 16\beta(n_x + n_y).$$

**Step 3 — Per-iteration time:**
$$T_{\text{iter}} = 6 n_x n_y t_c + 4\alpha + 16\beta(n_x + n_y).$$

**Step 4 — Speedup:**

Serial time per iteration is $T_1 = 5 N^2 t_c$ (5 FLOPs per cell, including the $-h^2 f / 4$ term averaged into the additions). Then:
$$S_P = \dfrac{T_1}{T_{\text{iter}}} = \dfrac{5 N^2 t_c}{6 n_x n_y t_c + 4\alpha + 16\beta(n_x + n_y)}.$$

**Step 5 — Behaviour as $P$ grows (fixed $N$):**

Substituting $n_x = N/p_x$, $n_y = N/p_y$ and $P = p_x p_y$:
$$S_P = \dfrac{5 P}{6 + \dfrac{4\alpha P}{N^2 t_c} + \dfrac{16\beta P (n_x + n_y)}{N^2 t_c}}.$$

- For small $P$: the denominator is dominated by 6, so $S_P \approx 5P/6 \approx P$ (linear scaling).
- As $P$ grows: the terms $\alpha P$ and $\beta P (n_x + n_y)$ dominate, and $S_P$ saturates.
- As $P \to \infty$: the second term $\alpha P$ dominates, and $S_P \to 5 N^2 t_c / (4 \alpha P) \to 0$ for $P > N^2 t_c / (4\alpha) \cdot 5$.

**Efficiency:**
$$E_P = \dfrac{S_P}{P} = \dfrac{5 N^2 t_c}{6 N^2 t_c + 4\alpha P + 16\beta P (n_x + n_y)}.$$

$E_P$ decreases monotonically with $P$ and approaches 0 for very large $P$.

**Conclusion:** The 2D Jacobi solver exhibits **good but bounded** strong scaling. The bottleneck shifts from computation (small $P$) to communication (medium $P$) and finally to latency (very large $P$). To improve scaling, the implementation should (i) use non-blocking halos, (ii) choose a balanced process grid (`MPI_Dims_create` with `MPI_DISTRIBUTE_CYCLIC` for non-square $N$), and (iii) overlap communication with computation.

**(7 marks — 2 for assumptions, 2 for the derivation, 3 for the analysis & discussion.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question, Module 4, PECST757]**
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **three performance properties** of a parallel algorithm — strong scaling, weak scaling, and iso-efficiency — with reference to the 2D Jacobi solver. Provide mathematical expressions for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider a 2D Jacobi solver running on a $1024 \times 1024$ grid with $P = 16$ processes arranged as a $4 \times 4$ grid. Given $\alpha = 2\,\mu s$, $\beta = 0.05\,\mu s/\text{byte}$, $t_c = 0.2\,\text{ns}$, calculate: (i) computation time per process per iteration, (ii) communication time per process per iteration, (iii) total per-iteration time, (iv) speedup and efficiency. State whether the solver is **compute-bound** or **communication-bound**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Three Performance Properties

**Property 1 — Strong Scaling:**
- **Setup:** Total problem size $N$ is fixed; number of processes $P$ is increased.
- **Metric:** Speedup $S_P = T_1 / T_P$ and efficiency $E_P = S_P / P$.
- **Jacobi behaviour:** $S_P$ increases sub-linearly because the **halo-to-interior ratio** grows. For a balanced 2D partition, each process has $N/\sqrt{P} \times N/\sqrt{P}$ interior cells and halo of size $4 \cdot N/\sqrt{P}$. The communication-to-computation ratio is
$$\rho(P) = \dfrac{4\alpha + 16\beta \cdot 2N/\sqrt{P}}{6 N^2 t_c / P}.$$
As $P \to \infty$, $\rho \to \infty$ (communication-bound at large $P$).

**Property 2 — Weak Scaling (Gustafson):**
- **Setup:** Work per process $W/P$ is fixed; total work $W$ grows linearly with $P$.
- **Metric:** Scaled speedup $S_P^{\text{scaled}} = T_1 + P(T_P - T_\infty)$ (informal definition) or simply that $T_P$ remains approximately constant.
- **Jacobi behaviour:** If $N^2 = c P$ for a constant $c$, then $n_x = n_y = \sqrt{c}$, and
$$T_P = 6 c t_c + 4\alpha + 32 \beta \sqrt{c} = \text{const}.$$
So **weak scaling is close to ideal** (efficiency remains high) as long as $\alpha$ is small. This makes Jacobi a textbook example of a **weakly scalable** algorithm.

**Property 3 — Iso-Efficiency:**
- **Setup:** Find the total work $W(P)$ such that $E_P = E_0$ is constant as $P$ grows.
- **Metric:** The iso-efficiency function $W(P)$.
- **Jacobi behaviour:** From the analysis in Section 3.3, $W(P) = \mathcal{O}(P^{3/2})$ for 2D Jacobi. This is **between optimal** ($\mathcal{O}(P)$) and dense matrix-vector ($\mathcal{O}(P^2)$). It is more scalable than many algorithms because of the *small surface area* of the 4-neighbour stencil.

**Comparison table (insert in answer):**

| Property | Variable | Fixed | Best for Jacobi | $W(P)$ growth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strong scaling | $P$ | $N$ | Up to $P \approx N$ | n/a |
| Weak scaling | $P$, $N$ | $N/P$ | Excellent | $W \propto P$ |
| Iso-efficiency | $N(P)$ | $E$ | Good | $W = \mathcal{O}(P^{3/2})$ |

**(7 marks — 2 marks per property + 1 for the table.)**

#### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Performance Calculation

**Given:**
- $N = 1024$, $P = 16$, $p_x = p_y = 4$.
- $\alpha = 2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}$, $\beta = 5 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{s/byte}$, $t_c = 2 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s}$.

**Step 1 — Sub-grid dimensions:**
$$n_x = n_y = \dfrac{1024}{4} = 256 \text{ cells per process}.$$

**Step 2 — Computation time per process per iteration:**

$$T_{\text{comp}} = 6 \cdot n_x \cdot n_y \cdot t_c = 6 \cdot 256 \cdot 256 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s}.$$

$$T_{\text{comp}} = 6 \cdot 65536 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-10} = 7.864 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{s} = 78.64\,\mu s.$$

**Step 3 — Communication time per process per iteration:**

Vertical halos: $m_{\text{vert}} = 8 \cdot n_x = 8 \cdot 256 = 2048\,\text{bytes}$.
Horizontal halos: $m_{\text{horiz}} = 8 \cdot n_y = 2048\,\text{bytes}$.

$$T_{\text{comm}} = 4\alpha + 2\beta m_{\text{vert}} + 2\beta m_{\text{horiz}} = 4 \cdot 2\,\mu s + 4 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 2048\,\text{s}.$$

$$T_{\text{comm}} = 8\,\mu s + 4 \times 1.024 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 8\,\mu s + 409.6\,\mu s = 417.6\,\mu s.$$

**Step 4 — Total per-iteration time:**

$$T_{\text{iter}} = T_{\text{comp}} + T_{\text{comm}} = 78.64 + 417.6 = 496.24\,\mu s.$$

**Step 5 — Serial reference time:**

$$T_1 = 5 \cdot N^2 \cdot t_c = 5 \cdot 1024^2 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-10} = 5 \cdot 1.048 \times 10^6 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-10} \approx 1.0486\,\text{ms} = 1048.6\,\mu s.$$

**Step 6 — Speedup and efficiency:**

$$S_{16} = \dfrac{1048.6}{496.24} \approx 2.11, \qquad E_{16} = \dfrac{2.11}{16} \approx 0.132 \;\;(13.2\%).$$

**Step 7 — Bound identification:**

Communication-to-computation ratio:
$$\rho = \dfrac{T_{\text{comm}}}{T_{\text{comp}}} = \dfrac{417.6}{78.64} \approx 5.31.$$

Since $\rho \gg 1$, the solver is **communication-bound**. The bandwidth term $\beta m$ (about $409.6\,\mu s$) dominates the latency term ($8\,\mu s$) by a factor of ~51. Optimisation must focus on **reducing message size** (e.g., packing multiple halos) or **overlapping communication with computation**.

**(7 marks — 1 mark per major calculation step + 1 for the bound identification.)**

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 4 (PECST757):**
> 1. **Confusing virtual with physical topology** — students write "MPI sets up a torus network." MPI does **not** alter hardware; it overlays a *logical* grid. (Lose 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Omitting halo cells in the diagram** — the 4-neighbour structure must be drawn clearly; missing one direction (e.g., showing only top/bottom) loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\beta m$ term** in the communication time — the Hockney model $t_m = \alpha + \beta m$ has **two** parts; a $T_{\text{comm}} = 4\alpha$ answer is incomplete and is marked for **at most half** the communication sub-question.
> 4. **Mixing up $f$ and $E$** in Amdahl's law — $f$ is the *serial fraction*, $E$ is the *efficiency*; they are not the same.
> 5. **Using `MPI_Send`/`MPI_Recv` without considering deadlock** — a typical student mistake in the Jacobi halo exchange is to call blocking sends in a cyclic order, causing deadlock. The safe approach is `MPI_Sendrecv` or non-blocking calls.
> 6. **Reporting speedup without efficiency** — speedup alone is insufficient; KTU expects the **ratio $E = S/P$** to interpret the quality of parallelism.
> 7. **Ignoring boundary processes** — at the edges of the grid, the neighbour rank is `MPI_PROC_NULL`; the solver must skip communication in those directions.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4, PECST757 (Virtual Topologies, Jacobi, MPI Implementation, Performance):**
> - **Virtual topology = logical grid/graph on processes**, *not* a physical reconfiguration.
> - **Cartesian topology** for regular grids; **Graph topology** for irregular data.
> - **Key MPI calls:** `MPI_Cart_create`, `MPI_Dims_create`, `MPI_Cart_coords`, `MPI_Cart_rank`, `MPI_Cart_shift`, `MPI_Cart_sub`.
> - **Jacobi iteration** uses the 4-point (2D) or 6-point (3D) stencil; needs neighbour values of the previous iteration.
> - **Domain decomposition** splits the global grid into sub-grids; each process owns one sub-grid.
> - **Halo cells** (ghost cells) are extra rows/columns holding neighbour data; they must be exchanged every iteration.
> - **Exchange pattern:** 4 messages per iteration per process (top, bottom, left, right) in 2D; 6 in 3D.
> - **Per-iteration runtime:** $T_{\text{iter}} = 6 n_x n_y t_c + 4\alpha + 16\beta(n_x + n_y)$ (2D Jacobi).
> - **Hockney model:** $t_m = \alpha + \beta m$ — *two* terms: latency $\alpha$ and inverse bandwidth $\beta$.
> - **Speedup** $S_P = T_1/T_P$ and **efficiency** $E_P = S_P/P$; always quote both.
> - **Amdahl's law** bounds strong scaling; **Gustafson** addresses weak scaling.
> - **Strong scaling** of Jacobi hits a wall at $P \approx N^2 t_c / (4\alpha)$.
> - **Weak scaling** of Jacobi is near-ideal because halo fraction per process stays constant.
> - **Iso-efficiency** of 2D Jacobi is $W(P) = \mathcal{O}(P^{3/2})$; of 3D Jacobi is $\mathcal{O}(P^2)$.
> - **Communication-to-computation ratio** $\rho = T_{\text{comm}} / T_{\text{comp}}$ decides whether the solver is compute- or communication-bound.
> - **Optimisation tactics:** non-blocking halos (`MPI_Irecv`/`MPI_Isend`), persistent communication (`MPI_Send_init`), overlap with computation, neighbour collectives (`MPI_Neighbor_allgather` in MPI 4.0).
> - **MPI-4.0 enhancements:** partitioned point-to-point communication (`MPI_Psend_init`/`MPI_Pready`/`MPI_Parrive`) for fine-grained overlap.
> - **Exam mantra:** "Virtual topology, halo exchange, Hockney model, three performance properties" — these are the four pillars of any 14-mark answer on this topic.
> - **Boundary handling:** Use `MPI_PROC_NULL` for edges; the MPI implementation silently no-ops on those messages.
> - **MPI_Dims_create:** Call it with the *number of dimensions* and the total number of processes; it returns a *suggested* balanced factorization.
> - **Reordering:** `MPI_Cart_create` may rearrange ranks to put logically adjacent processes on physically adjacent hardware (e.g., a node's cores). This is automatic and **transparent** to the user.
> - **Sub-communicators:** `MPI_Cart_sub` can split a 2D grid into 1D row/column communicators — useful for **redistribution** or **convergence checks** along a single direction.
> - **Communication patterns in MPI 4.0:** the *neighborhood collectives* are the modern way to do halo exchange; they automatically use the topology information and are typically faster than hand-written `MPI_Sendrecv` loops.
> - **Final check before submitting an exam answer:** Have you (i) defined virtual topology, (ii) listed 3–4 MPI routines, (iii) drawn the halo-exchange diagram, (iv) derived the runtime, (v) reported speedup AND efficiency, and (vi) discussed all three performance properties? If yes, you have a complete 14-mark answer.
